AAS 302.title: IMMIGRATION AND ETHNICITY
AAS 302.description: Examine the changing population of the United States from colonial times to present.

AAS 310.title: ASIAN AMERICAN VISUAL ARTS
AAS 310.description: An introduction to Asian American studies through a variety of disciplines. Examines the unique culturally-influenced psychologies of various Asian American groups, including such concepts as identity formation, enculturation, assimilation and resistance, and racial and psychological normativity.

AAS 312.title: INTRO TO ASIAN AMERICAN HIST
AAS 312.description: Introduces students to the national and transnational histories of Asian Americans in the United States. Explores a wide range of themes related to the Asian American experience. Asian American Studies 312 and History 317L (Topic 4) may not both be counted.

AAS 320K.title: THEORIZING THE HUMAN
AAS 320K.description: Trace the continuities and transformations in constructions of populations as more or less human, from settler-colonial conquest to the post-Trump era. Examine what logics mark racialized populations as disposable during the colonization of the Americas, and which systematically condition racialized subjects to a state of enduring siege.

AAS 325G.title: HIST SE ASIAN DIASP IN US

AAS 330L.title: ANTHROPOL OF THE HIMALAYAS
AAS 330L.description: Examine the history and culture of the Himalayan region, including Northeast India, sections of Pakistan and Afghanistan, Tibet, and especially Nepal.

ACC 151.title: ACCOUNTING CAREERS EXPLORATION
ACC 151.description: Presentations by professional accountants and managers.

ACC 152.title: MPA DISTINGUISHED SPKR LYCEUM
ACC 152.description: Discussion of current issues confronting the accounting profession.

ACC 310F.title: FOUNDATIONS OF ACCOUNTING
ACC 310F.description: An introduction to financial and managerial accounting, with emphasis on the content, interpretation, and uses of accounting reports. Discussion of the determination and reporting of net income and financial position, and the theories underlying business financial statements; consideration of managerial accounting topics designed to extend the student's knowledge to the planning and controlling of the operations of the firm. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Business Administration degree. Taught as a Web-based course.

ACC 311.title: FUNDAMENTALS OF FINANCIAL ACC
ACC 311.description: Concepts and their application in transaction analysis and financial statement preparation; analysis of financial statements. Accounting 311 and 311H may not both be counted.

ACC 311H.title: FUNDMNTLS OF FIN ACC: HON
ACC 311H.description: An exploration of concepts and their application in transaction analysis and financial statement preparation; and analysis of financial statements. Accounting 311 and 311H may not both be counted.

ACC 312.title: FUNDAMENTALS OF MANAGERIAL ACC
ACC 312.description: Introduction to cost behavior, budgeting, responsibility accounting, cost control, and product costing. Accounting 312 and 312H may not both be counted.

ACC 326.title: FINANCIAL ACC-INTERMEDIATE
ACC 326.description: Theoretical foundation, concepts, and principles underlying financial statements; current assets; current liabilities; property, plant, and equipment; short-term investments; present value analysis.

ACC 327.title: FINANCIAL STATEMENT ANALYSIS
ACC 327.description: Study of financial statements and their related footnotes; tools and procedures common to financial statement analysis; the relationships among business transactions, environmental forces (political, economic, and social), and reported financial information; and how financial statement information can help solve certain business problems.

ACC 329.title: MANAGERIAL ACC AND CONTROL
ACC 329.description: The origination, processing, reporting, and use in business operations of accounting information for management purposes.

ACC 334M.title: HEALTHCARE ACCOUNTING
ACC 334M.description: Identify and analyze healthcare revenues, expenses, management problems, and ethical dilemmas, applying theories and tools to recommend strategic solutions.

ACC 355.title: INTRODUCTION TO TAXATION
ACC 355.description: The role of taxes in contemporary society and their impact on individuals and business.

ACC 356.title: FINANCIAL ACC CONCEPTS & RSCH
ACC 356.description: Conceptual framework of financial accounting; research methods in financial reporting; and financial reporting institutions and regulations.

ACC 358C.title: AUDITING AND ASSURANCE SERVS
ACC 358C.description: Information quality assurance, auditing, and control, considered from the perspective of a business manager who must decide the type and amount of assurance to acquire.

ACC 359.title: MANAGERIAL/COST ACCOUNTING
ACC 359.description: Analysis of manufacturing costs, development of cost estimates, and preparation of relevant information for management decision making.

ACC 362.title: AUDITING AND CONTROL
ACC 362.description: Professional practice standards and procedures of auditing: ethics, legal liability, sampling methods, control systems, control design, and control evaluation.

ACC 364.title: FUNDAMENTALS OF TAXATION
ACC 364.description: Introduction to the role of taxes in contemporary society and their impact on individuals and business entities; emphasis on federal income taxation.

ACC 366C.title: INTL ACCTING/TRANSFER PRICING
ACC 366C.description: Discover how to price transactions between subsidiaries of multinational corporations buying and selling goods, services, intellectual property and other assets in light of tax obligations and other considerations.

ACC 367P.title: INTERNAL AUDITING PRACTICUM
ACC 367P.description: Accounting 367P and 372T (Topic: Internal Auditing Practicum) may not both be counted.

ACC 372T.title: 1-OIL/GAS ACCT/SUSTAINABILITY
ACC 372T.description: Study selected accounting topics. Introduction to basic concepts of personal wealth management and financial planning, including insurance and risk management, investment management, tax planning, estate planning, and retirement planning. Designed for upper-level finance and accounting majors interested in a career in financial services or students pursuing master in professional accounting with interest in this area of the accounting profession.

ADV 303.title: ADVERTISING/POPULAR CULTURE
ADV 303.description: An introduction to the role advertising plays in American society, and the values and relationships offered in the messages that are delivered. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Science in Advertising degree or the Bachelor of Science in Public Relations degree.

ADV 305.title: FUNDAMENTALS OF ADVERTISING
ADV 305.description: Fundamentals and practices of advertising in relation to economies, societies, and mass communication. Advertising 305 and 318J may not both be counted.

ADV 305S.title: INTEGRATED COMM FOR SPORTS
ADV 305S.description: Examination of the mass media industry in relation to the field of sports. Focus on the "off-the-field" industries of advertising, marketing, and public relations that impact sports; rights fees contracts; pre-game shows; gameday production; in-stadium activities; and press coverage.

ADV 309R.title: INTRO TO ADV/PUBL RELS RSCH
ADV 309R.description: Introduction to concepts and methods of statistics, with emphasis on analyzing personal and group behaviors. Includes exploratory data analysis, correlation and regression, descriptive statistics, sampling distributions, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 315.title: HISTORY & DEVEL OF ADV-WB
ADV 315.description: The evolution and development of advertising in the United States in a social, historical, economic, and cultural context. Taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 316.title: CREATIVITY/AMERICAN CULTURE
ADV 316.description: A cross-disciplinary view of the creative process and creative products. The conceptual core of film, fine arts, advertising, architecture, and literature. Advertising 316 and Public Relations 316 may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 318J.title: INTRO TO INTEGRTD BRAND COMM
ADV 318J.description: The functions of advertising and public relations; role in marketing/communications mix; economic and social influence; advertising and public relations institutions and media; campaigns and appropriations; retail and business-to-business aspects. Advertising 305 and 318J may not both be counted.

ADV 319.title: PSYCHOLOGY OF ADVERTISING
ADV 319.description: A review of basic findings of the behavioral sciences dealing with perception, personality, group behavior, psychological appeals, and their application to advertising, public relations, and marketing as persuasive communication. Taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 320.title: INTEG COMM FOR NONPROF ORGS
ADV 320.description: Explores integrated communication and branding in a variety of nonprofit settings. Examines the strategic, integrated use of multiple communication approaches, including advertising, public relations, new media, promotions, sponsorships, and special events. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 323.title: PUBLIC COMM OF SCI/TECH-WB
ADV 323.description: Explores the rapidly growing field of science communication. Examines the intersection of science, media, and society, focusing on media's impact on how people come to understand, perceive, and act relative to scientific issues. Special attention will be directed toward the strategic communication of science and maximizing scientists' engagement with public audiences. Taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 325.title: INTRO TO ADVERTISING CREATVTY
ADV 325.description: Introduction to the theory and practice of creativity, basic elements and principles of design and software applications as they relate to the development of advertising.

ADV 330.title: STRATEGIC SCIENCE COMM-WB
ADV 330.description: Using insights from strategic communication and social science, explore how to design effective science communication. Examine the crucial roles that strategic thinking plays in maximizing science communication's ability to achieve positive behavioral impacts. Investigate case studies and focus on how to evaluate the effectiveness of science communication efforts. Taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 331.title: ENVIRONMENTAL COMM CAMPAIGNS
ADV 331.description: Combine discussions of environmental current events with hands-on agency/client experience to understand how to develop authentic communication strategies and tactics that can protect the environment and advance cutting-edge sustainability technologies.

ADV 332.title: TECHNOLOGY MARKETING/ADV-WB
ADV 332.description: Explores case histories, interviews with leading technology advertising and marketing executives, and latest trends in digital advertising, both mobile and social, as well as its impact across marketing segments and industries. Taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 332C.title: NEW MEDIA ENTREPRNRLSM-WB
ADV 332C.description: Covers real life experience/exposure with a series of interviews with real-life practitioners from social media, venture capital, and others with expertise in the field. Explores important trends in digital content and digital business models; a primer for those who wish to learn about entrepreneurialism, technology, and it's intersection with media. Taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 332D.title: ENTREPRENEURIALISM IN COMM
ADV 332D.description: Project-oriented exercise where small teams conceptualize a business idea in communications, curate the insights and opinions of fellow team members, and ultimately develop a business plan around their new endeavor. A panel of local entrepreneurs will provide feedback and critique. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 334.title: INTERNATIONAL ADVERTISING
ADV 334.description: Major issues in international advertising and advertising directed at cultural minorities within countries.

ADV 339K.title: DIGITAL GRAPHIC COMM
ADV 339K.description: Introduction to the fundamentals of good graphic design, as well as production basics. Develops digital design skills using industry-standard software, from print (Adobe InDesign, Photoshop, Illustrator) to motion (Adobe Premiere, AfterEffects) and interactive (HTML, CSS, WordPress). Assumes no prior knowledge. Taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 339L.title: BRAND STORYTELLING
ADV 339L.description: Examination of how classic elements of storytelling, semiotics, and symbolism can be used to create and manage a modern brand's story and 'meaning'. Create visual, verbal, and video examples of brand storytelling that go beyond traditional advertising and public relations.

ADV 343K.title: PORTFOLIO I
ADV 343K.description: Basic advertising art direction and copywriting skills, including indications for graphics, headlines, and body copy. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

ADV 344K.title: ADVRT/PUBL RELATN RESEARCH
ADV 344K.description: Introduction to social science research methods as used in advertising and marketing; emphasis on survey research and secondary data.

ADV 345J.title: ADV/PUBL REL MEDIA PLANNING
ADV 345J.description: An investigation of media-market measurements and strategies across the full spectrum of the industry; development of media communications plan. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 100 or more students.

ADV 348S.title: THE BUSINESS OF SPORTS MEDIA
ADV 348S.description: Examination of the business of sports-related media, including over-the-air television, cable, satellite television, print, radio, social media, and internet-based media, and the impact of such media on sports presentation and development, professional franchises, colleges, and conferences. Emphasis on how the business of sports media generates revenue and content, and the business models for each type of media, the challenges to such models, and the likely future changes to them.

ADV 350.title: ADVERTISING INTERNSHIP
ADV 350.description: Practical work experiences in advertising sales, creative management, and research with advertisers, agencies, media, or auxiliary services.

ADV 353.title: ADV/PUBLIC RELS LAW/ETHICS
ADV 353.description: Advertising 353 and Public Relations 353 may not both be counted.

ADV 361.title: CREATIVITY IN AI-WB
ADV 361.description: Explore the intersection of artificial intelligence (AI) and creative processes. Examine how AI can enhance and augment human creativity in various domains such as art, music, writing, and design. Analyze the ethical implications and societal impacts of AI-driven creativity. Learn the knowledge and skills used to harness the power of AI to push the boundaries of human creativity. Taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 365.title: AUDIENCE DEVEL/ENGAGEMENT
ADV 365.description: Examine the latest methods used to better understand audiences and their motivations. Explore new approaches and tools for engaging audiences in different content strategies, and what metrics can be used to measure their engagement in ways that prove valuable to content creators, marketers, and audiences.

ADV 367.title: EXPERIMENTAL STORYTELLING
ADV 367.description: Working with real-world clients, examine design thinking and human-computer interaction techniques while ideating, synthesizing concepts, creating user experiences, and developing a proof of concept (PoC) exemplary towards becoming an experience designer.

ADV 368L.title: PORTFOLIO III
ADV 368L.description: Designed for aspiring art directors and copywriters in the context of building a creative advertising portfolio. Focus on the development of a personal brand, networking with professional contacts, and submission of creative work to industry competitions. Receive evaluations on portfolios by industry professionals. With consent of instructor, may be repeated once for credit. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

ADV 369.title: SOCIAL MEDIA
ADV 369.description: Introduction to social networking platforms and their application in relation to media plans, brand building, and insights. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 370J.title: INTEGRATED COMMUNICATNS MGMT
ADV 370J.description: Cases and problems dealing with the management of advertising and promotional programs; media and creative strategies; consumer, retail, industrial, and public service applications. Advertising 370J and Public Relations 367 may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

ADV 373.title: INTEGRATED COMM CAMPAIGNS
ADV 373.description: Concept of media mix; matching product, consumer, media profiles; conception, research, planning, and execution of advertising campaigns; special emphasis on advanced copywriting, layout, and production for print and broadcast media.

ADV 377.title: 1-ADVANCED MEDIA STRATEGIES
ADV 377.description: An in-depth dive into independent and group work with insights and strategies.

ADV 377M.title: 3-DIGITAL METRICS
ADV 377M.description: Topics in media and its effects on advertising and public relations strategies. An exploration of the evolving scope of digital metrics and analytics by looking at the three dimensions colliding in interactive media: business, communications, and technology. Advertising 377M and Public Relations 377M may not both be counted unless the topics vary.

ADV 378.title: CONSUMER BEHAVIOR
ADV 378.description: Explore the required skills to plan, capture, edit, and produce digital social media content. Advertising 378 (Topic: Digitl Social Media Prodctn) and 378 (Topic 44) may not both be counted.

ADV 378S.title: CREATING SPORTS BRANDS FOR NIL
ADV 378S.description: Topics in sports media related to issues in traditional and new media as they effect sports and entertainment advertising and public relations. An examination of the history of college sports media, with an emphasis on the evolution of sports coverage from NCAA-controlled limited broadcasting of football and basketball,to present day intercollegiate athletics coverage available via multiple outlets. Advertising 378S and Public Relations 378S may not both be counted unless the topics vary.

ADV 379J.title: 1-SUPV INDIV SPECIAL STUDIES
ADV 379J.description: Supervised individual special studies for which separate courses are not available.

ADV 650.title: ADVERTISING INTERNSHIP-UTNY
ADV 650.description: Practical work experiences in advertising sales, creative management, and research with advertisers, agencies, media, or auxiliary services.

AED 330.title: INTRO TO ART EDUCATION
AED 330.description: Introduction to art education. Examine philosophy, current trends, instructional methods, evaluation, and advocacy. Explore careers in art education, museum education, and cultural or social educational settings. Visual Art Studies 330 and Art Education 330 may not both be counted.

AED 341C.title: LEARNERS/INSTRUCTIONAL SITES
AED 341C.description: Explore instructional procedures, observations, and evaluation of early childhood through grade twelve classroom- and community-based art instruction. Visual Art Studies 341C and Art Education 341C may not both be counted.

AED 361C.title: CRITICISM/CONVERSATION ART
AED 361C.description: Explore individual and professional evaluations of visual imagery, artifacts, and artistic production in a variety of settings. Visual Art Studies 361C and Art Education 361C may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 304.title: FNDTNS OF ART/ENTERTAIN TECH
AET 304.description: Explore the industries, practices, and concepts defining arts and entertainment technologies in the twenty-first century.

AET 311.title: MEDIA AND MOTION
AET 311.description: Introduction to effective visual storytelling with 2D and 3D digital content. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 311 and 319 (Topic: Art & Content) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 312.title: GAMES AND INTERACTIVITY
AET 312.description: Introduction to user-centered design process, interactivity, and game design. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 312 and 319 (Topic: Design & Interactivity) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 313.title: CODE AND FABRICATION
AET 313.description: Introduction to building interactive physical projects with hands-on code, microcontrollers, and cardboard. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 313 and 319 (Topic: Media & Technology) may not both be counted.

AET 314.title: SOUND AND SPACE
AET 314.description: Introduction to dynamic sound, lighting, and video projection for screens and spaces. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 313 and 319 (Topic: Sound & Space) may not both be counted.

AET 320P.title: SOUND DESIGN
AET 320P.description: Explore the techniques and tools used in the creation of sound for film, television, and video games. Learn about the history of sound design, the role of the sound designer, and the process of creating a soundtrack from concept to final mix.

AET 321J.title: INTRODUCTION TO GAME AUDIO
AET 321J.description: Explore the sound design, foley, and audio implementation in video games. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 321J and 323E may not both be counted.

AET 322K.title: INTRODUCTION TO AUDIO CODING
AET 322K.description: Explore programming methods for MIDI and audio signal processing in the visual interface language Max/MSP. Examine how to manage audio signal flow and communications with external devices such as keyboard controllers, pedals and other sensors. Discuss the creation of systems for musical interactivity in live performance and immersive sound installation applications and integration with Ableton Live. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 322K and 320G may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 323D.title: INTERACTIVE MUSIC
AET 323D.description: Create real-time interactive music using Ableton Live and Musical Instrument Digital Interface (MIDI) controllers. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 323D and 339 (Topic: Interactive Music) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 324.title: THEMED ENTERTAINMENT DESIGN
AET 324.description: Explore best practices behind the design of themed entertainment experiences through a variety of lectures, group projects, and individual study. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 324 and 339 (Topic: Themed Entertainment Design) may not both be counted.

AET 324D.title: PRINCIPLES OF ANIMATION
AET 324D.description: An introduction to the principles of animation for 2D and 3D systems. Use various techniques to convey a sense of weight, emotion, and story for simple and complex objects in both 2D and 3D.

AET 324J.title: VISUAL STORYTELLING
AET 324J.description: Explore graphic communication, visual storytelling, and digital visualization of objects, spaces, environments, information, and data. Utilize pre-rendered realme, and procedural systems to explore 2D and 3D previsualization techniques, infographic design, data processing, and rendering systems.

AET 326E.title: 3D ANIMATION AND RIGGING
AET 326E.description: An introduction to 3D animation techniques and rigging systems.

AET 330C.title: BUSINESS OF ENTERTAINMENT
AET 330C.description: Explore the fundamentals of producing entertainment from inspiration to execution as well as all aspects involved in producing live stage and on screen commercial productions. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 330C and 339 (Topic: Business of Entertainment) may not both be counted.

AET 330T.title: SOUND DESIGN SKILLS
AET 330T.description: Exploration of innovative topics in design. Develop the ability to understand and execute the technical and mechanical sound and audio needs of a theatrical production. Discover sound physics, sound reinforcement, mixing, digital audio workstations, cueing and playback, and troubleshooting.

AET 333.title: MANAGING CREATIVE PROJECTS
AET 333.description: Topics in video game development. Effectively plan, manage, and oversee the development of team based projects and productions, from initial estimation to day-to-day execution.

AET 334C.title: LEVEL DESIGN
AET 334C.description: Design and build interactive 2D or 3D game levels (game maps) using an industry standard tool such as Unity or Unreal. Explore the design pipeline for creating levels from concept and layout, to whiteboxing, creating and integrating environment art and sound, worldbuilding, and adding simple scripted interactions, animations, sound effects, and VFX. Examine the different design process for small indie games compared to big AAA games. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 334C and 339 (Topic: Level Design) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 334E.title: VIDEO GAME ART PIPELINE
AET 334E.description: An introduction to creating and working with game ready assets in 2D and 3D. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 334E and 339 (Topic: Game Art Pipeline) may not both be counted.

AET 334F.title: VIDEO GAME SCRIPTING I
AET 334F.description: Introduction to scripting interactive content for games using an industry-standard scripting language and game engine. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 335M.title: 2D ANIMATION FOR GAMES
AET 335M.description: Learn the tools and comprehensive knowledge to create beautiful, functional, and dynamic art for video games. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 337N.title: INTRODUCTION TO NARRATIVE
AET 337N.description: Explore the origins, formal elements, and best practices of good storytelling across multiple mediums through study, analysis, and writing practice. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 337N and 339 (Topic: Intro to Narrative) may not both be counted.

AET 339.title: DESIGNING FOR CLIENTS
AET 339.description: Advanced topics in arts and entertainment technologies. Create complex micro-controller-based synthesizers for use in music production, live performance, sound design, and sound reactive immersive installations. Engage in projects that include programming with Arduino, audio electronics, sound synthesis methods and history, creative interface design, and creating sensor-based sound devices.

AET 340D.title: INTERACTIVE INSTALLATION DSGN
AET 340D.description: Explore the skillsets and collaborative processes involved in creating interactive installations. Learn interactive technology, physical computing, and user engagement to create immersive and participatory experiences. May be taken twice for credit.

AET 342C.title: 3D MODELING AND TEXTURING
AET 342C.description: Utilize hard surface and organic modeling workflows, modern texturing techniques, and physically-based shaders to create efficient assets based on clear content goals. May be taken three times for credit. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 350C.title: ADVANCED CREATIVE CODING
AET 350C.description: Explore advanced computational concepts and their application to more diverse creative media domains including; audio, video, music, and the web. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 339 (Topic: Advanced Creative Coding) and 350C may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AET 353.title: ART MAKING IN VR
AET 353.description: Advanced topics in game development. Explore game programming paradigms in C++ using the Unreal Engine. Examine event-driven, component-oriented and object-oriented programming, as well as collaborative software development using source control, bug-tracking, and code documentation. Develop and apply skills towards building a game on the Unreal Engine.

AET 357.title: SOLO STUDIO
AET 357.description: Build creative confidence through a range of methodologies including iterative design and prototyping, exploring research strategies, and exercising critical reflection.

AET 358.title: 1-NEXT LEVEL ARCADE
AET 358.description: Focus on team based projects that culminate in large events, presentations, or performances. Utilize game design skills to create experiences that improve lives. Learn about transformational game development that will support therapy, advance scientific research, bolster mental health, and revolutionize education. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 358 (Topic 7) and 353 (Topic: Games For Good) may not both be counted.

AET 374.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY
AET 374.description: Independent study or research in arts and entertainment technologies.

AET 376.title: GAME CAPSTONE: 2-D
AET 376.description: Work in small teams from different disciplines to develop 2D games. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. Additional hours to be arranged.

AET 378H.title: HONORS SENIOR THESIS
AET 378H.description: Participate in a large scale senior project. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

AFR 301.title: AFRICAN AMERICAN CULTURE
AFR 301.description: Survey of African American culture in the United States from the 1600s to the present. Subjects include the circumstances and responses of blacks during North American enslavement, the Civil War, Reconstruction, the Great Migration, the Harlem Renaissance, the civil rights movement, and contemporary contexts.

AFR 302D.title: ANALYZING SOCIAL DATA
AFR 302D.description: Subjects include conceptualization and operationalization in quantitative measurement, the calculation and interpretation of descriptive statistics and statistical relationships, the application of statistical techniques to understand social phenomenon, and techniques for presenting results from quantitative analysis.

AFR 303.title: INTRO TO BLACK STUDIES-WB
AFR 303.description: Introduction to canonical black studies literature, themes, and theories. Taught online during scheduled times. Includes live-streaming video and requires real-time participation. Go to http://laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

AFR 304.title: INTRO TO THE STUDY OF AFRICA
AFR 304.description: Examines the complex histories, intellectual entanglements, and enduring hierarchies of Africa and African studies. Explores the evolution of African studies (both intellectually and institutionally), particularly in regards to the emergence of black studies. African and African Diaspora Studies 304 and 317C (Topic: Introduction to the Study of Africa) may not both be counted.

AFR 314C.title: ART/ARCH OF ANCIENT EGYPT
AFR 314C.description: Introduction to the complex and intriguing culture of ancient Egypt and to its legacy in society through the examination of its art and archaeology.

AFR 315G.title: THE UNITED STATES AND AFRICA
AFR 315G.description: History of political, economic, and cultural relations between the United States and Africa from the early origins of the slave trade to the present.

AFR 315P.title: INTRO BLACK WOMEN'S STUDIES
AFR 315P.description: Interdisciplinary examination of the complexities of black womanhood in America through an investigation of history, identity, popular culture, and black feminism/womanism, as well as social and political activism.

AFR 315Q.title: BLACK QUEER ART WORLDS
AFR 315Q.description: Exploration of over two decades of work produced by and about black queer subjects throughout the circum-Atlantic world. Provides an introduction to various artists and intellectuals of the black queer diaspora, as well as an examination of the viability of black queer aesthetic practice as a form of theorizing.

AFR 315T.title: AFRICAN AMERICAN LIT AND CUL
AFR 315T.description: Explores texts, drawn from a variety of genres and periods, that indicate the range of African American experiences and how those experiences are influenced by issues such as class, ethnicity, gender, sexuality, and race.

AFR 315U.title: MUSIC OF AFRICAN AMERICANS
AFR 315U.description: Introduction to the variety of modes of expression of African American culture in music and other related genres.

AFR 316E.title: BLACKNESS AND COMICS
AFR 316E.description: Examine the relationship between race and the sequential aesthetics of comic books and comic strips. Explore comics strips, underground comics, and superhero comics from the colonial period to the present day.

AFR 316G.title: POLICE/PRISONS/INEQUALITY
AFR 316G.description: Explore the ethical issues related to inequality and the United States criminal legal system. Examine the experiences of the poor, Indigenous people, immigrants, racial minorities, women, sexual minorities, and gender-nonconforming people.

AFR 320C.title: POWER/PLACE MAKING TX HIST-WB
AFR 320C.description: Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

AFR 320T.title: TEXAS HISTORY'S HISTORY-WB
AFR 320T.description: Explore evolving ideas and meanings concerning the most significant events, personages, and social processes in Texas' history. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled.

AFR 325C.title: LATINX SEXUALITIES
AFR 325C.description: Examine Latinx sexualities from a historical perspective to comprehend the social, cultural, political, and economic factors, which have shaped these experiences.

AFR 330K.title: AFR AM LIT THRU HARL RENAIS
AFR 330K.description: A survey of African American writing, including autobiography, poetry, fiction, and drama. Authors may include Douglass, Jacobs, Frances E. W. Harper, Chestnutt, Du Bois, Hurston, and Hughes.

AFR 330R.title: LIT OF BLACK POLITICS
AFR 330R.description: Examines the novels, plays, and critical essays of Ralph Ellison, James Baldwin, and Toni Morrison as works of democratic political theorizing and political engagement.

AFR 330W.title: BLACK QUEER LITERATURE/FILM
AFR 330W.description: Analyzes written works, films, and videos by and about lesbians, bisexual, transgender, and gay black people, with an emphasis on understanding the historical and theoretical construction of sexual and gender identities and sexual/cultural practices in black communities.

AFR 331F.title: BLACK HORROR/PSYCHOANALYSIS
AFR 331F.description: Explore the intersection of race, horror aesthetics, and psychoanalytic theory.

AFR 335E.title: INTRO TO AFRICAN ART-WB
AFR 335E.description: Investigates the lives and works of several artists who have made substantial contributions to the definition, history, and interpretation of the visual arts. Taught as a Web-based course.

AFR 335J.title: CONTEMP ART AFR DIASPORA
AFR 335J.description: Examines a wide range of artists, practicing in different parts of the world, who are part of the African diaspora via such factors as the Atlantic slave trade and various patterns of international migration.

AFR 335L.title: BLACK FIGURE AM ART/POP CUL
AFR 335L.description: Examine how popular culture and artistic practices facilitate or challenge social constructions of "Blackness" in the United States.

AFR 335T.title: PHOTOGRAPHS/BLACKNESS IN US
AFR 335T.description: Examine issues raised at the intersection of Blackness and photography in contemporary art of the United States.

AFR 340F.title: AFRICAN HIST FILMS/PHOTOS
AFR 340F.description: Explore the social, economic, and political challenges of the past fifty years of Africa's history through an examination of several popular films.

AFR 340K.title: MEDICINE IN AFRICAN HISTORY
AFR 340K.description: An exploration of how communities have confronted disease throughout Africa's history.

AFR 340M.title: GEOGS INTL DEVEL IN AFRICA
AFR 340M.description: Critically examines the major approaches to "Development" in colonial and postcolonial eras, with a focus on a range of African resources: from water to wildlife, forests to farms, airways to rangelands, and including a consideration of African bodies themselves as sites of development and resistance. African and African Diaspora Studies 340M and Geography 330F may not both be counted.

AFR 340N.title: AFRICAN QUEER STUDIES
AFR 340N.description: Examines the development of African queer studies as the interdisciplinary study of sexuality on the African continent, and its impact on the academic and activist realm. Focus on intersectional feminist and queer theory and how this field offers tools that can be used to rethink issues of oppression and domination in relationship to race, nation, sex, gender, and sexuality on the African continent. Of particular interest will be LGBTQ activism and the ways in which queer Africans engage and shape their sexuality, and create forms of belonging in their countries and their communities.

AFR 341C.title: FASHION IN AFRICA
AFR 341C.description: Examine fashion history through readings, videos, social media, and textile making to provide an immersive experience in African design history. Address the ways in which textiles are tied to class distinctions and politics, wrapped up in the history of trade (including human trafficking and slavery), mixed into revolutionary struggles and post-independent governance, and inspire haute couture and global fashion trends.

AFR 345F.title: SEX & POWER IN AFR DIASPORA
AFR 345F.description: Exploration of various experiences and theories of sex, intimacy, and desire alongside intellectual and artistic engagements with power hierarchies and spirituality across transnational black communities. Subjects include the concept of "erotic subjectivity" from various theoretical and methodological angles, principally within African diasporic contexts.

AFR 345P.title: AFRICAN RELIGION IN NEW WORLD
AFR 345P.description: Explore the contributions of scholars and artists who engage African diaspora religions in their work through multiple conceptual approaches.

AFR 350S.title: BLACK CHURCH IN AFR AMER POLIT
AFR 350S.description: Explore the political role of the Black church in the African American experience.

AFR 352F.title: SOCIOCUL INFLUENCES ON LEARN
AFR 352F.description: Examine human learning in multisocial, multilingual, and multicultural contexts; realities of society and their impact on learning; social concerns such as prejudice, stereotyping, cross-cultural attitudes, bilingual issues, parent and community involvement.

AFR 352Q.title: BLACK GEOGRAPHIES
AFR 352Q.description: Explore the relationship between Blackness and the production of space and place. Examine the tensions and possibilities that emerge when Black people are rendered un-geographic as a fundamental component of racial capitalism while they are simultaneously creating spaces of freedom under constant threats to Black life.

AFR 352R.title: RHETORIC OF ETHNIC STUDIES
AFR 352R.description: Study the histories of ethnic (and gender) studies in both higher education and K-12 institutions, and the rhetoric of attacks on, and defenses of, ethnic and gender studies through an examination of primary and secondary source materials.

AFR 357C.title: AFRICAN AMERICAN HIST TO 1860
AFR 357C.description: Review of West African origins; New World settlement patterns, social life, and culture; discussion of the Atlantic slave trade, the development of capitalism and plantation slavery, and the origins of racism.

AFR 358F.title: RACE/POLICING: HIST/POL/PRAC
AFR 358F.description: Examine the origins and evolution of policing in the United States, with a focus on the development and impact of policing policies. Analyze how policies have supported or oppressed Black, working-class, and ethnic communities. African and African Diaspora Studies 358F and Government 372G may not both be counted.

AFR 358K.title: POWER/MONEY IN US EDU POLICY
AFR 358K.description: Examine how power dynamics and financial forces shape education opportunity, who controls what gets taught, how resources are allocated, the political influences behind educational decisions, and the impact of the decisions upon different groups of students.

AFR 360.title: RACE, LAW, AND US SOCIETY
AFR 360.description: Examines the intersection of racial ideology and legal culture in the United States.

AFR 360D.title: RACE/GENDER/SURVEILLANCE
AFR 360D.description: Overview of theories in the sociology of social control, with a focus on risk, power, ethics, and surveillance. Examines historical transformations in social control and the distributions of power in the United States and global contexts, with attention to race, gender, and class. Subjects include: the transatlantic slave trade; prisons and punishment; the gaze, voyeurism, and reality television watching; the Internet; travel and state borders; privacy; and biometrics and the body.

AFR 360F.title: URBAN UNREST
AFR 360F.description: Analysis of the roots of urban unrest, exploring a range of origins: joblessness, state violence, white flight, backlash against civil rights gains, new immigration, and interracial strife. Beyond race and class, subjects include exploring unrest as a mode of pushing the normative boundaries of gender and sexuality in public space. Course material will draw from film, literature, history, geography, and anthropology.

AFR 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
AFR 679HA.description: For honors candidates in African and African diaspora studies. Individual reading of selected works for one semester, followed in the second semester by the writing of an honors thesis.

AFS 100.title: LEADERSHIP LABORATORY-GMC
AFS 100.description: Various leadership techniques, including drill and ceremonies, customs and courtesies, and uniform standards.

AFS 102K.title: FOUNDATIONS OF THE USAF I
AFS 102K.description: Provides an introduction to the Air Force (AF), allowing students to examine general aspects of the Department of the Air Force, AF Leadership, AF benefits, and opportunities for AF officers; lays the foundation for becoming an Airman, including AF customs and courtesies, AF dress and appearance, team building, communication skills, AF Core values, and more. Provides a knowledge-level understanding of United States military operations, principles of war, and airpower from an institutional, doctrinal, and operational perspective.

AFS 111K.title: EVOL USAF AIR & SPACE POWER I
AFS 111K.description: Examines general aspects of the operational employment of air and space power through a historical and institutional lens. Utilizing these perspectives, the course examines principles of war and tenets of air and space power, summarizes the importance of joint operations to the military operating environment, and explores concepts of interpersonal leadership through the use of operational examples.

AFS 120L.title: LEADERSHIP LABORATORY-POC
AFS 120L.description: Leadership laboratory course for upper-division students. Further development of leadership skills through leadership positions within the cadet corps. Includes training of freshman and sophomore students as well as a practicum in Air Force unit operation.

AFS 321.title: AIR FORCE LEADERSHIP STDS I
AFS 321.description: Study of leadership, management fundamentals, professional knowledge, Air Force personnel and evaluation systems, leadership ethics, and communication skills required of an Air Force officer. Case studies throughout the course examine Air Force leadership and management situations as a means of demonstrating and exercising practical application of the concepts being studied.

AFS 333.title: SENIOR AIR FORCE STUDIES I
AFS 333.description: Examines national security policy making, decision, and execution processes, regional studies, advanced leadership ethics, and Air Force doctrine. Special subjects of interest focus on the military as a profession, officership, military justice, civilian control of the military, preparation for active duty, and current issues affecting military professionalism. Within this structure, continued emphasis is given to refining communication skills. Air Force Science 331 and 333 may not both be counted.

AHC 325.title: 7-ARCHAIC/CLASSICAL GREECE
AHC 325.description: Topics in the history of the Greek and Roman empires and the surrounding area. Examines the main works of ancient historiography, and provides grounding in the central issues with which these works engage.

AHC 330.title: 7-GREAT WORKS IN MEDICINE
AHC 330.description: Topics in premodern history with emphasis on regions outside of the ancient Mediterranean world. Examine signature works in the history of medicine.

AMS 310.title: INTRO TO AMERICAN STUDIES
AMS 310.description: An interdisciplinary introduction to the historical exploration of American culture. American Studies 310 and History 315G may not both be counted.

AMS 310D.title: NORTH AMERICAN BORDERLANDS
AMS 310D.description: Explore the formation of political borders in nineteenth- and twentieth-century North America, their contestation, and the multiple meanings attached to them.

AMS 311C.title: LATINX MATERIAL CULTURE
AMS 311C.description: Discuss utilizing material culture as a method of analysis to highlight histories of oppression, struggles towards liberation, and the politics of everyday Latinx life in the U.S..

AMS 311S.title: CRITICAL APPROACHES TO SCI-FI
AMS 311S.description: Writing, reading, and discussion on an American studies topic, with emphasis on the evaluation of information, analytical reading, and critical writing.

AMS 312C.title: BLACKNESS AND COMICS
AMS 312C.description: Examine the relationship between race and the sequential aesthetics of comic books and comic strips. Explore comics strips, underground comics, and superhero comics from the colonial period to the present day.

AMS 315.title: DIGITAL CULTURES
AMS 315.description: Interdisciplinary exploration of American cultural and intellectual life.

AMS 315Q.title: AMERICAN JEWISH HISTORY
AMS 315Q.description: Examine the history of Jews in the United States from the seventeenth century to the present. Explore the formation of a distinct American Judaism and the involvement of Jews in American cultural and political affairs.

AMS 321.title: SETTLER NATIONALISM IN AMER
AMS 321.description: A study of America through its geography, language, government, or cultures.

AMS 321E.title: AFRICAN AMERICAN HIST TO 1860
AMS 321E.description: Review of West African origins; New World settlement patterns, social life, and culture; discussion of the Atlantic slave trade, the development of capitalism and plantation slavery, and the origins of racism.

AMS 321V.title: RELIGION AND FILM
AMS 321V.description: Survey of representations of religious beliefs, practices, persons, and institutions in popular film. Examination of film as a vehicle to reflect how a shared religious imagination shaped the way Americans understand themselves.

AMS 336E.title: HISTORY OF ISLAM IN THE US-WB

AMS 336F.title: ARTS/ARTIFACTS IN AMERICAS
AMS 336F.description: Surveys the changing material culture of the western hemisphere from pre-Columbian times to the beginning of the industrial revolution.

AMS 337J.title: LATINX SEXUALITIES
AMS 337J.description: Examine Latinx sexualities from a historical perspective to comprehend the social, cultural, political, and economic factors, which have shaped these experiences.

AMS 339M.title: AMERICAN ART, 1958-1985
AMS 339M.description: Survey of major movements from 1958 to 1985, from pop art to graffiti art and new expressionism.

AMS 341C.title: AMERICANS IN PARIS
AMS 341C.description: Examine major social and political trends, events, and debates that have informed the works of artists living and working both at the margins of Parisian society as well as in its most celebrated salons. Interpret texts as artifacts that reflect the historical, social, and cultural circumstances of the environment that inspired their creation and reflect upon their role in the cultural symbiosis between America and France.

AMS 347R.title: RACE/MIGRATION: MEDITERRANEAN
AMS 347R.description: Examine the history of migration within and from the Mediterranean area.

AMS 352R.title: RHETORIC OF ETHNIC STUDIES
AMS 352R.description: Study the histories of ethnic (and gender) studies in both higher education and K-12 institutions, and the rhetoric of attacks on, and defenses of, ethnic and gender studies through an examination of primary and secondary source materials.

AMS 355.title: MAIN CURR OF AMER CUL TO 1865
AMS 355.description: Traces the development of American culture and society from the colonial era until the end of the Civil War. Major themes include racial conflict, religion, slavery, the development of democracy, and cultural reform.

AMS 356.title: MAIN CURR AMER CUL SINCE 1865
AMS 356.description: Traces the development of American culture and society from the end of the Civil War to the present. Major themes include racial conflict, pluralism, religion, urban development and reform, modernism, government centralization, cultural radicalism, and the rebirth of conservatism.

AMS 370.title: 21-SOCTY, CUL, POLIT IN 1960S
AMS 370.description: Interdisciplinary seminar on themes in American life. Trace the continuities and transformations in constructions of populations as more or less human, from settler-colonial conquest to the post-Trump era. Examine what logics mark racialized populations as disposable during the colonization of the Americas, and which systematically condition racialized subjects to a state of enduring siege.

AMS 371D.title: BLACK GEOGRAPHIES
AMS 371D.description: Explore the relationship between Blackness and the production of space and place. Examine the tensions and possibilities that emerge when Black people are rendered un-geographic as a fundamental component of racial capitalism while they are simultaneously creating spaces of freedom under constant threats to Black life.

AMS 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
AMS 679HA.description: Individual readings and conferences in connection with an original research paper.

ANS 301M.title: CHI POP CULTR/REFORM ERA
ANS 301M.description: Discussion of various problems involving language, history, and culture in Asia. Explores the concept of "East Asian" from the cinematic perspective, focusing on the martial arts film genre. Asian Studies 301M (Topic: Everybody is Kung Fu Fighting) and 301M (Topic 14) may not both be counted.

ANS 301R.title: HISTORY OF RELIGIONS OF ASIA
ANS 301R.description: Eastern religions: an introduction to the basic forms and the historical development of the religious traditions of India, China, and Japan.

ANS 302D.title: INTRO TO KOREAN CUL AND HIST
ANS 302D.description: Introduction to Korea's history, culture, and civilization from antiquity to the present. Asian Studies 301M (Topic 10) and 302D may not both be counted.

ANS 305C.title: KOREAN LITERATURE AND FILM
ANS 305C.description: Explore the literature and film of Korea from earliest times to the present.

ANS 320C.title: GENJI/GODZILLA ADAPTN JPN CLSC
ANS 320C.description: Examine how premodern, modern, and contemporary classics of Japanese literature, film, and theater get adapted over the centuries, and how culture, politics, gender, genre, and medium influence the adaptation process. Asian Studies 320 (Topic 2) and 320C may not both be counted.

ANS 321E.title: ARTS OF AVANT-GARDE MDRN JAPAN
ANS 321E.description: Explore avant-garde works of modern Japanese literature, film, and visual culture. Focus on how these works advanced global trends in avant-garde theory and practice by rebelling against tradition and searching for radically new ways of imagining the purpose and practice of modern art. Asian Studies 321E and 361 (Topic: Avant Garde Arts in Japan) may not both be counted.

ANS 323C.title: BIOMEDICINE/ETHICS/CULTURE
ANS 323C.description: Examines ethical questions such as the allocation of medical resources, stem cell research and cloning, organ transplantation, abortion, human experimentation, prolonging life and the right to die, suicide, euthanasia, and diagnosis and treatment of illnesses, explored from a global perspective.

ANS 330E.title: TOURISM/PLACE IN GLOBAL SOUTH
ANS 330E.description: Debunk the myth that tourism is inherently desirable by unpacking the mechanisms of violence and inequalities of power embedded in tourism and tourism development.

ANS 330F.title: DEVELOPMENT AND ITS CRITICS
ANS 330F.description: Discuss aspects of the contemporary international aid and development in Asia and beyond. Examine the decision-making within the development industry and the scope of aid as a economic and social force in the contemporary world.

ANS 340D.title: HIST OF HINDU RELIG TRADITN
ANS 340D.description: History of major doctrines, practices, and institutions that shaped the development of Hinduism; how religions adapt to social and cultural change and often provide the catalyst for change.

ANS 342F.title: BUDDHIST THOUGHT AND PRACTICE
ANS 342F.description: Explore important themes in Buddhist thought, various ways in which Buddhists practice their religion, and the relationships between religious concepts and practices.

ANS 348D.title: ANTHROPOL OF THE HIMALAYAS
ANS 348D.description: Examine the history and culture of the Himalayan region, including Northeast India, sections of Pakistan and Afghanistan, Tibet, and especially Nepal.

ANS 355C.title: JAPANESE FOREIGN POLICY
ANS 355C.description: Examine the foreign and domestic determinants of Japanese foreign policy.

ANS 355E.title: POLITICAL ECONOMY OF ASIA
ANS 355E.description: Explore the political economies of East Asia with an emphasis on Japan, China, South Korea, and Taiwan.

ANS 361.title: ASIAN DOCUMENTARIES
ANS 361.description: Selected topics in south and east Asian anthropology, economics, history, geography, government, and sociology. Asian Studies 320 and 361 may not both be counted unless the topics vary. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

ANS 361E.title: URBAN EXPERIENCES IN E ASIA
ANS 361E.description: Explores the process of urbanization in East and Southeast Asia focusing on the politics of capital accumulation and distribution.

ANS 361Q.title: THE AGE OF THE SAMURAI
ANS 361Q.description: Explore the history of Japan via an examination of the complex and ever-changing figure of the samurai.

ANS 379.title: 17-HISTORY/MEMORY JAPAN/KOREA
ANS 379.description: Explore the fraught and entangled histories of modern Japan and Korea as depicted in literature and film to consider experiences of imperialism, rapid modernization, war, decolonization, and trauma.

ANT 301.title: BIOLOGICAL ANTHROPOLOGY
ANT 301.description: Introduction to the principles and the methods of biological (physical) anthropology. Overview of the study of human beings in an evolutionary context, our relationship to other primates, and to the rest of the natural world. Examines anatomical, behavioral, and genetic similarities and differences among living primates and basic mechanisms of the evolutionary process. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled; proctoring charges may apply. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

ANT 302.title: CULTURAL ANTHROPOLOGY
ANT 302.description: The concept of culture; social and political organization; language; the supernatural; elementary cultural theory. Anthropology 302 and 302H may not both be counted.

ANT 304.title: INTRO ARCHAEOL STDS: PREHIST
ANT 304.description: Anthropological study of prehistory, from human beginnings to the appearance of written records.

ANT 305.title: EXPRESSIVE CULTURE
ANT 305.description: How cultural assumptions affect how we tell and respond to different kinds of stories, including fairy tales, movies, and televised news.

ANT 307.title: CULTURE AND COMMUNICATION
ANT 307.description: An introduction to the study of culture through communication and the theory of signs.

ANT 310D.title: INTRO TO BLACK STUDIES-WB
ANT 310D.description: Introduction to canonical black studies literature, themes, and theories. Taught online during scheduled times. Includes live-streaming video and requires real-time participation. Go to http://laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

ANT 310L.title: 6-BLACK QUEER ART WORLDS
ANT 310L.description: Exploration of over two decades of work produced by and about black queer subjects throughout the circum-Atlantic world. Provides an introduction to various artists and intellectuals of the black queer diaspora, as well as an examination of the viability of black queer aesthetic practice as a form of theorizing.

ANT 322I.title: NATIVE AMER CULTURE SOUTHWEST
ANT 322I.description: Explore the diverse Native cultures of the Southwest, focusing on the philosophical underpinnings and the frameworks of meaning and moral responsibility of indigenous peoples of the American Southwest.

ANT 322N.title: HIST OF HINDU RELIG TRADITN
ANT 322N.description: History of major doctrines, practices, and institutions that shaped the development of Hinduism; how religions adapt to social and cultural change and often provide the catalyst for change.

ANT 322V.title: ARCH/SCULPTURE MAYA WORLD
ANT 322V.description: Explore the ancient world of the Maya through the lens of architecture and sculpture.

ANT 322W.title: POWER/RESIST PUTIN'S RUSSIA
ANT 322W.description: Introduction to Russian political and protest cultures--beyond the headlines and outside the Kremlin walls.

ANT 323N.title: BIOMEDICINE/ETHICS/CULTURE
ANT 323N.description: Examines ethical questions such as the allocation of medical resources, stem cell research and cloning, organ transplantation, abortion, human experimentation, prolonging life and the right to die, suicide, euthanasia, and diagnosis and treatment of illnesses, explored from a global perspective.

ANT 323O.title: BLACK GEOGRAPHIES
ANT 323O.description: Explore the relationship between Blackness and the production of space and place. Examine the tensions and possibilities that emerge when Black people are rendered un-geographic as a fundamental component of racial capitalism while they are simultaneously creating spaces of freedom under constant threats to Black life.

ANT 323P.title: ANTHROPOL OF THE HIMALAYAS
ANT 323P.description: Examine the history and culture of the Himalayan region, including Northeast India, sections of Pakistan and Afghanistan, Tibet, and especially Nepal.

ANT 324E.title: CULTURE AND HEALTH
ANT 324E.description: Examine the social and cultural foundations of western medicine as well as an introduction to indigenous and alternative health systems.

ANT 324G.title: ENVIRONMENTAL ANTHROPOLOGY
ANT 324G.description: Examine the environment, environmental inequality, and the relationship between culture and ecology. Anthropology 324G and 324L (Topic: Environmental Anthropology) may not both be counted.

ANT 324L.title: ARCHAEOL OF COOPRTN/CONFLCT
ANT 324L.description: An exploration of the complex relationship between anthropological ideas and ethnographic practice. Introduces a broad spectrum of concepts, issues, and theories of culture, and critically examines how these theories and ideas shape anthropological methods and writings. Anthropology 324L (Topic: Ethnographic Theory and Practice) and 324L (Topic 44) may not both be counted.

ANT 325G.title: TECHNOCULTURE
ANT 325G.description: Analyze social groups using digital methods and a process of self-reflection focused on technological mediations of experience. Anthropology 325G and 325L (Topic: Technoculture) may not both be counted.

ANT 325I.title: SONIC ETHNOGRAPHY
ANT 325I.description: Examine sound, themes of listening, noise, silence, audio technology, vibration, and voice. Anthropology 324I and 324L (Topic: Sonic Ethnography) may not both be counted.

ANT 326N.title: CULTURAL RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
ANT 326N.description: Explore a thorough overview of Cultural Resource Management (CRM). Designed for students who are interested in pursuing a career in archaeology. Anthropology 324L (Topic: Cultural Resource Management) and 326N may not both be counted.

ANT 326P.title: HISTORICAL ARCHAEOLOGY
ANT 326P.description: Explore recent theoretical, methodological, and thematic developments in historical archaeology with particular focus in North America and the Caribbean. Anthropology 324L (Topic: Historical Archaeology) and 326P may not both be counted.

ANT 326R.title: LATINA/O/X CITIZENSHIP
ANT 326R.description: Explore the concepts of membership, belonging, immigration and citizenship in the Latina/o/x experience. Examine how historically, politically, socially and culturally, Latinas/os/xs have been construed as belonging or not belonging members of the United States society.

ANT 327E.title: ETHICS IN ANTHROPOLOGY
ANT 327E.description: Explore the complex ethical challenges which arise when studying people, their communities, their languages, and their past. Engage with real-world case studies, ethical frameworks, and debates, flipping the anthropological gaze to focus on the anthropologist's behaviors and decisions. Anthropology 324L (Topic: Ethics in Anthropology) and 327E may not both be counted.

ANT 328I.title: EXPRESSV ARTS OF GLOBAL AFRICA
ANT 328I.description: Explore music, dance, and other artistic forms as modes of political expression and community building.

ANT 340C.title: ETHNOGRAPHIC RESEARCH METHODS
ANT 340C.description: Introduction to methods used in conducting ethnographic research; emphasis on research design, analysis, writing, and ethical considerations.

ANT 346L.title: PRIMATE SOCIAL BEHAVIOR
ANT 346L.description: Studies primate behavior and why primates do what they do, including basic theoretical principles and the models used to explain primate behavior.

ANT 346M.title: COMPARATIVE PRIMATE ECOLOGY
ANT 346M.description: The basics of how organisms interact with their environment, focusing on a wide range of primates from a comparative perspective. Discusses how various aspects of ecology are used to conserve primate populations.

ANT 348E.title: ENVIRONMENTAL EPIGENETICS
ANT 348E.description: Explore major subjects in anthropology, toxicology, and public health pertaining to the study of environmental exposures and disease/illness in the context of the current understanding of epigenetic processes. Analyze epigenetic mechanisms, gene transcription, the relationship between the environment and the epigenome, and epigenetics, health. Assess the main findings in the field and discuss their relevance to anthropology, public health, medicine, and in general. Anthropology 348E and 348K (Topic: Environmental Epigenetics) may not both be counted.

ANT 348K.title: 8-EVOL ANATOMY OF HEAD & NECK
ANT 348K.description: An in-depth study of current topics, controversies, and literature on the evolution, morphology, genetics, and behavioral ecology of primates, including humans. The comparative and functional anatomy of the head and neck in primates and other mammals.

ANT 348R.title: RACE/SCIENCE/HUMAN VARIATION
ANT 348R.description: Explore the patterns of biological variation within and between human populations, including variation in phenotypic, genetic, and behavioral traits. Consider both the microevolutionary and cultural processes that influence that variation. Examine how studies of human variation have impacted society in the past and present. Discuss human biological variation; the history of the concept of race and of eugenics/race science; fundamental principles of genetics and evolution; human genetic and biocultural adaptation; and contemporary issues in human genetics, health, and disease. Anthropology 348R and 349C may not both be counted.

ANT 361L.title: MAYA STUDIES: PAST/PRES/FUTURE
ANT 361L.description: Explore the long history and cultural development of Maya peoples from Mexico and Central America, ranging from the earliest occupation of the Maya territory to the present. Explore several fields of the Maya population, including sociopolitical organization, technology, economy, religion, spirituality, genocides, cultural revitalization, and artistic expressions. Anthropology 324L (Topic: Maya Studs Past Present Future) and 361L may not both be counted.

ANT 379.title: PROBLEMS IN ANTHROPOLOGY
ANT 379.description: Supervised individual research on selected problems in anthropology.

ANT 432L.title: PRIMATE ANATOMY
ANT 432L.description: Comparative and functional anatomy of primates, including humans; emphasis on adaptations and evolution of the various taxa.

ANT 453.title: ARCHAEOLOGICAL ANALYSIS
ANT 453.description: Derivation of chronology and cultural information from archaeological data; the role of archaeology in modern life.

ANT 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
ANT 679HA.description: For honors candidates in anthropology. Individual reading of selected works for one semester, followed in the second semester by the writing of an honors thesis.

ARA 531K.title: INTENSIVE ARABIC V
ARA 531K.description: Not open to native speakers of Arabic.

ARA 601C.title: INTENSIVE ARABIC I
ARA 601C.description: Not open to native speakers of Arabic. First semester of intensive Arabic language instruction.

ARA 621K.title: INTENSIVE ARABIC III
ARA 621K.description: Not open to native speakers of Arabic. If taken in residence, only one of the following may be counted: Arabic 512K, 621K. Arabic 112C and 621K may not both be counted.

ARC 221K.title: VISUAL COMMUNICATION III
ARC 221K.description: Introduction to digital modeling as well as principles of digital fabrication. Includes various modes of output such as drawings, renderings, and physical models.

ARC 308.title: ARCHITECTURE AND SOCIETY
ARC 308.description: Introduction to the social contexts, potential, and consequences of architecture and interior design.

ARC 310K.title: DESIGN I
ARC 310K.description: An introduction to the forms and methods of design for architects and interior designers, with an emphasis on inhabitation including body, light, and movement. Taught in a studio format by faculty members under the direction of a faculty coordinator.

ARC 311K.title: VISUAL COMMUNICATION I
ARC 311K.description: Study and application of drawing and other communication skills for designers, including formal and spatial studies, life drawing, and perspective.

ARC 323D.title: DESIGN III INTERMEDIATE STUDIO
ARC 323D.description: Explore tectonic expression through structural systems, construction methods and materiality, and spatial and formal composition at the scale of a building. Architecture 321D and 323D may not both be counted.

ARC 327C.title: URBAN DESIGN HIST/THEORY/CRIT
ARC 327C.description: Study of critical theories and practices that affect the built environment. Architecture 327C and 350R (Topic: Urban Design History/Theory/Criticism) may not both be counted.

ARC 327G.title: REGENERATIVE ARCHITECTURE
ARC 327G.description: Architecture 327G and 350R (Topic: Regenerative Architecture) may not both be counted.

ARC 327R.title: COMMUN-DRIVEN DSGN WORKSHOP
ARC 327R.description: Advanced topics in architecture and associated disciplines to encourage critical and theoretical thinking. Architecture 327R (Topic 10) and 350R (Topic: Energy Modeling & Design Proc) may not both be counted.

ARC 328D.title: ADVANCED DRAWING
ARC 328D.description: Focus on personal design methodology by exploring multiple drawing methods, skills and approaches including manual, digital, and hybrid techniques. Refines design communication skills by pairing clearly articulated design intention with compelling drawings, and expressing them via effective verbal presentations. Architecture 328D and 351R (Topic: Advanced Drawing: Draw Blood) may not both be counted.

ARC 328R.title: DRAWING IN SPACE
ARC 328R.description: Advanced topics in various methods of visual communication.

ARC 328W.title: WOOD DESIGN
ARC 328W.description: Practice on basic woodworking skills: tool sharpening, layout, millwork, joinery, gluing, sanding, and finishing. Architecture 328W and 351R (Topic 11) may not both be counted.

ARC 334K.title: ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROLS I
ARC 334K.description: A survey of acoustics, color, light, illumination, and electrical and information systems in architectural interiors. Includes techniques of documentation. Architectural Interior Design 324K and Architecture 334K may not both be counted.

ARC 335T.title: ARCHITECTURAL DETAIL/MATERIALS
ARC 335T.description: Analyze building envelope, assemblies, detailing, and specifications. Architecture 335M and 335T may not both be counted.

ARC 342C.title: MEX ARCH: PRE-COLUMB-CONTEMP
ARC 342C.description: A survey of Mexican architecture and urbanism from its origins in pre-Columbian times to the twenty-first century. Evaluation of architecture as a cultural production intricately connected to its artistic and historical context and the landscape that supports it.

ARC 342E.title: HIST/THEORS OF LANDSCP ARCH I
ARC 342E.description: Overview and chronological series of in-depth studies in the history of major garden cultures and designed landscapes of the Western and Eastern worlds. Introduction to methodological approaches to the formal, social, and cultural history of gardens and landscapes, as well as to relevant theoretical frameworks for interpreting these designed landscapes and for use in conceptualizing landscapes, architecture, and urban projects in studio design.

ARC 342R.title: ART/ARCH MODERN LATIN AMER
ARC 342R.description: Seminars and lecture/seminars on advanced topics in the history of architecture. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

ARC 361S.title: PRP INTERNSHIP
ARC 361S.description: Architecture 327R (Topic: PROF RESIDENCY INTERN-PRP) and 361S may not both be counted.

ARC 361T.title: TECHNICAL COMMUNICATION
ARC 361T.description: Produce construction documents. Architecture 361T and 361U may not both be counted.

ARC 362.title: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE
ARC 362.description: Ethical, legal, and administrative responsibilities of the architect; organizations, processes, and roles in architecture.

ARC 415K.title: CONSTRUCTION I
ARC 415K.description: Introduction to building construction, materials, and structures.

ARC 435R.title: STRUCTURES I
ARC 435R.description: Examine theories of building construction and materials. Explore the analysis and design of structural components. Architecture 435K and 435R may not both be counted.

ARC 523F.title: DESIGN V INTERMEDIATE STUDIO
ARC 523F.description: Examine theory, raison-d'etre, program, spatial and formal composition, and overlaps with other disciplines in a studio setting. Architecture 521F and 523F may not both be counted.

ARC 561E.title: ADVANCED DESIGN STUDIO
ARC 561E.description: Study advanced problems in architectural design. May be taken four times for credit.

ARE 102.title: INTRO TO ARCHITECTURAL ENGR
ARE 102.description: Introduction to architectural engineering as a career by use of case studies.

ARE 217.title: COMP-AIDED DESIGN AND GRAPHICS
ARE 217.description: Introduction to procedures in computer-aided design and computer graphics used in producing plans and three-dimensional electronic models associated with building design and construction.

ARE 320K.title: INTRODUCTION TO DESIGN I
ARE 320K.description: Introduction to design principles, concepts, and problem-solving approaches. Issues addressed by a series of two- and three-dimensional studies.

ARE 323K.title: PROJECT MANAGEMENT & ECONOMICS
ARE 323K.description: Solving economic problems related to construction and engineering; construction project management techniques; characteristics of construction organizations, equipment, and methods.

ARE 335.title: MATLS AND MTHDS OF BLDG CONSTR
ARE 335.description: Elements and properties of construction materials and components; fabrication and construction technologies, methods, and processes; engineering systems characteristic of commercial buildings such as foundation, structural, and building envelope systems.

ARE 345K.title: MASONRY ENGINEERING
ARE 345K.description: Behavior and design of masonry with respect to architectural, economic, and structural criteria.

ARE 346N.title: BUILDING ENVIRONMENTAL SYSTEMS
ARE 346N.description: Analysis and design of building air conditioning systems; heating and cooling load calculations, air side systems analysis, air distribution, building electrical requirements, electrical and lighting systems.

ARE 346P.title: HVAC DESIGN
ARE 346P.description: Design and analysis of heating, ventilation, and cooling systems for buildings. Focus on application of fundamental energy and mass transfer principles to HVAC components.

ARE 366.title: CONTRACTS, LIABILITY, & ETHICS
ARE 366.description: Legal aspects of engineering and construction contracts; contract formation, interpretation, rights and duties, and changes; legal liabilities and professional ethics of architects, engineers, and contractors.

ARE 371.title: ENERGY SIMULATN IN BLDG DESIGN
ARE 371.description: Fundamentals of building energy simulations, analytical models for heat transfer in buildings, general numerical methods for solving equations from the analytical models, use of energy simulation tools in building design analysis, and parametric analyses used to study various operational parameters that affect energy use in buildings.

ARE 376.title: BLDG INFO MDLNG CAPITAL PROJS
ARE 376.description: Focuses on the skills and information needed to effectively use an existing Building Information Model for a building construction project. In this project-based course, students gain knowledge on the implementation of BIM concepts throughout the life cycle of a building from planning and design to construction and operations.

ARE 377K.title: 7-INDEPENDENT STUDY
ARE 377K.description: Various specified topics or conference course. Conduct independent research with a tenure track faculty member in the Fariborz Maseeh Department of Civil, Architectural, and Environmental Engineering. Prepare a project proposal and a final report, each of which is evaluated by the faculty supervisor. ARE 177K (Topic 7) and ARE 177K (Topic: Independent Study) may not both be counted.

ARH 301.title: INTRODUCTION TO VISUAL ARTS
ARH 301.description: A broad survey of selected traditions of art with an emphasis upon understanding their visual elements and cultural significance. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

ARH 302.title: SURV ANCIENT THRU MEDIEVAL ART
ARH 302.description: A study of selected visual works throughout the world from prehistoric time to 1400 CE.

ARH 303.title: SURV OF RENAIS THRU MODERN ART
ARH 303.description: A study of selected visual works throughout the world from 1400 CE to the present.

ARH 304.title: ISSUES IN VISUAL CULTURE
ARH 304.description: Examines the modern and contemporary history of art and visual culture in relation to a broader set of problems fundamental to visual representation throughout the history of art.

ARH 321.title: PROBLEMS ART HISTORICAL RSCH
ARH 321.description: Focus on developing research skills. Introduction to critical analysis in the context of using various resources and primary materials.

ARH 326K.title: MYTH/IMAGE GREEK/ROMN MEDITER
ARH 326K.description: Examines various methods of interpretation, such as archaeological and art historical analysis of mythical Greek and Roman narratives as they appear in visual arts of antiquity.

ARH 327N.title: ART/POLITICS IMPERIAL ROME
ARH 327N.description: Public art of the Roman Empire from Augustus to late antiquity, ca. 31 BC to AD 350. Art History 327N and Classical Civilization 340 (Topic 2) may not both be counted.

ARH 327U.title: LOVE, BEAUTY, AND PROTECTION
ARH 327U.description: Examine selected works of Greek and Roman art (sixth century B.C. to fourth century A.D.) to explore how ancients thought about themselves with regard to love, sexuality, divine and human beauty, and protection from demonic forces. Focus on social and cultural contexts, gender, and practices of daily life. Art History 327U and Women's and Gender Studies 345C may not both be counted.

ARH 329J.title: BYZANTINE ART
ARH 329J.description: Examination of the medieval art and architecture of the eastern Roman empire, including related traditions (Coptic, Armenian, Georgian, Crusader, Norman).

ARH 331P.title: ART/CITY IN RENAISSANCE ITALY
ARH 331P.description: Art, architecture, and emerging civic identity in Florence, Siena, Venice, and other Italian Renaissance city-states.

ARH 335J.title: NINETEENTH-CENTURY ART
ARH 335J.description: Examines European art and themes in art during the nineteenth century.

ARH 341P.title: CONTEMPORARY LATIN AM ART
ARH 341P.description: Development and sources of art in the Caribbean and Central and South America from the 1960s to the present.

ARH 341Q.title: WOMEN IN LATIN AMER ART
ARH 341Q.description: Development and sources of Latin American Women Artists considering feminist, gender and queer theories

ARH 344M.title: PHOTOGRAPHS/BLACKNESS IN US
ARH 344M.description: Examine issues raised at the intersection of Blackness and photography in contemporary art of the United States.

ARH 346K.title: INTRO TO AFRICAN ART-WB
ARH 346K.description: Investigates the lives and works of several artists who have made substantial contributions to the definition, history, and interpretation of the visual arts. Taught as a Web-based course.

ARH 347K.title: ART/ARCHAEOL OF ANC PERU
ARH 347K.description: The growth of civilization in South America from the earliest decorated textiles, pottery, and ceremonial buildings to the imperial Inca style. Art History 347K and Latin American Studies 327 (Topic 6) may not both be counted.

ARH 347R.title: ARCH/SCULPTURE MAYA WORLD
ARH 347R.description: Explore the ancient world of the Maya through the lens of architecture and sculpture.

ARH 375.title: THRY MTHD HISTORY OF ART
ARH 375.description: Examination of the theories and methods used by art historians and the changing nature of art historical inquiry.

ARH 376.title: READING TUTORIAL IN ARH PROBS
ARH 376.description: Individual projects to be completed under faculty supervision.

ARI 130.title: INTERIOR DESIGN INTERNSHIP
ARI 130.description: Practical application of design procedures in a professional design office.

ARI 221K.title: VISUAL COMMUNICATION III
ARI 221K.description: Introduction to digital modeling as well as principles of digital fabrication. Includes various modes of output such as drawings, renderings, and physical models.

ARI 310K.title: DESIGN I
ARI 310K.description: An introduction to the forms and methods of design for architects and interior designers, with an emphasis on inhabitation including body, light, and movement. Taught in a studio format by faculty members under the direction of a faculty coordinator.

ARI 311K.title: VISUAL COMMUNICATION I
ARI 311K.description: Study and application of drawing and other communication skills for designers, including formal and spatial studies, life drawing, and perspective.

ARI 318K.title: INTERIORS AND SOCIETY
ARI 318K.description: Concepts, principles, and elements of interior design, presented in artistic, philosophical, and professional contexts. Includes a basic historical overview of the development of interior design.

ARI 320K.title: DESIGN III-INTERIORS
ARI 320K.description: Focus on the physical and psychological needs of the inhabitants of interior space, with an emphasis on conceptual process and diagrammatic techniques. Projects deal with real building situations and introduce implications of fenestration, structure, and materials.

ARI 324K.title: ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROLS I
