ARI 324K.description: A survey of acoustics, color, light, illumination, and electrical and information systems in architectural interiors. Includes techniques of documentation. Architectural Interior Design 324K and Architecture 334K may not both be counted.

ARI 338.title: DESIGNING FOR HUMAN BEHAVIOR
ARI 338.description: Issues of mood, privacy, perception, proxemics, and preferences applied to the design of interiors.

ARI 350R.title: ARCHITECTURE COMPUTATION
ARI 350R.description: Seminar in a variety of topics. Designed to broaden the student's knowledge of interior design and to encourage critical and theoretical thinking in the discipline. Architectural Interior Design 350R (Topic: Design Firm Leadership) and 350R (Topic 1) may not both be counted.

ARI 368R.title: INTERIOR DESIGN HISTORY II
ARI 368R.description: Study of function and aesthetics, and decoration and use, emphasizing interiors from the nineteenth century to the present.

ARI 434J.title: CONSTRUCTION I
ARI 434J.description: Introduction to building construction, materials, and structures. Architectural Interior Design 434J and 350R (Topic: Construction I) may not both be counted.

ARI 530K.title: DESIGN V-INTERIORS
ARI 530K.description: Examination of the elements of interior space and scale, including specific human factors. Particular emphasis on the design, documentation, production, and placement of objects in interiors.

ARI 560E.title: ADVANCED DESIGN STUDIO
ARI 560E.description: Synthesize components such as human aspects, place-making, the interior envelope, transitional spaces, and conceptual processes. May be taken four times for credit.

ART 310P.title: INTRODUCTION TO PRINT
ART 310P.description: Introduction to the concepts, methods, and materials of print including a combination of intaglio, relief, lithography, serigraphy, and/or book arts.

ART 311C.title: CORE STUDIO: DRAWING
ART 311C.description: Explore techniques of drawing in a contemporary context. Focus on line, line weight, continuous line, contour line, sighting, figure, gesture, perspective (two point, three point, and isometric/orthogonal drawing), value, color, and texture. Studio Art 302C and 311C may not both be counted.

ART 311K.title: PAINTING I
ART 311K.description: Introduction to painting techniques, composition, and exploration of personal expression.

ART 312C.title: CORE STUDIO: 2D
ART 312C.description: Create planar works and explore formal principles and conceptual concerns in a contemporary context. Studio Art 301C and 312C may not both be counted.

ART 313C.title: CORE STUDIO: 3D
ART 313C.description: Create spatial works and explore formal principles and conceptual concerns in a contemporary context. Studio Art 303C and 313C may not both be counted.

ART 313K.title: BEGINNING SCULPTURE
ART 313K.description: Introduction to the processes involved in the production of object-oriented sculpture using direct methods of hot and cold construction.

ART 314C.title: CORE STUDIO: TIME/TECH
ART 314C.description: Explore time-based media and digital technology in a contemporary context.

ART 315K.title: BEGINNING DRAWING
ART 315K.description: Exploration of various methods, subjects, and expressive possibilities in drawing.

ART 316K.title: BEGINNING LIFE DRAWING
ART 316K.description: Problems in drawing and construction of the human figure in selected media.

ART 317C.title: TRANSMEDIA: PERFORMNC ART I
ART 317C.description: An introduction to the practice, history, and theory of performance art in a variety of contexts and spaces, including theatrical, the white cube, and the workaday world.

ART 317K.title: BEGINNING PHOTOGRAPHY
ART 317K.description: An introduction to still photography, including basic technical skills and concepts.

ART 318C.title: TRNSMEDIA: DIGTL TIME-ART I
ART 318C.description: Study in digital video, sound, and animation, with emphasis on the exploration of movement, image, and montage. Guided inquiry into pertinent conceptual, perceptual, and practical skills.

ART 320F.title: DIGITAL FABRICATION I
ART 320F.description: Study of the artistic culture and techniques associated with digital visualization, three dimensional data acquisition, and various forms of digital fabrication including 3D Printing, computer numerical controlled milling, and Laser Cutting.

ART 321K.title: PAINTING II
ART 321K.description: Problems in composition and exploration of personal expression. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 322K.title: INTERMEDIATE DRAWING
ART 322K.description: Continuation of Studio Art 315K. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 323K.title: INTERMEDIATE SCULPTURE
ART 323K.description: Exploration of the concepts and processes involved in the production of object-oriented sculpture, with emphasis on indirect methods of mold-making and casting. Encourages individual direction. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 324C.title: PUBLICATION STUDIO
ART 324C.description: Studio course. Advanced publishing projects in print and/or digital media.

ART 325G.title: INT PRINT: SERIGRAPHY
ART 325G.description: Practice in the theories and techniques of multicolor serigraphy and photoserigraphy. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 325K.title: INT PRINT: INTAGLIO
ART 325K.description: Instruction in the theories and techniques of intaglio printmaking, color, assemblage, stencil, viscosity, collography, photo process, and relief. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 325M.title: INT PRINT: LITHOGRAPHY
ART 325M.description: Instruction in the theories and techniques of metal plate, multicolor, and stone lithography and photolithography. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 335K.title: INTERMEDIATE PHOTOGRAPHY
ART 335K.description: Practice in still photography, including materials and processes.

ART 336C.title: ART AND ECOLOGY
ART 336C.description: Explore eco art through creative inquiry into how natural, social and technological systems co-shape life, relationships, and possible futures. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 337C.title: TRANSMEDIA: PERFORMANCE ART II
ART 337C.description: Advanced workshop in performance art that focuses on the development of researched solo and collaborative projects. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 338C.title: TRANSMEDIA: DIGTL TIME-ART II
ART 338C.description: Study in digital video, sound, and animation, with emphasis on the exploration of cinematic time and its time-based installation. Guided inquiry into the relationship between video and video projections, and technics and technology. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 340F.title: DIGITAL FABRICATION II
ART 340F.description: Advanced study of the artistic culture and techniques associated with digital visualization, three dimensional data acquisition, and various forms of digital fabrication including 3D Printing, computer numerical controlled milling, and Laser Cutting.

ART 341K.title: PAINTING III
ART 341K.description: Continuation of Studio Art 321K. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 346K.title: INTERMEDIATE LIFE DRAWING
ART 346K.description: Advanced problems in drawing and construction of the human figure. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 350.title: PHILOSOPHY/THEORY/CRITICISM
ART 350.description: Advanced exploration of philosophy, theory, and criticism from a studio art perspective.

ART 350D.title: ALT PHOTO PROC: PHOTO/PRNTMKNG
ART 350D.description: Explore a process-based journey through the history of photography and photographic printmaking. Discuss, innovate and create while engaging with the history of photography. Studio Art 350D and 350M (Topic: Alt Photo Proc: Photo/Prntmkng) may not both be counted.

ART 350N.title: COLOR IN THEORY AND PRACTICE
ART 350N.description: Work with a St. Elmo's fellow, learn about continuing art making through post-graduation, and review the path to obtaining a MFA. Discuss theoretical and practical issues that are central to making art in today's world.

ART 350P.title: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICES
ART 350P.description: Exploration of professional practices as they relate to the field of visual arts. Focus on writing an artist statement and resume, as well as learning about writing and applying for exhibition, grant, and residency opportunities.

ART 352C.title: PAINTING FOR NONMAJORS
ART 352C.description: Exploration of various methods, subjects, and expressive possibilities in painting. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 352D.title: DRAWING FOR NONMAJORS
ART 352D.description: Exploration of various methods, subjects, and expressive possibilities in drawing. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 352F.title: PRINT FOR NONMAJORS
ART 352F.description: Exploration of the concepts, methods, and materials of print. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 352G.title: SCULPTURE FOR NONMAJORS
ART 352G.description: Exploration of the processes involved in the production of object-oriented sculpture. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 352J.title: PHOTOGRAPHY FOR NONMAJORS
ART 352J.description: Exploration of still photography. May be taken twice for credit.

ART 357C.title: TRANSMEDIA: PERF ART III
ART 357C.description: Advanced independent inquiry into the development of performance art projects requiring focused research and time. May be taken three times for credit.

ART 358C.title: TRANSMEDIA: DIGTL TIME-ART III
ART 358C.description: Advanced study in video, sound, and animation with an emphasis on individual research reflecting students' artistic goals. May be taken three times for credit.

ART 361K.title: PAINTING IV
ART 361K.description: Continuation of Studio Art 341K. May be taken three times for credit.

ART 363K.title: ADVANCED SCULPTURE
ART 363K.description: Advanced research in the theory, technology, and methods involved in the production of sculpture, with emphasis on individual direction. May be taken three times for credit.

ART 366K.title: ADVANCED LIFE DRAWING
ART 366K.description: Problems in drawing and construction of the human figure. May be taken three times for credit.

ART 368N.title: ADVANCED DRAWING
ART 368N.description: Continuation of Studio Art 322K. May be taken three times for credit.

ART 376K.title: CONTEMPORARY ISSUES IN PHOTO
ART 376K.description: Inquiry of specific themes in photography which can include: photographic intervention, photography since 1945, photo book making, abstraction in photography, appropriation in photography, and lighting for photography. May be taken three times for credit.

ART 376P.title: CONTEMPORARY ISSUES IN PRINT
ART 376P.description: Explore advanced issues, subjects, and processes in contemporary print and the expanded field. May be taken three times for credit.

ASE 120K.title: LOW-SPEED AERODYNAMICS LAB
ASE 120K.description: Wind tunnel and water channel experiments at subsonic speeds; use of instrumentation and written reports. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

ASE 128.title: AEROSPACE ENGR PROJECTS LAB
ASE 128.description: Directed work on an organized student project in aerospace engineering or engineering mechanics.

ASE 162M.title: HIGH-SPEED AERODYNAMICS LAB
ASE 162M.description: Experiments using a variable-Mach number supersonic wind tunnel and shock tube. Aerodynamics of wedges, cones, spheres and diamond-shaped airfoils in supersonic flow. One-dimensional unsteady shock motion. High-speed flow measurement techniques. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

ASE 166M.title: SPACECRAFT SYSTEMS LABORATORY
ASE 166M.description: Overview of spacecraft subsystems, mission design program library, numerical techniques, mission planning references, mission constraints, and mission design projects. Includes written reports.

ASE 179K.title: RSCH IN AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
ASE 179K.description: Directed study or research in a selected area of aerospace engineering.

ASE 279K.title: RSCH IN AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
ASE 279K.description: Directed study or research in a selected area of aerospace engineering.

ASE 320.title: LOW-SPEED AERODYNAMICS
ASE 320.description: Fundamental concepts, fluid statics; integral and differential analysis; detailed analysis of inviscid, incompressible flows; aerodynamics of airfoils and wings.

ASE 324L.title: AEROSPACE MATERIALS LABORATORY
ASE 324L.description: Study of the deformation and fracture behavior of materials used in aerospace vehicles. Structure-property relations, methods of characterizing material behavior, use of properties in the design process. Case histories. Written reports.

ASE 330M.title: LINEAR SYSTEM ANALYSIS
ASE 330M.description: Explore the fundamentals of signals and systems; mathematical modeling of mechanical systems; transfer function; impulse response; Laplace transforms; response of linear, time-invariant systems; frequency response methods; time-domain analysis; introductory concepts for feedback control systems; multivariate linear dynamical systems; eigenvalues and eigenvectors; matrix exponentials. An introduction to fundamental elements of the theory of systems and signals and exposure to necessary concepts and tools required to perform modeling and analysis of linear dynamical systems. Demonstrate the theory through several simulation examples using MATLAB and Simulink relevant to applications of modern aerospace engineering systems.

ASE 339.title: ADVANCED STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
ASE 339.description: Curved beams, shear deformation, beam columns, beams on elastic foundations; inelastic behavior of members; elementary plate bending.

ASE 362K.title: COMPRESSIBLE FLOW
ASE 362K.description: Shock and expansion waves, quasi-one-dimensional flow, converging-diverging nozzles, diffusers, linearized flow, and compressibility effects on aerodynamics of airfoils and bodies.

ASE 364.title: APPLIED AERODYNAMICS
ASE 364.description: Detailed analysis of aerodynamics of compressible and incompressible flows about wings and airfoils; wing and airfoil parameters and force and moment coefficients; and thin-airfoil theory, lifting-line theory, panel methods, high-lift devices, delta wings, transonic flows, and supersonic flows over wings. Aerospace Engineering 364 and 379L (Topic: Airfoil and Wing Design Theory) may not both be counted.

ASE 365.title: STRUCTURAL DYNAMICS
ASE 365.description: Analysis of discrete and continuous vibrating systems; deriving equations of motion; determining response; and natural frequencies and modes of vibration.

ASE 366K.title: SPACECRAFT DYNAMICS
ASE 366K.description: Examine Newton's gravity law, Kepler's laws, basic orbit propagation, orbit properties, orbital elements, coordinate systems and transformations, radar observations, ground tracks, orbit maneuvers, and trajectory design principles.

ASE 367K.title: FLIGHT DYNAMICS
ASE 367K.description: Equations of motion for rigid aircraft; aircraft performance, weight and balance, static stability and control, and dynamic stability; design implications.

ASE 370C.title: FEEDBACK CONTROL SYSTEMS
ASE 370C.description: Fundamentals of linear control analysis and design for single-input, single-output systems; stability and performance measures; Routh Hurwitz analysis; root locus methods; frequency response (Bode and Nyquist); introduction to full-state feedback. Aerospace Engineering 370C and 370L may not both be counted.

ASE 372K.title: ATTITUDE DYNAMICS
ASE 372K.description: Examine attitude representations, rotational kinematics, rigid-body dynamics, and torque-free motion. Explore satellite's sensors and actuators, attitude determination algorithms, and passive and active attitude control systems.

ASE 374D.title: AEROSPACE SYS SENIOR DSGN I
ASE 374D.description: Introduction to systems engineering including the systems engineering process, requirements, design fundamentals, trade studies, cost and risk analyses, integration, technical reviews, case studies, and ethics.

ASE 374E.title: AEROSPACE SYS SENIOR DSGN II
ASE 374E.description: Examine aerospace systems characteristics, mission requirements, sensors, and consumables analyses; and mission phases, request for proposal, problem definition, ideation, proposal preparation, conceptual design review, preliminary design development and review, and design report preparation. Produce written reports.

ASE 375.title: ELECTROMECHANICAL SYSTEMS
ASE 375.description: Subjects include basic electronic circuits, operational amplifiers, concepts of impedance and feedback, sensors to measure temperature, displacement, strain, force and acceleration, impulse testing, shake testing, and triggered data acquisition. These concepts will be implemented via experiments that illustrate interesting phenomena in solids and structures. Aerospace Engineering 375 and Mechanical Engineering 340 may not both be counted.

ASE 376K.title: PROPULSION
ASE 376K.description: Review of control volume analysis and quasi-one-dimensional compressible flow. Simple propeller theory. Analysis and design of rocket nozzles and air-breathing engines, including performance and cycle analysis; flow in nozzles, diffusers, compressor, and turbine stages; combustion chamber processes and propellants. Includes an introduction to chemical rocket propulsion.

ASE 379K.title: RSCH IN AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
ASE 379K.description: Directed study or research in a selected area of aerospace engineering.

ASE 679HA.title: UNDERGRADUATE HONORS THESIS
ASE 679HA.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of an engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Engineering Honors Program. The student makes an oral presentation and writes a thesis. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and a Bachelor of Science in Engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirement for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

ASE 679HB.title: UNDERGRADUATE HONORS THESIS
ASE 679HB.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of an engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Engineering Honors Program. The student makes an oral presentation and writes a thesis. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and a Bachelor of Science in Engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirement for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

ASL 311D.title: AMER SIGN LANG III: INTERMED
ASL 311D.description: Focuses on maintaining and building upon a student's intermediate ASL skills. Grammar coverage includes complex constructions that involve the signing space, and vocabulary building includes comparisons between English words and ASL signs in order for the learner to make appropriate vocabulary choices in their ASL production. Complex questions facing the contemporary Deaf community are discussed. American Sign Language 311D and 312L may not both be counted. American Sign Language 611C and 311D may not both be counted.

ASL 326.title: SIGN LANGS & SIGNING COMMUNS
ASL 326.description: Examine the grammar of signed languages, their use in signing communities, and the acquisition of signed languages as first languages. No knowledge of American Sign Language is required. Conducted in English.

ASL 356.title: PERSPCTVS ON DEAF PPLS/COMMUNS
ASL 356.description: Conducted in English. Examine deaf peoples and communities from a number of perspectives: social and psychological meanings of deaf, the deaf community, education of deaf children, sign languages, and historical trends. American Sign Language 350 (Topic: Perspectives on Deafness) and 356 may not both be counted.

ASL 601D.title: AMERICAN SIGN LANGUAGE I: BEG
ASL 601D.description: This course focuses on the development of beginner-level comprehension and production skills in American Sign Language. Students learn how to attend to visual language and use their hands and body for producing ASL constructions. Elementary vocabulary and grammar is covered, along with information about Deaf culture.

ASL 610D.title: AMERICAN SIGN LANGUAGE II: BEG
ASL 610D.description: Focuses on the development of intermediate-level proficiency in ASL. An introduction to more complex grammatical constructions with the goal of engaging in conversations in ASL on a variety of topics. Examines the historical aspects of the Deaf community and related themes.

AST 110C.title: CONFERENCE COURSE IN ASTRONOMY
AST 110C.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register. Independent research course with supervision by astronomy faculty member or research scientist.

AST 175C.title: CONFERENCE COURSE IN ASTRONOMY
AST 175C.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register. Independent research course with astronomy faculty member or research scientist.

AST 210C.title: CONFERENCE COURSE IN ASTRONOMY
AST 210C.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register. Independent research course with supervision by astronomy faculty member or research scientist.

AST 212G.title: GALAXY EVOLUTION - VIP II
AST 212G.description: Collaborate with senior mentoring team to define and develop capstone research project. Complete original project initiated in Astronomy 211G, and present results at the CNS Undergraduate Research Fair. Analyze and discuss current scientific papers related to astronomy.

AST 222G.title: GALAXY EVOLUTION-VIP CAPSTONE
AST 222G.description: Continue capstone research project developed in Astronomy 212G. Discuss and pursue publication possibilities. Present poster of research results at the CNS Undergraduate Research Fair and in an oral presentation.

AST 275.title: WHITE DWARF STARS-FRI
AST 275.description: Designed for science and engineering majors.

AST 275C.title: CONFERENCE COURSE IN ASTRONOMY
AST 275C.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register. Independent research course with astronomy faculty member or research scientist.

AST 301.title: INTRODUCTION TO ASTRONOMY
AST 301.description: General introduction to astronomy for nonscience majors. The solar system, stars, galaxies, and cosmology. Taught as a Web-based course.

AST 307.title: INTRODUCTORY ASTRONOMY
AST 307.description: Introduction to astronomy for science and engineering students. The solar system, stars, galaxies, and cosmology.

AST 309C.title: BIRTH OF STARS AND PLANETS
AST 309C.description: Study of how stars and planets form, including discussions on the implications for the formation of our own solar system. Designed for non-College of Natural Sciences majors. Astronomy 309 (Topic: Birth of Stars and Planets) and 309C may not both be counted. May not be counted toward a degree in the College of Natural Sciences.

AST 309L.title: SEARCH FOR EXTRATERRESTRL LIFE
AST 309L.description: Origin of life in the solar system, existence of other planetary systems, possibilities and techniques for detection of and communication with other intelligences. Designed for non-College of Natural Sciences majors. May not be counted toward a degree in the College of Natural Sciences.

AST 310C.title: CONFERENCE COURSE IN ASTRONOMY
AST 310C.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register. Independent research course with supervision by astronomy faculty member or research scientist.

AST 322G.title: GALAXY EVOLUTION-VIP CAPSTONE
AST 322G.description: Continue capstone research project developed in Astronomy 212G. Discuss and pursue publication possibilities. Present poster of research results at the CNS Undergraduate Research Fair and in an oral presentation. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Offered on the letter-grade basis only. May be repeated for credit.

AST 352K.title: STELLAR ASTRONOMY
AST 352K.description: Properties of stars and starlight: principles of radiation; interpretation of stellar spectra. Observational techniques such as photometry, spectroscopy, and telescopes and detectors; variable stars; binary stars.

AST 353.title: ASTROPHYSICS
AST 353.description: Survey of the physics of stellar and nonstellar radiation laws, stellar atmospheres and interiors; high-energy astrophysics. Designed for science and engineering majors.

AST 375.title: WHITE DWARF STARS-FRI
AST 375.description: Designed for science and engineering majors.

AST 375C.title: CONFERENCE COURSE IN ASTRONOMY
AST 375C.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register. Independent research course with astronomy faculty member or research scientist.

AST 379H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
AST 379H.description: Research project and thesis for students electing to take the honors program in astronomy. May be repeated once for credit.

B A 101H.title: PROF DEVEL & CAREER PLAN: HON
B A 101H.description: Professional development issues including self-assessment, identification of personal life goals, identification of business majors and exploration of potential career fields for each major, and analysis and discussion of the academic planning process and how it relates to professional development and career planning.

B A 101S.title: PROF DEVLOP & CAREER PLANNING
B A 101S.description: Discussion of issues surrounding career planning, implementation, and evaluation in order to establish career goals. Strategies for executing a successful job search, including interviewing techniques, resumes, networking, and job search ethics. Focus on career management as a lifelong process.

B A 101T.title: PROF DEVLOP/CAREER PLANNING
B A 101T.description: Discussion of issues surrounding career planning, implementation, and evaluation.

B A 102F.title: CAREER PLANNING
B A 102F.description: Practice career exploration, planning, and networking techniques. Focus on exploring interests, job search, and alignment of personal and professional goals. Explore experiential learning and peer-to-peer assessment to optimize resume, storytelling, and interviewing techniques. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Business Administration degree.

B A 105.title: LEADERSHIP-MSS
B A 105.description: Explores current theory and research related to peer mentoring and leadership with higher education and business.

B A 324.title: BUSN COMM: ORAL AND WRITTEN
B A 324.description: Theory and practice of effective communication, using models from business situations. Students practice what they learn with a variety of in-class activities, written assignments, and oral presentations. Teamwork and use of interpersonal skills are included.

B A 324H.title: BUSN COMM: ORAL/WRIT: HON
B A 324H.description: Theory and practice of effective communication, using models from business situations. Students practice what they learn with a variety of in-class activities, written assignments, and oral presentations. Teamwork and use of interpersonal skills are included.

B A 353.title: INTERNSHIP BUSINESS ADMIN-WB
B A 353.description: Provides a format for reflection during a professional internship, resulting in improved ability to both articulate preferences and skills, and to match them with appropriate career-related choices. Taught as a Web-based course.

B A 653.title: INTERNSHIP BUSINESS ADMIN-WB
B A 653.description: Provides a format for reflection during a professional internship, resulting in improved ability to both articulate preferences and skills, and to match them with appropriate career-related choices. Taught as a Web-based course.

BAH 151H.title: LYCEUM
BAH 151H.description: Observe professionals from various fields of business.

BAX 304.title: INTRO PROB SOLV/PROGRAMMING
BAX 304.description: Programming skills for creating easy-to-maintain systems for business applications. Object-oriented and structured methodologies with Python.

BAX 325.title: DATABASE MANAGEMENT
BAX 325.description: Beginning and intermediate topics in data modeling for relational database management systems.

BAX 338.title: SUPPLY CHAIN MODLNG/OPTIMIZ
BAX 338.description: Framing, formulating, and applying quantitative optimization and descriptive models to support supply chain and operations management decisions, using spreadsheets and other software. Requires familiarity with spreadsheets.

BAX 357.title: PREDICTIVE ANALYTICS
BAX 357.description: Introduction to machine learning and artificial intelligence techniques with a focus on business applications and decision-making. Examine predictive frameworks, including tree-based techniques and artificial neural networks and their applications in business contexts. Explore solid foundations for evaluating models to understand their impact in a given business context, discuss issues related to algorithmic decision-making, and algorithmic bias and fairness.

BAX 358.title: OPTIM METHODS/DECISION MAKING
BAX 358.description: Examine decision-making in deterministic settings. Discuss optimization and planning in various contexts including portfolio selection, production planning, marketing allocations, revenue management and pricing. Explore linear, quadratic, nonlinear, and integer programming. Utilize Python for hands-on work.

BAX 360.title: INFORMATION AND ANALYSIS
BAX 360.description: The development and analysis of information for marketing management sources. Marketing 360, 460 and Business Analytics 360, 460 may not both be counted.

BAX 372T.title: GEN AI IN BUSINESS/SOCL SCI
BAX 372T.description: Explore quantitative methods and techniques in optimization and simulation, and their use in financial decision making. Discuss theory and application in portfolio selection, options and other derivative pricing, index tracking, risk measures, volatility estimating. Examine linear, quadratic, nonlinear, and integer programming; dynamic programming; robust optimization; Monte Carlo methods and variance reduction techniques. Emphasis will be placed on problem solving with advanced computational programming languages.

BAX 375.title: BUSINESS ANALYTICS IN PRACTICE
BAX 375.description: Examine the role of business analytics in the context of the broader operations of a firm. Explore the use of business analytics in firms, the impact on business performance, and the ways that it can enhance firm value, through a series of case studies.

BCH 206K.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
BCH 206K.description: Introduction to research practices; supervised individual undergraduate research in biochemistry. May be taken for a letter grade no more than twice. No more than six semester hours may be counted toward a degree in biochemistry.

BCH 219L.title: INTRO BIOCHM LAB PRACTS/SKILLS
BCH 219L.description: Explore basic skills used by biochemists and an introduction to future career options. Examine basic biochemical concepts and laboratory, computational, and professional skills. Biochemistry 219L and Molecular Biosciences 219L may not both be counted.

BCH 339F.title: FOUNDATIONS OF BIOCHEMISTRY
BCH 339F.description: Metabolism of carbohydrates, lipids, amino acids, and nucleotides; structure and function of proteins.

BCH 339J.title: CHEMICAL AND SYNTHETIC BIOLOGY
BCH 339J.description: Topics include enzymatic reaction mechanisms and how they can be manipulated using tools from both chemistry and molecular biology. Designed for students pursuing pharmaceutical and biotechnology careers.

BCH 339M.title: STRUCT/FUNCT OF MOL MACHINES
BCH 339M.description: Function of proteins and protein complexes as machines in the cell, including the interaction of proteins with nucleic acids, synthesis of proteins, and degradation of proteins.

BCH 339N.title: SYSTEMS BIO/BIOINFORMATICS
BCH 339N.description: Understanding how the cell works as a system with emphasis on the methods used to gather and analyze data and develop/test models of systems level data. Biochemistry 339N and 364C may not both be counted.

BCH 364F.title: ASTROBIOLOGY
BCH 364F.description: An overview of the science used in the search for extraterrestrial life, life origins, earth history, evolution, metabolism of extremophiles, biochemistry, and astronomy.

BCH 368H.title: CAPSTONE HONORS RESEARCH
BCH 368H.description: Conduct a laboratory research project in a specific field of biochemistry under the supervision of one or more faculty members. May be taken twice for credit.

BCH 368L.title: CPSTN COURSE-BASED RSCH EXPER
BCH 368L.description: Explore experimental design, data collection, data analysis and fundamental principles of biochemistry research.

BCH 368M.title: CAPSTONE EXPERIENCE IN BIOCHEM
BCH 368M.description: Explore approved capstone experiences such as Biochemistry Research Experience Abroad, Biochemistry/Biotechnology Internships or other external, intensive biochemistry-related research experiences.

BCH 368R.title: SUPERVISED CAPSTONE RESEARCH
BCH 368R.description: Perform supervised independent research and write a capstone thesis on a selected subject in Biochemistry by individual arrangement with the instructor.

BCH 368W.title: SUPERVISED LITERATURE REVIEW
BCH 368W.description: Perform a supervised literature review and write a capstone thesis on a selected subject in Biochemistry by individual arrangement with the instructor.

BCH 369.title: FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOCHEMISTRY
BCH 369.description: The basics of protein structure and function, carbon and nitrogen metabolism, and molecular biology of macromolecules.

BCH 369K.title: TECHNIQUES OF RESEARCH
BCH 369K.description: Advanced laboratory practice and introduction to research. May be taken for a letter grade no more than twice. No more than six semester hours may be counted toward a degree in biochemistry. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. May be repeated for credit.

BCH 370.title: PHYSICAL METHODS OF BIOCHEM
BCH 370.description: Theory of electrophoresis, ultracentrifugation, spectroscopy, electron microscopy, and diffraction as applied to biological macromolecules. Biochemistry 370 and Chemistry 370 may not both be counted.

BCH 379H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
BCH 379H.description: Laboratory research project in a specific field of biochemistry under the supervision of one or more faculty members. May be repeated once for credit. Must be taken in addition to the required hours for a degree in biochemistry. Students must enroll no later than the first semester of the year of graduation.

BDP 101.title: 3-EXPLORG DIGITAL ART/MEDIA
BDP 101.description: Discuss various contemporary issues in leadership and ethics, with an emphasis on interdisciplinary perspectives and critical discourse. Bridging Disciplines 101 (Topic: Narrative Leadership) and 101 (Topic 14) may not both be counted. Class meets August 24-October 19.

BDP 319.title: TPCS SPECZD FORUM SMNR SERIES
BDP 319.description: Discuss various contemporary issues in museum studies, with an emphasis on multidisciplinary perspectives and critical discourse. Bridging Disciplines 319 (Topic: Museum Studies) and 319 (Topic 2) may not both be counted.

BDP 320L.title: CONNECTING RSRCH EXPERIENCE
BDP 320L.description: Supervised research with a faculty member, related to the interdisciplinary themes of a Bridging Disciplines Program. Research may consist of an individual project and must meet the criteria for the independent inquiry flag.

BDP 325K.title: CONNECTING INTERN EXPERIENCE
BDP 325K.description: Supervised internship experience related to the interdisciplinary themes of a Bridging Disciplines Program.

BDP 329.title: CURATING ACROSS THE ARTS
BDP 329.description: Discussion of contemporary issues related to the topics of a Bridging Disciplines Program, with an emphasis on interdisciplinary perspectives, research, and critical discourse. Discuss contemporary issues related to race and medicine in American life, with an emphasis on interdisciplinary perspectives, research, and critical discourse. Bridging Disciplines 329 (Topic: Race/Medicine in Amer Life) and 329 (Topic 1) may not both be counted.

BGS 372.title: STRATEGIC CORP SOCIAL RESP
BGS 372.description: A study of how corporations can engage in corporate social responsibility and sustainable activities to improve the world with strategic considerations kept in mind.

BGS 372T.title: BUSINESS AND SOCIETY
BGS 372T.description: Explore the role of sustainability in the corporate sector. Examine how businesses partner with others for sustainability goals, how to value sustainable business practices, and how to report on corporate sustainability.

BGS 373.title: STRATEGIC CORP COMM
BGS 373.description: Studies corporate efforts to inform, motivate, and persuade various constituencies, including investors, employees, communities, and regulators. Subjects include public relations, investor relations, and government relations.

BGS 374.title: GLOBAL POLITICAL ECONOMY
BGS 374.description: Explore how firms can successfully integrate their traditional strategies with non-market strategy in the global arena, including how to successfully engage with international non-market actors, such as foreign governments, regulators, political parties, NGOs, and the media. Examine the necessary tools to craft successful, integrated firm strategies in diverse institutional and political environments.

BIO 102C.title: CONFERENCE COURSE-BSP
BIO 102C.description: Supervised study of selected subjects in biology, by individual arrangement with the instructor.

BIO 206L.title: INTRO LAB EXPERIMENTS IN BIOL
BIO 206L.description: The organizing principles of biology (such as molecular and cellular functions, reproduction, development, homeostatic mechanisms, and organismal physiology and behavior) are used within a comparative and evolutionary framework to train students in modern laboratory techniques, bioinformatics, experimental design, and interpretation of results. Biology 106M and 206L may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

BIO 301E.title: PROBLEMS IN MODERN BIOLOGY
BIO 301E.description: An introduction to major concepts in biology, with emphasis on topics, such as genetics, that are relevant to current issues in the field. Biology 301E and 301L may not both be counted; Biology 301E and 301M may not both be counted. May not be counted toward a degree in biology.

BIO 302D.title: SL/N: ECOLOGY AND EVOLUTION
BIO 302D.description: Explore science literacy and numeracy skills through methods of inquiry; dealing with quantitative data; and correcting common misconceptions regarding rational and quantitative thought. Examine these skills in the context of news events that include reference to some or all of the following: Mendelian genetics; microevolution; macroevolution; population ecology; community ecology; ecosystem ecology. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

BIO 311C.title: INTRODUCTORY BIOLOGY I
BIO 311C.description: Introduction to biological energy transformation, cell structure and physiology, and gene expression.

BIO 311D.title: INTRODUCTORY BIOLOGY II
BIO 311D.description: Introduction to mechanisms of inheritance, evolution, physiology, and species interactions. Biology 301L and 311D may not both be counted. Biology 301M and 311D may not both be counted.

BIO 315H.title: ADV INTRO TO GENETICS: HONORS
BIO 315H.description: Basic principles of genetics and cell biology. Emphasis on gene structure and regulation; transmission of heritable traits; structure and function of cells; bacterial and viral genetics; and recombinant DNA technology. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

BIO 325.title: GENETICS
BIO 325.description: Basic principles of Mendelism, molecular genetics, structure and function of genes and chromosomes, populations and evolution. Biology 325 and 325H may not both be counted.

BIO 325L.title: LAB EXPERIENCE IN GENETICS
BIO 325L.description: Experimentation and direct observation in fundamental aspects of transmission genetics.

BIO 337.title: GREAT WORKS IN BIOLOGY
BIO 337.description: Recent developments and research methods in the biological sciences. Explore the history of biology through a close study of several major texts. Analyze controversies about authority, classification, extinction, evolution, and genetics. Discuss topics including: what life is, how technology has aided our understanding of living things, whether "species" actually exist, and what natural selection selects.

BIO 372C.title: BIO PEER MENTORS RSCH/TCH
BIO 372C.description: Students work as peer mentors and assistants in the teaching of biology, with emphasis on developing instructional materials that teach fundamental biology with real world data. Biology 372C and Chemistry 372C may not both be counted.

BME 177.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH PROJECT
BME 177.description: Initiate opportunity individually and select project in conjunction with a faculty member on the Department of Biomedical Engineering's Graduate Studies Committee (GSC) or in another approved University department. A final written report or the equivalent is required. Recommended for students considering graduate study or medical school.

BME 214L.title: COMPUT FUND OF BME DESIGN
BME 214L.description: Overview of computational methods used to model biomedical systems, analyze data, and assist in the engineering design process. Subjects and hands-on learning applications include 3D modeling tools and computer aided manufacturing, flow simulation, instrumentation, biomedical imaging modalities, and biomedical informatics. Biomedical Engineering 314 and 214L may not both be counted.

BME 245L.title: EXPERIMNTL PRINC OF BME DESIGN
BME 245L.description: Exploration of biomedical engineering design principles through open-ended, hands-on research applications involving mechanical testing, cell culture, and biomaterials. Analysis and interpretation of experimental data, including written reporting of outcomes in scientific journal format. Biomedical Engineering 221 and 245L may not both be counted.

BME 277.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH PROJECT
BME 277.description: Initiate opportunity individually and select project in conjunction with a faculty member on the Department of Biomedical Engineering's Graduate Studies Committee (GSC) or in another approved University department. A final written report or the equivalent is required. Recommended for students considering graduate study or medical school.

BME 303.title: INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTING
BME 303.description: Introduction to computing and programming, focusing on high-level programming languages. Emphasis on programming skills using C++ and Python as well as data acquisition using open-source computer hardware. Emphasis is on biomedical engineering applications for computing.

BME 303L.title: INTRODUCTION TO BME DESIGN
BME 303L.description: Biomedical engineering design concepts with hands-on learning applications. Biomedical Engineering 102L and 203L, 303L may not both be counted.

BME 311.title: NETWORK ANLY IN BIOMED ENGR
BME 311.description: Basic concepts in circuit analysis and design of systems for biomedical engineering; Ohm's law, Kirchhoff's laws, and nodal and loop analysis; Theyenin's and Norton's theorem; operational amplifiers; high-order circuit and basic AC circuit analysis using Fourier and Laplace transforms.

BME 313L.title: INTRO NUMERICAL MTHDS IN BME
BME 313L.description: Introduces principles and techniques of numerical analysis of biomedical engineering problems. Examines numerical methods of integration, differentiation, interpolation, curve fitting, data analysis, sampling and estimation, error analysis, analysis of ordinary differential equations, numerical modeling of biomedical engineering systems, symbolic computation, and scientific visualization.

BME 335.title: ENGR PROBABILITY/STATISTICS
BME 335.description: Fundamentals of probability, random processes, and statistics with emphasis on biomedical engineering applications. Includes hypothesis testing, regression, and sample size calculations.

BME 336.title: CANCER BIOENGINEERING
BME 336.description: Comprehensive overview of the biology and pathology of cancer. Emphasis on the fundamental nature of cancer, the roles of genetics and the environment, and the contributions of heterotypic tissues in the tumor microenvironment. Exploration of current challenges in the field and ways that various engineering tools are transforming the future of cancer research. Biomedical Engineering 336 and 377T (Topic: Cancer Bioengineering) may not both be counted.

BME 342.title: BIOMECHANICS OF HUMAN MOVEMENT
BME 342.description: Modeling and simulation of human movement; neuromuscular control; computer applications; introduction to experimental techniques. Biomedical Engineering 342 and Mechanical Engineering 354M may not both be counted.

BME 343.title: BIOMED ENGR SIGNAL/SYS ANLY
BME 343.description: Signals and systems representation; sampling and quantization; time and frequency domains; Laplace and z-transforms, transfer functions, and frequency response; two-port networks; Bode plots; convolution; stability; Fourier series; Fourier transform; AM/FM modulation; filter design; and applications in biomedical engineering.

BME 344.title: BIOMECHANICS
BME 344.description: Examine the principles of engineering mechanics and their applications to basic problems in biomechanics and biomedical engineering. Explore internal forces and deformations in solids, including stress, strain, tension, compression, torsion, bending, internal pressure, and failure analysis under combined mechanical loading. Biomedical Engineering 344 and 377T (Topic: Biomechanics) may not both be counted.

BME 347.title: FUNDMNTLS OF BIOMEDICAL OPTICS
BME 347.description: Introduction to the field of biophotonics. Subjects include light scattering theory, tissue optical properties, analytical and numerical techniques for determining light propagation in tissue, light-tissue interactions, and bioheat transfer including solution methods. Biomedical Engineering 347 and 377T (Topic: Fundamentals of Biomedical Engineering Optical Imaging) may not both be counted.

BME 350.title: COMP MTHDS FOR BIOMED ENGRS
BME 350.description: Study of and hands-on experiences with computational methods commonly employed in biomedical engineering research. Biomedical Engineering 350 and 377T (Topic: Computational Methods for Biomedical Engineers) may not both be counted.

BME 352.title: ENGINEERING BIOMATERIALS
BME 352.description: Overview of properties of metallic, ceramic, polymeric, and composite biomaterials used in biomedical applications. Material synthesis and processing. Analysis of mechanical and chemical properties, including stress-strain. Material interactions with the body and blood. Soft and hard biomaterials applications.

BME 353.title: TRANSPORT PHENOM IN LIVING SYS
BME 353.description: Introduction to the principles of heat, mass and momentum transfer and their application to solve problems in living systems.

BME 354.title: MOLEC SEN/NANODEV FOR BME APPL
BME 354.description: Introduction to major types of molecular sensor systems, device miniaturization, and detection mechanisms, including molecular capture mechanisms; electrical, optical, and mechanical transducers; micro-array analysis of biomolecules; semiconductor and metal nanosensors; microfluidic systems; and microelectromechanical systems (MEMS, BioMEMS) fabrication and applications for biomedical engineering.

BME 355.title: MOLECULAR ENGINEERING
BME 355.description: Working principles of biomolecules such as proteins and nucleic acids. Physical and chemical basis for biomolecular structure, energetics, and function.

BME 363.title: BIOELECTRONICS/BIOINTERFACES
BME 363.description: Examine critical concepts and strategies in materials development, electronics fabrication and genetic innovations that interface with biological systems. Explore neural interfacing technology, flexible electronics, wearable technology, bio-nanotechnology, and genetic engineering. Focus on the recently developed technology for recording and manipulating neural systems. Biomedical Engineering 363 and 377T (Topic: Bioelectronics/Biointerfaces) may not both be counted.

BME 364.title: BIOLGCL RSPNS MEDCL DEVICES
BME 364.description: Explore common biological responses elicited by biomaterials and the impact of these responses on material performance. Emphasis on key challenges in the development and assessment of biomaterials used in medical devices. Includes material surface properties, modification, and characterization; protein/cell interactions with materials; biocompatibility, inflammation and wound healing, cell-mediated biodegradation of materials; thrombosis, infection and calcification of medical devices. Biomedical Engineering 364 and 377T (Topic: Biol Responses to Medical Dev) may not both be counted.

BME 365R.title: QUANTITATIVE ENGR PHYSIOL I
BME 365R.description: A quantitative, model-oriented approach to human physiology, including basic cellular physiology, electrophysiology of nerve and muscle, the motor system, the central nervous system, and the cardiovascular system.

BME 370.title: BME CAPSTONE DESIGN I
BME 370.description: Structured methodologies for designing systems or to interface with living systems. Creative design, analysis, selection, development, and fabrication of biomedical components and systems.

BME 372C.title: DELIVERY OF THERAPEUTIC AGENTS
BME 372C.description: Explore molecular, biological, chemical, and mathematical fundamentals of the use of macromolecular carriers for the release of small and high molecular weight therapeutic agents in vitro and in vivo. Examine design and advanced techniques of protein, peptide, antibodies, and gene therapy. Discuss targeted delivery, mucoadhesive therapy, and theranostics, as well as advanced biomedical devices for treatment of diseases. Biomedical Engineering 372C and 377T (Topic: Delivery of Therapeutic Agents) may not both be counted.

BME 373.title: TISSUE/SCAFFOLD/CELL BIOMCH
BME 373.description: Fundamentals of biosolid mechanics via the mechanical behavior of scaffolds and living tissues and cells. Emphasis on biomechanical applications to contemporary problems in biomedical research and medical devices. Biomedical Engineering 373 and 377T (Topic: Cell, Tissue and Scaffold Biomechanics for Contemporary Biomedical Engineering Applications) may not both be counted.

BME 377.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH PROJECT
BME 377.description: Initiate opportunity individually and select project in conjunction with a faculty member on the Department of Biomedical Engineering's Graduate Studies Committee (GSC) or in another approved University department. A final written report or the equivalent is required. Recommended for students considering graduate study or medical school.

BME 377M.title: MEDICAL INTERNSHIP
BME 377M.description: Initiate opportunity individually, which may include participation in a variety of medical and clinical activities which apply engineering principles to solve problems in healthcare. A substantial final report or the equivalent is required. Designed for students considering medical school.

BME 377R.title: OFF-CAMPUS RESEARCH
BME 377R.description: Initiate opportunity individually and select project in conjunction with a faculty member at an approved institution. A final written report or the equivalent is required.

BME 377S.title: INDUSTRIAL INTERNSHIP
BME 377S.description: Practical work experience in industry or a government agency under the supervision of an engineer, scientist or business professional. A substantial final report or the equivalent is required.

BME 377T.title: MRI IN RESEARCH/MEDICINE
BME 377T.description: Explore the physics and physiology of modern MRI techniques in clinical medicine (radiology, cardiology) and research (neuroscience, engineering), including relaxometry, functional MRI, diffusion, arterial spin labeling and phase contrast imaging in the brain, heart and body. Additional prerequisite: Biomedical Engineering 343 or Electrical and Computer Engineering 313 (or Electrical Engineering 313) with a grade of at least C-.

BME 379.title: TISSUE ENGINEERING
BME 379.description: Introduction to biomedical research in tissue engineering. Includes case studies of tissues and organs of the body, physiology and biology of tissue, pathologies of tissue, current clinical treatments, the role of engineers in development of new technologies to diagnose and treat pathologies, quantitative cellular and molecular techniques, and applications of synthetic and natural biomaterials.

BME 678A.title: UNDERGRAD THESIS BIOMED ENGR
BME 678A.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of a biomedical engineering faculty member or other approved faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and faculty member. The student provides a progress report at the end of the first semester and writes a thesis and gives an oral presentation at the end of the second semester. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and a Bachelor of Science in Biomedical Engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirements for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

BME 678B.title: UNDERGRAD THESIS BIOMED ENGR
BME 678B.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of a biomedical engineering faculty member or other approved faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and faculty member. The student provides a progress report at the end of the first semester and writes a thesis and gives an oral presentation at the end of the second semester. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and a Bachelor of Science in Biomedical Engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirements for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

BME 679HA.title: UNDERGRADUATE HONORS THESIS
BME 679HA.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of an engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Engineering Honors Program. The student makes an oral presentation and writes a thesis. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and a bachelor's degree in engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirement for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

BME 679HB.title: UNDERGRADUATE HONORS THESIS
BME 679HB.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of an engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Engineering Honors Program. The student makes an oral presentation and writes a thesis. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and a bachelor's degree in engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirement for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

C C 301.title: INTRO TO ANCIENT GREECE
C C 301.description: Greatness of Greece as reflected in Greek history, literature, philosophy, art, religion, and politics. No knowledge of Greek is required.

C C 302.title: INTRO TO ANCIENT ROME-WB
C C 302.description: Survey of the highlights and the influence of Roman civilization. No knowledge of Latin is required. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

C C 303.title: INTRO TO CLASSICAL MYTHOLGY-WB
C C 303.description: Survey of major Greek and Roman myths and their influence on literature, art, and music. Classical Civilization 303 and 352 may not both be counted. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

C C 304C.title: 6-ANCIENT PHILOSOPHY
C C 304C.description: Primarily for lower-division students. An introductory survey of the highlights of Greek and Roman civilization and early Christianity. No knowledge of Greek or Latin is required. An introduction to the philosophical achievements of the ancient world, concentrating on Plato and Aristotle. Classical Civilization 304C and 348 may not both be counted unless the topics vary. Only one of the following may be counted: Classical Civilization 304C (Topic: Ancient Philosophy), 304C (Topic 6), Philosophy 301K.

C C 304J.title: ART/ARCH OF ANCIENT EGYPT
C C 304J.description: Introduction to the complex and intriguing culture of ancient Egypt and to its legacy in society through the examination of its art and archaeology.

C C 306M.title: INTRO TO MEDCL & SCIENTIF TERM
C C 306M.description: A systematic study of medical and scientific terminology based on Greek and Latin roots. No knowledge of Greek or Latin is required. Classical Civilization 306M and 336M may not both be counted.

C C 307D.title: INTRO TO ROMAN ARCHAEOLOGY
C C 307D.description: A survey of the artifacts, monuments, and sites of ancient Rome, and their value for documenting Roman religious, social, and cultural history. No knowledge of Latin is required.

C C 322D.title: ANCIENT HISTORIANS
C C 322D.description: Examines the main works of ancient historiography, and provides grounding in the central issues with which these works engage.

C C 348.title: 4-HIST OF ANCIENT PHILOSOPHY
C C 348.description: The development and progress of ancient civilization, including history, philosophy, literature, and culture. No knowledge of Greek or Latin is required. Development of Western philosophy from the pre-Socratics to the early Christian era; emphasis on Plato and Aristotle. Classical Civilization 304C and 348 may not both be counted unless the topics vary. Classical Civilization 348 and 375 may not both be counted unless the topics vary. Classical Civilization 348 (Topic 4) and Philosophy 329K may not both be counted.

C C 348L.title: HORROR IN CLASSICS AND FILM
C C 348L.description: Examine themes of modern horror films as they appear across genres and periods of ancient texts. Classical Civilization 348 (Topic: Horror in Classics and Film) and 348L may not both be counted.

C C 348O.title: ARISTOTLE
C C 348O.description: Study the philosophy of Aristotle through the examination of key texts. Classical Civilization 348O and Philosophy 329E may not both be counted.

C C 348S.title: SEX/POWER: THE CLEOPATRAS: HON
C C 348S.description: Explore the social, economic, and political roles of women in the Hellenistic age through the lens of the royal women sharing the dynastic name 'Cleopatra.'

C C 363.title: CONF COURSE IN CLASSICAL CIV
C C 363.description: Supervised work in various specialized aspects of classical civilization. No knowledge of Greek or Latin is required.

C C 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
C C 679HA.description: Supervised conference course for honors candidates in classics.

C C 679HB.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
C C 679HB.description: Supervised conference course for honors candidates in classics.

C E 301.title: CIVIL ENGINEERING SYSTEMS
C E 301.description: Introduction to civil engineering as a career; engineering problem solving; use of computers for text, graphics, and data analysis; introduction to civil engineering measurements; breadth of disciplines within civil engineering; engineering ethics, sustainability.

C E 311K.title: INTRO TO COMPUTER METHODS
C E 311K.description: Organization and programming of civil engineering problems for computer solutions.

C E 311S.title: PROBABIL/STATS FOR CIVIL ENGRS
C E 311S.description: Basic theory of probability and statistics with practical applications to civil engineering problems, including statistical inference and sampling. Additional subjects may include reliability and risk analyses, estimation and regression analyses, and experimental design.

C E 319F.title: ELEMENTARY MECHANICS OF FLUIDS
C E 319F.description: Fluid properties, hydrostatics, elements of fluid dynamics, energy and momentum, boundary layers, similitude, pipe flow, metering instruments, drag forces. Civil Engineering 319F and Mechanical Engineering 330 may not both be counted.

C E 321.title: TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
C E 321.description: Planning, economics, location, construction, operation, maintenance, and design of transportation systems; concepts of various modes of transportation.

C E 324P.title: PROPS AND BEHAV OF ENGR MATLS
C E 324P.description: Structure, properties, and behavior of engineering materials, including concrete and metals. Laboratory exercises illustrate mechanical behavior of typical materials and demonstrate selected principles of mechanics.

C E 329.title: STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS
C E 329.description: Classical methods of analysis for determinate and indeterminate structures under stationary and moving loads.

C E 331.title: REINFORCED CONCRETE DESIGN
C E 331.description: Design of reinforced concrete beams and columns.

C E 335.title: ELEMENTS OF STEEL DESIGN
C E 335.description: Analysis and design of tension members, beams, columns, and bolted and welded connections.

C E 341.title: INTRO TO ENVIRONMENTAL ENGR
C E 341.description: Quantitative evaluation of the environmental, economic, and technical problems involved in control of pollutants of the air, water, and land.

C E 342.title: WATER & WASTEWTR TREATMT ENGR
C E 342.description: Application of chemical, biological, and physical principles to the analysis and design of treatment processes for drinking water, industrial process water, municipal wastewater, and water reuse applications.

C E 356.title: ELEMENTS OF HYDRAULIC ENGR
C E 356.description: Flow in closed conduits, hydraulic machinery; open-channel flow; flow measurement; design of storm sewers.

C E 357.title: GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING
C E 357.description: Engineering properties of soils; hydraulic conductivity and shear strength of soils; soil compaction and consolidation; stresses with the soil mass; settlement of foundations of structures; laboratory measurements.

C E 358.title: INTRODUCTORY OCEAN ENGINEERING
C E 358.description: Wave theory and its applications to coastal engineering, wave energy conversion, and offshore structure technology. Includes fundamentals of inviscid and viscous flow of incompressible fluids, and applications of computational fluid dynamics (CFD) in design.

C E 360K.title: FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
C E 360K.description: Effect of geotechnical conditions on the behavior, proportioning, and choice of foundation type; design of shallow and deep foundations; study of foundation case histories.

C E 363.title: ADVANCED STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS
C E 363.description: Structural analysis for forces and deflections using stiffness and flexibility approaches; application of energy methods in structural analysis; stiffness methods for computer-based structural analysis.

C E 366K.title: DESIGN OF BITUMINOUS MIXTURES
C E 366K.description: Fundamental properties of asphalt and aggregates, design and construction of asphalt mixtures, special mixtures, and superpave design method.

C E 367P.title: PAVEMENT DESIGN & PERFORMANCE
C E 367P.description: Basic principles of design of pavements for highways, airfields, and railroads; pavement construction, maintenance, and rehabilitation.

C E 369L.title: AIR POLLUTION ENGINEERING
C E 369L.description: Characterization of sources, emissions, transport, transformation, effects, and control of outdoor and indoor air pollutants. Applications of chemistry, thermodynamics, and fluid mechanics in the selection and design of air pollution control equipment.

C E 370P.title: ENGINEERING PROFESSIONALISM
C E 370P.description: Examines professional engineering licensure, ethics, leadership, public service, and public policy, with an emphasis on multidisciplinary perspectives, legal and business considerations, and the importance of lifelong learning. Includes participation in a culminating major design project in public service, reflecting knowledge from technical electives and base level coursework.

C E 374U.title: 2-SMART WATER INFRASTRUCTURE
C E 374U.description: Explore the application of engineering hydraulics to design and analyses of urban water distribution systems. Examine computational modeling and control of pipe networks as well as considerations of variable user demand, fireflow, and contaminant transport.

C E 377K.title: PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
C E 377K.description: Various specified topics or conference course. Conduct independent research with a tenure track faculty member in the Fariborz Maseeh Department of Civil, Architectural, and Environmental Engineering. Prepare a project proposal and a final report, each of which is evaluated by the faculty supervisor. C E 177K, 277K, 377K (Topic 7) and C E 177K (Topic: Independent Study) may not both be counted.

C E 679HA.title: UNDERGRADUATE HONORS THESIS
C E 679HA.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of an engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Engineering Honors Program. The student makes an oral presentation and writes a thesis. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and a bachelor's degree in engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirement for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

C L 305.title: MEXICAN WOMEN WRITERS
C L 305.description: Study of masterpieces of world literature; of different literary genres; and the relationship between literature and other disciplines such as psychology, philosophy, and film. Explore the literature and film of Korea from earliest times to the present.

C L 315.title: WORLD LITERATURE
C L 315.description: Introduction to masterpieces of world literature, emphasizing historical, generic, and thematic connections.

C L 323.title: 13-INTRO TO ARABIC LITERATURE
C L 323.description: Study of masterpieces of world literature; of different literary genres; of the relationship between literature and other disciplines, such as psychology, philosophy, and film; and of special topics of a comparative nature. Explores the historical, political, psychological, theological, and cultural fallout of the Holocaust, as well as literary and cinematic responses in Europe and the United States.

C L 323G.title: HISTORY/MEMORY JAPAN/KOREA
C L 323G.description: Explore the fraught and entangled histories of modern Japan and Korea as depicted in literature and film to consider experiences of imperialism, rapid modernization, war, decolonization, and trauma.

C L 324C.title: HANS CHRISTIAN ANDERSEN
C L 324C.description: Introduction to such Hans Christian Andersen tales as "The Little Mermaid," "The Ugly Duckling," and "The Emperor's New Clothes." Examination of their author and the romantic period in which they were written, as well as their historical, philosophical, and literary context.

C S 104C.title: COMPETITIVE PROGRAMMING
C S 104C.description: Explores the algorithms needed to solve a given problem, reason about corner cases, and write correct implementations under time pressure. Focuses on skills useful for international programming competitions, whiteboard job interviews, and programming tasks that require quickly solving challenging problems with creative use of algorithms. Computer Science 104 and 109 (Topic: Competitive Programming) may not both be counted.

C S 109.title: THINK LAB
C S 109.description: Explore a holistic approach to success in the computer science field, both in college and beyond.

C S 174S.title: LONGHORN STARTUP SEMINAR
C S 174S.description: An introduction to the world of startups through the experiences of people who have built successes and learned from failures.

C S 303E.title: ELEMS OF COMPUTERS/PROGRAMMING
C S 303E.description: Problem solving and fundamental algorithms for various applications in science, business, and on the World Wide Web, and introductory programming in a modern object-oriented programming language. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

C S 311.title: DISCRETE MATH FOR COMPUTER SCI
C S 311.description: A focus on discrete mathematical tools of fundamental importance to the working computer scientist. An emphasis is placed on using logical notation to express rigorous mathematical arguments. Subjects include proof by induction, introduction to graph theory, recurrences, sets, functions, and an introduction to program correctness. Computer Science 311 and 311H may not both be counted.

C S 311H.title: DISCRETE MATH COMP SCI: HONORS
C S 311H.description: A focus on discrete mathematical tools of fundamental importance to the working computer scientist. An emphasis is placed on using logical notation to express rigorous mathematical arguments. Subjects include proof by induction, graph theory, number theory, combinatorics, recurrences, sets, functions, an introduction to program correctness, and discrete probability. Computer Science 311 and 311H may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

C S 312.title: INTRODUCTION TO PROGRAMMING
C S 312.description: First part of a two-part sequence in programming. Fundamental concepts of structured programming; procedures and data structures with a focus on problem solving strategies and implementation; introduction to concepts of informal specification, informal reasoning about program behavior, debugging, and ad hoc testing.

C S 313E.title: ELEMENTS OF SOFTWARE DESIGN
C S 313E.description: Object-oriented design of software using Python, a modern high-level language. Introduction to abstract data types, fundamental algorithms, and basics of complexity analysis. May not be counted toward a degree in computer science.

C S 314.title: DATA STRUCTURES
C S 314.description: Second part of a two-part sequence in programming. Introduction to specifications, simple unit testing, and debugging; building and using canonical data structures; algorithm analysis and reasoning techniques such as assertions and invariants. Computer Science 314 and 314H may not both be counted.

C S 314H.title: DATA STRUCTURES: HONORS
C S 314H.description: Second part of a two-part sequence in programming. Introduction to specifications, simple unit testing, and debugging; building and using canonical data structures; algorithm analysis and reasoning techniques such as assertions and invariants. Computer Science 314 and 314H may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

C S 323E.title: ELEMENTS OF SCIENTIF COMPUTING
C S 323E.description: Explore the fundamentals of software issues related to scientific computing. Discuss subjects including floating-point computations, numerical computation errors, interpolation, integration, solution of linear systems of equations, optimization, and initial value problems of ordinary differential equations. Examine implementation of algorithms that are investigated using Python for all scientific computations. Computer Science 323E and 323H may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

C S 324E.title: ELEM OF GRAPHICS/VISUALIZATN
C S 324E.description: Basics of two- and three-dimensional computer graphics systems, modeling and rendering, and selected graphics software APIs. Other topics may include interactive graphics, animation, graphical user interfaces, and the graphical presentation of information. May not be counted toward a degree in computer science. Priority is for students pursuing the Elements of Computing Certificate

C S 327E.title: ELEMENTS OF DATABASES
C S 327E.description: A practical introduction to database management systems, with discussion of database administration and management. Survey of logical modeling, database design with a focus on relational databases, SQL query language, and current applications. Subjects may include data integrity, performance, concurrency, transaction processing, recovery, security, and Web applications. May not be counted toward a degree in computer science.

C S 329E.title: ELEMENTS OF DATA ANALYTICS
C S 329E.description: May not be counted toward a degree in computer science. Priority is for students pursuing the Elements of Computing Certificate.

C S 330E.title: ELMNTS SOFTWARE ENGINEER I
C S 330E.description: Explore software engineering using various programming languages. Use tools to improve the quality of software development. Computer Science 329E (Topic: Elems of Software Engineering) and 330E may not both be counted.

C S 331.title: ALGORITHMS AND COMPLEXITY
C S 331.description: An investigation of algorithmic paradigms: divide and conquer, dynamic programming, greedy algorithms, graph algorithms, randomized algorithms, undecidability, NP-completeness, and approximation algorithms.

C S 342S.title: INTRO TO SPEECH/AUDIO PROCESSG
C S 342S.description: Explore the theory and practice that underlies modern automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems and related technologies. Analyze the acoustic theory of speech production, digital signal processing for speech signals, Hidden Markov Models, Gaussian Mixture Models, language models, finite state transducers, decoding algorithms, and deep neural networks. Computer Science 342S and 378 (Topic: Intro to Speech and Audio Proces) may not both be counted.

C S 343.title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
C S 343.description: An introduction to the field of artificial intelligence (AI), starting from foundations in search and knowledge representation, and then sampling from major AI topics such as probabilistic reasoning, intelligent agents (including for games), machine learning, planning, natural language processing, robotics, computer vision, and philosophical foundations. Programming projects are required.

C S 343H.title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE: HON
C S 343H.description: An introduction to the field of artificial intelligence (AI), starting from foundations in search and knowledge representation, and then sampling from major AI topics such as probabilistic reasoning, intelligent agents (including for games), machine learning, planning, natural language processing, robotics, computer vision, and philosophical foundations. Programming projects are required.

C S 347.title: DATA MANAGEMENT
C S 347.description: Concepts of database design and database system implementation. Data models, query processing, database design theory, crash recovery, concurrent control, and distributed databases.

C S 349.title: CONTEMP ISSUES IN COMPUTER SCI
C S 349.description: An examination of social, professional, and ethical issues involved in the use of computer technology. Subjects may include software engineering ethics, computer safety and reliability, constitutional issues, intellectual property, computer crime, societal impact, emerging technologies, and philosophical issues. Computer Science 349 and 378 (Topic: Contemporary Issues in Computer Science) may not both be counted.

C S 350M.title: ENERGY-EFFICIENT COMPUTING
C S 350M.description: Explore the technical aspects related to energy efficiency. Focus on hardware techniques for managing energy, operating system interfaces, cloud computing, high-performance computing, and Blockchain technology. Computer Science 350M and 378 (Topic: Energy Efficient Computing) may not both be counted.

C S 354P.title: GAME PROGRAMMING PARADIGMS
C S 354P.description: Explore game programming paradigms in C++ using the Unreal Engine. Examine event-driven, component-oriented and object-oriented programming, as well as collaborative software development using source control, bug-tracking, and code documentation. Develop and apply skills towards building a game on the Unreal Engine. Computer Science 354P and 378 (Topic: Game Programming Paradigms) may not both be counted.

C S 354R.title: GAME TECHNOLOGY
C S 354R.description: Examine major technological components of computer games and their integration, modeling and animation, physics simulation, networking for games, interface and interaction techniques, and special effects. Computer Science 354R and 378 (Topic: Game Technology) may not both be counted.

C S 354S.title: GAME DEV CAPSTNE: 2-D GAMES
C S 354S.description: Participation in an interdisciplinary team to gain a thorough understanding of the two-dimensional game development process, through modeling of the environment and practices that are used in game studios. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

C S 356.title: COMPUTER NETWORKS
C S 356.description: Introduction to computer networks with emphasis on the Internet.

C S 356R.title: INTRO TO WIRELESS NETWORKS
C S 356R.description: An introduction to basic knowledge in wireless networks ranging from the physical layer to the application layer, and across different types of wireless networks such as WiFi, cellular, and multihop networks. Computer Science 356R and 378 (Topic: Introduction to Wireless Networks) may not both be counted.

C S 358H.title: INTRO TO QUANTUM INFO SCI: HON
C S 358H.description: Introduction to the theory of quantum computing and information. Explore the rules of quantum mechanics (qubits, unitary transformations, measurements, density matrices); quantum gates and circuits; entanglement; the Bell inequality; protocols for teleportation, quantum key distribution, and other tasks; basic quantum algorithms such as Shor's and Grover's; basic quantum complexity theory; basic quantum error correction; decoherence and the measurement problem; and the challenges of building scalable quantum computers. Previous exposure to quantum mechanics is not required. Computer Science 358H and 378H (Topic: Intro to Quantum Info Sci: Hon) may not both be counted.

C S 360V.title: VIRTUALIZATION
C S 360V.description: Examine the latest in virtualization technologies such as virtual machines, containers and serverless computing. Computer Science 360V and 378 (Topic: Virtualization) may not both be counted.

C S 361S.title: NETWORK SECURITY AND PRIVACY
C S 361S.description: An examination of common computer security threats; fundamental techniques for implementing secure software systems and networks; practical experience in attacking and defending networked systems.

C S 363M.title: PRINCIPLES OF MACHINE LEARN I
C S 363M.description: Introduction to machine learning: data processing, regression, classification, clustering, dimensionality reduction, and neural networks. Taught as a Web-based course.

C S 370.title: UNDERGRAD READING AND RESEARCH
C S 370.description: Supervised study of selected problems in computer science, by individual arrangement with supervising instructor. Computer Science 370 and 370F may not both be counted. No more than three semester hours may be counted toward a degree in computer science.

C S 370F.title: UNDERGRAD RDG/RSCH: WRITING
C S 370F.description: Supervised study of selected problems in computer science, by individual arrangement with supervising instructor. Computer Science 370 and 370F may not both be counted. No more than three semester hours may be counted toward a degree in computer science.

C S 371L.title: IOS MOBILE COMPUTING
C S 371L.description: Overview of mobile computing with an emphasis on developing applications for the iOS operating system. Computer Science 371L and 378 (Topic: iOS Mobile Computing) may not both be counted. Course requires access to a Mac computer.

C S 371N.title: NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
C S 371N.description: Explore modern natural language processing using machine learning and deep learning approaches. Examine linguistics fundamentals (syntax, semantics, distributional properties of language), machine learning models (classifiers, sequence taggers, deep neural networks), key algorithms for inference, and a range of applications.
