Build systems to do tasks including sentiment classification, text generation, and more. Computer Science 371N and 378 (Topic: Natural Language Processing) may not both be counted.

C S 371P.title: OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
C S 371P.description: Programming using class derivation, inheritance, and dynamic polymorphism. Application of a simple object-oriented design methodology to several software development problems.

C S 371R.title: INFO RETRIEVAL AND WEB SEARCH
C S 371R.description: Introduction to traditional and recent methodologies for indexing, processing, querying, and classifying unstructured and semi-structured textual data, including hypertext and World Wide Web documents. Computer Sciences 371R and 378 (Topic: Intelligent Information Retrieval and Web Search) may not both be counted.

C S 373.title: SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
C S 373.description: Introduction to current knowledge, techniques, and theories in large software system design and development.

C S 374H.title: ADV UNDGR SPRVSD RSCH: HON
C S 374H.description: Engage in directed research in areas of computer science, under supervision of a faculty member, leading to a presentation. Computer Science 374H and 378H (Topic: Adv Undgr Supervised Research) may not both be counted.

C S 375.title: COMPILERS
C S 375.description: An examination of formal description of languages, lexical analysis, syntax analysis, syntax-directed translation, run-time system management, code generation, code optimization, compiler-building tools. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

C S 376.title: COMPUTER VISION
C S 376.description: Explores computer vision, a discipline that develops methods that enable machines to interpret or analyze images and videos. Includes the study of image formation, feature detection, segmentation, multiple-view geometry, recognition and learning, and motion and tracking. Computer Science 376 and 378 (Topic: Computer Vision) may not both be counted.

C S 378.title: AUTON INTELLGNT ROBTCS II-FRI
C S 378.description: Students should have completed CS 439: Computer Systems with a grade of C- or better as the prerequisite for Cloud-Native Computing. Taught as a Web-based course.

C S 379H.title: COMPUTER SCIENCE HONORS THESIS
C S 379H.description: Conduct directed research in areas of computer science leading to an honors thesis.

C S 429.title: COMP ORGANIZATN AND ARCH
C S 429.description: An introduction to low-level computer design ranging from the basics of digital design to the hardware/software interface for application programs. Includes basic systems principles of pipelining and caching, and requires writing and understanding programs at multiple levels. Computer Science 429 and 429H may not both be counted.

C S 439.title: PRINCIPLES OF COMPUTER SYS-C S
C S 439.description: An introduction to computer systems software abstractions with an emphasis on the connection of these abstractions to underlying computer hardware. Key abstractions include threads, virtual memory, protection, and I/O. Requires writing of synchronized multithreaded programs and pieces of an operating system. Computer Science 439 and 439H may not both be counted.

C S 439H.title: PRINS OF COMPUTER SYS: HONORS
C S 439H.description: An introduction to computer systems software abstractions with an emphasis on the connection of these abstractions to underlying computer hardware. Key abstractions include threads, virtual memory, protection, and I/O. Requires writing of synchronized multithreaded programs and pieces of an operating system. Computer Science 439 and 439H may not both be counted.

CDI 355.title: 2-MAKING SYS WRK PPL DISBLT-WB
CDI 355.description: Explore an overview of disability policy. Examine the history of the disability rights movement, relevant federal and state legislation, legal and ethical issues, services and supports through federal and state programs, the political economy of disability, and disability culture, advocacy, and leadership issues. Social Work 360K (Topic 24) and Critical Disability Studies 355 (Topic 2) may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

CGS 360.title: 1-INTRO TO COGNITIVE SCIENCE
CGS 360.description: An introduction to the study of mind known as cognitive science, focusing on key areas such as vision and language, cognition and problem solving, artificial intelligence.

CH 104M.title: INTRO TO CHEMICAL PRACTICE I
CH 104M.description: Introduction to the techniques of modern experimental chemistry. Designed to provide basic laboratory and analytical skills. May include organic, analytical, and physical chemistry, as well as materials science. Your instruction classroom location will alternate week to week. You will either meet in WEL 2.110 or WEL 5.130. Please go to the posted assigned room your first class day where faculty direction will be provided for future weeks.

CH 104N.title: INTRO TO CHEMICAL PRACTICE II
CH 104N.description: Introduction to the techniques of modern experimental chemistry. Designed to provide basic laboratory and analytical skills. May include organic, analytical, and physical chemistry, as well as materials science. Your instruction classroom location will alternate week to week. You will either meet in WEL 3.134 or WEL 5.140. Please go to the posted assigned room your first class day where faculty direction will be provided for future weeks.

CH 108.title: INTENSIVE CHEMISTRY SEMINAR
CH 108.description: May not be counted toward a major or minor in chemistry. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. Offered on the pass/fail basis only. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

CH 119C.title: PRO DEVELOPMENT FOR CHEMISTS
CH 119C.description: Explore professional development in chemistry, excellence in research, scientific communication, ethics, and career planning.

CH 206K.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
CH 206K.description: Introduction to research practices; supervised individual undergraduate research in chemistry.

CH 207K.title: INTRO SCI OUTREACH IN ELEM SCH
CH 207K.description: Developing and presenting level-appropriate science laboratories to students in local elementary schools. A hands-on, discovery learning approach to science is emphasized. May be counted as an elective only. Following course orientation and submission of student schedules, students will be assigned to one of five small-group meeting times (Mon 5:00-6:30 p.m., Tue 8:00-9:30 a.m., Tue 5:00-6:30 p.m., Wed 8:00-9:30 a.m., or Thu 8:00-9:30 a.m.). Additional fieldwork hours will be arranged separately.

CH 207L.title: PEER TEACHING
CH 207L.description: Students act as peer teaching assistants in other University chemistry courses, mainly large general chemistry lecture sections. May not be counted toward any degree in chemistry.

CH 220C.title: ORGANIC CHEM LABORATORY-FRI
CH 220C.description: Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

CH 301C.title: FOUNDATIONS OF CHEMISTRY I
CH 301C.description: An introduction to chemistry for chemistry majors. Includes gases, atomic theory, bonding, and thermodynamics.

CH 301N.title: CHEMISTRY IN OUR WORLD I
CH 301N.description: Designed for non-science and Textiles and Apparel majors. Examine the nature of matter, energy, chemical reactions, and chemical thermodynamics. Discuss how these fundamentals of chemistry relate to real-world applications. Not intended as preparation for Chemistry 301.

CH 302.title: PRINCIPLES OF CHEMISTRY II
CH 302.description: Development and application of concepts, theories, and laws underlying chemistry.

CH 308.title: CHEM METHODOLOGY-LA
CH 308.description: May not be counted toward a major or minor in chemistry.

CH 320M.title: ORGANIC CHEMISTRY I
CH 320M.description: Primarily for premedical, predental, life sciences, and pharmacy majors. Development of organic chemical structure, nomenclature, and reactivity. Mandatory exams 6-9 pm Thursdays, September 24, October 22, and November 19.

CH 320N.title: ORGANIC CHEMISTRY II
CH 320N.description: Primarily for premedical, predental, life sciences, and pharmacy majors. The development of organic chemical functional group reactivity, reaction mechanics, with analogous aqueous transformations in biochemical settings.

CH 328C.title: STRCT/REACTVTY ORGANIC MOLS I
CH 328C.description: Explore the development of organic chemical structure, nomenclature, and reactivity.

CH 328M.title: ORGANIC CHEMISTRY I-ENGR MAJS
CH 328M.description: Primarily for chemistry and chemical engineering majors. The development of organic chemical structure, nomenclature, and reactivity.

CH 328N.title: ORGANIC CHEMISTRY II-ENGR MAJS
CH 328N.description: Primarily for chemistry and chemical engineering majors. The development of organic chemical reactivity, with an emphasis on synthesis and polymers.

CH 353.title: PHYSICAL CHEMISTRY I
CH 353.description: For chemistry and chemical engineering majors. Equations of state, laws of thermodynamics, ideal and nonideal solutions, phase equilibria, thermodynamics of chemical reactions. Chemistry 353 and 353M may not both be counted.

CH 353M.title: PHYS CHEM I FOR LIFE SCIENCES
CH 353M.description: For biochemistry and biology majors. Thermochemistry and kinetics of reactions in cells, enzyme catalysis, electrical and transport properties of membranes. Chemistry 353 and 353M may not both be counted.

CH 354.title: QUANTUM CHEM AND SPECTROSCOPY
CH 354.description: Fundamental principles of quantum mechanics, exactly soluble model problems, electronic structure of atoms and molecules, spectroscopy.

CH 354C.title: STATISTICAL MECHANICS
CH 354C.description: An introduction to statistical mechanics. Chemistry 354C and 354L may not both be counted.

CH 366D.title: BIO INORG CHEM/SPECTROSCOPY
CH 366D.description: Covers the roles of transition metals in biology, mostly with respect to protein structure, function, and catalysis. Also covers methods of spectroscopy that are used to probe the geometrical and electronic structure of metal ions in proteins. Exposure to modern areas of study in bio-inorganic chemistry. Explores the principles of transition metal chemistry in the context of biological systems. Chemistry 366D and 368 (Topic: Bio-inorganic Chemistry) may not both be counted.

CH 366E.title: CH S-/P-/D-/F- BLOCK ELMNTS
CH 366E.description: Focuses on the electronic structure of molecules and complexes, and how such information can predict important properties such as chemical reactivity, spectroscopy, and magnetism. Intended for upper-division chemistry majors with a basic understanding of concepts in inorganic chemistry. Chemistry 366E and 368 (Topic: Inorganic Reactions and Structures) may not both be counted.

CH 367C.title: MATERIALS CHEMISTRY
CH 367C.description: Introduction to structural and physical properties of materials and synthetic strategies for making new materials in the nanoscale and mesoscale regimes, in addition to covering instrumental methods used to probe materials properties. Some aspects of applications (semiconductor devices, solar and fuel cells, smart and responsive materials) will be covered to illustrate the kinds of material properties that need to be considered in designing novel materials of specific function.

CH 367P.title: INTRO TO POLYMER SCIENCE
CH 367P.description: Explore an overview of macromolecular soft materials including the fundamentals of polymer chemistry, physics, and engineering.

CH 368.title: ADVANCED QUANTUM CHEMISTRY
CH 368.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

CH 369K.title: TECHNIQUES OF RESEARCH
CH 369K.description: Advanced laboratory practice and introduction to research. May be taken for a letter grade no more than twice. No more than six semester hours may be counted toward a degree in chemistry or biochemistry. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. May be repeated for credit.

CH 371K.title: SCI OUTREACH IN ELEM SCHOOLS
CH 371K.description: Students develop and present level-appropriate science laboratories to students in local elementary schools. Students also plan and create the infrastructure needed to administer the science program in concert with the science curriculum at a specific elementary school. A hands-on, discovery learning approach to science is emphasized. May be taken for a letter grade no more than twice. No more than six semester hours may be counted toward a degree in chemistry. Following course orientation and submission of student schedules, students will be assigned to one of five small-group meeting times (Mon 5:00-6:30 p.m., Tue 8:00-9:30 a.m., Tue 5:00-6:30 p.m., Wed 8:00-9:30 a.m., or Thu 8:00-9:30 a.m.). Additional fieldwork hours will be arranged separately.

CH 372S.title: STUDY/RESEARCH ABROAD: AIF
CH 372S.description: Designed specifically for students participating in the AIF program. Facilitates student-led mini-seminars and video conference call discussions. Explores student research and wider learning and cultural experiences in Austin and abroad.

CH 375C.title: PHYSICAL ORGANIC CHEMISTRY
CH 375C.description: Explore reaction mechanisms and reactivity of organic chemicals and high-energy intermediates. Develop skills to design experiments and explain observed phenomena. Chemistry 375C and 368 (Topic: Physical Organic Chemistry) may not both be counted.

CH 378L.title: CAPSTONE RESEARCH/THESIS
CH 378L.description: Develop a capstone project with a faculty adviser, with a focus on advanced laboratory techniques and experimental design.

CH 379H.title: CHEM HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
CH 379H.description: Laboratory research project in a specific field of chemistry under the supervision of one or more faculty members. May be taken twice for credit. Must be taken in addition to the required hours for the Bachelor of Science in Chemistry degree. Students must enter no later than the first semester of the year of graduation.

CH 431.title: INORGANIC CHEMISTRY
CH 431.description: Survey of the chemistry of the elements, incorporating both descriptive and theoretical aspects. Open-ended experiments designed to illustrate a variety of synthetic techniques.

CH 455.title: FUNDMNTLS OF ANALYTICAL CHEM
CH 455.description: For biochemistry, engineering, and clinical laboratory science majors. Chemical and instrumental methods in analytical chemistry. Chemistry 455 and 456 may not both be counted.

CH 456.title: ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY
CH 456.description: For chemistry majors. Chemistry 455 and 456 may not both be counted.

CHE 018C.title: PEER MENTORSHIP
CHE 018C.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to take this course. Best practices for delivery of tutoring and/or grading for undergraduate chemical engineering majors.

CHE 077C.title: UNDERGRAD RESEARCH PRACTICUM
CHE 077C.description: Discuss best practices for participation in research for undergraduate chemical engineering majors.

CHE 079I.title: UNDERGRAD RESEARCH INTERNSHIP
CHE 079I.description: Participate in an internship or career experience program.

CHE 102.title: INTRO TO CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
CHE 102.description: Introduction to chemical engineering, including problem solving and study skills. Opportunities and responsibilities of a career in chemical engineering. May not be counted toward any engineering degree.

CHE 177K.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH PROJECT
CHE 177K.description: Recommended for students considering graduate study. Topic to be selected in conjunction with individual chemical engineering faculty member, with approval by the department chair. A final written report is required.

CHE 177L.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH PROJECT
CHE 177L.description: Subject matter to be selected in conjunction with individual chemical engineering faculty member, with approval by the department chair. A final written report is required.

CHE 210.title: INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTING
CHE 210.description: Computer programming focusing on basics of computing, high-level programming environments, and spreadsheets, with application to chemical engineering. Chemical engineering majors must make a grade of at least C- in this course.

CHE 253K.title: APPLIED STATISTICS
CHE 253K.description: Statistical methods such as data exploration and summary, least-squares fitting, probability and probability distributions, statistical inference and hypothesis testing, analysis of variance, design of experiments, statistical quality control, and use of professional statistical software. Chemical engineering majors must make a grade of at least C- in this course.

CHE 253M.title: MEAS, CNTRL, AND DATA ANLY LAB
CHE 253M.description: Laboratory safety; measurement and statistical analysis of transport process variables like temperature, pressure, and flow rate; computer data acquisition; feedback control; statistical process control and design of experiments; and production of professional-level lab reports.

CHE 264.title: CHEM ENGR PROCESS AND PROJ LAB
CHE 264.description: Experimental studies of unit operations. Laboratory safety. Statistical data analysis. Written and oral reports.

CHE 277K.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH PROJECT
CHE 277K.description: Recommended for students considering graduate study. Topic to be selected in conjunction with individual chemical engineering faculty member, with approval by the department chair. A final written report is required.

CHE 277L.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH PROJECT
CHE 277L.description: Subject matter to be selected in conjunction with individual chemical engineering faculty member, with approval by the department chair. A final written report is required.

CHE 317.title: INTRO TO CHEM ENGR ANALYSIS
CHE 317.description: Principles and applications of material and energy balances in process analysis. Chemical engineering majors must make a grade of at least C- in this course in order to take upper-division courses in chemical engineering.

CHE 319.title: TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
CHE 319.description: Basic study of momentum, energy, and mass transport. Includes viscous and turbulent flow, heat transfer, and mass diffusion. Chemical Engineering 319 and Chemical Engineering 353 may not both be counted.

CHE 322.title: THERMODYNAMICS
CHE 322.description: Introductory course in thermodynamics with special reference to chemical process applications: basic laws, thermodynamic properties of single component systems, expansion and compression of fluids, heat engines, multicomponent systems, physical equilibrium, chemical equilibrium. Chemical engineering majors must make a grade of at least C- in this course.

CHE 323.title: CHEM ENGR FOR MICRO-/NANOFAB
CHE 323.description: Definition and description of the terminology and processes of microelectronics, including semiconductor facilities and chemical processes for integrated circuit manufacture, with an emphasis on unit processes; the major unit process, including thin-film metals and dielectrics deposition and etching, silicon oxidation and etching, ion implantation, diffusion, lithography, planarization, and process control; and an overview of promising nanopatterning and nanofabrication techniques, such as particle-beam imaging, nanoimprint, and near-field probe imaging, implantation, diffusion, lithography, planarization, and process control.

CHE 338.title: BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
CHE 338.description: Introduction to basic biological processes including transcription, translation, protein/enzyme function, cellular energetics, protein secretion and modifications. Application of quantitative engineering principles to the analysis of biological processes, including thermodynamics, kinetics and stoichiometry. Chemical Engineering 338 and 379 (Topic: Biochemical Engineering) may not both be counted.

CHE 339T.title: CELL AND TISSUE ENGINEERING
CHE 339T.description: Introduction to biomedical research in tissue engineering. Includes case studies of tissues and organs of the body, physiology and biology of tissue, pathologies of tissue, current clinical treatments, the role of engineers in development of new technologies to diagnose and treat pathologies, quantitative cellular and molecular techniques, and applications of synthetic and natural biomaterials.

CHE 348.title: NUMERCL MTHDS IN CHE/PROB SOLV
CHE 348.description: Numerical solutions to algebraic and differential equations; numerical methods to integration, interpolation, and regression analysis, with application to chemical engineering. Chemical engineering majors must make a grade of at least C- in this course.

CHE 350.title: CHEMICAL ENGINEERING MATERIALS
CHE 350.description: Metallic, ceramic, polymeric, and composite materials. Crystal structures, phase diagrams, diffusion, and mechanical properties. Emphasis on structure-property-processing relationships.

CHE 354.title: TRANSPORT PROCESSES
CHE 354.description: Design and analysis of heat exchangers, fluid-flow systems and equipment, and interphase-contact devices. Chemical engineering majors must make a grade of at least C- in this course.

CHE 355.title: INTRODUCTION TO POLYMERS
CHE 355.description: Synthesis, structural characterization, physical properties, and applications of polymers.

CHE 359.title: ENERGY TECHNOLOGY AND POLICY
CHE 359.description: Technology and policy related to energy supply and demand, oil and gas production, coal utilization, hydrogen production, fuel cells, transportation, nuclear power, solar and wind energy, biomass utilization, energy conservation, and climate change.

CHE 360.title: PROCESS CONTROL
CHE 360.description: Analysis of process dynamics and methods for the design of automatic control systems for chemical process plants.

CHE 363.title: SEPARATN PROC & MASS TRANSFER
CHE 363.description: Design and analysis of equilibrium and mass transfer based on separations such as absorption, chromatography, crystallization, distillation, extraction, and membrane-based processes. Chemical engineering majors must make a grade of at least C- in this course.

CHE 364S.title: CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
CHE 364S.description: Emphasizes quantitative engineering analysis based on the application of mass and energy balances, fluid mechanics of incompressible, compressible and two-phase fluids, heat transfer and conservation of energy, diffusion and dispersion under highly variable conditions, reaction kinetics, and process control. Subjects include various probabilistic and statistical methods to characterize accident and loss performance, techniques for process hazard analysis, risk assessment, and accident investigations. Chemical Engineering 364S and 379 (Topic: Chemical Process Safety) may not both be counted.

CHE 372.title: CHEM REACTOR ANALYSIS AND DSGN
CHE 372.description: Planning and design of commercial chemical and biochemical reaction systems for producing fuels, polymers, specialty and consumer products, pharmaceuticals, solid-state devices, and other products. Chemical engineering majors must make a grade of at least C- in this course.

CHE 377K.title: UNDERGRAD RESEARCH PROJ: HON
CHE 377K.description: Recommended for students considering graduate study. Topic to be selected in conjunction with individual chemical engineering faculty member, with approval by the department chair. A final written report is required.

CHE 377L.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH PROJECT
CHE 377L.description: Subject matter to be selected in conjunction with individual chemical engineering faculty member, with approval by the department chair. A final written report is required.

CHE 379.title: ADV ANALYSIS FOR CHEM ENGINEER
CHE 379.description: Special topics of current interest. Chemical Engineering 379 (Topic: Entrepreneurship) and 379 (Topic 43) may not both be counted

CHE 473K.title: PROCESS DESIGN AND OPERATIONS
CHE 473K.description: Process design, economics, and safety; design projects representing a variety of industries and products.

CHE 679HA.title: UNDERGRADUATE HONORS THESIS
CHE 679HA.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of a chemical engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the department chair. The student makes two oral presentations and writes a thesis. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and the Bachelor of Science in Chemical Engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirement for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

CHE 679HB.title: UNDERGRADUATE HONORS THESIS
CHE 679HB.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of a chemical engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the department chair. The student makes two oral presentations and writes a thesis. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and the Bachelor of Science in Chemical Engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirement for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

CHI 312K.title: SECOND-YEAR CHINESE I
CHI 312K.description: Modern Standard Chinese (Mandarin). Intended for non-native speakers of Chinese. Chinese 612 and 312K, 412K may not both be counted.

CHI 312L.title: SECOND-YEAR CHINESE II
CHI 312L.description: Intended for non-native speakers of Chinese. Continuation of Chinese 312K, 412K. Chinese 612 and 312L, 412L may not both be counted.

CHI 320K.title: READINGS IN MODERN CHINESE I
CHI 320K.description: Readings in expository prose: selections from journals, newspapers, and other sources.

CHI 320L.title: READINGS IN MODERN CHINESE II
CHI 320L.description: Readings in modern fiction, poetry, and drama.

CHI 322.title: INTRO TO CLASSICAL CHINESE
CHI 322.description: Beginning study of wen yen, the particles, and syntax of the Chinese classics.

CHI 330.title: CHINESE FOR PROFESSIONALS
CHI 330.description: Fourth-year Chinese readings on special topics.

CHI 604.title: ACCELERATED FIRST-YEAR CHINESE
CHI 604.description: Learn Modern Standard Chinese (Mandarin). Chinese 604 and 506 may not both be counted; Chinese 604 and 507 may not both be counted.

CHI 606.title: FIRST-YEAR CHINESE I
CHI 606.description: Modern Standard Chinese (Mandarin). Intended for students who do not understand or speak Mandarin Chinese. Chinese 604 and 506, 606 may not both be counted.

CHI 607.title: FIRST-YEAR CHINESE II
CHI 607.description: Continuation of Chinese 506, 606. Intended for non-native speakers of Chinese. Chinese 604 and 507, 607 may not both be counted.

CIV 101J.title: FIRST YEAR FOUNDATIONS I
CIV 101J.description: Discuss foundations for success in life, college, and career. Develop skills for civic leadership, well-being, and academic scholarship. Civics 101J and 109 (Topic: First Year Foundations I) may not both be counted.

CIV 177I.title: INTERNSHIP IN CIVIC LEADERSHIP
CIV 177I.description: Participate in a professional internship in civic leadership. Reflect on internship to improve ability to both articulate preferences and skills, and to match them with appropriate career-related choices. May be taken three times for credit. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CIV 278I.title: INTERNSHIP IN CIVIC LEADERSHIP
CIV 278I.description: Participate in a professional internship in civic leadership. Reflect on internship to improve ability to both articulate preferences and skills, and to match them with appropriate career-related choices. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CIV 301.title: PERENNIAL PROBS CIVIC THOUGHT
CIV 301.description: Examine civic conflicts and controversies, with a view to understanding their root causes. Explore readings from classic and contemporary texts on political theory, ethics, and practice.

CIV 302.title: TRUTH AND PERSUASION
CIV 302.description: Examine what humans can know, how they reason, what thinking is, and how humans communicate to persuade others. Discuss readings in logic, epistemology, and rhetoric including classical writers such as Plato, Aristotle, Cicero, and Quintilian as well as modern and contemporary thinkers and leaders, with emphasis on case studies of effective persuasion. Civics 302 and Philosophy 306 (Topic: Truth and Persuasion) may not both be counted.

CIV 302C.title: CLASSICS OF SOCL/POLIT THOUGHT
CIV 302C.description: Explores the origins of social scientific thought in the history of political philosophy and traces the development of one or more of the social sciences in modern times. Focuses on fundamental ideas about human nature, civil society, and politics, explored through reading such authors as Aristotle, Aquinas, Locke, Rousseau, Marx, Weber, Durkheim, and Freud.

CIV 303C.title: FOUNDATIONS OF AMERICAN CIVICS
CIV 303C.description: Examine debates about constitutionalism and citizenship in American and Texas political experience. Investigate the philosophical and historic foundations of American institutions from the founding period through the contemporary era. Discuss the rights and responsibilities of citizenship.

CIV 303J.title: ORIGINS AMERICAN INSTITUTIONS
CIV 303J.description: Examine the roots of American institutions and practices from ancient times (Greek city-states, the Mosaic law, Roman politics), medieval practices and institutions, English constitutional history (Magna Carta, Glorious Revolution), and the Enlightenment (Locke, Montesquieu, Sydney). Discuss the American colonial experiment in self-government. Explore the Revolution, the Articles of Confederation, and the Constitutional convention. Address the early republic and significant constitutional moments in American experience.

CIV 303K.title: DEMOCRACY AND CAPITALISM
CIV 303K.description: Examine classical liberal thought as it relates to political and economic liberty. Investigate how classical liberal ideas about political and economic liberty influence the modern development of democracy and capitalism. Explore potential tensions between democracy and capitalism. Study empirical evidence and classic writings on relationship between democracy, capitalism, and human flourishing.

CIV 304.title: FOUNDATIONS RSCH MTHDS CIVICS
CIV 304.description: Introduction to statistical methods used in civics with emphasis on concepts and applications. Utilize a variety of real data to discuss key concepts, such as sampling distributions and basic data analyses.

CIV 305.title: EXCELLENCE OF CHAR: VIRTUES
CIV 305.description: Investigate the nature, grounds, and consequences of moral excellence in political communities. Discuss close reading of classical texts (such as Aristotle, Marcus Aurelius, Cicero, Aquinas), contemporary philosophical analysis (such as Foot, Anscombe, MacIntyre), results of scientific research, and reflections on history, biography, and literature (such as Shakespeare, Austen). Focus on application to contemporary problems.

CIV 310K.title: ETHICS/LDRSHP ANC GREEK DRAMA
CIV 310K.description: Study selected plays of Sophocles, Euripides, Aeschylus, Aristophanes, and others with a focus on themes of virtue, justice, and leadership qualities.

CIV 312P.title: CONSTITUTNL PRINS: CORE TEXTS
CIV 312P.description: Close readings from primary texts that have shaped or that reflect deeply upon American democracy, including the Declaration of Independence, The Federalist Papers, and Tocqueville's Democracy in America. May be paired with Government 310L to satisfy the legislative Core requirement for government. Civic Leadership 312P and Government 312P may not both be counted.

CIV 320.title: CONSTITUTIONALISM: ANC/MODERN
CIV 320.description: Examine the ancient and medieval origins of modern constitutionalism. Explore the works of authors such as Plato, Aristotle, Cicero, Augustine, Machiavelli, Montaigne, Hobbes, Locke, Montesquieu, Rousseau, Gibbon, Hamilton, Madison, Tocqueville, Thoreau, Marx, Mill, Arendt, Berlin, Rawls, and Nozick.

CIV 322K.title: POLITICS AND THE TRANSCENDENT
CIV 322K.description: Examine the relationship between theology (both revealed and natural) and political theory in the Western world. Discuss the political significance of theological ideas such as the proposition that all human beings are created equal; that all human beings are created in the image of God; beliefs in divine providence and judgment; metaphysical foundations of natural law and free will.

CIV 325K.title: ECON ANALYSIS CIVIC LEADERS
CIV 325K.description: Discuss a practical application of the studies of strategic behavior to leadership. Examine games leaders must play and strategies they can use to orient a team toward a common goal.

CIV 331F.title: AMERICAN POLITICAL ECONOMY
CIV 331F.description: Survey the ideas that have structured the American economic system and the implications of these ideas for civic life and citizenship. Explore the philosophical and theoretical questions of political economy, as well as the policy challenges of the modern welfare state and globalization. Discuss readings that may include Adam Smith, Alexander Hamilton, Franklin Delano Roosevelt, as well as modern economists and social scientists. Civics 301F and Civics 331F may not both be counted.

CIV 360.title: ADV CONST HIST/PRNCPLS
CIV 360.description: Analyze the history of the writing, approval, development, and interpretation of the United States Constitution.

CIV 361.title: STATESMANSHIP EAST AND WEST
CIV 361.description: Discuss one or more great works influencing the development of the Western intellectual tradition by such authors as Sophocles, Herodotus, Thucydides, Plato, Aristotle, Tacitus, Augustine, Aquinas, and Dante. Topic title: Statesmanship East and West. Topic description: Examine how thinkers in the ancient world, both Western and Eastern, conceived of political authority. Engage in close readings of texts by Homer, Sophocles, Plato, Confucius, Laozi, and Han Feizi to confront enduring questions about virtue, order, and political wisdom

CIV 364.title: ADV TPCS CIVIC THOUGHT
CIV 364.description: Discuss an advanced study of topics in civic thought and culture.

CIV 365.title: GRT THINKRS: REALSM/GEOPOL
CIV 365.description: Discuss an advanced study of topics in strategy and statecraft. Explore the works from twentieth-century geopolitical authors such as Hans Morgenthau, Henry Kissinger, Halford Mackinder, and Zbigniew Brzezinski. Discuss current and historical geopolitical issues in a seminar format. Identify connections between these works and international politics.

CIV 379.title: INTERNSHIP IN CIVIC LEADERSHIP
CIV 379.description: Perform fieldwork in research and analysis related to civic leadership. May be taken twice for credit. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CLA 201.title: LOWER-DIV CLARINET: SECONDARY
CLA 201.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. May be repeated for credit.

CLA 210.title: LOWER-DIV CLARINET: PRINCIPAL
CLA 210.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. May be repeated for credit.

CLA 251.title: UPPER-DIV CLARINET: SECONDARY
CLA 251.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. May be repeated for credit.

CLA 260.title: UPPER-DIV CLARINET: PRINCIPAL
CLA 260.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. May be repeated for credit.

CLA 312.title: LOWER-DIV CLARINET: MAJOR
CLA 312.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

CLA 362.title: UPPER-DIV CLARINET: MAJOR
CLA 362.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

CLD 301.title: INTRO TO COMM/LEADERSHIP
CLD 301.description: Introduces the study of leadership with an emphasis on communication dimensions and interdisciplinary understanding of social problems. Serves as a foundation for subsequent coursework in the areas of communication and leadership. Communication 301L and Communication and Leadership 301 may not both be counted.

CLD 302.title: COMMUNICATE DATA TO LEADERSHP
CLD 302.description: Contemporary issues and practices in communication and leadership.

CLD 303.title: LDRSHP PRESENCE/PRESENTATION
CLD 303.description: Discuss the theory and application of the fundamentals of professional speaking, leader presence, persuasive arguments, and presentations for the modern workforce.

CLD 321.title: SEMINAR COMM/LEADERSHIP
CLD 321.description: Builds on the foundation introduced in Communication and Leadership 301 advancing student work in theories, processes, and practices of leadership and communication. Communication 320L and Communication and Leadership 321 may not both be counted.

CLD 322.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY
CLD 322.description: Pursue special supervised individual research studies.

CLD 332.title: FACILITATIVE LEADERSHIP
CLD 332.description: Explore facilitation and consensus-building into leadership in the private, nonprofit, and public sectors; how to engage stakeholders in powerful, productive, and prudent ways; and how to collaborate with team members to advance a leader's vision. Communication and Leadership 320 (Topic: Facilitative Leadership) and 332 may not both be counted.

CLD 351.title: INTERNSHIP COMM/LEADERSHIP-WB
CLD 351.description: Practical work experience related to the practice of ethical leadership and communication. Communication 351L and Communication and Leadership 351 may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

CLD 360.title: COMM FOR EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP
CLD 360.description: Explore the theories, strategies, and skills required for organizational leaders to be effective and lead strong teams. Communication and Leadership 320 (Topic: Comm for Executive Leadership) and 360 may not both be counted.

CLD 365.title: LEADERSHIP AND PUBLIC MEMORY
CLD 365.description: Study communication and leadership in social issues.

CLD 371.title: CAPSTONE COMM/LEADERSHIP
CLD 371.description: Engagement in experiential learning. Development and presentation of ideas and plans for addressing a leadership challenge given by a partner organization. Communication 370L and Communication and Leadership 371 may not both be counted.

CLD 651.title: INTERNSHIP COMM/LEADERSHIP-WB
CLD 651.description: Practical work experience related to the practice of ethical leadership and communication. Communication 351L and Communication and Leadership 351 may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 178K.title: STUDENT-INITIATED RESEARCH
CMS 178K.description: Supervised independent research on a project initiated, designed, and implemented by the student. Student secures consent of a faculty member willing to supervise the project prior to registering. No more than six semester hours in the following courses may be counted: Communication Studies 177K, 377K, 477K, 178K, 278K, 378K, 478K, 578K, 678K.

CMS 210.title: FORENSICS WORKSHOP
CMS 210.description: Open to all University students. Training for participation in extracurricular speech activities, including intercollegiate debate. May be taken three times for credit.

CMS 278K.title: STUDENT-INITIATED RESEARCH
CMS 278K.description: Supervised independent research on a project initiated, designed, and implemented by the student. Student secures consent of a faculty member willing to supervise the project prior to registering. No more than six semester hours in the following courses may be counted: Communication Studies 177K, 377K, 477K, 178K, 278K, 378K, 478K, 578K, 678K.

CMS 306M.title: PROFESSNL COMMUNICATION SKILLS
CMS 306M.description: Designed to help students develop skills in one-on-one interactions, small group communication, and presentation skills. Basic communication theories as they relate to skill development are explored.

CMS 307K.title: INTERNSHIP-WB
CMS 307K.description: Internship to be arranged by student and approved by instructor. Focuses on career goals of students in communication positions with public and private organizations. Communication Studies 301 (Topic: Internship) and 307K may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 310K.title: TEAM-BASED COMMUNICATION
CMS 310K.description: Analysis of small-group communication: cohesiveness, social climate, role structure, leadership, conformity, dynamics of interaction; participation in small-group communication situations.

CMS 313M.title: ORGANIZATIONAL COMMUNICATION
CMS 313M.description: Communication processes within government, private, and volunteer organizations. Taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 314L.title: LANG, COMMUNICATION, & CULTURE
CMS 314L.description: The role of language in communication. Analysis of the complexity of human languages, languages in contact, language modality, and communication interaction.

CMS 315M.title: INTERPERSONAL COMM THEORY - HB
CMS 315M.description: Introduction to the study of communication in relationships; topics include self-disclosure, conflict, long-distance relationships, stereotyping, and persuasion. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 316L.title: INTERVIEWING PRINS & PRACTICES
CMS 316L.description: Introduction to interviewing theory, emphasizing the acquisition and application of interviewing skills.

CMS 317C.title: SPEECH IN AMERICAN CULTURE
CMS 317C.description: The impact of public discourse on the ideas and issues of culture and history in the United States.

CMS 318L.title: STRATEGIC AND RISK COMM
CMS 318L.description: Introduction to the closely related areas of strategic communication, being purposeful and goal driven in messaging across various contexts, and risk communication, exchanging/assessing risk information to manage decisions across various contexts.

CMS 320.title: ADVANCED PRESENTATION SKILLS
CMS 320.description: Designed to help students develop skills in delivering informative and persuasive presentations and speeches. Study of major theories related to oral presentations. Focus on audience analysis and adaptation, building strong arguments, speech organization, and use of new technologies. Communication Studies 320 and 367 (Topic: Advanced Presentation Skills) may not both be counted.

CMS 332.title: ARGUMENTATION AND ADVOCACY
CMS 332.description: Nature of argumentative controversy; variables of form, method, and ethics; analysis of argumentative rhetorical works.

CMS 332K.title: THEORIES OF PERSUASION-WB
CMS 332K.description: A study of motivational factors involved in persuasive speaking to secure belief and action. Taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 334K.title: NONVERBAL COMMUNICATION
CMS 334K.description: Survey of the effects of space, physical appearance, movement, eye behavior, and vocal behavior on interpersonal communication.

CMS 335.title: STRATEG SALES/EVENT PLANNING
CMS 335.description: Theory and practice related to the preparation of large-scale sales events and conferences. Designed to develop communication skills and planning techniques. May include client research, investigation of potential venues, telephone-based information interviews, individual or group sales presentations, and event overviews. Communication Studies 335 and 367 (Topic: Strategic Sales and Event Planning) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 337.title: BUILDING SALES RELATIONSHIPS
CMS 337.description: Explores the theoretical and practical role of communication in the development of long-term client relationships. Explores the consultative sales process, including prospecting, assessing needs, handling objections, presenting, closing, and following up with clients. Focuses on how technology can help or hinder communication. Communication Studies 337 and 367 (Topic: Communication to Build Sales Relationships) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 338L.title: LEADERSHIP AND PUBLIC MEMORY
CMS 338L.description: Examines how public memory is created, shaped, and shared through processes and forms like commemorations, museum exhibits, historic sites, and monuments.

CMS 339K.title: WORKING VIRTUALLY-WB
CMS 339K.description: Explore the dynamics of new work arrangements and the factors that shape communication when working in dispersed work arrangements. Examine important skills for navigating these new ways of working. Taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 341.title: DIGITAL COMMUNICATIONS-WB
CMS 341.description: Uses communication and interdisciplinary perspectives to explore interactions involving technology. May include the study of impression formation, identity, surveillance, privacy, distributed teams, trust and deception, online gaming, social support, and uses and impacts of new information and communication technology. Communication Studies 341 and 367 (Topic: Computer-Mediated Communication) may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 342C.title: COMM & CIVIC PARTICIPATION
CMS 342C.description: An exploration of the status of American democracy, particularly as it pertains to those born between 1960 and 1990. Subjects include the meaning of citizenship and democratic participation, and the strengths and limitations of marketing efforts targeted at getting youth involved in the democratic system. Communication Studies 342C and 367 (Topic: Communication and Civic Participation) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 342K.title: POLITICAL COMMUNICATION
CMS 342K.description: A study of the role of symbols in political communication and the techniques and strategies employed by politicians; special attention is given to recent election campaigns.

CMS 344K.title: LYING AND DECEPTION
CMS 344K.description: Examines lying and deception as civil, strategic, and manipulative behavior. Secrets, privacy, disclosures, and confidentiality are examined in a variety of familiar contexts. Communication Studies 344K and 367 (Topic: Lying and Deception) may not both be counted.

CMS 345P.title: COMMUNICATION/PUBLIC OPINION
CMS 345P.description: An investigation of the definition and measurement of public opinion. Explores the relationship between communication and public opinion, how changes to public opinion are affected, and the influence of perception of public opinion on personal opinion. Communication Studies 345P and 367 (Topic: Communication and Public Opinion) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 346.title: USING COMM TECHNOLOGY AT WORK
CMS 346.description: Examines how communication technologies affect workplace communication. Considers case studies involving the use of social networking, handheld devices, and e-mail by for-profit and nonprofit organizations. Communication Studies 346 and 367 (Topic: Using Communication Technology at Work) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 348.title: COMMUNICATION RESEARCH METHODS
CMS 348.description: A practical introduction to research methods, focusing on designing a study, conducting research, analyzing data, and presenting results. Studies survey design, interviews, focus groups, and experiments. Communication Studies 348 and 367 (Topic: Communication Research Methods) may not both be counted.

CMS 352D.title: DARK SIDE OF ORGANZTINL COMM
CMS 352D.description: Examine key organizational processes that often operate in the shadows through a communication-based approach. Assess how individuals and organizations respond ethically to these processes. Survey hidden organizations and the ethical response to dark collectives in society.

CMS 353S.title: SOCIAL MEDIA AND ORGS
CMS 353S.description: Explores the relationship between social media use and organizational communication. Subjects include how organizations use social media for communication with external stakeholders and to facilitate internal communications processes. Communication Studies 353S and 367 (Topic: Social Media and Organizations) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 354.title: CONFLICT RESOLUTION
CMS 354.description: Systematic analysis of conflict and communication to examine some of the effects of communication on conflict and of conflict on communication. Readings, analysis of conflicts, and practice with and evaluation of communication behaviors thought to be effective in conflict talk.

CMS 355K.title: INTERCULTURAL COMMUNICATION
CMS 355K.description: Theories of speech and language that concern interaction between persons from different cultures who speak different languages or dialects. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 359T.title: HOW PEOPLE TALK-HB
CMS 359T.description: Explore how people creatively use, manipulate, and re-invent language when they talk to each other. Examine qualitative research methods, including conversation and discourse analysis, interactional sociolinguistics, ethnography of speaking, and microethnography. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 360P.title: EXPERTISE/PUBLIC CULTURE-HB
CMS 360P.description: Introduction to the various functions that scientists and other experts serve in public discourse. Explore how scientists and experts influence public opinion, policy, and cultural attitudes. Examine how new media habits and digital networks offer platforms for experts and laypersons to communicate. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 363C.title: COMMUNICATION AND SPORTS
CMS 363C.description: Explore interpersonal, mediated, organizational, and public communication in sports. Examine foundational myths and metaphors that demonstrate the various ways in which sport is used to communicate in public life. Communication Studies 363C and 367 (Topic: Communication and Sports) may not both be counted.

CMS 366C.title: CELEBRITY CULTURE
CMS 366C.description: Examines the importance of fame and celebrity throughout the nineteenth, twentieth, and twenty-first centuries, in relation to education, entertainment, and politics. Communication Studies 366C and 367 (Topic: Celebrity Culture) may not both be counted.

CMS 367.title: MEDIA CULTURE-PUBLIC MEMORY
CMS 367.description: Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 367C.title: COMM IN THE AGE OF AI

CMS 367I.title: HOW TO TALK TO YOURSELF-HB
CMS 367I.description: Examine recent and noteworthy scholarship in the social scientific literature regarding the often-stigmatized processes of interpersonal/relational communication. Distinguish aspects of communication that are typically seen as dysfunctional, problematic, and destructive; aspects of communication that appear to be negative but also serve a function or produce desirable outcomes; and aspects of communication that are poorly understood or hidden in the shadows of other scholarly work. Communication Studies 367I (Topic: Dark Side of Interpersonal) and 367I (Topic 3) may not both be counted.

CMS 367P.title: 2-SOCIAL MEDIA ANALYSIS
CMS 367P.description: Analyze content on social media platforms using computational methods. Use the Python programming language to understand how individuals, companies, political campaigns, and social movements communicate using social media to advance their goals. Combine data collection, analysis, and visualization to develop insights using social media data.

CMS 367S.title: 2-SALES LEADERSHIP AND TECH
CMS 367S.description: Explore the core principles of sales leadership strategies and the vital role technology plays in driving sales success. Communication Studies 367S (Topic: Sales Leadership and Tech) and 367S (Topic 2) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 370K.title: INTERNSHIP IN COMM STUDIES-WB
CMS 370K.description: Internships to be arranged by student and approved by instructor. Focuses on career goals of students in communication positions with public and private organizations. Taught as a Web-based course.

CMS 371K.title: PRACT IN CONFLICT MEDIATION
CMS 371K.description: Provides hand on training and practice in conflict intervention techniques, including various forms of mediation, facilitation and dialogue.

CMS 372D.title: POLITICS OF NATIONAL MEMORY
CMS 372D.description: Explores issues of power in our nation's capital. Students study Washington, DC via visits to sites around the city. Communication Studies 372D and 374D may not both be counted.

CMS 372T.title: TIME MATTERS
CMS 372T.description: An exploration of twentieth and twenty-first century time management issues resulting from the development of communication technologies. Subjects include the concept of twenty-four-hour, seven-day-a-week availability; information overload; increased work hours; sleep deprivation; hurry sickness; multitasking; the human experience of time across diverse social, cultural, and historical contexts; and other time-related issues that shape, and are shaped by, communication behaviors. Communication Studies 367 (Topic: Time Matters) and 372T may not both be counted.

CMS 373D.title: ADVOCACY AND POLITICS-DC
CMS 373D.description: An introduction to the issues faced when advocating for an issue, idea, or one's self. The course aims to have students grasp concepts they will see and experience during their internship in Washington, DC.

CMS 378K.title: STUDENT-INITIATED RESEARCH
CMS 378K.description: Supervised independent research on a project initiated, designed, and implemented by the student. Student secures consent of a faculty member willing to supervise the project prior to registering. No more than six semester hours in the following courses may be counted: Communication Studies 177K, 377K, 477K, 178K, 278K, 378K, 478K, 578K, 678K.

COE 179K.title: RSCH IN COMPUTATIONAL ENGR
COE 179K.description: Directed study or research in a selected area of computational engineering.

COE 279K.title: RSCH IN COMPUTATIONAL ENGR
COE 279K.description: Directed study or research in a selected area of computational engineering.

COE 301.title: INTRO TO COMPUTER PROGRAMMING
COE 301.description: Basic computer programming concepts applied to engineering computations. Development of structured solutions to engineering and mathematical problems and an understanding of coding practices. Programming in MATLAB and C++. Aerospace Engineering 301 and Computational Engineering 301 may not both be counted.

COE 311K.title: ENGINEERING COMPUTATION
COE 311K.description: Fundamental numerical methods and software tools used in engineering computation. Subjects include linear systems of equations, matrix computations, nonlinear equations, least squares approximations, interpolation, numerical integration and numerical solution of differential equations.

COE 322.title: SCIENTIFIC COMPUTATION
COE 322.description: Explores the basic tools needed for developing scientific computing software. These include advanced programming languages (e.g. C, C++, python), object oriented programming and data structures. Subjects may include abstract data types; creation, initialization, and destruction of objects; class hierarchies; polymorphism, inheritance and dynamic binding; generic programming using templates, linked lists, queues, stacks, trees and algorithms such as searching, sorting, and hashing. Computational Engineering 322 and Statistics and Data Sciences 322 may not both be counted.

COE 352.title: ADVANCED SCIENTIFIC ENGR
COE 352.description: Topics in advanced numerical methods and scientific computation. Subject matter may vary.

COE 362.title: ENGR PROBABILITY AND STATS
COE 362.description: Introduction to mathematical representations and computations to qualitatively and quantitatively describe data, compare data, ascribe distributions to apparent patterns and structure in data via inference methods. Describe and model discrete and continuous random variables. Aerospace Engineering 379L (Topic: Engr Probability and Stats) and Computational Engineering 362 may not both be counted.

COE 371.title: APPLIED MATHEMATICS I
COE 371.description: Subjects include real analysis of functions of one variable, linear operator theory, and ordinary differential equations.

COE 374D.title: COMPUTATIONAL SYS SEN DSGN I
COE 374D.description: Introduction to computational systems engineering: the systems engineering process, requirements, design fundamentals, trade studies, cost and risk analyses, integration, technical reviews, case studies, and ethics. Produce written reports and oral presentations. Computational Engineering 374D and 379L (Topic: Senior Design I) may not both be counted Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

COE 379K.title: RSCH IN COMPUTATIONAL ENGR
COE 379K.description: Directed study or research in a selected area of computational engineering.

COE 379L.title: 1-INTRO MACHN LEARN/DATA SCI
COE 379L.description: Current topics in computational engineering. Focus on algorithms, mathematics, implementations (on Scikit-learn), and real-life applications of machine learning and data sciences including machine learning algorithms such as supervised learnings (linear models, support vector machines, kernel methods, tree-based algorithms), unsupervised learnings (Density estimation, clustering, dimensionality reduction, PCA), and deep neural networks. Explore the foundations of machine learning including optimization, probability, advanced linear algebra, statistics, and methods to evaluate machine learning methods. Computational Engineering 379L (Topic: Intro to Machine Learning) and 379L (Topic 1) may not both be counted.

COE 679HA.title: UNDERGRADUATE HNRS THESIS
COE 679HA.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of an engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Engineering Honors Program. Entails an oral presentation and a written thesis.

COE 679HB.title: UNDERGRADUATE HNRS THESIS
COE 679HB.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of an engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Engineering Honors Program. Entails an oral presentation and a written thesis.

COM 040H.title: HONORS REFLECTION-WB
COM 040H.description: Reflect on the honors experience. Taught as a Web-based course.

COM 102.title: INTRO TO HEALTH COMMUNICATION
COM 102.description: Covers contemporary issues in health communication theory and practice with an emphasis on interdisciplinary perspectives.

COM 125.title: LONGHORN STARTUP SEMINAR
COM 125.description: Contemporary issues, practices, and skills related to leadership and communication.

COM 214.title: STUDENT SUCCESS
COM 214.description: Contemporary issues and practices in communication.

COM 301E.title: INTRO TO COMM FOUNDATIONS
COM 301E.description: Introduction to the various communication fields and their key concepts and axioms. Includes multicultural communication, global communication, media literacy, and social media.

COM 302E.title: INTERDSCPLNRY COMM FOUNDATIONS
COM 302E.description: Explore in greater depth the concepts that connect and unite diverse areas of communication theory and practice. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

COM 307H.title: LIFE OF THE MIND: HONORS
COM 307H.description: A guided experience in critical thinking and discussion based on consideration of cross-disciplinary modes of inquiry to probe big questions about knowledge, meaning, and communication throughout history.

COM 310.title: SPORTS MEDIA PRACTICUM
COM 310.description: Introduction to various contemporary and historical issues, emphasizing interdisciplinary perspectives and critical discourse. Communication 310 and Journalism 313S may not both be counted unless the topics vary.

COM 316.title: PHOTOGRAPHIC COMMUNICATION
COM 316.description: Introduction to photographic technique and recent trends, evaluation, visual design, and use of images in the media. Students must provide their own digital 35-mm format camera.

COM 321.title: COMM SOCL JUSTICE CAPSTONE-WB
COM 321.description: Produce content incorporating practical and theoretical understandings of media's role in promoting and/or obstructing social justice. Taught as a Web-based course.

COM 323.title: 1-SPORTS MEDIA INTERNSHIP
COM 323.description: Practical work experience, arranged by the student and approved by instructor. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

COM 326.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY
COM 326.description: Pursue special supervised individual research studies.

COM 330H.title: HONORS CAPSTONE
COM 330H.description: Independent project agreed upon by the student and instructor, includes a variety of approaches in a variety of media, from creative work, to campaign planning and implementation, research papers, long-form journalism, community engagement, and service learning initiatives. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

COM 331.title: HLTH COM THEORY/PRCTC/INNOV-WB
COM 331.description: Explore contemporary issues in health communication theory and practice with an emphasis on interdisciplinary perspectives. Communication 331 and 334 (Topic: Health Com Theory Practice) may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

COM 340.title: LEADERSHIP/URB ENGAGEMNT-UTNY
COM 340.description: Explore leadership skills and goals in the urban context. Read and discuss leadership challenges that arise when stakeholders come together to plan, make decisions, and take action in organizational and community settings. Class meets September 8-December 7. Taught in New York City, New York. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

COM 350.title: COMM INTERNSHIP-UTNY-WB
COM 350.description: Internships to be arranged by student and approved by instructor.

COM 350L.title: SEM IN LOS ANGELES INTERNSHIP
COM 350L.description: Practical work experience in the entertainment industry in Los Angeles. Students must make their own arrangements to secure relevant internships. Communication 350L, 650L and Radio-Television-Film 350L, 650L may not both be counted. Taught in Los Angeles, California.

COM 370H.title: SCIENCE AND ART OF HEALTH COMM
COM 370H.description: Special topics for students in the Moody College of Communication Honors Program. Study health communication across contexts and populations, and how it translates to public health practice and interventions.

COM 650.title: COMM INTERNSHIP-UTNY-WB
COM 650.description: Internships to be arranged by student and approved by instructor.

COM 650L.title: SEMESTER IN L A INT-L A
COM 650L.description: Practical work experience in the entertainment industry in Los Angeles. Students must make their own arrangements to secure relevant internships. Communication 350L, 650L and Radio-Television-Film 350L, 650L may not both be counted. Taught in Los Angeles, California.

COM 679HA.title: HONORS THESIS
COM 679HA.description: Write and research two consecutive semesters under the supervision of a College of Communication faculty member; subjects are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Honors Program. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

COM 679HB.title: HONORS THESIS
COM 679HB.description: Write and research two consecutive semesters under the supervision of a College of Communication faculty member; subjects are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Honors Program. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

CRW 315D.title: PLAYWRITING I
CRW 315D.description: The study and practice of writing plays. Creative Writing 315D and Theatre and Dance 315 may not both be counted.

CRW 315F.title: INTRO TO WRITING FICTION
CRW 315F.description: Intended for students with minimal prior experience in creative writing.

CRW 315P.title: INTRO TO WRITING POETRY
CRW 315P.description: Intended for students with minimal prior experience in creative writing.

CRW 325.title: WRITING LITERARY SPECTACLES
CRW 325.description: Detailed study and practice of the techniques of biography, creative nonfiction, or other special topics of creative writing; includes reading and analysis of contemporary models.

CRW 325E.title: PERSONAL ESSAY
CRW 325E.description: Creative Writing 325 (Topic: Personal Essay) and 325E may not both be counted.

CRW 325F.title: FICTION WRITING
CRW 325F.description: Detailed study and practice of the techniques of fiction writing; includes reading and analysis of contemporary models.

CRW 325P.title: POETRY WRITING
CRW 325P.description: Detailed study and practice of the techniques of poetry writing; includes reading and analysis of contemporary models.

CRW 340D.title: PLAYWRITING II
CRW 340D.description: Emphasis on the form and writing of the full-length play or equivalent. Creative Writing 340D and Theatre and Dance 325 may not both be counted.

CRW 340F.title: SHORT STORY WORKSHOP
CRW 340F.description: Practice in writing the short story, with study of contemporary models.

CRW 355F.title: ADVANCED FICTION WORKSHOP
CRW 355F.description: A third semester seminar-style workshop in fiction for experienced creative writers.

CRW 355P.title: ADVANCED POETRY WORKSHOP
CRW 355P.description: A third semester seminar-style workshop in poetry for experienced creative writers.

CSE 370.title: INDIVIDUAL READING/RESEARCH
CSE 370.description: Supervised study or research in a selected area of computational science, engineering, and mathematics by individual arrangement with a supervising instructor.

CTI 301G.title: INTRO TO ANCIENT GREECE
CTI 301G.description: Greatness of Greece as reflected in Greek history, literature, philosophy, art, religion, and politics. No knowledge of Greek is required.

CTI 302.title: CLASSICS OF SOCL/POLIT THOUGHT
CTI 302.description: Explores the origins of social scientific thought in the history of political philosophy and traces the development of one or more of the social sciences in modern times. Focuses on fundamental ideas about human nature, civil society, and politics, explored through reading such authors as Aristotle, Aquinas, Locke, Rousseau, Marx, Weber, Durkheim, and Freud.

CTI 305G.title: INTRO TO THE OLD TESTAMENT
CTI 305G.description: Introduction to the many interpretations of the Hebrew Bible (the Old Testament) and its meaning in the context of its historical and cultural setting in the ancient Near East.

CTI 306D.title: HISTORY OF RELIGIONS OF ASIA
CTI 306D.description: Eastern religions: an introduction to the basic forms and the historical development of the religious traditions of India, China, and Japan.

CTI 310.title: 3-INTRO TO PHILOS OF RELIGION
CTI 310.description: Introduction to fundamental texts and questions that have helped shape the world. A critical examination of various conceptions of God and of the relationship of the human and the divine. Core Texts and Ideas 310 and Western Civilization 303 may not both be counted unless the topics vary. Only one of the following may be counted: Core Texts and Ideas 310 (Topic: Introduction to the Philosophy of Religion), 310 (Topic 3), Philosophy 305, Religious Studies 305.

CTI 315C.title: THE BIBLE/ITS INTERPRETERS
CTI 315C.description: Seeks to develop a wide-ranging familiarity with the Jewish and Christian Bibles and with the dominant modes of ancient, medieval, and early modern biblical interpretation. Readings include an extensive range of primary sources, including both the Scriptures themselves and some of their most influential exegetes.

CTI 321.title: THEOR FOUNDTNS MODERN POLITICS
CTI 321.description: Examines competing foundations of the ongoing development of political and social modernity. Examines a selection of major authors from Machiavelli to Nietzsche.

CTI 322.title: CRITICS OF MODERN LIBERALISM
CTI 322.description: Selected critics of the philosophy of the Enlightenment from both the Left and the Right, and from the time of Rousseau to the present.

CTI 323.title: MIGHT AND RIGHT AMONG NATIONS
CTI 323.description: Major alternative approaches to the question of the moral character of international relations, as elaborated by some of the greatest political thinkers.

CTI 324.title: POLITICS AND LITERATURE
CTI 324.description: Explores the "old battle between the poets and philosophers," in which the two sides battle for recognition as the deepest source of wisdom about politics and ethics. Includes readings from great works of political philosophy and literature. Core Texts and Ideas 324 and Government 335O may not both be counted.

CTI 325.title: MORALITY AND POLITICS
CTI 325.description: Interdisciplinary readings from major works exploring issues of ethics and leadership.

CTI 325M.title: LIBERTY/EMPIRE: MACHIAVELLI
CTI 325M.description: Explore Machiavelli's reputations through the examination of his works and analyze how those reputations relate to Machiavelli's own positioning and self-understanding.

CTI 326C.title: CONSTITUTIONAL INTERPRETATN
CTI 326C.description: Designed to improve reasoning and communication skills through constitutional interpretation. Determining what the Constitution means, how to determine what it means, and who should determine what it means.

CTI 329.title: ANCIENT HISTORIANS
CTI 329.description: Examines the main works of ancient historiography, and provides grounding in the central issues with which these works engage.

CTI 331.title: ORIGINS OF LIBERALISM
CTI 331.description: Examine the theory of liberal democracy through a study of its development in historical, religious, and political contexts.

CTI 335M.title: MARX AND MARXIST THEORY
CTI 335M.description: Introduction to the writings of Karl Marx as well as to those of his intellectual successors in Europe and around the globe in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

CTI 339.title: THE PURSUIT OF HAPPINESS
CTI 339.description: Investigate happiness and how it is attained. Examine contrasting perspectives on happiness or human flourishing offered by major thinkers, both religious and secular, representing different epochs, cultures, and theoretical approaches. Core Texts and Ideas 339 and Philosophy 365P may not both be counted.

CTI 350.title: MASTERWORKS OF WORLD DRAMA
CTI 350.description: Studies major tragedies, comedies, and historical plays from various epochs, including at least one of Shakespeare's plays. Explores themes related to ethics, politics, and human nature, as well as the craft of the playwright. Students attend and discuss at least one play performance.

CTI 371.title: EINSTEIN IN AGE OF CONFLICT
CTI 371.description: Following the life and work of Albert Einstein, course examines the rise of the theories of relativity and quantum mechanics upon the stage of international political upheaval.

CTI 371S.title: SCIENCE AND RELIGION TO NEWTON
CTI 371S.description: Explore the complex relationship between science and religious faith in medieval and early modern Europe beginning with Galileo Galilei's trial before the Roman Inquisition. Examine themes including the understanding of God and nature, authority (classical and scriptural) versus observation, the wide range of knowledge-making practices, the persistence of magic, and the influence of power and patronage.

CTI 372M.title: GREAT WORKS IN BIOLOGY
CTI 372M.description: Explore the history of biology through a close study of several major texts. Analyze controversies about authority, classification, extinction, evolution, and genetics. Emphasize primary source readings from influential figures who addressed these and other relevant questions.

CTI 373.title: GREAT WORKS IN MEDICINE
CTI 373.description: Examine signature works in the history of medicine.

CTI 375.title: 4-ART/CITY IN RENAISSANCE ITAL
CTI 375.description: Study of classic texts, connected historically or thematically. Art, architecture, and emerging civic identity in Florence, Siena, Venice, and other Italian Renaissance city-states. Core Texts and Ideas 375 and Western Civilization 320 may not both be counted unless the topics vary. Only one of the following may be counted: Art History 331P, 364 (Topic: Art and the City in Renaissance Italy), Core Texts and Ideas 375 (Topic: Art and the City in Renaissance Italy), 375 (Topic 4), European Studies 347 (Topic: Art and the City in Renaissance Italy), 347 (Topic 33), Italian Civilization 349 (Topic: Art and the City in Renaissance Italy), Western Civilization 320 (Topic: Art and the City in Renaissance Italy).

CTI 379.title: CONFERENCE COURSE
CTI 379.description: Intensive tutorial study of selected major texts.

CZ 412K.title: SECOND-YEAR CZECH I
CZ 412K.description: Listening, speaking, reading, and writing at the second-year level. Czech 611C and 312K, 412K may not both be counted.

CZ 506.title: FIRST-YEAR CZECH I
CZ 506.description: Emphasis on four-skills proficiency: listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

D S 235.title: INTRO TO DECISION SCIENCE
D S 235.description: Examine modeling of business problems using methods from decision analysis, simulation and optimization. Decision Science 235 and 235H may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

DES 131.title: PORTFOLIO/PERFORMANCE REVIEW
DES 131.description: Develop and review a portfolio of work, guided by feedback from the instructor, group critiques, and reviews with visiting critics.

DES 160I.title: INTERNSHIP IN DESIGN-WB
DES 160I.description: Participate in a professional design internship in a field of interest. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Offered on the letter-grade basis only. May be repeated for credit. Taught as a Web-based course.

DES 301.title: INTRO TO DESIGN THINKING
DES 301.description: Introduces design thinking and design methods, with a focus on design process. Design 301 and Integrated Design 301D may not both be counted.

DES 304.title: DESIGN STUDIO I
DES 304.description: Explore the vocabulary, principles and techniques of design across disciplines through project work in a studio setting.

DES 305.title: DESIGN STUDIO II
DES 305.description: Build upon vocabulary, principles and techniques of design across disciplines through project work in a studio setting.

DES 306.title: DESIGN STUDIO III
DES 306.description: Refine and apply vocabulary, principles and techniques of design across disciplines through project work in a studio setting.

DES 307.title: DESIGN AND CULTURE
DES 307.description: Explore the broad social, economic, technological, and political forces that shape design, and how design influences the human experience in today's world. Examine the critical discourse surrounding the creation, production, consumption, and interpretation of design.

DES 307C.title: EVERYDAY DESIGN
DES 307C.description: Explore the influence of design in daily life, its impact on society, and how design disciplines shape the world through purposeful choices and creative practices. Design 307C and Integrated Design 307C may not both be counted.

DES 310T.title: INTRO TO PROTOTYPING
DES 310T.description: Problem solve and prototype mass-produced products through an iterative design process. Explore 3D design conceptualization through hands-on building techniques and digital fabrication methods.

DES 321.title: IMAGES IN COMMUNICATION
DES 321.description: Studio course. Explores the selection and creation of images appropriate to specific communication goals and contexts, such as promotional images, infographics, logos, instructions, and/or narratives.

DES 323.title: COLOR IN THEORY AND PRACTICE
DES 323.description: Explore contemporary color notation systems and color management techniques. Survey economic, health and safety, environmental, cultural, legal, political, and other ethical considerations pertinent to using color.

DES 325.title: TYPOGRAPHY I
DES 325.description: Studio course. Projects introduce the fundamental principles, conventions, and techniques of typography.

DES 326.title: OBJECTS AND SPACES
DES 326.description: Studio course. Projects introduce rendering and prototyping techniques used for solving problems in three-dimensional media such as product design, packaging design, environmental graphics, and/or architectural/landscape design.

DES 330T.title: ADVANCED INTERACTION
DES 330T.description: Exploration of innovative topics in design. Explore the emerging field of play design and its potential to create better conditions in our rapidly changing societies. Examine how play can contribute to the development of social and creative processes exploring boundary lines of a practice in designing for play and designing through play. Gain a deeper understanding of play design theory as well as embodied experiences of play to inform design choices in projects.

DES 334C.title: INTERACTION DESIGN I
DES 334C.description: Explore theories, methods, and techniques for designing time-based and interactive experiences, including video, motion graphics, and web and mobile applications.

DES 337.title: 1-HISTORY OF GRAPHIC DESIGN
DES 337.description: Lectures and discussions focusing on specialized or advanced topics in the history of design. Survey the history of graphic design from c.3000 BCE to the present, with an emphasis on the last 200 years. Design 337C and Design 337 (Topic 1) may not both be counted.

DES 346.title: PROJECT STUDIO
DES 346.description: Studio course. Professional experience solving real-world problems, including commissioned projects, student-initiated projects, and/or pro bono projects. May be taken three times for credit.

DES 347.title: PUBLICATION STUDIO
DES 347.description: Studio course. Advanced publishing projects in print and/or digital media.

DES 350.title: COLLAB LAB: DESIGNING IN TEAMS
DES 350.description: Studio course. Projects focusing on contemporary design practice, featuring approaches and subfields not represented elsewhere in the curriculum. May be repeated twice for credit, but not with the same instructor in the same semester. Use CAD software and digital tools to abbreviate the process from concept to physical artifact. Study additive and subtractive manufacturing processes such as 3D printing, laser cutting, and CNC machining to increase the fidelity of projects.

DES 374.title: CAPSTONE DESIGN PROJECT
DES 374.description: Studio course. Students define their own research problem and propose appropriate design solutions, guided by feedback from desk critiques with the instructor, group critiques, and reviews with a visiting critic. Design 370 and 374 may not both be counted.

DES 376.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY: DESIGN
DES 376.description: Student-defined projects completed under faculty supervision.

DEV 000M.title: BASIC MATH AND ALGEBRA SKILLS
DEV 000M.description: Developmental mathematics instruction paired with a core math course. Emphasis is on mathematical concepts that support success in the core math course.

DEV 000R.title: BASIC READING SKILLS
DEV 000R.description: Developmental reading and writing instruction paired with a credit reading intensive course. Emphasis is on strategies for improving reading comprehension across the disciplines, reading study skills, and elements of composition. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

DEV 300W.title: BASIC WRITING SKILLS
DEV 300W.description: Developmental reading and writing instruction paired with a credit writing intensive course. Emphasis is on strategies for improving reading comprehension across the disciplines, reading study skills, and elements of composition.

DEV 301M.title: DEVELOPMENTAL MATHEMATICS I
DEV 301M.description: Developmental mathematics instruction paired with a core math course. Emphasis is on mathematical concepts that support success in the core math course.

E 303C.title: PLAN II WORLD LIT PART I
