GEO 455S.description: Examine the fundamentals of acquiring, processing, and interpreting remote sensing data. Explore the development of skills in the use and analysis of remote sensing data. Survey the physics of electromagnetic radiation and its interaction with geologic materials; common techniques for remote sensing; science goals and instruments onboard ongoing and planned remote sensing missions; and techniques for analysis of remote sensing data. Geological Sciences 455S and 471T (Topic: Intro Remote Sensing Geosci) may not both be counted.

GEO 476K.title: GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
GEO 476K.description: Introduction to subsurface hydrology, emphasizing geological controls on groundwater flow; quantitative methods of analyzing aquifer systems; regional hydrology; water quality and pollution.

GER 322D.title: CONV/COMP: IND/SOCIETY
GER 322D.description: Explore everyday lives and language use in German-speaking societies through readings and media texts discussing popular culture, university life, and work. Focus on developing practical German skills: comprehension (reading and listening), speaking (conversations and informational presentations), and writing. German 322D and 328 may not both be counted.

GER 322E.title: CONV/COMP: CONTEMP CONTROV
GER 322E.description: Explore contemporary and often controversial issues confronting German-speaking populations (such as migration) through readings and diverse media texts. Produce oral and written work for specific audiences and purposes (recommendations, comparisons, evaluations), with a review of advanced grammar resources.

GER 342K.title: MID AGE-EARLY MOD ERA CUL HIST
GER 342K.description: Explore German-language cultures between the Middle Ages and Early Modern Europe (ca. 1200 to 1750), from medieval kings through religious reforms, globalizing wars, and the rising middle class. Examine how texts reflect changing social, political, and religious values, tying stories and essays to important moments in emerging German-language cultures. German 340C and 342K may not both be counted.

GER 506.title: FIRST-YEAR GERMAN I
GER 506.description: Basic training in grammatical patterns and usage of modern German.

GER 507.title: FIRST-YEAR GERMAN II
GER 507.description: Advanced training in grammatical patterns and usage of modern German.

GER 612.title: ACCEL SEC-YR GER: READ MOD GER
GER 612.description: Grammar review, composition, readings and recitation, discussion of literary works, and German culture.

GER 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
GER 679HA.description: Supervised individual research on a literary or linguistic problem, culminating in an honors paper of some length.

GER 679HB.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
GER 679HB.description: Supervised individual research on a literary or linguistic problem, culminating in an honors paper of some length.

GK 311.title: INTERMEDIATE GREEK I
GK 311.description: Continuation of Greek 601C or 507. Introductory readings from classical authors such as Lysias, Plato, and Xenophon. Includes grammar review.

GK 324.title: 7-APOLLONIUS
GK 324.description: Reading and analysis of classical authors such as Homer, Herodotus, Euripides, and Plato. Greek 324 (Topic: Xenophon) and 324 (Topic 9) may not both be counted.

GK 365.title: ARCHAIC/CLASSICAL GK LIT
GK 365.description: Critical study of authors such as Thucydides, Demosthenes, and Aeschylus.

GK 506.title: FIRST-YEAR GREEK I
GK 506.description: Studies the fundamentals of grammar and reading in ancient Greek.

GOV 307.title: INTRO HOLOCAUST/GENOCD STUDIES
GOV 307.description: Examine interdisciplinary perspectives (including historical, political, socio-psychological, and cultural methods and insights) on genocide as a global phenomenon.

GOV 310L.title: AMERICAN AND TEXAS GOV-WB
GOV 310L.description: A basic survey of American government, including fundamental political institutions, federal, state, and local; special attention to the United States and Texas Constitutions. May be paired with Government 305C, 306C, 312L, 312P, or Civic Leadership 312P to satisfy the legislative Core requirement for government. Taught as a Web-based course.

GOV 312L.title: ISS & POLICIES IN AMER GOV-WB
GOV 312L.description: Analysis of underlying themes concerned with American political institutions and policies, including the United States Constitution. May be paired with Government 310L to satisfy the legislative Core requirement for government. Taught online with established deadlines. Includes live-streaming video and requires real-time participation. Go to http://laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

GOV 312P.title: CONSTITUTNL PRINS: CORE TEXTS
GOV 312P.description: Close readings from primary texts that have shaped or that reflect deeply upon American democracy, including the Declaration of Independence, The Federalist Papers, and Tocqueville's Democracy in America. May be paired with Government 310L to satisfy the legislative Core requirement for government. Civic Leadership 312P and Government 312P may not both be counted.

GOV 314E.title: CLASSICS OF SOCL/POLIT THOUGHT
GOV 314E.description: Explores the origins of social scientific thought in the history of political philosophy and traces the development of one or more of the social sciences in modern times. Focuses on fundamental ideas about human nature, civil society, and politics, explored through reading such authors as Aristotle, Aquinas, Locke, Rousseau, Marx, Weber, Durkheim, and Freud.

GOV 324L.title: GOVS & POLIT OF WESTERN EUROPE
GOV 324L.description: Comparative study of peoples, institutions, parties, interest groups, and bureaucracy in the countries of Western Europe, concentrating on the major political systems of Britain, France, Germany, and Italy. European Studies 350 and Government 324L may not both be counted.

GOV 324O.title: CONSTITUTNLSM IN DIV SOCIETIES
GOV 324O.description: Analyze constitutional strategies for divided societies where social divisions like race, ethnicity, language, or religion are a driving force in politics and risk group exclusion or violence. Government 324O and 355M (Topic: Cnstitutnlsm in Divided Scties) may not both be counted.

GOV 330K.title: THE AMERICAN PRESIDENT
GOV 330K.description: Development of the power and influence of the president; nomination, election, and responsibility; case studies of presidential problems; comparison of president and other executives.

GOV 335I.title: POLITICAL BIOETHICS
GOV 335I.description: Analyze the decisional challenges facing political communities in deciding how to regulate future genetic manipulation of human embryos as therapy for diseases and as enhancement of normal capacities.

GOV 344L.title: INTRO TO COMPARATIVE POLIT-WB
GOV 344L.description: Introductory survey of basic concepts, perspectives, approaches, and trends in comparative politics, with emphasis on the formal-legal, group, class, elite, political culture, structural-functional, and systems approaches. Taught as a Web-based course.

GOV 350K.title: STATISTICAL ANLY IN POL SCI-WB
GOV 350K.description: Elementary statistical techniques and their applications to problems in political science. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

GOV 351D.title: THEOR FOUNDTNS MODERN POLITICS
GOV 351D.description: Examines competing foundations of the ongoing development of political and social modernity. Examines a selection of major authors from Machiavelli to Nietzsche.

GOV 351J.title: MIGHT AND RIGHT AMONG NATIONS
GOV 351J.description: Major alternative approaches to the question of the moral character of international relations, as elaborated by some of the greatest political thinkers.

GOV 351M.title: LIBERTY/EMPIRE: MACHIAVELLI
GOV 351M.description: Explore Machiavelli's reputations through the examination of his works and analyze how those reputations relate to Machiavelli's own positioning and self-understanding.

GOV 351O.title: FORENSIC FILES: PHILOS CRIMES
GOV 351O.description: Explore political philosophies from the continental tradition (Heidegger/Arendt, Nietzsche, Adorno/Horkheimer, and Agamben/Schmitt) implicated in radical politics and answering to political turning points, with a focus on research and reading in historical contexts.

GOV 355C.title: APPLIED RSRCH METHODS I
GOV 355C.description: Introduction to the research process in the social sciences. The primary emphasis is on engaging in research projects, writing research proposals, analyzing data, and presenting research.

GOV 355M.title: JUDICIAL POLITICS
GOV 355M.description: Emphasis on varying topics in government and politics of contemporary interest and concern.

GOV 357C.title: CONSTITUTIONAL INTERPRETATN
GOV 357C.description: Designed to improve reasoning and communication skills through constitutional interpretation. Determining what the Constitution means, how to determine what it means, and who should determine what it means.

GOV 357J.title: LAW OF POLITICS
GOV 357J.description: Explore theoretical and philosophical issues at the intersection of law and politics.

GOV 360C.title: INTRO TO INTNL RELATIONS
GOV 360C.description: Government 360C and 360N (Topic 10) may not both be counted.

GOV 360D.title: INTERNATIONAL SECURITY
GOV 360D.description: Explore the conditions and motivations behind the use of military force in world politics.

GOV 360F.title: GLOBAL GOVERNANCE-WB
GOV 360F.description: Explore the forces that shape global stability and instability with a focus on international agreements. Taught as a Web-based course.

GOV 360O.title: BUSINESS AND SOCIETY
GOV 360O.description: Explore the relationship between firms and society, including the role of regulation and government policy in shaping economic outcomes. Examine how governments encourage economic activity.

GOV 360T.title: MULTINATIONAL CORP/INTL LAW
GOV 360T.description: Study the ways governments use international economic law to shape international business. Government 355M (Topic: Multinational Corp/Intl Law) and 360T may not both be counted.

GOV 362L.title: GOVERNMENT RESEARCH INTERNSHIP
GOV 362L.description: Fieldwork in research and analysis on governmental and political problems. Only six semester hours of an internship course in government may be counted toward a major in government.

GOV 364C.title: ISLAM AND POLITICS
GOV 364C.description: An introduction to Islamic political thought.

GOV 364V.title: POLITICAL WARFARE/PROPAGNDA
GOV 364V.description: Examine the phenomenon of political warfare in a contemporary global context, focusing on Russia and Eastern Europe. Explore the evolution of techniques of political warfare, including psychological warfare and propaganda.

GOV 365C.title: JAPANESE FOREIGN POLICY
GOV 365C.description: Examine the foreign and domestic determinants of Japanese foreign policy.

GOV 365E.title: POLITICAL ECONOMY OF ASIA
GOV 365E.description: Explore the political economies of East Asia with an emphasis on Japan, China, South Korea, and Taiwan.

GOV 365V.title: COMPR NOTIONS EUR SECURITY
GOV 365V.description: Examines the conceptual and practical aspects of European comprehensive security via the institutions charged with implementing it: the European Union (EU), the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), and the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE).

GOV 366L.title: INTL DEVELOPMENT/GLOBAL JUSTIC
GOV 366L.description: Explore global development including patterns of development, variation in development, and the impact of development. Government 365N (Topic: Intl Dev and Global Justice) and 366L may not both be counted.

GOV 366M.title: INTL POL ECON LOW-INCM COUNTR
GOV 366M.description: Explore the ways that China, India, Brazil and other lower-income countries are transforming the patterns in global finance, trade, aid, women's empowerment, and the control or corruption.

GOV 366U.title: MIDDLE EAST OUTLAWS/LAWMEN
GOV 366U.description: Explore the evolution of crime and law enforcement in the modern Middle East.

GOV 370D.title: THE MILITARY AND AMER POLITICS
GOV 370D.description: Study the United States armed forces and the implications of military service and international conflict for domestic politics and policy. Government 355M (Topic: The Military and Amer Politics) and 370D may not both be counted.

GOV 370E.title: CONGRESSIONAL ELECTIONS
GOV 370E.description: Examination of congressional campaigns and election outcomes from both historical and contemporary perspectives. Government 370E and 370L (Topic 12) may not both be counted.

GOV 370M.title: RESEARCH ON THE US CONGRESS
GOV 370M.description: Independent research on lawmaking in the modern United States Congress. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

GOV 370P.title: POLICY-MAKING PROCESS-DC
GOV 370P.description: Focus on the role of Congress and the President in the policy-making process.

GOV 370R.title: MONEY IN US POLITICS: HON
GOV 370R.description: Study of the nature and consequences of campaign finance on American politics.

GOV 370S.title: SOCIAL MOVEMENTS: THRY/PRAC
GOV 370S.description: Government 370L (Topic 9) and 370S may not both be counted.

GOV 370V.title: THE POLITICS OF HEALTH CARE
GOV 370V.description: The role of political institutions and the public in the development of the American health care system.

GOV 371F.title: BLACK CHURCH IN AFR AMER POLIT
GOV 371F.description: Explore the political role of the Black church in the African American experience.

GOV 371L.title: LATINO POLITICS
GOV 371L.description: Explore the political experiences of the United States Latino populations.

GOV 371V.title: LATINA/O/X CITIZENSHIP
GOV 371V.description: Explore the concepts of membership, belonging, immigration and citizenship in the Latina/o/x experience. Examine how historically, politically, socially and culturally, Latinas/os/xs have been construed as belonging or not belonging members of the United States society.

GOV 372C.title: US IMM POLCY/STORS OF MGRTN
GOV 372C.description: Explore the history of immigration policy to the United States with memoirs of migration, immigration policy, and incorporation to provide a fuller picture of how immigrants are affected by these policies in the United States.

GOV 372E.title: POWER/MONEY IN US EDU POLICY
GOV 372E.description: Examine how power dynamics and financial forces shape education opportunity, who controls what gets taught, how resources are allocated, the political influences behind educational decisions, and the impact of the decisions upon different groups of students.

GOV 372G.title: RACE/POLICING: HIST/POL/PRAC
GOV 372G.description: Examine the origins and evolution of policing in the United States, with a focus on the development and impact of policing policies. Analyze how policies have supported or oppressed Black, working-class, and ethnic communities. African and African Diaspora Studies 358F and Government 372G may not both be counted.

GOV 374N.title: POLITICAL INTERNSHIP-WB
GOV 374N.description: Students perform research and related activities for an entity directly related to government and politics. Written reports required. Only six semester hours of an internship course in government may be counted toward a major in government. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

GOV 379C.title: REGIME PERSP ON AMER POLITICS
GOV 379C.description: Explore American politics and culture and examine how the parts of America fit together as a whole.

GOV 662N.title: ARCHER CEN WASH INTERNSHIP
GOV 662N.description: Fieldwork in research and analysis on governmental and political problems.

GOV 679HB.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
GOV 679HB.description: Lectures and supervised individual research and the writing of a substantial paper on a special topic in the field of government. No grade is awarded until the student has completed the two-semester sequence.

GRG 301K.title: WEATHER AND CLIMATE
GRG 301K.description: A survey of meteorological phenomena and climatological processes of the earth.

GRG 305.title: THIS HUMAN WORLD: INTRO TO GRG
GRG 305.description: Introductory survey of human geography, including human-environment relations, cultural patterns and processes, and geography's relation to other fields of study.

GRG 307C.title: INTRODUCTION TO URBAN STUDIES
GRG 307C.description: A multidisciplinary study of cities and complex urban environments; historical and contemporary issues from both national and international perspectives. Geography 307C and Urban Studies 301 may not both be counted.

GRG 312K.title: NORTH AMERICAN BORDERLANDS
GRG 312K.description: Explore the formation of political borders in nineteenth- and twentieth-century North America, their contestation, and the multiple meanings attached to them.

GRG 320M.title: GEOGRAPHY OF MEDIA
GRG 320M.description: Explores communication media from a geographical perspective. Subjects include place images, changing attitudes about public and private spaces, social networking, and the geography of communications infrastructure.

GRG 321U.title: RACE/SOCIAL JUSTICE/CITY
GRG 321U.description: Examine the way that race and inequality are built into the structures and ideas surrounding urban life through the spatial and racial structures of capitalist development, racialized migration, and redevelopment and gentrification campaigns. Analyze how United States cities are racial geographies.

GRG 326G.title: INTL POL ECON LOW-INCM COUNTR
GRG 326G.description: Explore the ways that China, India, Brazil and other lower-income countries are transforming the patterns in global finance, trade, aid, women's empowerment, and the control or corruption.

GRG 327G.title: INTRO TO GEOSPATIAL AI
GRG 327G.description: Focus on artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) methods and their applications on geospatial problems. Explore python programming and GIS data preparation for machine learning models, ML foundations, regression, clustering, decision tree and random forest, neural network, embedding, and location representation learning. Geography 327G and 356T (Topic: Intro to Geospatial AI) may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Offered on the letter-grade basis only.

GRG 328F.title: BLACK GEOGRAPHIES
GRG 328F.description: Explore the relationship between Blackness and the production of space and place. Examine the tensions and possibilities that emerge when Black people are rendered un-geographic as a fundamental component of racial capitalism while they are simultaneously creating spaces of freedom under constant threats to Black life.

GRG 330F.title: GEOGS INTL DEVEL IN AFRICA
GRG 330F.description: Critically examines the major approaches to "Development" in colonial and postcolonial eras, with a focus on a range of African resources: from water to wildlife, forests to farms, airways to rangelands, and including a consideration of African bodies themselves as sites of development and resistance. African and African Diaspora Studies 340M and Geography 330F may not both be counted.

GRG 332L.title: COMMUNITY AND CITIZEN SCIENCE
GRG 332L.description: Examine the contributions of volunteered geographic information (VGI) to geographic knowledge alongside an analysis of alternative forms of community-based knowledge production. Explore an overview of citizen science, community science, and VGI datasets. Develop and carry out community-based participatory research projects informed by the growing literature on community and citizen science. Geography 332L and 356T (Topic: Community and Citizen Science) may not both be counted.

GRG 335N.title: LANDSCAPE ECOLOGY
GRG 335N.description: Discuss spatial patterns in the Earth's biosphere found within landscapes, typically areas measured in square kilometers. Examine the processes that create those patterns, drawing from ecology, biogeography, and many other disciplines. Use Geographic Information Systems (GIS) to analyze and understand the spatial arrangements and configurations of natural and human-modified environments.

GRG 342C.title: SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
GRG 342C.description: Historical and contemporary analysis of international development with a focus on the prospects for environmental sustainability.

GRG 343E.title: MAPPING LATIN AMERICA
GRG 343E.description: Discuss the role of maps in the creation of Latin America as a specific sort of place. As such, allows familiarity with a broad overview of Latin American history from Pre-Columbian civilizations to the modern period.

GRG 348E.title: TOURISM/PLACE IN GLOBAL SOUTH
GRG 348E.description: Debunk the myth that tourism is inherently desirable by unpacking the mechanisms of violence and inequalities of power embedded in tourism and tourism development.

GRG 349S.title: GLOBAL SUSTAINABILITY AND SOIL
GRG 349S.description: Examine soil as a resource from the perspectives of natural and sustainability science, including soil ecology and biodiversity, formation, fertility for ecosystems and farming systems, soil health, distinguishing characteristics in nature, taxonomy, and soil spatial variability around the globe.

GRG 350E.title: GEOPROCESSING
GRG 350E.description: Computer programming and scripting applied to geospatial data. Geography 350E and 356T (Topic: Geoprocessing) may not both be counted.

GRG 356T.title: SPATIAL DATA SCIENCE AND MAPS
GRG 356T.description: Explore geospatial big data and cutting-edge geospatial artificial intelligence (GeoAI) methods to solve real-world geographic and environmental challenges. Learn a wide range of spatial analytical methods from spatial regression, network analysis, machine learning, and visualization tools to support decision-making in environment, geoscience, health, psychology, crime, housing, planning, climate, etc., to make impacts on the world around us.

GRG 379K.title: CONFERENCE COURSE
GRG 379K.description: Supervised individual study of selected problems in geography.

GRG 401C.title: THE NATURAL ENVIRONMENT
GRG 401C.description: An introduction to the study of the Earth from a holistic perspective including geologic, atmospheric, ecological, and water sciences and its relevance to present-day environmental problems.

GRG 412E.title: DIGITAL EARTH
GRG 412E.description: Explore concepts in geospatial technologies that are becoming increasingly important tools in research, policy, and everyday life. Discuss geospatial data sources (ex. GPS, citizen science, satellite imagery), applications (ex. disaster relief, biodiversity conservation, disease spread), and broader implications of their use (ex. privacy, ethical, legal issues). Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Additional hour(s) to be arranged.

GRG 440L.title: LANDUSE/LANDCVR CHANGE PRAC
GRG 440L.description: Focuses on best practices for classifying and mapping land use and land cover, as well as how they change over time in an experiential learning environment. Develop testable hypotheses answered by the original data analysis and interpretation. Geography 440L and 356T (Topic: Landuse/Landcover Change Practicum) may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

GRG 460G.title: ENVIR GEOGRAPHIC INFO SYSTEMS
GRG 460G.description: An introduction to the creation and use of geographic information systems.

GRG 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
GRG 679HA.description: For honors candidates in geography. Individual reading of selected works for one semester, followed in the second semester by the writing of an honors thesis. Regular conferences with the faculty supervisor are also required.

GRG 679HB.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
GRG 679HB.description: For honors candidates in geography. Individual reading of selected works for one semester, followed in the second semester by the writing of an honors thesis. Regular conferences with the faculty supervisor are also required.

GSD 301.title: INTRO STUDY OF NORTHERN EUR
GSD 301.description: Introduces significant moments in and monuments from the cultural histories of the German-speaking, Scandinavian, and Dutch nations from their beginnings to the present. Designed to discuss representative problems in research and analysis in cultural studies and the humanities.

GSD 311J.title: CHILDREN'S LIT NORTHERN EUR
GSD 311J.description: Explore nineteenth- to twenty-first-century children's literature from Norway, Sweden, Denmark, Germany, Switzerland, Austria, Finland, Belgium and the Netherlands. Focus on the prominent place of children's literature in the popular culture of central and northern Europe, as well as the serious issues and themes which north Americans might otherwise consider "adult" that are often found in this genre-death, war, poverty, social justice, and family conflict-alongside whimsy, warmth and wonder.

GSD 331J.title: GERMAN CINEMA 1918-1945
GSD 331J.description: Explore the history and aesthetics of German cinema from 1918 until 1945.

GSD 341E.title: HANS CHRISTIAN ANDERSEN
GSD 341E.description: Introduction to such Hans Christian Andersen tales as "The Little Mermaid," "The Ugly Duckling," and "The Emperor's New Clothes." Examination of their author and the romantic period in which they were written, as well as their historical, philosophical, and literary context.

GSD 360.title: CRIME IN GRMN AND US MEDIA/CUL
GSD 360.description: In-depth explorations of contemporary and historical moments in the cultures, social relations, cultural histories, and intellectual histories of the nations in Northern Europe and its cultural networks. Explores the historical, political, psychological, theological, and cultural fallout of the Holocaust, as well as literary and cinematic responses in Europe and the United States.

GSD 361E.title: TOLERANCE IN DUTCH CULTURE
GSD 361E.description: The historical roots of Dutch "tolerance" and how it plays a role in current Dutch culture and attitudes towards religious, gender, and sexual differences, as well as drug use, prostitution, and euthanasia. Exploration of the stereotypes and the actuality of these Dutch attitudes and policies from an international comparative perspective.

GSD 361P.title: SPORTS/POLITICS IN GERMANY
GSD 361P.description: Explore how fascism, communism, liberal democracy, and the current nationalistic backlash against immigration have all used the popularity of sports to achieve their strategic goals, from the 1936 Nazi Olympics to Germany's 2014 World Cup victory and beyond.

GSD 362Q.title: FORENSIC FILES: PHILOS CRIMES
GSD 362Q.description: Explore political philosophies from the continental tradition (Heidegger/Arendt, Nietzsche, Adorno/Horkheimer, and Agamben/Schmitt) implicated in radical politics and answering to political turning points, with a focus on research and reading in historical contexts.

H E 101P.title: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT I
H E 101P.description: Explore common career paths, identify professional and personal goals, and develop entry-level career portfolios. Human Development and Family Sciences 340 and Human Ecology 101P may not both be counted.

H E 102P.title: ETHICS ACROSS LIFE
H E 102P.description: Examine applied ethics in the workplace and in adult life with a focus on working in teams and organizations effectively. Human Development and Family Sciences 340 and Human Ecology 102P may not both be counted.

H E 103P.title: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT II
H E 103P.description: Explore a practical approach to professional self-presentation, leadership and decision-making, and career development. Human Development and Family Sciences 340 and Human Ecology 103P may not both be counted.

H S 301.title: INTRO TO HEALTH & SOCIETY
H S 301.description: A broad, multidisciplinary overview that introduces students to the study of health and society.

H S 330C.title: CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH
H S 330C.description: Explore the basic science of climate change, including historical changes and how they have led to health problems and wars. Focus on global warming and how it will cause health problems in the coming decades. Examine solutions and moral and ethical dimensions to both global warming and its effects on human health. Health and Society 330C and 340 (Topic: Climate Change and Health) may not both be counted.

H S 340.title: ADDICTED AMERICA
H S 340.description: Selected topics on health and society. Explore causes of addiction and learn about common addictions. Examine systematic responses to addiction treatment, how interventions are based on underlying causality assumptions, and popular treatment options. Analyze trends and influences in healthcare policy and propose a feasible solution for an addiction based on understanding what addiction is, what is effective, and how the issue fits within current policy and economic considerations.

H S 341C.title: COMPARATIVE US HLTH SYSTEMS
H S 341C.description: Examine the social and economic distribution of health and health care and how underlying social values shape both the health of a population and its approach to health care. Analyze health care systems worldwide to see what the advantages and disadvantages of these models are for different segments of society compared to the US model. Health and Society 340 (Topic: Comparative US Health Systems) and 341C may not both be counted.

H S 350E.title: FOUNDATIONS OF EPIDEMIOLOGY
H S 350E.description: Introduction to the basic tenets of epidemiology as well as the different types of epidemiological study designs.

H S 378.title: SEMINAR IN HEALTH & SOCIETY
H S 378.description: Focuses on a key topic or debate within the Health and Society area of study and develops students' abilities to use data and write a research or policy paper that informs that topic or debate.

H S 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
H S 679HA.description: A supervised conference course to provide training in research and writing.

HDF 113L.title: CHILD DEVELOPMENT LABORATORY
HDF 113L.description: Observe children at the University Child and Family Laboratory and relate observations to the issues discussed in related coursework. Human Development and Family Sciences 113L and 114H may not both be counted.

HDF 155R.title: PROBS CRS: RESEARCH PRACTICUM
HDF 155R.description: Intensive study of selected problems of a transdisciplinary nature.

HDF 159.title: ADV APPLD RESEARCH EXPERIENCE
HDF 159.description: Apply research experience in areas of a transdisciplinary nature. May be taken twice for credit.

HDF 255R.title: PROBS CRS: RESEARCH PRACTICUM
HDF 255R.description: Intensive study of selected problems of a transdisciplinary nature.

HDF 259.title: ADV APPLD RESEARCH EXPERIENCE
HDF 259.description: Apply research experience in areas of a transdisciplinary nature. May be taken twice for credit.

HDF 266C.title: GUIDANCE IN ADULT-CHILD RELS
HDF 266C.description: Explore the theory and implementation of positive child and adult interactions, communication, and guidance strategies.

HDF 266L.title: GUIDANCE ADULT-CHILD REL LAB
HDF 266L.description: Interact with children and families at the Priscilla Pond Flawn Child and Family Laboratory while under supervision, to explore the development of skills in positive child and adult interactions, communication, guidance strategies, and observational data collection.

HDF 304.title: FAMILY RELATIONSHIPS
HDF 304.description: The process of family interaction over the life cycle. Application of research findings to the understanding of relationships.

HDF 304H.title: FAMILY RELATIONSHIPS: HONORS
HDF 304H.description: The process of family interaction over the life cycle. Application of research findings to the understanding of relationships.

HDF 305.title: HEALTH/DEVEL ACROSS LIFESPAN
HDF 305.description: Investigate the experiences of health and well-being across the life span of both individuals and families. Apply research findings to explore how physical and mental health are influenced by societal contexts.

HDF 313.title: CHILD DEVELOPMENT
HDF 313.description: Motor, language, cognitive, social, and emotional development in the family context.

HDF 315L.title: RSCH METH IN HUMAN DEV/FAM SCI
HDF 315L.description: Survey of research methods, including observational and experimental techniques.

HDF 322.title: PERSONAL AND FAMILY FINANCE
HDF 322.description: Overview from the individual and family perspectives of financial planning tools, cash management, consumer credit, basic tax preparation, and insurance selection. Includes application of knowledge to hypothetical situations and case studies. Human Development and Family Sciences 322 and Finance 322F may not both be counted.

HDF 335.title: ADULT DEVELOPMENT
HDF 335.description: Adulthood and the development, changes, and maturation that occurs, including the impact of relationships in adulthood.

HDF 342.title: DEV PSYCHPATHOL INF THRU ADOL
HDF 342.description: A developmental approach to the study of emotional and behavioral disorders from infancy through adolescence, including attachment disorders, autism, attention deficit disorder, conduct disorder, phobias, obsessive-compulsive disorder, depression, eating disorders, and schizophrenia. Also includes contrasting theories of psychopathology, epidemiology and outcomes of childhood disorders, therapeutic approaches and their efficacy, and developmental resilience. Human Development and Family Sciences 342 and Psychology 339 may not both be counted.

HDF 343.title: HUM DEV IN MINORITY/IMMIG FAMS
HDF 343.description: Examines the theories of human development and cultural psychology as they apply to the developmental issues of minority and immigrant children and families. Human Development and Family Sciences 343 and 378K (Topic: Child Development in Minority and Immigrant Families) may not both be counted.

HDF 351.title: INFANT DEVEL/ATTACHMENT RELS
HDF 351.description: The development of emerging social language and cognitive capacities during infancy and toddlerhood and the development and consequences in infant-caregiver attachment security.

HDF 352.title: FIELD EXPERIENCE: COMMUNITY
HDF 352.description: Practicum in applied settings concerning human development and family sciences. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Offered on the letter-grade basis only. May be repeated for credit.

HDF 355H.title: PROBLEMS COURSE: HONORS
HDF 355H.description: Intensive study of selected research problems.

HDF 355R.title: PROBS CRS: RESEARCH PRACTICUM
HDF 355R.description: Intensive study of selected problems of a transdisciplinary nature.

HDF 357.title: APPLIED CHILD DEVELOPMENT
HDF 357.description: Apply theoretical concepts in child development through fieldwork with children and families in a laboratory school setting. Observe children and practice professional interactions with children and families. May be taken twice for credit.

HDF 359.title: ADV APPLD RESEARCH EXPERIENCE
HDF 359.description: Apply research experience in areas of a transdisciplinary nature. May be taken twice for credit. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. May be repeated for credit.

HDF 371.title: ADOLESCENT DEVEL IN CONTEXT
HDF 371.description: The biological, cognitive, and social changes that occur during the second decade of life, including the developmental issues faced by adolescents.

HDF 378K.title: CTXT INFL ON INDIV/FAM DEV
HDF 378K.description: Concepts, theories, and issues in human development and family sciences.

HDF 379H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
HDF 379H.description: Supervised individual research on a special topic in human development and family sciences; oral presentation and preparation of a scholarly paper covering the research. May be based on laboratory, library, or field research. May be repeated once for credit.

HDF 652F.title: FIELD PRACTICUM: COMMUNITY
HDF 652F.description: Designed for students in their last semester. Practicum in settings concerning human development and family sciences. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Offered on the letter-grade basis only. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

HDF 652P.title: FIELD PRACTICUM: EARLY CHLDHD
HDF 652P.description: Designed for students in their last semester. Study and implementation of skills necessary for planning, guiding, and interacting with young children and their families; and for the management of facilities that provide services for young children. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Offered on the letter-grade basis only. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

HDF 655R.title: PROBS CRS: RESEARCH PRACTICUM
HDF 655R.description: Intensive study of selected problems of a transdisciplinary nature.

HDF 659.title: ADV APPLD RESEARCH EXPERIENCE
HDF 659.description: Apply research experience in areas of a transdisciplinary nature. May be taken twice for credit. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. May be repeated for credit.

HDO 301.title: INTRO HMN DIMENSIONS OF ORGS
HDO 301.description: Introduction to organizations and how the liberal arts can examine, analyze, and change them. Defines organizations from a liberal arts standpoint; survey of career opportunities related to the human dimensions of organizations.

HDO 310.title: RSRCHING PEOPLE-CENTERED PROBS
HDO 310.description: Introduction to mixed-methods research using active learning and project-based assignments. Explore the basics of qualitative and quantitative research methods, research ethics, teamwork, project management, and problem-solving. Human Dimensions of Organizations 310 and 320 may not both be counted.

HDO 322L.title: LAW AND ORGANIZATIONS: HON
HDO 322L.description: Examine the intersection of law and organizational/workplace practices. Explore how law regulates the workplace (e.g., laws about sexual harassment), how organizations do or do not comply with the law, and the ways that organizational practices may affect legal standards.

HDO 330C.title: CREATIVITY/INNOVATN IN ORGS
HDO 330C.description: Explore creative tools people use to solve problems. Human Dimensions of Organizations 330 (Topic: Creativity/Innovatn in Orgs) and 330C may not both be counted.

HDO 350.title: IDENTITIES IN ORGANIZATIONS
HDO 350.description: Explore how intersecting identities shape both individuals' and groups' experiences of various organizations. Human Dimensions of Organizations 330 (Topic: Cultural Competence in Orgs) and 350 may not both be counted.

HDO 360.title: CONFERENCE COURSE
HDO 360.description: Study selected problems in the field of human dimensions of organizations, which can include independent study of a content area, research assistance, or other faculty-supervised independent activity.

HDO 379.title: APPLYING HUMAN DIMENS OF ORGS
HDO 379.description: Explores multidisciplinary methods of the human dimensions of organizations with an external entity. Focus on the role of the liberal arts viewpoint in practical settings.

HEB 303K.title: BIBLICAL HEBREW I
HEB 303K.description: Introduction to biblical Hebrew, including basic lexicon and grammar. Focus on reading the Hebrew Bible; including texts such as the Creation, the Tower of Babel, the binding of Isaac, the Joseph story, and the David and Goliath story. Hebrew 602C and Hebrew 303K may not both be counted.

HEB 346D.title: ISRAELI SOCIETY AND CULTURE
HEB 346D.description: Explore vignettes from the many facets of contemporary Israeli society through the prisms of journalism, media, film, and literature in the Hebrew language. Examine new aspects of Israeli society and learn about their history and their place in modern Israel. Hebrew 346 (Topic: Israeli Society and Culture) and 346D may not both be counted.

HEB 601C.title: INTENSIVE HEBREW I
HEB 601C.description: Not open to native speakers of Hebrew. First semester of intensive Hebrew language instruction. Hebrew 601C and 506 may not both be counted. Hebrew 601C and 507 may not both be counted.

HED 112.title: FOUNDATIONS OF STRESS MGMT
HED 112.description: Examine stress, mindfulness, and multiple means of stress management. Explore the science of stress and how it impacts health, the history and benefits of mindfulness, and engage with different stress management practices each week; including learning the practices' history, scientific benefits, and varying experiences. Use healthy coping strategies on a consistent basis and tools to address stressors that will arise from being a college student.

HED 178E.title: 4-PEER SUPPORT PRACT SEMINAR
HED 178E.description: Literature and discussion of current and specific issues in health. Health Education 178E (Topic: Peer Support Practicum) and 178E (Topic 4) may not both be counted.

HED 178J.title: FIELDWORK IN PEER EDUCATION
HED 178J.description: Continue to explore the skills necessary to be effective public health promotion peer educators. Deliver workshops, serve as mentors to the new class of peer educators, attend Longhorn Wellness Center (LWC) events, represent the LWC at campus functions, and develop initiatives and projects in committees. Examine how peer educator skills apply to professional development skills.

HED 311.title: INTRO TO HEALTH PROMOTION
HED 311.description: Provides an introduction to the concepts and core principles of health promotion and behavioral science.

HED 329K.title: CHILD & ADOLESCENT HEALTH
HED 329K.description: The foundations of child and adolescent health; health education; and the biological, environmental, and behavioral health determinants of health. Includes the application of evidence-based child and adolescent health promotion concepts; prominent health risk behaviors established during youth that increase the risk of morbidity and mortality; and the application of personal health and wellness information. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

HED 335.title: THEORS OF SUBSTNC USE & ABUSE
HED 335.description: An introduction to the psychological, social, pharmacological, and cultural aspects of substance use and abuse. Includes a study and critique of the theories of substance use and abuse.

HED 343.title: FOUNDATIONS OF EPIDEMIOLOGY
HED 343.description: Introduction to the basic tenets of epidemiology as well as the different types of epidemiological study designs.

HED 350.title: THRY HLTH PROMO/BEHAV SCI
HED 350.description: Introduction to the field of health promotion, as applicable in the fields of health and education: theories, processes, activities, and settings for health promotion practice. Health Education 350 and 370K (Topic 1) may not both be counted.

HED 360.title: ADOLESCENT HLTH RISK BEHAV
HED 360.description: Overview of the biological, psychological, social, and environmental determinants of adolescent risk-taking behavior. Focuses on selected behaviors that can result in adolescent mortality, morbidity and social problems including: violence, sexual behavior, obesity-related behaviors, depression, alcohol, tobacco, and other substance use. Health Education 360 and 370K (Topic 2) may not both be counted.

HED 361.title: PSYCHSOCL ISS WOMENS HEALTH
HED 361.description: Explores psychosocial issues in women's physical and mental health, including traditional reproductive issues, disorders that are more common in women than in men, and the leading causes of death in women. Covers gender influences on health risk behaviors, and societal influences on women's health through a consideration of social norms and roles.

HED 365.title: SOCL DTRMNTS HLTH/DISPARITY
HED 365.description: Explore social determinants of health and health disparities in the United States and globally, as well as strategies to address them.

HED 366.title: HUMAN SEXUALITY
HED 366.description: Introduction to health and sexuality. Explore sexuality, health, and rights and how they relate to the past, present, future, emerging issues, controversial issues, and cutting-edge areas. Examine the evolution of sexual rights, global sexual health, how health and sexuality constructs positive and negative messages throughout communities, nationally, and globally, methods to improve sexual health, and advocacy.

HED 370K.title: 4-PUB HLTH CHLLNGS US-MX BRDR
HED 370K.description: Identification, causes, incidence, prevention, control, and social implications of major problems in health. Explore border health issues and health promotion strategies to address them using a bi-national framework. Health Education 370K (Topic: Publ Hlth Challngs US/Mex Brdr) and 370K (Topic 4) may not both be counted.

HED 373.title: EVALUATION AND RESEARCH DESIGN
HED 373.description: Develop knowledge and application of research methods and program evaluations in health promotion settings. Subjects include experimental and non-experimental research, validity, reliability, experimental and quasi-experimental research designs, data collection procedures, measurement of health knowledge, attitudes, behaviors, and data analysis.

HED 378I.title: FNDTNS OF PEER HEALTH LDRSHP
HED 378I.description: As incoming peer educators, explore content related to health promotion among college students and examine how to effectively use that information in their role. Deliver workshops, attend Longhorn Wellness Center (LWC) events, represent the LWC at campus functions, and develop public health initiatives and projects. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Offered on the letter-grade basis only.

HIN 312K.title: SECOND-YEAR HINDI I
HIN 312K.description: Not open to native speakers of Hindi. Modern Hindi of India. Hindi 612 and 312K may not both be counted.

HIN 330E.title: REALISM/URBANISM IN HINDI
HIN 330E.description: Focus on texts or films in Hindi to develop vocabulary and grammar, and explore Realism and Urbanism as two prominent themes in twentieth-century Hindi literature. Consider how authors addressed changes in life and family values in the Indian urban middle class, and how they transformed the literary taste of Hindi readership from marvelous tales to reading short stories with social critique. Hindi 330 (Topic: Panorama of Hindi Literature) and 330E may not both be counted.

HIN 506.title: FIRST-YEAR HINDI I
HIN 506.description: Not open to native speakers of Hindi. Modern Hindi of India. Hindi 604 and 506 may not both be counted; Hindi 506 and Urdu 604 may not both be counted.

HIN 604.title: ACCELERATED FIRST-YEAR HINDI
HIN 604.description: For speakers of Hindi who have not studied it formally. Hindi 604 and 506 may not both be counted; Hindi 604 and 507 may not both be counted; Hindi 604 and Urdu 604 may not both be counted; Hindi 604 and Urdu 506 may not both be counted; Hindi 604 and Urdu 507 may not both be counted.

HIS 301J.title: GLOBALIZATION: A MODERN HIS
HIS 301J.description: Survey of world history from the sixteenth century to the present.

HIS 301R.title: INTRO RUS/E EUR/EURASN STDS
HIS 301R.description: An introduction to the former Soviet Union and Eastern Europe through each of the major disciplines represented in the program: language, literature, anthropology, geography, history, government, sociology, and economics.

HIS 305C.title: KOREAN LITERATURE AND FILM
HIS 305C.description: Explore the literature and film of Korea from earliest times to the present.

HIS 306K.title: MID EAST: REL/CUL/HIST FND-WB
HIS 306K.description: A survey of the history and civilization of the Middle East from the sixth to the fourteenth century. Taught online during scheduled times.

HIS 306N.title: 3-KEY IDEAS & ISS IN LAT AMER
HIS 306N.description: Broad introductory course to acquaint students with the main areas of interest in Latin American studies.

HIS 307C.title: INTRO TO THE HISTORY OF INDIA
HIS 307C.description: Survey of the history of the Indian subcontinent from prehistoric times to the present.

HIS 307Q.title: INTRO HOLOCAUST/GENOCD STUDIES
HIS 307Q.description: Examine interdisciplinary perspectives (including historical, political, socio-psychological, and cultural methods and insights) on genocide as a global phenomenon.

HIS 311J.title: HISTORY OF ISRAEL
HIS 311J.description: An introduction to the history of Israel, from the emergence of the modern Zionist movement beginning in 1881 to the present.

HIS 312D.title: NORTH AMERICAN BORDERLANDS
HIS 312D.description: Explore the formation of political borders in nineteenth- and twentieth-century North America, their contestation, and the multiple meanings attached to them.

HIS 314K.title: HISTORY OF MEXICAN AMERS IN US
HIS 314K.description: Examines the origin and growth of the Mexican American community in the United States. History 314K and Mexican American Studies 316 may not both be counted.

HIS 315G.title: INTRO TO AMERICAN STUDIES
HIS 315G.description: An interdisciplinary introduction to the historical exploration of American culture. American Studies 310 and History 315G may not both be counted.

HIS 315K.title: THE UNITED STATES, 1492-1865
HIS 315K.description: Survey of United States history from the colonial period through the Civil War.

HIS 315L.title: THE UNITED STATES SINCE 1865
HIS 315L.description: Survey of United States history since the Civil War.

HIS 317L.title: 1-COLONIAL AMERICA
HIS 317L.description: Explore the experiences of Black people during the age of segregation, more commonly referred to as Jim Crow. Discuss the timeframe of 1890-1954, while also talking about the legacy of slavery and the Juneteenth generation. Examine how Black southerners responded to segregation by creating a vibrant community culture rooted in self-help, entrepreneurship, family, and faith. History 317L (Topic: Blk Southrners Age Jim Crow) and 317L (Topic 27) may not both be counted.

HIS 318N.title: DISCOVERY HISTORY
HIS 318N.description: Analyzes claims from popular books in the light of accurate historical sources. Introduction to the process of making discoveries in historical research.

HIS 320W.title: THINKING LIKE A HISTORIAN
HIS 320W.description: To understand history as an academic discipline in terms of research methods, evidence, and analysis.

HIS 322G.title: HIST OF MODERN LIFE SCIENCES
HIS 322G.description: History of the life sciences from the eighteenth century to the present.

HIS 325C.title: GLOBAL CATHOLICISM 1400-1800
HIS 325C.description: Explore the global reach of the Catholic Church before modernity. Examine the tension between the local and the "universal" church, that is, how encounter, religious devotion, and political power were shaped by local circumstances as well as global connections. Analyze recent scholarship, texts, artwork, and objects made by early modern people who shaped Catholicism in this period. History 325C and 366N (Topic: World Catholicism 1400-1800) may not both be counted.

HIS 325Q.title: RUS ORTHODOX RELIGION/CULTR
HIS 325Q.description: Examine the role and force of the Orthodox Church in Russian history, from the Christianization of the pagan Slavs in the 10th century, through the Russian Revolution into the Soviet era, and up to the present, while considering the prescriptive tenets of the Orthodox faith (religion) alongside descriptive experiences of life within and under Russian Orthodoxy (religiosity).

HIS 325T.title: HISTORY OF SEPHARDI JEWS
HIS 325T.description: Examine the early-modern networks established by the Sephardi Jews (Sephardim) across the Mediterranean and Atlantic, the rich Ladino Judeo-Spanish culture that thrived in the Ottoman Empire, and Sephardi encounters with the modern world.

HIS 330S.title: HIST OF MODERN MEDIA/CULTR
HIS 330S.description: Examine the history of modern media and media culture. History 330S and 366N (Topic: Hist of Modrn Media/ Cultr) may not both be counted.

HIS 331D.title: ARAB CITIZENS OF ISRAEL
HIS 331D.description: Examine the historical, social, and political processes of change experienced by the Palestinian-Arab community in Israel, including the fundamental aspects pertaining to the founding of Israel as "a Jewish and democratic state" and the relations between the Arab minority and the Jewish majority.

HIS 332Q.title: FORENSIC FILES: PHILOS CRIMES
HIS 332Q.description: Explore political philosophies from the continental tradition (Heidegger/Arendt, Nietzsche, Adorno/Horkheimer, and Agamben/Schmitt) implicated in radical politics and answering to political turning points, with a focus on research and reading in historical contexts.

HIS 332R.title: MARX AND MARXIST THEORY
HIS 332R.description: Introduction to the writings of Karl Marx as well as to those of his intellectual successors in Europe and around the globe in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

HIS 334I.title: CULTR TURKEY: MYTHS/REALTS
HIS 334I.description: Explore how Turkish culture is neither static nor monolithic but is continuously contested and redefined. Investigate tensions between secularism and Islam, Orientalist and Occidentalist fantasies, and imperialist and nationalist desires to better understand the sociopolitical dynamics that define Turkish society today.

HIS 334P.title: MIDDLE EAST OUTLAWS/LAWMEN
HIS 334P.description: Explore the evolution of crime and law enforcement in the modern Middle East.

HIS 337D.title: GERMAN CINEMA 1918-1945
HIS 337D.description: Explore the history and aesthetics of German cinema from 1918 until 1945.

HIS 337N.title: GERMANY IN THE 20TH CEN: HON
HIS 337N.description: Survey of German political and military institutions, economic development, culture, and society.

HIS 341J.title: HISTORY/MEMORY JAPAN/KOREA
HIS 341J.description: Explore the fraught and entangled histories of modern Japan and Korea as depicted in literature and film to consider experiences of imperialism, rapid modernization, war, decolonization, and trauma.

HIS 343W.title: WITCHES, WORKERS, AND WIVES
HIS 343W.description: Explores the role of families and concepts of gender as expressed in key economic, social, political, and cultural patterns in early modern Europe.

HIS 346J.title: COLONIAL LAT AMER THRU OBJS
HIS 346J.description: Examines objects, such as paintings, reliquaries, monstrances, and churches from colonial Latin America in order to understand colonial culture. Includes critical reading of original texts.

HIS 346K.title: COLONIAL LATIN AMERICA
HIS 346K.description: Basic survey course, designed as an introduction to Latin American history in the colonial period.

HIS 346L.title: MODERN LATIN AMERICA
HIS 346L.description: Continuation of History 346K and Latin American Studies 366 (Topic 2).

HIS 346W.title: CHURCH/STATE IN LAT AMER
HIS 346W.description: History of Church-state relations and religious politics in modern Latin America, with emphasis on the nineteenth to early twentieth-century periods.

HIS 349S.title: SURV MILITARY HIST, 1640-1900
HIS 349S.description: An investigation of world military systems and of the evolution of military technology from about the time of the Thirty Years' War to the end of the nineteenth century.

HIS 350L.title: 64-EINSTEIN IN AGE OF CONFLICT
HIS 350L.description: Lectures, discussion, reading, and research on selected topics in the field of history. Following the life and work of Albert Einstein, course examines the rise of the theories of relativity and quantum mechanics upon the stage of international political upheaval. History 350L and 350R may not both be counted unless the topics vary. Only one of the following may be counted: Core Texts and Ideas 370 (Topic: Einstein in the Age of Conflicts), History 350L (Topic: Einstein in the Age of Conflicts), 350L (Topic: 64).

HIS 350R.title: 1-COASTAL COMMUN EARLY AMER
HIS 350R.description: Lectures, discussion, reading, and research on selected topics in the field of United States history. Surveys the changing material culture of the western hemisphere from pre-Columbian times to the beginning of the industrial revolution. History 350L and 350R may not both be counted unless the topics vary. Only one of the following may be counted: American Studies 336F, 370 (Topic 59), History 350R (Topic 27).

HIS 352F.title: MEXICAN REVOLUTION, 1910-40
HIS 352F.description: Explore the initial decade of the Mexican Revolution, the first of the twentieth-century nationalist social revolutions. Examine the historical antecedents and the political, economic, social, and intellectual elements of the upheaval.

HIS 354E.title: ARCHAIC/CLASSICAL GREECE
HIS 354E.description: Survey of Greek history from the emergence of the city-states to the rise of Macedonia.

HIS 354F.title: THREE FRENCH WARS 20TH CEN
HIS 354F.description: Focuses on 20th century France, with an emphasis on three critical conflicts: World War One, World War Two, and the Algerian war. History 354F and 362G (Topic: Three French Wars: 20th Cen) may not both be counted.

HIS 355F.title: THE UNITED STATES, 1877-1920
HIS 355F.description: Examines the Gilded Age and Progressive Era to depict the rise of modern America.

HIS 355N.title: MAIN CURR OF AMER CUL TO 1865
HIS 355N.description: Traces the development of American culture and society from the colonial era until the end of the Civil War. Major themes include racial conflict, religion, slavery, the development of democracy, and cultural reform.

HIS 355S.title: US CONSTITUTIONAL HISTORY
HIS 355S.description: A lecture and discussion course dealing with the history of the development of the American constitutional tradition from colonial times to the present. History 355S and 366N (Topic: United States Constitutional History) may not both be counted.

HIS 356K.title: MAIN CURR AMER CUL SINCE 1865
HIS 356K.description: Traces the development of American culture and society from the end of the Civil War to the present. Major themes include racial conflict, pluralism, religion, urban development and reform, modernism, government centralization, cultural radicalism, and the rebirth of conservatism.

HIS 357C.title: AFRICAN AMERICAN HIST TO 1860
HIS 357C.description: Review of West African origins; New World settlement patterns, social life, and culture; discussion of the Atlantic slave trade, the development of capitalism and plantation slavery, and the origins of racism.

HIS 358C.title: HISTORY INTERNSHIP
HIS 358C.description: Apply historical methods in office and organizational workplaces. Utilize research, analytical, writing, and information skills.

HIS 359D.title: FASHION IN AFRICA
HIS 359D.description: Examine fashion history through readings, videos, social media, and textile making to provide an immersive experience in African design history. Address the ways in which textiles are tied to class distinctions and politics, wrapped up in the history of trade (including human trafficking and slavery), mixed into revolutionary struggles and post-independent governance, and inspire haute couture and global fashion trends.

HIS 361W.title: RUSS/UKRAINE IN WORLD HIST
HIS 361W.description: Focus on multicultural history, shared imperial and national spaces, and geopolitics of entangled Russian-Ukrainian relations.

HIS 362R.title: JEWS OF EASTERN EUROPE
HIS 362R.description: Explores the history and culture of Jews in Eastern Europe.

HIS 363E.title: MAPPING LATIN AMERICA
HIS 363E.description: Discuss the role of maps in the creation of Latin America as a specific sort of place. As such, allows familiarity with a broad overview of Latin American history from Pre-Columbian civilizations to the modern period.

HIS 364C.title: HIST OF HINDU RELIG TRADITN
HIS 364C.description: History of major doctrines, practices, and institutions that shaped the development of Hinduism; how religions adapt to social and cultural change and often provide the catalyst for change.

HIS 364F.title: AFRICAN HIST FILMS/PHOTOS
HIS 364F.description: Explore the social, economic, and political challenges of the past fifty years of Africa's history through an examination of several popular films.

HIS 368S.title: THE AGE OF THE SAMURAI
HIS 368S.description: Explore the history of Japan via an examination of the complex and ever-changing figure of the samurai.

HIS 374C.title: RACE/MIGRATION: MEDITERRANEAN
HIS 374C.description: Examine the history of migration within and from the Mediterranean area.

HIS 375N.title: HIST OF BRITAIN, 1500-1700
HIS 375N.description: Explore how the archipelago of islands that make up the nations of England, Scotland, Wales, and Ireland underwent a series of dramatic transformations in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. Focus on politics, religious upheaval, and the beginnings of "British empire," a phrase first coined in this period.

HIS 378W.title: CAPSTONE IN HISTORY
HIS 378W.description: Pursue in-depth the research interests and skills developed in other history classes. History 379C and 378W may not both be counted. Taught online during scheduled times.

HIS 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
HIS 679HA.description: An individual instruction course to provide training in the methods and teaching of historical research and writing. May not be included in the thirty semester hours of coursework required for the major.

HMN 102T.title: DESIGN A PATH
HMN 102T.description: Design a humanities contract of courses and write a personal statement. Examine the research process and academic writing.

HMN 104.title: INTRO TO HUMANITIES HON RES
HMN 104.description: Explore research from an interdisciplinary perspective. Develop a thesis project proposal in groups.

HMN 351C.title: THE JOHNSON YEARS
HMN 351C.description: Humanities 351C and Liberal Arts Honors 350 (Topic 18) may not both be counted.

HMN 368H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE I
HMN 368H.description: Read and research under direction. Write a report or create a project.

HMN 369H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE II
HMN 369H.description: Read and research under direction. Write a report or create a project.

HMN 370.title: SENIOR TUTORIAL COURSE
HMN 370.description: A tutorial program of supervised reading and writing, including an individual paper or papers in which the student draws together the central directions and discoveries of his or her studies in the humanities.

I 301.title: INTRODUCTION TO INFORMATICS
I 301.description: Introduction to the field of informatics, including cultural heritage informatics, health informatics, human-centered data science, social informatics, social justice informatics, and user experience design. Informatics 301 and Information Studies 304D may not both be counted.

I 302.title: ACADEMIC SUCCESS IN DGTL UNIV
I 302.description: Examine a wide variety of information and academic resources, tools, and skills; with a focus on the digital landscape of higher education. Explore the features of the primary learning management system; communication and organization tools; library resources; basic online research resources; and ethical and safety concerns. Informatics 302 and Information Studies 303D may not both be counted.

I 304.title: PROGRAMMING FOR INFORMATICS
I 304.description: Examine the fundamentals of computer programming and practice developing and documenting code.

I 305.title: RESEARCH METHDS FOR INFMTCS
I 305.description: Explore diverse research approaches to investigate informatics-related problems. Examine principles and hands-on practices of data collection and analysis with respect to qualitative, quantitative, and mixed research methods. Informatics 305 and Informatics 310 (topic: Resrch Methods for Informatics) may not both be counted.

I 306.title: STATISTICS FOR INFORMATICS
I 306.description: Examine fundamental principles of probability and statistics. Cultivate an understanding of descriptive and inferential statistics. Conduct and interpret statistical analyses using statistical analysis software, and apply these analyses to common issues in informatics.

I 310D.title: INTRO HUMAN-CENTERED DATA SCI
I 310D.description: Examine the fundamentals of collecting, managing, and analyzing data; with consideration for the ethical responsibility of data science and the importance of critical thinking.

I 310M.title: INTRO TO HEALTH INFORMATCS
I 310M.description: Explore designing and implementing information technologies to improve healthcare delivery, healthcare management, and health outcomes.

I 310S.title: INTRO TO SOCIAL INFORMATICS
I 310S.description: Explore the leveraging of data, information, and technology to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of organizations while also providing a more ethical and humane environment for workers.

I 310U.title: INTRO USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
I 310U.description: Introduction to user experience, human-computer interaction, and the human-centered design of interfaces and applications for mobile devices and the web.

I 320M.title: CONSUMER HEALTH INFORMATICS
I 320M.description: Topic title: Research Design and Analytics in Health Informatics.

I 320U.title: CIVIC ENGAGEMENT/TECHNOLOGY
I 320U.description: Topic Title: Eye Tracking & Human-Centered Physiological Computing.

I 372.title: CAREER SUCCESS IN THE DGTL ORG
I 372.description: Examine the essential skills for obtaining a job and sustaining a successful career with an emphasis on integrity, teamwork, time management, responsiveness to feedback, and written and oral communication. Explore tools for computer-supported cooperative work that facilitate online collaboration.

I 379C.title: CAPSTONE
I 379C.description: Partially taught as a Web-based course.

I 679HA.title: HONORS THESIS
I 679HA.description: Research, read, and develop an honors thesis subject and proposal for one semester; followed in the second semester by the writing and defense of a final honors thesis.

I 679HB.title: HONORS THESIS
I 679HB.description: Research, read, and develop an honors thesis subject and proposal for one semester; followed in the second semester by the writing and defense of a final honors thesis.

I B 320F.title: FOUNDTNS OF INTERNATL BUSN-WB
I B 320F.description: Fundamentals of international trade and the international economy; international dimensions of several functional areas of business, including management, marketing, finance, and human resource management; theoretical, institutional, and functional foundations of international business. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Business Administration degree. Only one of the following courses may be counted: Economics 339K, European Studies 348 (Topic 2: International Trade), International Business 320F, 350, 350S. Taught as a Web-based course.

I B 350.title: INTERNATIONAL TRADE
I B 350.description: Study of the principles, policies, and problems of the international exchange of goods and investments.

I B 360D.title: INTERNATIONAL SECURITY
I B 360D.description: Explore the conditions and motivations behind the use of military force in world politics.

I B 362.title: GLOBAL REGULATORY STRATEGY
I B 362.description: Examine management of a firm in a rapidly evolving regulatory environment across the world as new technology reshapes social norms, legal rules, and society itself.

I B 366.title: INTL ACCTING/TRANSFER PRICING
I B 366.description: Discover how to price transactions between subsidiaries of multinational corporations buying and selling goods, services, intellectual property and other assets in light of tax obligations and other considerations.

I B 367C.title: INTERCULTURAL MANAGEMENT
I B 367C.description: Examine a conceptual framework and specific tools for interacting successfully in international teams, negotiations, and partnerships. Discuss cultural patterns characteristic of world regions and particular countries in terms of their implications for corporate management.

I B 367D.title: GLOBAL ENTREPRENEURSHIP
I B 367D.description: Explore how to start, manage, and exit a business in a global context. Examine aspects of global entrepreneurship including opportunity analysis, business planning, fundraising, competing in a global marketplace, and managing through crisis explored through practical experience and real-world examples.

I B 368.title: GLOBAL VALUE CHAINS
I B 368.description: Investigate problems that complicate the operation of supply chains on a global scale, such as political risk.

I B 372T.title: BUSINESS/GEOPOLITICS/DIPLOMACY
I B 372T.description: Topic title: Business, Geopolitics, and Diplomacy. Topic description: Explore how geopolitics and heightened global competition impact U.S. private sector leadership in the world, and the role U.S. government plays in advocating for American companies to compete and win in markets abroad. Discuss global deal-making in strategic industries such as AI and advanced tech, energy, and infrastructure, and the nexus between business and national security.

I B 376.title: INTERNATIONAL FINANCE
I B 376.description: Explore the international financial environment, with emphasis on the factors affecting exchange rates and how exchange rate changes affect the firm. Finance 376 and International Business 376 may not both be counted.

INB 122L.title: STRUCT/PHYS/REP SEED PLANT LAB
INB 122L.description: Observe the structure and reproduction in seed plants and employ experimental techniques that demonstrate physiological processes, especially processes of growth and development. Biology 122L and Integrative Biology 122L may not both be counted.

INB 165U.title: HUMAN SYSTEMS PHYSIO LAB
INB 165U.description: Examine human physiology through case studies and hands on experience. Explore the scientific method through the reading of scientific journal articles; writing protocols for basic physiological experiments; and collecting, analyzing, and presenting data. Biology 165U and Integrative Biology 165U may not both be counted.

INB 177.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
INB 177.description: Conduct laboratory or field research in the various fields of biological science under the supervision of one or more faculty members. May be taken three times for credit.

INB 237.title: ECOLOGY OF NOVEL ECOSYSTEMS
INB 237.description: Examine recent developments and research methods in the biological sciences.

INB 277.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
INB 277.description: Conduct laboratory or field research in the various fields of biological science under the supervision of one or more faculty members. May be taken three times for credit.

INB 321G.title: PRINCPLES OF COMP BIO-FRI
INB 321G.description: Introduction to computational methods for the analysis of nucleic acid and protein sequences, with applications towards biological problems. Biology 321G and Integrative Biology 321G may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

INB 322.title: STRUC/PHYS/REPRO OF SEED PLANT
INB 322.description: Explore the principles of structure and functioning of higher plants with special attention to the dynamics of growth and development and reproduction. Biology 322 and Integrative Biology 322 may not both be counted.

INB 337.title: COMPARATIVE PRIMATE ANATOMY
INB 337.description: Examine recent developments and research methods in the biological sciences. Explore an overview of the major types of parasites and pathogens of primates and other mammals, their natural history, ecology, and population dynamics, and their role as selective agents on aspects of primate physiology, behavior, and sociality.

INB 346.title: HUMAN BIOLOGY
INB 346.description: Introduction to human evolution, genetics, sexuality, senescence, and population growth.

INB 359K.title: PRINCIPLES OF ANIMAL BEHAVIOR
INB 359K.description: Introduction to the study of animal behavior: descriptive analysis of behavior; physiological basis of behavior; development of behavior; adaptive significance and evolution of behavior; communication and social behavior. Biology 359K and Integrative Biology 359K may not both be counted.

INB 364.title: MICROBIAL ECOLOGY
INB 364.description: Examine population, community, and ecosystem ecology of microbes and microbiomes. Biology 364 and Integrative Biology 364 may not both be counted.

INB 365S.title: HUMAN SYSTEMS PHYSIOLOGY
INB 365S.description: Explore an overview of human physiology, including the cardiovascular, respiratory, and renal systems, with an emphasis on critical thinking, integration, and application. Examine membrane movement, osmolarity and tonicity, endocrinology, and neurophysiology. Integrative Biology 365S and Biology 365S may not both be counted.

INB 369L.title: HERPETOLOGY
INB 369L.description: Examine the biology of amphibians and reptiles, including evolution, ecology, behavior, physiology, life history, and identification. Biology 369L and Integrative Biology 369L may not both be counted.

INB 370.title: EVOLUTION
INB 370.description: Introduction to modern evolutionary biology, focusing on the evolution of molecular, developmental, morphological, and behavioral traits. Examine the genetic and ecological bases of evolutionary changes within populations and of evolutionary divergence in animals and plants. Biology 370 and Integrative Biology 370 may not both be counted.

INB 373.title: ECOLOGY
INB 373.description: Introduction to ecology, the study of relationships among organisms and between organisms and their environment; adaptations, population, communities, and ecosystems. Examine both plants and animals and both terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems. Biology 373 and Integrative Biology 373 may not both be counted.

INB 373L.title: ECOLOGY LABORATORY
INB 373L.description: Explore intensive field ecology, including group field experimentation and observation, independent projects, and field trips to other vegetation zones. Biology 373L and Integrative Biology 373L may not both be counted. Taught at the Brackenridge Field Laboratory in Austin, Texas.

INB 377.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
INB 377.description: Conduct laboratory or field research in the various fields of biological science under the supervision of one or more faculty members. May be taken three times for credit. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. May be repeated for credit.

INB 379H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
INB 379H.description: Perform original laboratory or field research project under the direction of a faculty mentor, leading to a thesis or research presentation for students in the honors program in biology.

INB 446L.title: HUMAN MICROSCOPIC/GROSS ANAT
INB 446L.description: Designed for students preparing for biomedical research and the health professions. Explore microscopic and gross anatomy of human tissues and organs, with an emphasis on structure function relationships. Examine the effects of disease and aging in addition to normal human anatomy. Integrative Biology 446L and Biology 446L may not both be counted.

INB 453L.title: ENTOMOLOGY
INB 453L.description: Examine the characteristics, importance, and biology of the major groups of insects. Biology 453L and Integrative Biology 453L may not both be counted.

INB 463L.title: PLANT SYSTEMATICS
INB 463L.description: Examine the principles of plant classification, phylogeny, and diversity as exemplified by families and species of flowering plants found seasonally in Texas with an emphasis on the local flora. Biology 463L and Integrative Biology 463L may not both be counted

IRG 320F.title: FOUNDATN INTL RELS/GLOBAL STDS
IRG 320F.description: Survey of contemporary debates related to international relations and global studies.

IRG 678HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
IRG 678HA.description: Supervised individual research on an international relations and global studies topic.

ITC 347R.title: RACE/MIGRATION: MEDITERRANEAN
ITC 347R.description: Examine the history of migration within and from the Mediterranean area.

ITD 101.title: INTRO TO INTEGRATED DESIGN
ITD 101.description: Introduction to the concepts, mindsets, and methods of design thinking as a fundamental skill for all disciplines. Class meets September 29-November 2.

ITD 102.title: SKETCHNG FOR THINKING/COMM-WB
ITD 102.description: Discussion of sketching as the fastest way to convey ideas, both in an ideation session or taking notes in a meeting. Explores the basic elements of sketching to visualize concepts and quickly bring alignment to any team. Taught as a Web-based course.

ITD 106.title: PRES/IMPROV SKILLS IN DSGN
ITD 106.description: Focus on presentation skills and improvisational/impromptu speaking skills in meetings. Class meets August 24-September 28.

ITD 130T.title: DSGN, BUILD, AND THNK W/ AI
ITD 130T.description: Explore innovative topics in design. Use large language models, generative AI, and no-code tools to design and build projects through established design methodologies and product development processes. Compare and evaluate categories of AI tools that support research, synthesis, design, and delivery. Apply design and business value frameworks to assess AI-enablement and automation and analyze their implications for execution, strategic direction, and professional practice.

ITD 150.title: CO-DESIGNING PEACE
ITD 150.description: Advanced studies in integrated design. Explore contemporary ideas of peace through the creative practices of design. Examine historical and modern movements to understand how design influences collaboration, systems, and social transformation. Develop approaches that connect design to global challenges and collective well-being. Class meets August 24-September 28.

ITD 301D.title: INTRO TO DESIGN THINKING
ITD 301D.description: Introduces design thinking and design methods, with a focus on design process. Design 301 and Integrated Design 301D may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

ITD 307C.title: EVERYDAY DESIGN
ITD 307C.description: Explore the influence of design in daily life, its impact on society, and how design disciplines shape the world through purposeful choices and creative practices. Design 307C and Integrated Design 307C may not both be counted.

ITD 310T.title: PROTOTYPING PRODUCTS
ITD 310T.description: Exploration of topics in innovative design. Problem solve and prototype mass produced products through an iterative design process. Explore 3D design conceptualization through hands-on building techniques and digital fabrication methods.

ITD 330T.title: PLAY DESIGN LAB
ITD 330T.description: Explore innovative topics in design. Explore the emerging field of play design and its potential to create better conditions in our rapidly changing societies. Examine how play can contribute to the development of social and creative processes exploring boundary lines of a practice in designing for play and designing through play. Gain a deeper understanding of play design theory as well as embodied experiences of play to inform design choices in projects.

ITD 350.title: COLLAB LAB: DESIGNING IN TEAMS
ITD 350.description: Advanced studies in integrated design. Learn and practice techniques that maximize participation on creative teams.

ITD 375.title: CAPSTONE IN INTEGRATED DSGN
ITD 375.description: Multidisciplinary groups of students research an integrated design problem and propose and prototype an appropriate solution, guided by feedback from supervising instructor(s).

ITL 320.title: ADVANCED ITALIAN
ITL 320.description: Designed to develop the listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills needed for the analysis of literary and cultural readings in Italian.

ITL 328.title: COMPOSITION AND CONVERSATION
ITL 328.description: Focuses on idioms, grammar, syntax, and style.
