MNS 320.description: Study of ecological processes at different levels of integration in marine ecosystems.

MNS 354T.title: BIOLOGICAL OCEANOGRAPHY-WB
MNS 354T.description: Introduction to the organisms in the sea, their adaptations to the environment, and the factors that control their distribution and abundance; may include laboratory and field work with organisms found in the coastal waters of Texas. Taught as a Web-based course.

MSE 360M.title: EXPERIMENTS IN MSE
MSE 360M.description: Hands-on lab-based course designed to teach practical techniques in the synthesis and characterization of materials and their properties, and to use experiments to explore fundamental and potentially abstract materials concepts. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

MUS 007P.title: STRING EDUCATION: TEACH/LEARN
MUS 007P.description: Discussion of curriculum planning, lesson observation, and program management topics, as well as guest pedagogues and master teacher presentations.

MUS 101Q.title: BEGINNING GUITAR CLASS
MUS 101Q.description: Class instruction in beginning guitar performance for non-music majors or music majors studying a secondary instrument.

MUS 101V.title: BEGINNING VIOLIN CLASS
MUS 101V.description: Class instruction in beginning violin performance for non-music majors or music majors studying a secondary instrument.

MUS 111E.title: ENGLISH DICTION & PHONET TRANS
MUS 111E.description: Open only to music majors. Study of English diction and phonetic translation specifically for musicians. Emphasis on the International Phonetic Alphabet.

MUS 115D.title: STRING INSTRUMENT FUNDAMENTALS
MUS 115D.description: Beginning instruction in string instrument performance and pedagogy. This course is offered in the following instruments: double bass, viola, violin, and violoncello.

MUS 115E.title: BRASS INSTRUMENT FUNDAMENTALS
MUS 115E.description: Beginning instruction in brass instrument performance and pedagogy. This course is offered in the following instruments: euphonium, French horn, trombone, trumpet, and tuba.

MUS 115F.title: WOODWIND INSTRUMENT FUNDMNTLS
MUS 115F.description: Beginning instruction in woodwind instrument performance and pedagogy. This course is offered in the following instruments: bassoon, clarinet, flute, oboe, and saxophone.

MUS 115G.title: GUITAR FUNDAMENTALS
MUS 115G.description: Beginning instruction in guitar performance and pedagogy. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

MUS 155C.title: TECHNIQUES OF PERCUSSION PERF
MUS 155C.description: Percussion performance techniques for individual and ensemble settings, including appropriate literature and rehearsal procedures.

MUS 155S.title: VOCAL TECH FOR INSTRUMNTALISTS
MUS 155S.description: Explore vocal techniques for individual and instructional settings. Examine appropriate literature and rehearsal procedures. Music 155S and 176C (Topic: Voice Fundamentals) and may not both be counted.

MUS 160C.title: SENIOR COMPOSITION RECITAL
MUS 160C.description: Open only to music composition majors. Preparation and performance of a public recital of the student's original compositions.

MUS 176M.title: MUSICAL LIVES
MUS 176M.description: Explores wellness concepts and activities relevant to instrumental performers and teachers that promote mental and physical health and prevent physical injury. Topics include Body Mapping, Alexander Technique, fitness, nutrition, creating productive habits, protecting your hearing, and coping with performance anxiety.

MUS 178C.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY: MUSIC PERF
MUS 178C.description: Individual projects to be completed under faculty supervision.

MUS 178M.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY: MUSIC STDS
MUS 178M.description: Individual projects to be completed under faculty supervision.

MUS 201M.title: BEG MUSIC PERF: CLASS PIANO
MUS 201M.description: Open only to music majors. Credit granted only when taken as a secondary instrument.

MUS 210J.title: BEG MUS PERF: 3RD-SEM CLS PIA
MUS 210J.description: Open only to music majors. Continuation of Music 201N.

MUS 214C.title: BEGINNING COMPOSITION
MUS 214C.description: Introduction to contemporary composition through the analysis and writing of short studies and through supervised original projects. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. May be repeated for credit.

MUS 218J.title: BEGINNING JAZZ IMPROVISATION
MUS 218J.description: Study of basic jazz improvisational skills through performance of standard literature.

MUS 220P.title: PRACTICUM IN WORLD MUSIC TRAD
MUS 220P.description: Open to any University student who can qualify by audition. Advanced training on a non-Western musical instrument such as Javanese gender barung, Indian sitar, Arabic qanun, or Korean kayakeum, or advanced training in a non-Western vocal style, and other approved musical practices.

MUS 222J.title: INSTRUMENTAL CONDUCTING
MUS 222J.description: Designed for those who have had no experience in conducting. Includes rudimentary use of baton, regular and irregular beat patterns, subdivisions and beat pattern variations as applied to simple instrumental literature, and practical experience in conducting instrumental groups.

MUS 223.title: JAZZ IMPROVISATION I
MUS 223.description: Study and practice jazz improvisation and the harmonic, rhythmic, melodic and aural skills associated with the art form.

MUS 223J.title: CHORAL CONDUCTING
MUS 223J.description: Designed for those who have had no experience in conducting. Includes regular and irregular beat patterns, subdivisions and beat pattern variations as applied to simple choral literature, and practical experience in conducting vocal groups.

MUS 226J.title: ORCHESTRATION & INSTRUMENTATN
MUS 226J.description: Study of the characteristics of individual instruments; writing for various combinations; study of scores of different periods; listening to recordings and live performances.

MUS 228C.title: JAZZ HARMONY IN PRACTICE I
MUS 228C.description: Study the harmonic language standardized in jazz and popular American music.

MUS 246.title: JAZZ PIANO TECHNIQUES I
MUS 246.description: Study basic skills in chord notation, chord interpretation and voicing, voice leading, and jazz piano styles. Music 228K and 246 may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

MUS 255F.title: TECHNIQUES OF WOODWIND PERF
MUS 255F.description: Woodwind instrument performance techniques for individual and ensemble settings, including appropriate literature and rehearsal procedures.

MUS 255M.title: MARCHING BAND TECHNIQUES
MUS 255M.description: Literature, materials, and techniques of the marching band. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

MUS 255V.title: TECHNIQUES OF VOCAL PERFORMNC
MUS 255V.description: Technique of individual and ensemble singing practiced through the study of vocal literature.

MUS 262C.title: INTERMED INSTRMNTAL CONDUCTING
MUS 262C.description: Problems and interpretation of larger band and orchestral works; analytical study of musical form as it relates to conducting; a synthesis of musical understanding and expansion of comprehensive musicianship through conducting problems. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. May be repeated for credit.

MUS 271P.title: 1-PEDAGOGY: PIANO I
MUS 271P.description: Designed primarily for students planning teaching careers. Offered in piano, voice, and orchestral instruments. Methods include individual and class instruction through the use of music literature and the teaching repertoire. Practice teaching and laboratory for diagnostic and corrective methods are required. Work with students in the Butler School of Music's Piano Project.

MUS 278C.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY: MUSIC PERF
MUS 278C.description: Individual projects to be completed under faculty supervision.

MUS 278G.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY: MUSIC LIT
MUS 278G.description: Individual projects to be completed under faculty supervision.

MUS 302L.title: INTRO TO WESTERN MUSIC-WB
MUS 302L.description: Introduction to musical styles in the Euro-American art music tradition between the Middle Ages and the present day. Designed to foster critical listening and thinking about music, and deepen understanding of the vital role that music has played and continues to play in society and in individual lives. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Taught as a Web-based course.

MUS 303C.title: FUNDMNTL MUS: CLASSRM INSTRMNT
MUS 303C.description: Explores how music intelligently combines reading, analysis, discussion, composition, and active involvement in music making. Through analysis, performance, and practice of the pedagogy of music for children, explores the roles of music in human culture and examine music's contributions to human communication, creative expression, and well-being. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

MUS 303D.title: FUNDAMENTALS OF MUSIC: GUITAR
MUS 303D.description: Explores how music intelligently combines reading, analysis, discussion, composition, and active involvement in music making. Through analysis, performance, and discussion of the guitar in the folk and popular music of Western and non-Western cultures, explores music's contributions to human communication, creative expression, and well-being. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

MUS 303E.title: FUNDAMENTALS OF MUSIC: PIANO
MUS 303E.description: Explores how music intelligently combines reading, analysis, discussion, composition, and active involvement in music making. Through analysis, performance, and discussion of the piano in the folk and popular music of Western and non-Western cultures, explores music's contributions to human communication, creative expression, and well-being. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

MUS 303M.title: INTRO MUS IN WORLD CULTURES
MUS 303M.description: Exploration of practices, beliefs, and issues through the study of various musical genres in the regions of Asia, the Middle East, Africa, the Pacific, Europe, and the Americas. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

MUS 306M.title: ELEMENTS OF MUSIC
MUS 306M.description: Open to all University students except music majors. Fundamentals of music including the study of notation and the elements of rhythm, melody, and harmony; development of elementary aural skills; and writing of simple compositions.

MUS 307.title: AUSTIN SOUNDSCAPES
MUS 307.description: Open to all University students. Studies of the popular music of a selected culture or geographical area. Taught online during scheduled times. Includes live-streaming video and requires real-time participation. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

MUS 311G.title: GERMAN FOR MUSICIANS
MUS 311G.description: Open only to music majors. German language and diction specifically for musicians. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

MUS 313M.title: HISTORY OF MUSIC I
MUS 313M.description: The history of music from the beginning of notation to the eighteenth century.

MUS 319D.title: FOUNDATIONS DIGITAL SOUND/MUS
MUS 319D.description: Introduction to the practice of creating music with computer technologies and the basic techniques of recording, editing and producing digital audio tracks, including introduction to musical acoustics and psychoacoustics, fundamentals of audio recording, editing, mixing, and processing, MIDI, digital audio workstations, and syncing sound to video.

MUS 321J.title: 20TH-CENT MUSICAL ANALYSIS
MUS 321J.description: Introduction to the theory and practice of music since 1900. Includes various analytical approaches to the wide range of musical styles developed over the course of the twentieth- and twenty-first centuries, as well as the music of mainstream twentieth century composers and living composers. Both analytical and aural skills are emphasized.

MUS 325L.title: COUNTERPOINT
MUS 325L.description: Development of contrapuntal skill in sixteenth-century style and in related late-twentieth-century styles; the teaching of counterpoint, including Fuxian species.

MUS 329E.title: INTRO TO ELECTRONIC MEDIA
MUS 329E.description: Introduction to the fundamentals of recording, tape editing, and electronic music synthesis. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

MUS 329J.title: INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER MUSIC
MUS 329J.description: An introduction to the basic concepts of digital music synthesis and signal processing. Arts and Entertainment Technologies 331 and Music 329J may not both be counted.

MUS 330L.title: HISTORY OF MUSIC III
MUS 330L.description: The history of music from the early twentieth century to the present.

MUS 334.title: 8-MEXICAN SOUNDSCAPES
MUS 334.description: Studies of both indigenous and borrowed traditions in the popular, folk, and art music of the Americas from the colonial period to the present. Examine culturally and historically the music and sound cultures that exist in Mexico and in Mexican American communities in the United States. Explore the social, cultural, religious, and political currents that have impacted the development of the musical and aural cultures in Mexico and on the US-Mexico border, and how these currents have shaped conceptions of identity and cultural nationalism, using the concept of soundscape as the dominant framework. Engage with readings in popular culture, popular media (cinema and television), cultural theory, history and musicology. Music 334 (Topic 8) and 342 (Topic: Mexican Soundscapes) may not both be counted.

MUS 337.title: MUSIC AND FILM SOUND
MUS 337.description: History of music and the soundtrack; analysis of interactions between image and sound; and aesthetics and issues of production and postproduction.

MUS 343J.title: HISTORY OF JAZZ
MUS 343J.description: Survey of the history of jazz from its origins to the present.

MUS 354C.title: CHILDREN'S MUS LIT AND PERF I
MUS 354C.description: Literature, materials, and music performance activities appropriate for young children. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

MUS 376J.title: FILM SCORING
MUS 376J.description: Explore a practical guide to contemporary film composition, in both narrative and documentary styles. Subjects include working with directors, finding the sound language of the film, spotting the film, composing and developing themes, transitions and underscore.

MUS 378C.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY: MUSIC PERF
MUS 378C.description: Individual projects to be completed under faculty supervision.

MUS 378G.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY: MUSIC LIT
MUS 378G.description: Individual projects to be completed under faculty supervision.

MUS 378M.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY: MUSIC STDS
MUS 378M.description: Individual projects to be completed under faculty supervision.

MUS 379G.title: CAPSTONE IN MUSIC
MUS 379G.description: Drawing from the chosen concentration for the Bachelor of Arts in Music, generate scholarly or creative work to enhance preparation for a post-baccalaureate education or career.

MUS 379K.title: EXPER MEDIA PERF ART
MUS 379K.description: Two or more topics may be taken concurrently.

MUS 411A.title: EAR TRAINING AND SIGHT-SINGING
MUS 411A.description: Material drawn from all musical styles. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

MUS 420J.title: JUNIOR JAZZ RECITAL
MUS 420J.description: Preparation and performance of a public recital in the major jazz instrument.

MUS 420R.title: JUNIOR RECITAL
MUS 420R.description: Preparation and performance of a public recital in the major instrument.

MUS 456G.title: CHORAL ENS LITERATURE/PERF
MUS 456G.description: Choral music literature; rehearsal and performance techniques for choirs and small ensembles. Taught in Austin-area middle schools and high schools.

MUS 460J.title: SENIOR JAZZ RECITAL
MUS 460J.description: Open only to jazz performance majors. Preparation and performance of a public recital in the major jazz instrument.

MUS 460R.title: SENIOR RECITAL
MUS 460R.description: Open only to music performance majors. Preparation and performance of a public recital in the major instrument.

MUS 605A.title: MUSICIANSHIP
MUS 605A.description: Study of the fundamentals of music for music majors through tonal harmony, ear training, sight-singing, keyboard drill, analysis, and composition of music.

MUS 612A.title: STRUCTURE OF TONAL MUSIC
MUS 612A.description: Elements of tonal harmony and form; tonal analysis. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

N 117H.title: INTRO NUR SCHOLRSHP: HON SMNR
N 117H.description: Introduction to the nature of nursing scholarship, along with related research and clinical and educational career opportunities. Topics include leadership, research, and ethics as they relate to nursing. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

N 127P.title: CLINICAL NUR SKILLS I (PRAC)
N 127P.description: Laboratory instruction and practice in clinical nursing skills.

N 137.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY-NURSING
N 137.description: Study in a specific area; topic and mode of study are agreed upon by student(s) and instructor.

N 157P.title: CLINICAL NUR SKILLS II PRAC
N 157P.description: Laboratory instruction and practice in clinical nursing skills.

N 223.title: GENETICS IN HEALTH CARE
N 223.description: The integration of genetic information into nursing practice, including ethical, legal, psychological, and social issues. Nursing 223 and 323 may not both be counted.

N 224.title: HEALTH ASSESSMENT SKILLS
N 224.description: Discussion, application, and documentation of health assessment skills needed to provide data for health promotion and nursing interventions.

N 237.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY-NURSING
N 237.description: Study in a specific area; topic and mode of study are agreed upon by student(s) and instructor.

N 244.title: PERINATAL NURSING CARE
N 244.description: Examine concepts, theories, and processes essential to understanding the health concerns and problems of women and their families during the childbearing years. Nursing 244 and 265 may not both be counted.

N 250.title: INTERPROFESSIONAL COLLAB PRAC
N 250.description: Focuses on developing the competencies needed for interprofessional collaborative practice among health care professionals. Core interprofessional competencies addressed include: values and ethics for interprofessional practice; knowledge of roles and responsibilities of different health professions; interprofessional communication; and teams and teamwork.

N 252.title: CAREER PLAN/PROF DEVEL
N 252.description: Develop foundational knowledge and skills needed to launch a successful career in health care. Explore career management and professional development as a continuous lifelong process. Nursing 247 (Topic: Nur Career Plan/Prof Devel) and Nursing 252 may not both be counted.

N 255C.title: ADULT HEALTH NURSING IIA
N 255C.description: First of a two-part sequence in adult health nursing. Advanced concepts and theories to promote and restore health of adults with biological problems and related physiological and psychological responses. Nursing 455 and 255C may not both be counted.

N 255D.title: ADULT HEALTH NURSING IIB
N 255D.description: Second part of a two-part sequence in adult health nursing. Advanced concepts and theories to promote and restore health of adults with biological problems and related physiological and psychological responses Nursing 455 and 255D may not both be counted.

N 256P.title: PROB IN MENTAL HLTH NUR (PRAC)
N 256P.description: Application of strategies for the care of individuals, groups, and families experiencing mental health problems. Nursing 256P and 356P may not both be counted. Clinical hours taught at Austin-area hospitals.

N 264.title: NURSING RESEARCH
N 264.description: An introduction to key components of the research process, interpretation of basic statistics in research, and critical examination of patient-outcome research studies. Nursing 264 and 264H may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

N 264H.title: NURSING RESEARCH: HONORS
N 264H.description: An introduction to key components of the research process, interpretation of basic statistics in research, and critical examination of patient-outcome research studies. Nursing 264 and 264H may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

N 266.title: NUR CARE OF CHILDREN AND FAMS
N 266.description: Concepts, theories, and developmental processes essential to the health concerns and problems of children, adolescents, and their families.

N 273.title: QUALITY/SAFETY NUR PRACTICE
N 273.description: Designed to prepare students for professional nursing practice. Provides opportunities for synthesis of theoretical, evidence-based, and clinical knowledge with emphasis on quality care and patient safety. Nursing 273 and 278 may not both be counted.

N 274.title: COMPLEX NURSING CARE
N 274.description: Addresses the management of complex and critical needs of patients and their families across the continua of care. The focus will be on people with multiple chronic conditions experienced by various populations such as age groups, disease/health conditions, and settings. In addition, transitional care, critical care concepts, palliative, and end-of-life care will be explored. Class meets August 26-October 28.

N 275.title: PUBLIC HEALTH NURSING
N 275.description: Presents public health nursing concepts, theories, and processes used in health promotion, disease or injury prevention, health protection, and risk reduction with individuals, families, groups, communities, and populations. Class meets August 27-October 29. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

N 277P.title: CLINICAL CARE MANAGEMENT PRAC
N 277P.description: Application of selected concepts and theories of management in the planning and delivery of health care. Nursing 277P and 377P may not both be counted. Class meets August 24-November 2. Taught in Austin area hospitals.

N 279P.title: CAPSTONE PRECEPTORSHIP
N 279P.description: Designed to provide students with clinical experiences similar to those that confront newly employed registered nurses. Focuses on the role transformation of students and on clinical competence in communication, collaboration, negotiation, delegation, coordination, and evaluation of interdisciplinary work. Class meets November 3-December 7. Clinical hours taught in Austin-area hospitals.

N 309.title: GLOBAL HEALTH
N 309.description: Overview of global health, with emphasis on the determinants of health, health indicators, human rights, globalization, current socioeconomic factors, health care systems, and public health systems.

N 310.title: COMM IN HEALTH CARE SETTINGS
N 310.description: Introduction to theories and models of communication in relation to health care; basic factors affecting interpersonal communication in health care settings. Required for nursing majors. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

N 320.title: INTRO TO PATIENT-CTRD NUR CARE
N 320.description: Examines the profession of nursing and introduces the concept of nursing process as a framework for patient-centered care of younger and older adults. Special emphasis will be placed on topics that apply to the bio-psycho-social-spiritual needs of the aging adult. Nursing 227 and 320 may not both be counted.

N 321.title: ETHICS OF HEALTH CARE
N 321.description: Examination of ethical issues of health care and related legal concerns; contradictions, inconsistencies, and competing views that lead to dilemmas in health care. Nursing 321 and 321H may not both be counted.

N 325.title: ADULT HEALTH NURSING I
N 325.description: Discussion of the concepts necessary to promote and restore the health of adults with biological problems including related physiological and psychological responses.

N 325P.title: ADULT HEALTH NURSING I (PRAC)
N 325P.description: For nursing majors admitted to the professional sequence. Application of the concepts and theories necessary to promote and restore health of adults with biological problems; related physiological and psychological responses. Clinical hours taught in Austin-area hospitals.

N 337.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY-NURSING
N 337.description: Study in a specific area; topic and mode of study are agreed upon by student(s) and instructor.

N 344P.title: PERINATAL NURSING CARE PRACTIC
N 344P.description: Apply concepts, theories, and processes pertinent to care of women and their families during the childbearing years. Nursing 344P and 365P may not both be counted. Taught in Austin area hospitals.

N 354.title: SPANISH FOR HLTH CARE PROFESNL
N 354.description: Basic medical Spanish language skills and phrases related to assessment and nursing activities. Focus on the cultural values of Latino patients who seek health care in the United States.

N 355P.title: ADULT HEALTH NURSING II (PRAC)
N 355P.description: Application of advanced concepts and theories to promote and restore health of adults with biological problems and their related physiological and psychological responses. Clinical hours taught in Austin-area hospitals.

N 356.title: MNTL HLTH NUR ACROSS LIFE SPAN
N 356.description: Biological, environmental, cultural, and interpersonal factors predisposing individuals across the life span to mental health problems. The course is organized around the nursing process, as well as multidisciplined therapeutic modalities, that assist the individual and family to adapt, recover, and grow through these problems. Current relevant research, and the sociocultural, legal, and ethical implications of providing nursing care to the mentally ill.

N 366P.title: NUR CARE CHLD/THEIR FAMS PRAC
N 366P.description: Application of concepts, theories, and developmental processes essential to the health concerns and problems of children, adolescents, and their families. Clinical hours taught in Austin-area hospitals.

N 375P.title: PUBLIC HLTH NURSING PRACTICUM
N 375P.description: Application of public health nursing concepts, theories, and processes used in health promotion, disease or injury prevention, health protection, and risk reduction with individuals, families, groups, communities, and populations. Class meets August 24-November 2. Clinical hours taught in Austin-area hospitals.

N 377.title: LDRSHP & MGMT OF NURSING CARE
N 377.description: Investigation,synthesis and application of laws,rules,theories and concepts in nursing leadership and management that promote professional nursing practice and healthcare leadership in diverse settings. Nursing 377 and 377H may not both be counted. Class meets August 26-October 28.

N S 302.title: INTRODUCTION TO NAVAL SCIENCE
N S 302.description: A general introduction to sea power and the naval service, including the mission, organization, regulations, warfare components, and personnel programs.

N S 315.title: NAVAL ENGINEERING
N S 315.description: Introduction to types, structures, and purposes of naval ships and systems. Naval Science 603 and 315 may not both be counted.

N S 330.title: LEADERSHIP AND ETHICS
N S 330.description: Principles of leadership and ethics reinforced through seminar discussion and case studies. Discussion of the duties and responsibilities of a naval officer.

N S 335.title: LEADERSHIP AND MANAGEMENT
N S 335.description: Study of leadership and management theory in organizations, with emphasis on examining the leadership process in the context of the dynamic interaction of the leader, the followers, and the situation.

N S 369.title: NAVIGATION AND NAVAL OPS II
N S 369.description: Study of the celestial sphere and nautical astronomy to determine positions on the earth by mathematical analysis, and an introduction to relative motion and the maneuvering board.

NEU 277.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
NEU 277.description: Laboratory or research in the various fields of neuroscience under the supervision of one or more faculty members. Up to three semester hours may be counted toward the major requirement for the Bachelor of Science in Neuroscience.

NEU 330.title: NEURAL SYSTEMS I
NEU 330.description: Introduction to the nervous system with an emphasis on brain organization, neuron physiology, perceptual systems, and motor systems.

NEU 335.title: NEURAL SYSTEMS II
NEU 335.description: Introduction to the nervous system with an emphasis on neural development and on the neural mechanisms of memory, emotions, and other higher cognitive functions. Biology 337 (Topic: Neural Systems II) and Neuroscience 335 may not both be counted.

NEU 337.title: NEUROANATOMY
NEU 337.description: Topics include recent developments and research methods in the field of neuroscience. Study the anatomy of the human central and peripheral nervous systems and selected details of the development of the nervous system. Explore comparative anatomy of the nervous systems of humans and model organisms and how model organisms are used in neuroscience research.

NEU 340.title: NEURAL SYSTEMS III: QUAN TOOLS
NEU 340.description: Introduction to quantitative approaches central to the study of the brain, with an emphasis on mathematical and statistical methods required for the rigorous design and interpretation of neuroscience experiments and data.

NEU 365D.title: PRINCIPLES OF DRUG ACTION
NEU 365D.description: Introduction to the basic principles of pharmacology; including how drugs get into the body, exert their actions, and are metabolized and excreted.

NEU 365L.title: NEUROBIOLOGY LABORATORY
NEU 365L.description: An introduction to physiological, morphological, and molecular techniques used for analysis of the nervous system. Experiments and computer simulations illustrate basics of information processing by the nervous system.

NEU 365T.title: NEUROBIOLOGY OF DISEASE
NEU 365T.description: The neurobiological basis of disorders of the brain, with the main focus on mental illness. Emphasizes the neural circuitries and neurochemical events that underlie specific mental processes and behaviors.

NEU 365W.title: NEUROBIOLOGY OF ADDICTION
NEU 365W.description: Study of the neurobiology of neurotransmitters, and the influence of alcohol and drugs of abuse on neurotransmitters.

NEU 366L.title: NEUROIMAGING LABORATORY
NEU 366L.description: Basic principles of image formation and techniques of fluorescent imaging and confocal laser-scanning microscopy. Includes image processing and analysis to extract quantitative information from digital images. Survey of imaging techniques, including electron microscopy and MRI.

NEU 377.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
NEU 377.description: Laboratory or research in the various fields of neuroscience under the supervision of one or more faculty members. Up to three semester hours may be counted toward the major requirement for the Bachelor of Science in Neuroscience.

NEU 379H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
NEU 379H.description: Original laboratory research project under the direction of a faculty mentor leading to a thesis or research presentation.

NEU 466G.title: FUNCTIONAL SYNAPTIC NEUROANATO
NEU 466G.description: Neuroanatomy and function of synapses as a basis for brain function and behavior. Examine the ultrastructure and function of synapses, dendrites and axons underlying basic synaptic transmission and plasticity. Laboratory projects involve three-dimensional reconstructions from serial section electron microscopy.

NOR 327.title: ADVANCED NORWEGIAN I
NOR 327.description: Norwegian language and culture with a focus on the skills of speaking, comprehension, reading, and writing Norwegian. Norwegian 327 and Scandinavian 369 (Topic: Advanced Norwegian Language and Culture I) may not both be counted.

NSC 107J.title: THE EFFECTIVE HLTH CARE PROF
NSC 107J.description: Introduction to careers in the health professions. Discuss the intra- and inter- personal core competencies of the professions sought in healthcare providers. Work on the critical reasoning skills needed to succeed on reading comprehension and situational judgment tests found on traditional professional school placement exams. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

NSC 107M.title: INTRO TO HLTH PROF ACTION PLAN
NSC 107M.description: Discuss the steps necessary to complete a competitive application to professional school including the generation of an effective application, personal statement, and the refinement of skills for a successful professional school interview. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

NSC 109.title: CNS FOUNDATIONS
NSC 109.description: Restrictions vary with the topic. Restricted to students in the Dean Scholars Honors Program Develop a feasible capstone experience and consult with subject matter experts. Explore substantive writing instruction to develop a proposal that describes and justifies the capstone experience. Natural Sciences 109 (Topic: Planning Your Capstone Project) and 109 (Topic 1) may not both be counted.

NSC 110H.title: NAT SCI HONORS SEMINAR
NSC 110H.description: Emphasis on student participation. Format may include student speakers, outside speakers, discussions, visits to laboratories, or other enrichment activities. Taught as a Web-based course.

NSC 120E.title: INTERNSHIP IN THE SCIENCES-WB
NSC 120E.description: Practical work experience related to the student's area of interest in Natural Sciences. Internships may be on or off campus, paid or unpaid, and may include work with nonprofit agencies, government offices, or private corporations. Students work in a professional environment and apply analysis, communication, and other academic skills to practical work. Taught as a Web-based course.

NSC 209.title: BIOLOGY SCHOLARS SEMINAR-FRI
NSC 209.description: Restrictions vary with the topic. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

NSC 321.title: INTRO PEER MENTORING & LDRSHP
NSC 321.description: Explores current theory and research related to peer mentoring and leadership within higher education. Includes interactive lectures; and the opportunity for students to identify and work through ethical choices as peer leaders; to study leadership concepts such as conflict resolution, diversity, and group dynamics; to work collaboratively; and to conduct independent research.

NSC 322.title: INTRNSHP/EXP LEARN NAT SCI-WB
NSC 322.description: Restrictions vary with the topic. Taught as a Web-based course.

NSC 325.title: HEALTH OUTCOMES/AI/ETHICS
NSC 325.description: Design, research, prototype, and test a STEM-related product or solution.

NTR 126L.title: NUTRITIONAL SCIENCES LAB
NTR 126L.description: Basic laboratory techniques in nutritional sciences.

NTR 155.title: UNDERGRAD RSCH IN NUTRITION
NTR 155.description: Supervised individual undergraduate research in nutrition.

NTR 162.title: STANDRD/ETH/CRED DIETETIC PRAC
NTR 162.description: Identification of standards and discussion of current issues in ethics and credentialing for dietetics practice.

NTR 245C.title: CLIN PRAC MEDCL NTR THERAPY I
NTR 245C.description: Application of principles of medical nutrition therapy to the care of clients in the practice setting.

NTR 306.title: FUNDAMENTALS OF NUTRITION
NTR 306.description: Essential food components and their functions in life processes. Taught as a Web-based course.

NTR 307.title: INTRODUCTORY FOOD SCIENCE
NTR 307.description: Application of the principles of food chemistry to processing and preparation techniques.

NTR 312.title: INTRO TO NUTRITIONAL SCIENCE
NTR 312.description: Biochemical, physiological, and cellular functions of energy macronutrients, vitamins and minerals, and the scientific basis for current dietary and nutrient recommendations. Designed for science majors.

NTR 315.title: NUTRITION THROUGH LIFE CYCLE
NTR 315.description: Adapting nutrition recommendations to physiological changes throughout the life span.

NTR 316.title: CULTURE AND FOOD
NTR 316.description: Influence of culture on foodways around the world.

NTR 317L.title: INTRO TO NUTRITN ASSESSMT/EDUC
NTR 317L.description: Explore the fundamentals of nutrition assessment, nutrition education, and behavior change theories as well as their application to the care of individuals and groups in community and clinical settings. Analyze the use of anthropometric, biochemical, clinical, and dietary intake data assessments for effective care of individuals. Identify strategies for successful development and implementation of nutrition education planning using behavior change theory as the guide for development.

NTR 321.title: INTERNATIONAL NTR: DEVEL WORLD
NTR 321.description: Nutrition-related issues in the developing world, including nutrient deficiency and disease, concerns in vulnerable populations (pregnancy, infancy, childhood, and old age), and food aid.

NTR 326.title: INTERMED NUTR AND METABOLISM
NTR 326.description: Integration of nutrition, genetics, cell biology, and molecular biology. Focuses on the cellular and molecular basis of nutrition-related diseases and nutrient-gene interactions.

NTR 327L.title: ADV FOOD SCIENCE LABORATORY
NTR 327L.description: Apply fundamentals of nutrition science, food science, and chemistry to scientific research in creating a novel food product for a chosen target audience. Explore food science, food research and development, fundamentals of nutrition, ingredient functionality, food product sensory evaluation, scientific inquiry, study design, and science communication. Design and execute a scientific study by engineering a unique food product, create scientific abstracts, and create and participate in scientific poster sessions.

NTR 330L.title: ADV NUTRITION ASSESSMT/COUNS
NTR 330L.description: Examine the fundamentals and application of nutrition assessment, nutrition education, counseling, and behavior change theories to the care of individuals and groups in community and clinical settings. Explore the use of nutrition focused physical exam, nutrition care process standardized language, anthropometric, biochemical, clinical, and dietary intake data assessments for effective care of individuals. Identify the implementation of nutrition counseling techniques and communication skills through the understanding of behavior change in individuals.

NTR 332.title: COMMUNITY NUTRITION
NTR 332.description: National and international issues in public health and nutrition programs.

NTR 334.title: FOODSERVICE SYSTEMS MANAGEMENT
NTR 334.description: Procurement, production, and service delivery in foodservice systems.

NTR 338W.title: ISSUES IN NUTRITION AND HEALTH
NTR 338W.description: Identifying, reading, analyzing, writing, and presenting scientific research on selected topics in nutrition and human health. Nutrition 338H and 338W may not both be counted.

NTR 339L.title: EXPER MTHDS/DSGN NUTRITNL SCI
NTR 339L.description: Explore nutritional sciences methods and research. Examine overall study design, observational and intervention studies, animal and cell-based research in nutritional sciences, nutritional research assessment methods (such as dietary assessment and biomarkers), and research ethics. Practice assessing and interpreting research findings to evaluate scientific research in nutritional sciences and nutritional epidemiology.

NTR 342.title: ADVANCED NUTRITIONAL SCIENCES
NTR 342.description: Biochemical and molecular biological aspects of carbohydrate, fat, and amino acid metabolism.

NTR 355.title: UNDERGRAD RSCH IN NUTRITION
NTR 355.description: Supervised individual undergraduate research in nutrition.

NTR 355H.title: HONORS RESEARCH
NTR 355H.description: Research in biological, biochemical, or nutritional science, coordinated with readings of scientific literature, and a written research report for each semester in which credit is sought. Nutrition 355 and 355H may not both be counted. May be repeated for credit, but no more than nine semester hours may be counted toward the major in nutrition. Any additional hours must be taken on the pass/fail basis.

NTR 365.title: NUTR BIOCHM: BUSTING MYTHS
NTR 365.description: Implement community engagement activities, such as gardening, cooking, and nutrition lessons within a school or other community environment with the goal of improving nutrition and health. Explore evidence-based nutrition programs, such as fundamental principles of food gardening with an emphasis on central Texas, application of skills in the university teaching garden and school gardens, and application of nutrition curriculum in a school setting.

NTR 366L.title: RSCH METHS IN NUTRITIONAL SCI
NTR 366L.description: Focuses on state-of-the-art research in nutrition, including biochemistry and molecular biological techniques for nutrient-gene interactions, enzyme and coenzyme functions, and nutrient analysis of biologic materials. Includes data analysis and statistical methods.

NTR 371.title: MEDICAL NUTRITION THERAPY II
NTR 371.description: Nutritional care of critically ill patients, including techniques of nutrition support.

NTR 379H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
NTR 379H.description: Supervised individual research on a special topic in nutrition; oral presentation and preparation of a scholarly paper covering the research. May be based on laboratory, library, or field research. May be taken twice for credit.

O M 235.title: OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
O M 235.description: The operations or production function and the skills required for analyzing and solving related problems. Taught as a Web-based course.

O M 334M.title: HEALTHCARE OPERATIONS MGMT
O M 334M.description: Overview of healthcare operations management. Focus on process diagramming, vocabulary, and analysis of effects of variability on healthcare operations in terms of increasing waiting lines, inventory, and patient safety issues. Focus on modern process improvement methodologies.

O M 338.title: SUPPLY CHAIN MODLNG/OPTIMIZ
O M 338.description: Framing, formulating, and applying quantitative optimization and descriptive models to support supply chain and operations management decisions, using spreadsheets and other software. Requires familiarity with spreadsheets.

O M 360.title: PROCUREMENT/SUPPLIER MGMT
O M 360.description: Explore strategic issues in procurement and supplier management; a review of competitive analysis and benchmarking; the purchasing role in fulfilling a firm's operational and competitive strategies; supplier evaluation, development, and relationship management; negotiating with suppliers for results; and commodity planning.

O M 366P.title: OPERATIONS MGMT PRACTICUM
O M 366P.description: Students apply skills in their major area and focus on additional project management skills through group projects conducted in a professional setting. Students may work with a private or a public enterprise.

ORI 366.title: OPERATIONS RESEARCH MODELS
ORI 366.description: Formulation and solution-interpretation for operations research models requiring, for example, optimization, simulation, or analysis of Markov chains or queues. Applications include manufacturing design and control, routing and scheduling, plant location, inventory analysis, and management of queueing systems. Mechanical Engineering 366L and Operations Research and Industrial Engineering 366 may not both be counted.

P A 136.title: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT
P A 136.description: Practice entering the professional realm of policymaking through a series of workshops that focus on different aspects of professional development such as resume-writing, mock job interviews, drafting cover letters for jobs and internships, and professional networking.

P A 301.title: FOUNDATIONS OF PUBLIC AFFAIRS
P A 301.description: Explore key concepts and debates in the study of public policy. Review foundational historical texts, influential theoretical writings, and important case studies. Discover a range of policy issues, both domestic and international.

P A 311L.title: QUALITATIVE METHODS PUBL AFFR
P A 311L.description: Survey a variety of qualitative empirical methods, drawn from the fields of history, anthropology, political science, and sociology, that are essential for making and evaluating public policies and programs.

P A 311N.title: QUANTITATIVE MTHDS PUBL AFFRS
P A 311N.description: Survey a variety of quantitative empirical methods, drawn from the fields of data science and management science, that are essential for making and evaluating public policies and programs.

P A 315.title: ECONOMIC ANLY FOR PUBL AFFAIRS
P A 315.description: Use the tools of microeconomics to analyze the public policymaking role of governments. Examine rationales for policy interventions including addressing market failures, distributional goals, generating revenue, among other objectives. Explore specific policy interventions and topics relevant to contemporary public policy debates such as taxation, health, social welfare, energy and the environment, and income inequality.

P A 320.title: COMMUNICATION FOR PUBL AFFAIRS
P A 320.description: Develop strategies to engage with policy audiences outside of academic circles by practicing clear concise communication. Evaluate and practice various genres of policy communication with an emphasis on learning by doing.

P A 325.title: AI/INNOVATION/TECH POLICY
P A 325.description: Survey various topics in public affairs and policy.

P A 345.title: ARTS ORGS/COM ENGMNT/POLICY
P A 345.description: Examine various advanced topics in public affairs and policy.

P A 355.title: SKILLS/WIN CAMPAIGNS/ELECTN
P A 355.description: Explore advanced qualitative or quantitative methods to better shape and evaluate public policies.

P R 305.title: FUNDMNTLS OF PUBLIC RELATIONS
P R 305.description: Restrcited to public relations majors. An introduction to public relations history and practice.

P R 305S.title: INTEGRATED COMM FOR SPORTS
P R 305S.description: Examination of the mass media industry in relation to the field of sports. Focus on the "off-the-field" industries of advertising, marketing, and public relations that impact sports; rights fees contracts; pre-game shows; gameday production; in-stadium activities; and press coverage.

P R 309.title: INTRO TO ADV/PUBL RELS RSCH
P R 309.description: Introduction to concepts and methods of statistics, with emphasis on analyzing personal and group behaviors. Includes exploratory data analysis, correlation and regression, descriptive statistics, sampling distributions, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

P R 317.title: WRITING FOR PUBLIC RELATIONS
P R 317.description: Introduction to writing skills for the media, including readability, clarity, verification, and style.

P R 320.title: INTEG COMM FOR NONPROF ORGS
P R 320.description: Explores integrated communication and branding in a variety of nonprofit settings. Examines the strategic, integrated use of multiple communication approaches, including advertising, public relations, new media, promotions, sponsorships, and special events.

P R 323.title: PUBLIC COMM OF SCIENCE/TECH-WB
P R 323.description: Explores the rapidly growing field of science communication. Examines the intersection of science, media, and society, focusing on media's impact on how people come to understand, perceive, and act relative to scientific issues. Special attention will be directed toward the strategic communication of science and maximizing scientists' engagement with public audiences. Taught as a Web-based course.

P R 332.title: TECHNOLOGY MARKETING/ADV-WB
P R 332.description: Explores case histories, interviews with leading technology advertising and marketing executives, and latest trends in digital advertising, both mobile and social, as well as its impact across marketing segments and industries. Taught as a Web-based course.

P R 332C.title: NEW MEDIA ENTREPRNRLSM-WB
P R 332C.description: Covers real life experience/exposure with a series of interviews with real-life practitioners from social media, venture capital, and others with expertise in the field. Explores important trends in digital content and digital business models; a primer for those who wish to learn about entrepreneurialism, technology, and it's intersection with media. Taught as a Web-based course.

P R 332D.title: ENTREPRENEURIALISM IN COMM
P R 332D.description: Project-oriented exercise where small teams conceptualize a business idea in communications, curate the insights and opinions of fellow team members, and ultimately develop a business plan around their new endeavor. A panel of local entrepreneurs will provide feedback and critique. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

P R 339K.title: DIGITAL GRAPHIC COMM
P R 339K.description: Introduction to the fundamentals of good graphic design, as well as production basics. Develops digital design skills using industry-standard software, from print (Adobe InDesign, Photoshop, Illustrator) to motion (Adobe Premiere, AfterEffects) and interactive (HTML, CSS, WordPress). Assumes no prior knowledge. Taught as a Web-based course.

P R 339L.title: BRAND STORYTELLING
P R 339L.description: Examination of how classic elements of storytelling, semiotics, and symbolism can be used to create and manage a modern brand's story and 'meaning'. Create visual, verbal, and video examples of brand storytelling that go beyond traditional advertising and public relations.

P R 348.title: PUBLIC RELATIONS TECHNIQUES
P R 348.description: Analysis and production of print, electronic, and oral messages to achieve organizational objectives; fundamentals of media relations; Internet applications. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

P R 348S.title: THE BUSINESS OF SPORTS MEDIA
P R 348S.description: Examination of the business of sports-related media, including over-the-air television, cable, satellite television, print, radio, social media, and internet-based media, and the impact of such media on sports presentation and development, professional franchises, colleges, and conferences. Emphasis on how the business of sports media generates revenue and content, and the business models for each type of media, the challenges to such models, and the likely future changes to them.

P R 350.title: PUBLIC RELATIONS INTERNSHIP
P R 350.description: Internship to be arranged by student and approved by instructor.

P R 352.title: STRATEGIES IN PUBLIC RELATIONS
P R 352.description: Strategies relating to public relations disciplines, including the management of external, internal, community, nonprofit, and media issues, and public relations marketing programs.

P R 353.title: ADV/PUBLIC RELS LAW/ETHICS
P R 353.description: Advertising 353 and Public Relations 353 may not both be counted.

P R 360.title: BUSNS FOUNDTNS FOR PUBL REL
P R 360.description: Examine how communication professionals gain fluency in the language and issues of corporate/nonprofit organizations, discover how to build trust with C-suite leaders, and learn how to become strategic communications counselors for leadership.

P R 361.title: CREATIVITY IN AI-WB
P R 361.description: Explore the intersection of artificial intelligence (AI) and creative processes. Examine how AI can enhance and augment human creativity in various domains such as art, music, writing, and design. Discuss the latest AI technologies and techniques used to generate innovative content. Analyze the ethical implications and societal impacts of AI-driven creativity. Learn the knowledge and skills to harness the power of AI to push the boundaries of human creativity. Taught as a Web-based course.

P R 367.title: INTEGRATED COMMUNICATNS MGMT
P R 367.description: Public relations as a managerial problem-solving process; strategic management of programs to enhance public-organizational relationships. Advertising 370J and Public Relations 367 may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

P R 377K.title: INTEGRATED COMM CAMPAIGNS
P R 377K.description: Integration of theory, research methods, and communication techniques for planning, implementing, and evaluating public relations campaigns; client proposal writing and presentation.

P R 378.title: ENTERTAINMENT BRAND COMM
P R 378.description: Engage with issues of diversity, political activism, public relations, and marketing communication through the lens of popular music. Taught as a Web-based course.

P R 378S.title: WIDE WORLD OF SPORTS BUSN
P R 378S.description: Topics in sports media related to issues in traditional and new media as they affect sports and entertainment advertising and public relations. An examination of the history of college sports media, with an emphasis on the evolution of sports coverage from NCAA-controlled limited broadcasting of football and basketball,to present day intercollegiate athletics coverage available via multiple outlets. Advertising 378S and Public Relations 378S may not both be counted unless the topics vary.

P S 303.title: INTRO PHYS SCI I: MECH & HEAT
P S 303.description: Inquiry laboratory approach to basic concepts of measurement, forces, motion, energy, temperature, and heat. Designed for students with minimum prior preparation in mathematics and physics. Especially appropriate for prospective elementary school teachers.

P S 304.title: INTRO PHYS SCI II: EL/LIGHT/OP
P S 304.description: Inquiry laboratory approach to electricity, magnetism, waves, light, and optical instruments.

PBH 137.title: INDEPENDENT STUDY
PBH 137.description: Recent developments and research methods in the field of public health will be explored. Discuss public health importance, learn how to demonstrate scholarly and applied experience in public health, and prepare to enter the public health workforce or continue in advanced public health or other professional programs.

PBH 317.title: INTRODUCTION TO PUBLIC HEALTH
PBH 317.description: Overview and basic principles of public health, including the public health system, concepts and tools for measuring health in populations, the relationship between public health and the medical care system, and the role of law and government in public health. Biology 317 and Public Health 317 may not both be counted.

PBH 334.title: GLOBAL HEALTH
PBH 334.description: An investigation of global health issues, including the principles of global health, the burden of morbidity and mortality, health determinants, health care and public health systems, socioeconomic development, and human rights. Biology 334 and Public Health 334 may not both be counted.

PBH 337.title: COMP SEX EDU IN GLBL SETTINGS
PBH 337.description: Recent developments and research methods in the field of public health will be explored.

PBH 338.title: ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH
PBH 338.description: Introduction to the major areas of environmental health presented in the context of epidemiology, toxicology, and health effects. Subjects include water and air quality, solid and liquid waste, hazardous chemicals, radiation, infectious agents, food safety, and occupational health.

PBH 341R.title: PUBLIC HEALTH RESEARCH
PBH 341R.description: Conduct research mentored by public health practitioners at governmental agencies, non-governmental organizations, or health care facilities.

PBH 354.title: EPIDEMIOLOGY I
PBH 354.description: Introduction to basic principles and concepts in epidemiology, including descriptive epidemiology, association and causation, basic epidemiological study design, evidence-based decision analysis, and applications of epidemiology methods to basic and clinical science.

PBH 356.title: HLTH BEHAVIOR THRY/PRACTICE
PBH 356.description: An introduction to social and behavioral theories that inform the discipline of public health and applied public health practice, including practical examples of how these theories inform and influence health promotion to understand and modify health-related behaviors. Focus on health inequities and inequalities of populations living in the United States and how underlying cultures and social structures impact the health of individuals and communities.

PBH 358D.title: HEALTH POLICY/HEALTH SYSTEMS
PBH 358D.description: Explore an overview of the U.S. health care system and the impact of policy and legislative actions on the essentials of organization, financing, management, and delivery of private and public health care.

PBH 363.title: PROJECT MGMNT, PLAN, AND EVAL
PBH 363.description: Investigate the importance of and the key approaches used to lead, conduct and evaluate effective public health programs. Examine practical management and program evaluation tools and techniques such budgeting, proposal/application development and use of logic models. Apply these concepts and tools within the context of service delivery and policy making in the field of public health. Recommended for students who are interested in the Certified Associate in Project Management (CAPM) exam from the Project Management Institute.

PBH 364.title: FIELD EPIDEMIOLOGY
PBH 364.description: Explore an overview and basic principles of field epidemiology including initiating operations, collecting and managing data, analyzing and interpreting data, developing interventions, and communicating during investigations

PBH 365.title: PUBLIC HEALTH INFORMATICS
PBH 365.description: Examine fundamental concepts in the application of informatics and information technology to the practice of public health. Investigate how interoperable information systems are used to facilitate a variety of public health functions, including outbreak management, bio-surveillance, disease prevention, and electronic laboratory reporting.

PBH 366.title: GLOBAL HEALTH IN ACTION
PBH 366.description: Interact with non-profit organizations to improve social determinants of health in communities globally. Participate in activities including needs assessments; Internal Review Board (IRB) applications; data collection, management, analysis, and visualization; report preparation; and resource identification as well as gain cultural understanding of the community. Public Health 337 (Topic: Global Health in Action) and 366 may not both be counted.

PBH 368.title: MATERNAL/CHILD/FAMILY HEALTH
PBH 368.description: Explore an overview of maternal, child, and family related services in the US and the historical and current paradigms in the field with a focus on the life course perspective, health equity, and social determinants of health.

PBH 379H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
PBH 379H.description: Supervised individual research on a special topic in public health, leading to an honors thesis and an oral presentation. May be based on laboratory, library, or field research. May be repeated once for credit.

PED 101J.title: 1-BEGINNING SWIMMING I
PED 101J.description: For nonswimmers. Elementary physical and mental adjustments, four basic strokes, water safety.

PED 103L.title: 1-BEGIN SOCIAL DANCE: LEADS
PED 103L.description: Introduction to popular social dances, including swing, waltz, bachata, nightclub two-step, cross-step waltz, salsa, west coast swing, and tango. Emphasis on connecting with a dance partner and the art of leading and following. Must register for the role you want to learn: lead or follow.

PED 105R.title: 1-BEGINNING KARATE/TAE KWON DO
PED 105R.description: Includes self-defense. Explore beginner and intermediate hand strikes, kicks, blocks, and fighting combinations. Engage in strikes on pads and learn practical self-defense techniques. Examine light sparring, form training, conditioning, respect, and discipline.

PED 105U.title: BEGIN HAPKIDO/SELF-DEFENSE
PED 105U.description: Physical Education 105T (Topic: Beginning Hapkido/Self-Defense) and 105U may not both be counted.

PED 106C.title: SOCCER
PED 106C.description: Explore the principles and benefits of mind-body fitness through the lens of yoga and examine the relationship between exercise, health, and wellness. Discover a yogic approach to cardio and strength training exercises through hatha and vinyasa styles of yoga, as well as mental focus and breathing techniques. Explore aerobic capacity, muscular endurance, muscular strength, mental focus, and overall flexibility. Physical Education 106C (Topic 9) and 106C (Topic 12) may not both be counted.

PED 108C.title: 4-BASKETBALL: ALL LEVEL
PED 108C.description: Physical Education 108C (Topic: Basketball: All Level) and 108C (Topic 4) may not both be counted.

PED 109D.title: 4-SAND VOLLEYBALL
PED 109D.description: Explore basic motor skills and apply systems and skills of outdoor sand volleyball. Examine physical activity, service learning, cardiovascular health and fitness, and overall well-being. Physical Education 106C (Topic: Sand Volleyball) and 109D (Topic 4) may not both be counted.

PGE 311.title: NUMERICAL METHODS/PROGRAMMING
PGE 311.description: Introduction to computer programming and mathematical equations typically encountered in petroleum and geosystems engineering; methods to visualize data and solve engineering and mathematical equations with numerical methods; and applications of computers to general problem solving. Petroleum and Geosystems Engineering 310 and 311 may not both be counted.

PGE 322K.title: TRANSPORT PHENOM IN GEOSYSTEMS
PGE 322K.description: Applications of mass, heat, and momentum balances to fluid flow problems; shell balances; non-Newtonian fluids; transport processes through permeable media.

PGE 323K.title: RESERVOIR ENGR I: PRIMARY RECV
PGE 323K.description: Classification of subsurface reservoirs by type and recovery mechanism; reserve estimates based on material balance; steady-state and transient fluid flow in permeable reservoir rocks as applied to subsurface engineering problems.

PGE 323M.title: RESERVOIR ENGINEERING III
PGE 323M.description: Mathematical equations governing fluid flow in reservoirs; numerical methods to solve the equations; numerical reservoir simulation; treatment of wells; history matching; a simulation project performed using a commercial simulator.

PGE 326.title: THERMODYNAMIC/PHASE BEHAVIOR
PGE 326.description: Basics of phase behavior, classical thermodynamics in terms of material and energy balances, and applications to changes of state of petroleum fluids.

PGE 333T.title: ENGINEERING COMMUNICATION
PGE 333T.description: Advanced technical communication skills, with emphasis on writing strategies for technical documents, oral presentations, and visual aids.

PGE 334.title: RESERVOIR GEOMECHANICS
PGE 334.description: Basic stress and strain analysis; pore pressure and in situ stress estimation and measurement; deformation mechanisms in rock; rock fracture description and analysis; wellbore stresses and failure; wellbore stability analysis; fault stability analysis; depletion-induced reservoir deformation; and hydraulic fracturing. Emphasis on applications to petroleum engineering.

PGE 338.title: GEOSTATISTICS/DATA ANALYSIS
PGE 338.description: Explore basic frequentist and Bayesian probability and statistics, confidence and significance models, multivariate and spatial correlated features, feature engineering, spatial debiasing and estimation, stochastic simulation, uncertainty modeling, basic machine learning, and optimum decision making in the presence of uncertainty. Examine applications to spatial problems such as geology and subsurface resource development. Petroleum and Geosystems Engineering 337 and 338 may not both be counted.

PGE 358.title: PRINCIPLES FRMTN EVALUATION
PGE 358.description: Integrated petrophysical interpretation of well logs, core laboratory measurements, and geological data for static, dynamic, and geophysical appraisal of subsurface rock formations. Petroleum and Geosystems Engineering 358 and 368 may not both be counted.

PGE 365.title: RESOURCE ECONOMICS & VALUATION
PGE 365.description: Derivation of profitability criteria for earth resource investments, project analysis in terms of the interrelation of technical and economic factors, investment analysis in the presence of uncertainty, and project planning.

PGE 373L.title: GEOSYS ENGR DESIGN AND ANLY
PGE 373L.description: Team-oriented design projects involving the application of geologic and engineering methods to the solution of subsurface problems, using field case histories. Projects are selected for each student based on his or her petroleum engineering technical area option.

PGE 376.title: SPEC PROB IN PETRO/GEOSYS ENGR
PGE 376.description: Independent investigation of an advanced subject in petroleum and geosystems engineering, for superior students only.

PGE 379.title: NUCLEAR WASTE STORAGE
PGE 379.description: Special courses or seminars on recent developments in engineering. Review technologies that help us monitor the pulse of the planet. Learn about systems that directly measure carbon dioxide and methane emissions from both natural sources like wetlands and permafrost and human-made sources like agriculture and fossil fuels. Study how modern technologies - drones, satellites, aerial systems - are revolutionizing the way we measure carbon emission from energy production and use. Petroleum and Geosystems Engineering 379 (Topic: Global Carbon Monitoring Sys) and 379 (Topic 22) may not both be counted.

PGE 424.title: PETROPHYSICS
PGE 424.description: Properties of rocks; measurement and interpretation of petrophysical properties; application of petrophysics to subsurface engineering problems; interaction of resident fluids with rocks. Extensive written reporting.

PGE 427.title: PROPERTIES OF PETROLEUM FLUIDS
PGE 427.description: Principles of organic chemistry, phase behavior of multicomponent mixtures, properties of hydrocarbon gases and liquids and oil field waters, overview of laboratory phase behavior measurements, and material balance calculations. Petroleum and Geosystems Engineering 312 and 427 may not both be counted.

PGE 430.title: DRILLING AND WELL COMPLETIONS
PGE 430.description: Elements of rock mechanics, drilling fluids, factors affecting rate of penetration, and well completions, including casing and tubing design.

PGE 679HA.title: UNDERGRADUATE HONORS THESIS
PGE 679HA.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of an engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Engineering Honors Program. The student makes an oral presentation and writes a thesis. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and a bachelor's degree in engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirement for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

PGE 679HB.title: UNDERGRADUATE HONORS THESIS
PGE 679HB.description: Research performed during two consecutive semesters under the supervision of an engineering faculty member; topics are selected jointly by the student and the faculty member with approval by the director of the Engineering Honors Program. The student makes an oral presentation and writes a thesis. Students pursuing both the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II, and a bachelor's degree in engineering may use this course to fulfill the thesis requirement for the Bachelor of Arts, Plan II.

PHL 301.title: INTRODUCTION TO PHILOSOPHY
PHL 301.description: Primarily for lower-division students. A survey of principal topics and problems in areas such as ethics, theory of knowledge, and philosophy of religion. Philosophy 301 and 610QA may not both be counted.

PHL 301K.title: ANCIENT PHILOSOPHY
PHL 301K.description: Primarily for lower-division students. An introduction to the philosophical achievements of the ancient world, concentrating on Plato and Aristotle.

PHL 301L.title: EARLY MODERN PHILOSOPHY
PHL 301L.description: Primarily for lower-division students. An introduction to the philosophical achievements of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, concentrating on such figures as Descartes, Hume, and Kant.

PHL 302.title: WORLD PHILOSOPHY
PHL 302.description: Primarily for lower-division students. Basic issues of philosophy in Western and non-Western traditions, such as the nature of philosophy, its relation to religion and science, the self, knowledge, and virtue.

PHL 303.title: HUMAN NATURE
PHL 303.description: Primarily for lower-division students. Theories of human nature, such as those of Plato, Christianity, Marxism, and existentialism. Modern psychological and biological theories are included, as the interplay of nature and nurture in determining human conduct is explored.

PHL 303M.title: MIND AND BODY
PHL 303M.description: Primarily for lower-division students. Introduction to philosophical issues about the nature of mind and its relation to body: What is mind? Do people have free will? How does psychology relate to neuroscience?

PHL 304.title: CONTEMPORARY MORAL PROBLEMS
PHL 304.description: Primarily for lower-division students. Philosophical examination of selected moral problems arising out of contemporary society and culture.

PHL 304T.title: INTRO TO TECHNOLOGY ETHICS
PHL 304T.description: Examine ethical challenges that have been introduced or exacerbated by recent technological advancements.

PHL 305.title: INTRO TO PHILOS OF RELIGION
PHL 305.description: A critical examination of various conceptions of God and of the relationship of the human and the divine.

PHL 310.title: KNOWLEDGE AND REALITY
PHL 310.description: An introduction to basic issues in epistemology and metaphysics. Philosophy 310 and 610QA may not both be counted.

PHL 311K.title: GAMES AND DECISIONS
PHL 311K.description: Introduction to the theories of games and rational decision, with applications to ethical, social, and political issues.

PHL 313.title: INTRODUCTORY SYMBOLIC LOGIC
PHL 313.description: Introduction to symbolic logic (through first-order predicate logic); interpretations; formal proofs, consistency; some practical applications.

PHL 313Q.title: LOGIC AND SCIENTIFIC REASONING
PHL 313Q.description: Introduction to formal proofs, semantics, quantifiers, inductive methods, decision theory, and scientific reasoning.

PHL 317K.title: INTRO TO PHILOS OF THE ARTS
PHL 317K.description: Classic issues in the philosophy of art and beauty, illustrated from the fine arts and contemporary media: literature, drama, music, painting, film, and television.

PHL 318.title: INTRODUCTION TO ETHICS
PHL 318.description: Study of basic principles of the moral life, with critical examination of traditional and contemporary theories of the nature of goodness, happiness, duty, and freedom. Philosophy 610QB and 318 may not both be counted.

PHL 318K.title: INTRO TO POLITICAL PHILOSOPHY
PHL 318K.description: Views of major political philosophers on humanity, nature, and society; discussions of contemporary political ideologies.

PHL 322K.title: HISTORY OF ETHICS
PHL 322K.description: Survey of ethical theories from ancient times through the nineteenth century.

PHL 323K.title: METAPHYSICS
PHL 323K.description: Problems of substance, change, categories of being, mind, body, space and time, approached either systematically or historically.

PHL 323S.title: PHILOSOPHY OF SCIENCE
PHL 323S.description: Examine philosophical issues pertaining to science, with an emphasis on the metaphysics and epistemology of science. Philosophy 323S and Philosophy 363L (Topic: Philosophy of Science) may not both be counted.

PHL 325D.title: ENVIRONMNTL ETHICS/PHILOSOPHY
PHL 325D.description: Examine moral issues concerning the relation of human beings to the environment, including biodiversity, resource depletion, and animal rights. Philosophy 325C and 325D may not both be counted.

PHL 325E.title: BIOMEDICAL ETHICS
PHL 325E.description: Apply ethics to problems of medical practice and theory including abortion, euthanasia, sterilization, psychosurgery, genetic engineering, and concepts of health, cure, insanity, and death. Philosophy 325E and 325M may not both be counted.

PHL 325K.title: ETHICAL THEORIES
PHL 325K.description: Major traditional and contemporary ethical theories discussed and critically examined.

PHL 329E.title: ARISTOTLE
PHL 329E.description: Study the philosophy of Aristotle through the examination of key texts. Classical Civilization 348O and Philosophy 329E may not both be counted.

PHL 329K.title: HIST OF ANCIENT PHILOSOPHY
PHL 329K.description: Development of Western philosophy from the pre-Socratics to the early Christian era; emphasis on Plato and Aristotle. Classical Civilization 348 (Topic 4) and Philosophy 329K may not both be counted.

PHL 332.title: PHILOSOPHY OF LANGUAGE
PHL 332.description: Contemporary theories of meaning and linguistic structure, and their relationships to epistemology, metaphysics, and ethics.

PHL 334F.title: FORENSIC FILES: PHILOS CRIMES
PHL 334F.description: Explore political philosophies from the continental tradition (Heidegger/Arendt, Nietzsche, Adorno/Horkheimer, and Agamben/Schmitt) implicated in radical politics and answering to political turning points, with a focus on research and reading in historical contexts.

PHL 342M.title: MARX AND MARXIST THEORY
PHL 342M.description: Introduction to the writings of Karl Marx as well as to those of his intellectual successors in Europe and around the globe in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

PHL 342R.title: PHILOSOPHY OF RACE/GENDER
PHL 342R.description: Examine philosophical issues pertaining to race and gender. Philosophy 327 (Topic: Philosophy of Race and Gender) and Philosophy 342R may not both be counted.

PHL 344M.title: PHILOSOPHY OF MATHEMATICS
PHL 344M.description: Philosophical issues concerning mathematics and its foundations, such as the correlation of mathematics to logic, mathematical truth, and mathematical knowledge.

PHL 347.title: PHILOSOPHY OF LAW
PHL 347.description: The significance and function of law in political and ethical contexts; comparison of common and statutory to scientific and moral law; readings from among Plato, Kant, Hegel, Bentham, Austin, Hart, Dworkin, Feinberg, and others.

PHL 356E.title: BUDDHIST THOUGHT AND PRACTICE
PHL 356E.description: Explore important themes in Buddhist thought, various ways in which Buddhists practice their religion, and the relationships between religious concepts and practices.

PHL 366K.title: EXISTENTIALISM
PHL 366K.description: Existentialism and its relationship to literature, psychoanalysis, and Marxism.

PHL 375M.title: 3-WITTGENSTEIN
PHL 375M.description: Examine the later philosophy of Ludwig Wittgenstein in the context of the development of analytic philosophy in the early twentieth century. Philosophy 375M (Topic: Wittgenstein) and 375M (Topic 3) may not both be counted.

PHL 379K.title: CONFERENCE COURSE
PHL 379K.description: Intensive tutorial study of selected problems in philosophy.

PHL 610QA.title: PROBS OF KNOWLEDGE & VALUATION
PHL 610QA.description: Methods and aims of selected sciences, arts, and philosophy in the attainment of knowledge and in providing the basis for valuation. Philosophy 301 and 610QA may not both be counted; Philosophy 310 and 610QA may not both be counted; Philosophy 610QB and 318 may not both be counted.

PHL 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
PHL 679HA.description: Supervised individual reading for one semester, followed by research and writing to produce a substantial paper on a special topic in philosophy, to be completed during the second semester.

PHL 679HB.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
PHL 679HB.description: Supervised individual reading for one semester, followed by research and writing to produce a substantial paper on a special topic in philosophy, to be completed during the second semester.

PHM 160J.title: BASIC STD PHRMCEUT RSCH NONMAJ
PHM 160J.description: Original investigation in any area of the pharmaceutical sciences.

PHM 260J.title: BASIC STD PHRMCEUT RSCH NONMAJ
PHM 260J.description: Original investigation in any area of the pharmaceutical sciences.

PHM 338.title: INTRODUCTION TO PHARMACOLOGY
PHM 338.description: Survey of basic concepts and principles in pharmacology. Pharmacy 338 and Pharmacy PharmD 338 may not both be counted.

PHM 360J.title: BASIC STD PHRMCEUT RSCH NONMAJ
PHM 360J.description: Original investigation in any area of the pharmaceutical sciences.

PHY 105M.title: LAB FOR PHY 302K/303K/317K
PHY 105M.description: Participate in an introductory mechanics laboratory.

PHY 105N.title: LAB FOR PHY 302L/303L/317L
PHY 105N.description: Participate in an introductory electricity, magnetism, and optics laboratory.

PHY 110C.title: SCIENCE OF THE TIMES
PHY 110C.description: Supervised study of selected topics in physics, by individual arrangement with department and instructor.

PHY 115L.title: LABORATORY FOR PHY 315
PHY 115L.description: May not be counted toward a degree unless prerequisite is observed.

PHY 208F.title: INTRODUCTION TO RESEARCH-FRI
PHY 208F.description: Introductory laboratory experience; use of tools and test equipment; beginning apprenticeship in active physics research. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

PHY 239C.title: PHYSICS COGNITION AND PEDAGOGY
PHY 239C.description: Investigate physics-specific education literature, and think about how the results of these studies apply to teaching practice. Discuss obstacles to students' academic success such as stereotype threat, identity, implicit bias, and mindset. Reflect on how to make a difference in students' learning as a physics learning assistant.

PHY 301.title: MECHANICS
PHY 301.description: Designed for students who intend to major in science or mathematics. Mandatory exams 6pm-9pm Mondays, September 21, October 19, and November 16.

PHY 302K.title: GEN PHY TCH CRS: MECH/HEAT/SND
PHY 302K.description: Noncalculus technical course in physics. Completion of high school trigonometry or Mathematics 305G is highly recommended. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Mandatory exams 6pm-9pm Mondays, September 21, October 19, and November 16.

PHY 302L.title: GEN PHY TCH CRS: ELEC/LGHT/NUC
PHY 302L.description: Noncalculus technical course in physics. Mandatory exams 6pm-9pm Mondays, September 21, October 19, and November 16.

PHY 303E.title: ELECMAG/QUANTUM/SEMICOND PHYS
PHY 303E.description: Discuss electricity, magnetism, electromagnetic waves, photons, matter waves, atomic physics, band structure, semiconductors, diodes, and transistors. Mandatory exams 6pm-9pm Mondays, September 21, October 19, and November 16.

PHY 303K.title: ENGINEERING PHYSICS I
PHY 303K.description: A general survey of physics; primarily laws of motion, heat, and wave phenomena. Mandatory exams 6pm-9pm Mondays, September 21, October 19, and November 16.

PHY 303L.title: ENGINEERING PHYSICS II
PHY 303L.description: Electricity and magnetism, optics, and atomic phenomena. Mandatory exams 6pm-9pm Mondays, September 21, October 19, and November 16.

PHY 308F.title: INTRODUCTION TO RESEARCH-FRI
PHY 308F.description: Introductory laboratory experience; use of tools and test equipment; beginning apprenticeship in active physics research.

PHY 309K.title: ELEM PHY FOR NONTECH STUDENTS
PHY 309K.description: Mechanics, heat, and sound. Designed for students who do not intend to do further work in natural sciences, engineering, mathematics, or medicine.

PHY 315.title: WAVE MOTION AND OPTICS
