PHY 315.description: Study of general properties of waves; examples include sound, electromagnetic, and mechanical waves; special emphasis on light and optics. Mandatory exams 6pm-9pm Mondays, September 21, October 19, and November 16.

PHY 316.title: ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETISM
PHY 316.description: Mandatory exams 7pm-9pm Mondays, September 21, October 19, and November 16.

PHY 317K.title: GENERAL PHYSICS I
PHY 317K.description: Mechanics, heat, and sound, with biomedical applications. An introductory course designed and recommended primarily for premedical students and others in the biomedical sciences whose professional or preprofessional training includes an introductory course in calculus. Satisfies most medical and dental school requirements for physics. Mandatory exams 7pm-10pm Mondays, September 17, October 15, and November 12.

PHY 317L.title: GENERAL PHYSICS II
PHY 317L.description: Electricity and magnetism, light, atomic and molecular physics, nuclear physics, and their biomedical applications. Designed and recommended primarily for premedical students and others in the biomedical sciences whose professional or preprofessional training includes an introductory course in calculus. Satisfies most medical and dental school requirements for physics. Mandatory exams 6pm-9pm Mondays, September 21, October 19, and November 16.

PHY 329.title: INTRO TO COMPUTATIONAL PHYSICS
PHY 329.description: Computational methods for problem solving and research in physics; numerical analysis and computer simulation methods for physics applications using different types of computers.

PHY 336K.title: CLASSICAL DYNAMICS
PHY 336K.description: Elementary linear vector algebra, Newtonian mechanics, Lagrangian mechanics, central force motion, dynamics of rigid bodies, and theory of small oscillations.

PHY 338K.title: ELECTRONIC TECHNIQUES
PHY 338K.description: Elementary circuit theory, amplifiers, feedback, pulse and digital techniques, signal processing, and microprocessors as applied to physics instrumentation.

PHY 341.title: INTRO TO QUANTUM INFO SCI: HON
PHY 341.description: Study of a variety of ideas treated very seriously by the communications media but having no basis in fact, including astrology, extrasensory perception, and flying saucers; why such areas are not part of science. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Science in Physics degree without prior approval of the department.

PHY 352K.title: CLASSICAL ELECTRODYNAMICS I
PHY 352K.description: Electrostatic fields, magnetostatic fields, derivation of Green's theorems and functions and of Maxwell's equations.

PHY 352L.title: CLASSICAL ELECTRODYNAMICS II
PHY 352L.description: A continuation of Physics 352K, emphasis is placed on conservation laws, electromagnetic waves, potentials and fields, radiation, and Lorentz invariance. One or more special subjects, such as Rayleigh scattering and energy loss by relativistic charged particles in matter will be developed.

PHY 353L.title: MODERN PHYSICS LABORATORY
PHY 353L.description: Laboratory experiments investigating the breakdown of classical physics for microscopic phenomena. Includes absorption and emission spectra, the photoelectric effect, blackbody radiation, the Compton effect, X-ray diffraction, and other experiments in modern physics.

PHY 355.title: MODERN PHYSICS & THERMODYNAMS
PHY 355.description: Introduction to modern physics and thermodynamics: photons (spectra, photoelectric effect, blackbody radiation, Compton effect), atoms (Rutherford, Bohr), matter waves (Planck, deBroglie, probability interpretation, Schroedinger), nuclei, particles, special relativity, the laws of thermodynamics, and statistical physics.

PHY 362K.title: QUANT PHYS II: ATOMS/MOLECULES
PHY 362K.description: The two-electron atom; spin and statistics; coupling schemes for many-electron atoms; atoms and the radiation field; perturbation methods for decay and collisions; thermal, electrical, and magnetic properties of solids; and free-electron metal and band theory. May include subjects such as superconductivity, Josephson tunneling, and others.

PHY 362L.title: QUANT PHYS III: PARTCLS/NUCLEI
PHY 362L.description: Nuclei and nucleons, their gross properties; the hadrons; symmetries and conservation laws; nuclear stability; electromagnetic, weak, and hadronic interactions; nuclear reactions at low, medium, and high energies; nucleon structure; tools of experimental nuclear physics; models of theoretical nuclear physics; nuclear technology.

PHY 369.title: THERMODYNAMICS AND STAT MECH
PHY 369.description: Basic concepts of thermal physics; entropy, enthalpy, free energy, phase transitions, equilibrium distribution functions, applications.

PHY 371C.title: INDIVIDUAL STUDY IN PHYSICS
PHY 371C.description: Supervised reading or research in physics.

PHY 373.title: QUANTUM PHYSICS I: FOUNDATIONS
PHY 373.description: Postulates of quantum mechanics, the bound states of the finite square well, the harmonic oscillator, operator-eigenvalue formulism and selected examples, the hydrogen atom, angular momentum, rigid rotor, and spin. May include simple scattering theory.

PHY 375C.title: COSMLGY/ASTROPARTICLE PHYSICS
PHY 375C.description: Discuss the physics of the expanding universe, Einstein's equations, inflationary expansion, the basics of nuclear physics and big bang nucleosynthesis, and the content of the Universe: the known particles and forces of the standard model of particle physics as well as the unknown dark matter and dark energy.

PHY 375S.title: INTRO SOLID-STATE PHYSICS
PHY 375S.description: Crystal structure, classification of solids, cohesion, thermal and electrical properties of solids, magnetic properties of solids, imperfections.

PHY 379H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
PHY 379H.description: Research project, resulting in a thesis, for outstanding students electing to take the honors program in physics.

PHY 474.title: ADVANCED LABORATORY I
PHY 474.description: Modern experimental techniques, theory of error, and analysis of experiments; both modern and classical experiments in atomic and nuclear physics, electricity and magnetism, optics and heat. With consent of instructor, may be repeated for credit. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

POL 312K.title: SECOND-YEAR POLISH I
POL 312K.description: Emphasis on four-skills proficiency. Polish 611C and 312K may not both be counted.

POL 325.title: THIRD-YEAR POLISH I
POL 325.description: Oral expression, reading, and composition. Polish 325 and Russian, East European, and Eurasian Studies 325 (Topic: Third-Year Polish I) may not both be counted.

POL 506.title: FIRST-YEAR POLISH I
POL 506.description: Emphasis on four-skills proficiency. Polish 601C and 506 may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

POR 311C.title: PORTUGUESE CONVERSATION/CULTR
POR 311C.description: Explore the development of speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills, and build vocabulary. Review rules and terminology of Portuguese grammar. Examine complex grammar structures and Portuguese speaking cultures, with a focus on Brazil. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

POR 314C.title: INTERMED WRIT/GRAMMAR IN CTXT
POR 314C.description: Review rules and terminology of Portuguese grammar. Explore complex grammar structures in the context of reading and writing. Build vocabulary in the context of Portuguese speaking cultures, with a focus on Brazil. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

POR 328C.title: INTRO TO LIT/CUL
POR 328C.description: Overview of Luso-Brazilian literatures and cultures, including the arts and popular expressions from a multidisciplinary perspective. Among the regions studied are Brazil, Portugal, and related areas in Africa. Latin American Studies 370P (Topic 2) and Portuguese 328C may not both be counted.

POR 601D.title: FIRST-YEAR PORTUGUESE I
POR 601D.description: Development of speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills; building vocabulary, learning basic rules and terminology of grammar; and gaining a better understanding of Portuguese-speaking cultures, with focus on Brazil, in order to communicate in an accurate, effective, and informed manner within a variety of socio-cultural situations.

POR 610D.title: FIRST-YEAR PORTUGUESE II
POR 610D.description: Development of speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills; building vocabulary, learning basic rules and terminology of grammar; and gaining a better understanding of Portuguese-speaking cultures, with focus on Brazil, in order to communicate in an accurate, effective, and informed manner within a variety of socio-cultural situations.

POR 610S.title: PORTUGUESE FOR SPANISH SPKR I
POR 610S.description: Designed to provide qualified Spanish-speaking students a rapid introduction to Portuguese language. Development of speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills; building vocabulary, learning basic rules and terminology of grammar; and gaining a better understanding of Portuguese-speaking cultures, with a focus on Brazil, in order to communicate in an accurate, effective, and informed manner within a variety of socio-cultural situations.

PPE 301.title: INTRO TO PHILOS/POLITICS/ECON
PPE 301.description: Cultivate an awareness of the relevance of ethics, politics, and economics, as well as an appreciation for the limitations of each discipline and the necessity of thinking through their interactions.

PPE 379.title: CAPSTONE COURSE
PPE 379.description: Practice researching and prepare to share findings for public discourse related to philosophy, politics, and economics.

PRC 320E.title: CULTURE/MEDIA/TECH IN BRAZIL
PRC 320E.description: Focus on issues in Brazilian society and Portuguese society and culture.

PRS 322K.title: INTERMEDIATE PERSIAN I
PRS 322K.description: Not open to native speakers of Persian. First semester of intermediate Persian language instruction.

PRS 601C.title: INTENSIVE PERSIAN I
PRS 601C.description: Not open to native speakers of Persian. First semester of intensive Persian language instruction. Persian 601C and 506 may not both be counted. Persian 601C and 507 may not both be counted.

PSY 120R.title: R PROGRAMMING FOR BEHAV SCI-WB
PSY 120R.description: Introduction to the R programming language. Psychology 317L and 120R may not both be counted. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

PSY 301.title: INTRO TO PSYCHOLOGY: HON
PSY 301.description: Basic problems and principles of human experience and behavior. Taught online during scheduled times. Includes live-streaming video and requires real-time participation. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity

PSY 305.title: INTRO TO COGNITIVE PSYCHOLOGY
PSY 305.description: Introduction to the study of how people perceive, act, communicate, and reason.

PSY 306.title: INTRO TO HUMAN SEXUALITY-WB
PSY 306.description: Introduction to the science of sex and mating. Provides an overview of the psychological, biological, and social factors that contribute to sexual well-being and human sexual attraction. Psychology 306 and 346K may not both be counted. Taught online during scheduled times. Includes live-streaming video and requires real-time participation. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

PSY 308.title: BIOPSYCHOLOGY
PSY 308.description: Introduction to the biological bases of psychological processes and behavior. Overview of the physiology and anatomy of the nervous system, followed by a survey of brain mechanisms of perception, cognition, learning, and emotion; biological perspectives on drug action and mental disease.

PSY 309.title: PERSONALITY-WB
PSY 309.description: Research and theory concerning personality structure, dynamics, development, and assessment. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

PSY 317L.title: INTRO STATS FOR BEHAVRL SCI-WB
PSY 317L.description: Explore the R programming language, data visualization, data wrangling, descriptive statistics (measures of central tendency and variation), measures of association (correlation, regression). Examine inferential statistics including parametric tests (such as t-tests and ANOVA) and non-parametric tests (such as Kruskal-Wallis and chi-squared tests). Psychology 317L and 120R may not both be counted. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

PSY 319K.title: SOCIAL PSYCHOLOGY
PSY 319K.description: Theory and research on the analysis of human conduct in social settings.

PSY 323.title: PERCEPTION
PSY 323.description: Theory and research in the ways we extract information from the environment.

PSY 326K.title: PRINS OF CONDITION/LEARNING
PSY 326K.description: Explore how people learn emotional and motor responses without conscious intent.

PSY 332.title: BEHAVIORAL NEUROSCIENCE
PSY 332.description: Neuroscientific study of behavioral functions: fundamental structure and function of the human nervous system, sensory systems and perception, motor systems and behavior, motivation and learning, brain disorders and maladaptive behavior.

PSY 332C.title: HORMONES AND BEHAVIOR
PSY 332C.description: Neuroscientific study of hormones and behavior in animals and humans. Includes sexual behavior, sexual differentiation, parental behavior, aggressive behavior, feeding and drinking, stress, learning, and memory.

PSY 332M.title: NEUROSCI OF MOTIVATN/REWARD
PSY 332M.description: Examination of mechanisms of the brain that control behaviors such as incentive motivation, reward, aversion, and addiction.

PSY 332P.title: NEURAL PLASTICITY/BEHAVIOR
PSY 332P.description: Examine basic concepts and current research directions in brain plasticity. Includes theory and research on how animals learn and remember, how brain areas and neurons adapt to life experiences, trauma, injury, and aging and new treatment directions based on neural plasticity principles. Psychology 332P and 341K (Topic: Neural Plasticity And Behavior) may not both be counted.

PSY 333D.title: INTRO TO DEVELOPMENTAL PSYC-WB
PSY 333D.description: Physical, social, and cognitive development in humans. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity

PSY 333M.title: INFANT DEVELOPMENT
PSY 333M.description: Examine the development of motor, perceptual, cognitive, language and socio-emotional capacities during the infancy and toddler years (0-2years). Discuss theoretical foundations, empirical findings, and methodological advances related to these areas.

PSY 337.title: PSYCHOLOGY OF LANGUAGE
PSY 337.description: Consideration of approaches to the study of language, its development in children, and its functioning; important research from psychology and linguistics.

PSY 341K.title: ADVENTURES IN SOCIAL PSYCH-HON
PSY 341K.description: Topics of contemporary interest that may vary from semester to semester.

PSY 345.title: INDIVIDUAL DIFFERENCES
PSY 345.description: Study of person-to-person variation in intelligence, personality, and interests, with an emphasis on genetic and environmental determinants, developmental processes, and their relation to real-world outcomes.

PSY 352P.title: PSYCHOPATHOLOGY-WB
PSY 352P.description: Explore biological and social factors in the development and treatment of psychopathology. Psychology 352 and 352P may not both be counted. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

PSY 353K.title: PSYCHOPHARMACOLOGY
PSY 353K.description: The pharmacology and the neurochemical, neurophysiological, and psychological effects of psychoactive drugs, with regard to their use as therapeutic and behavioral research tools.

PSY 355N.title: COGNITIVE NEUROSCIENCE
PSY 355N.description: Exploration of the link between the brain, mind, and behavior and cognitive neuroscience techniques.

PSY 357.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
PSY 357.description: Supervised research experience. May not be counted toward a major in psychology.

PSY 357R.title: ADV UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
PSY 357R.description: Deepen supervised research experience in psychology. Implement advanced laboratory experience and skills within a leadership role in collaboration with principal investigators and a team to assist with research projects.

PSY 364.title: INTRO TO CLINICAL PSYCH-WB
PSY 364.description: Introduction to techniques of assessment and treatment of psychopathology. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

PSY 364C.title: CHILDHOOD TRAUMA/ADVERSITY
PSY 364C.description: Focus on how childhood trauma impacts behavioral, emotional, cognitive, and biological functioning across the lifespan. Psychology 341K (Topic: Childhood Trauma/Adversity) and 364C may not both be counted.

PSY 364P.title: POSITIVE PSYCH/THE GOOD LIFE
PSY 364P.description: Analyze how to live a more satisfying life based on modern psychological science. Survey the emerging field of positive psychology, including assessment and determinants of well-being; human strengths and virtues; the good life considered from evolutionary, economic, sociological, and cross-cultural perspectives; and applications to issues such as social change.

PSY 364T.title: MULTICULTURAL PSYCHOTHERAPY-WB
PSY 364T.description: Introduction to multicultural approaches to personality assessment and counseling psychotherapy. Taught as a Web-based course. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

PSY 365D.title: BEHAV SCI/EQTY/INCLUSN-WB
PSY 365D.description: Apply the principles of experimental behavioral science to problems of creating an equitable and inclusive society in the twenty-first century. Taught as a Web-based course.

PSY 365L.title: ADVANCED SOCIAL PSYCHOLOGY
PSY 365L.description: Experimental research in social psychology, social influence, decision making, affiliation, risk taking, stress, and other subjects.

PSY 371.title: LEARNING AND MEMORY
PSY 371.description: Analysis of theory and research in learning.

PSY 371E.title: PSY DATA SCIENCE FOUNDATIONS I
PSY 371E.description: Introduction to foundational concepts and techniques in data science. Psychology 341K (Topic: Psychological Data Sci Fndns I) and 371E may not both be counted.

PSY 371F.title: PSYCH DATA SCI FOUNDATIONS II
PSY 371F.description: Explore foundational concepts and techniques in data science, including in-depth Python programming, advanced plotting techniques, curve/model fitting, computer simulations, and machine learning. Psychology 341K (Topic: Psy Data Sci Foundations II) and 371F may not both be counted.

PSY 371Q.title: ETHICS IN BEHAV DATA SCI
PSY 371Q.description: Examine and discuss important ethical issues in the context of behavioral data science in real-world case-studies such as emotion recognition, surveillance, targeted marketing, and more. Psychology 341K (Topic: Ethics/Dat Sci/Affctv Cmptng) and 371Q may not both be counted.

PSY 373N.title: NEUROBIOLOGY OF ATTENTION
PSY 373N.description: Examination of behavioral and brain mechanisms of attention in humans and other animals.

PSY 420M.title: PSYCHGCL METHODS/STATISTICS
PSY 420M.description: Explore the basic and intermediate scientific methods and statistical techniques commonly employed in psychological research. Psychology 418 and 420M may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

PSY 458.title: EXPERIMENTAL PSYCHOLOGY
PSY 458.description: Techniques of psychological research illustrated in a series of laboratory experiments.

R E 358.title: INTRO R ESTATE/URB LAND DEV
R E 358.description: An examination of the principles of real estate and urban land economics. Subjects include investment, valuation, financing, and public policy in real estate and mortgage markets.

R E 366P.title: REAL ESTATE PRACTICUM
R E 366P.description: Apply skills in real estate finance to focus on private equity opportunities conducted in a professional setting with private companies.

R E 376G.title: REAL ESTATE INVESTMENT
R E 376G.description: Study of the setting and measurement of property values in real estate markets and an analysis of real estate assets as investments.

R M 357E.title: INTRO TO RISK MANAGEMENT
R M 357E.description: Principles of risk management for individuals and organizations, financial aspects of insurance companies and markets, industry structure, managerial aspects of underwriting and pricing, and public policy issues.

R S 302.title: HISTORY OF RELIGIONS OF ASIA
R S 302.description: Eastern religions: an introduction to the basic forms and the historical development of the religious traditions of India, China, and Japan.

R S 305.title: INTRO TO PHILOS OF RELIGION
R S 305.description: A critical examination of various conceptions of God and of the relationship of the human and the divine.

R S 310.title: INTRO TO THE STUDY OF RELIGION
R S 310.description: Introduction to scholarly methods in the study of religion. Religious Studies 310 and Sociology 313K may not both be counted.

R S 311E.title: RELIGION AND THE ENVIRONMENT
R S 311E.description: Explore the relationship between religions and the environment from multidisciplinary perspectives. Religious Studies 311 (Topic: Religion & the Environment) and 311E may not both be counted.

R S 313C.title: INTRO TO THE OLD TESTAMENT
R S 313C.description: Introduction to the many interpretations of the Hebrew Bible (the Old Testament) and its meaning in the context of its historical and cultural setting in the ancient Near East.

R S 314K.title: MID EAST: REL/CUL/HIST FND-WB
R S 314K.description: A survey of the history and civilization of the Middle East from the sixth to the fourteenth century. Taught online with established deadlines.

R S 315C.title: THE BIBLE/ITS INTERPRETERS
R S 315C.description: Seeks to develop a wide-ranging familiarity with the Jewish and Christian Bibles and with the dominant modes of ancient, medieval, and early modern biblical interpretation. Readings include an extensive range of primary sources, including both the Scriptures themselves and some of their most influential exegetes.

R S 315N.title: INTRO TO THE NEW TESTAMENT
R S 315N.description: Examines representative examples of the texts found in the Christian New Testament and selected noncanonical writings. Focuses on historical setting and systematic methods of interpretation.

R S 317N.title: INTRO MODERN NORTH AFRICA
R S 317N.description: Introduction to the major themes of North African history from the sixteenth century to the present.

R S 318.title: THE RISE OF CHRISTIANITY
R S 318.description: Introduction to the origins and development of Christianity.

R S 321.title: HIST OF HINDU RELIG TRADITN
R S 321.description: History of major doctrines, practices, and institutions that shaped the development of Hinduism; how religions adapt to social and cultural change and often provide the catalyst for change.

R S 337.title: RELIGION AND SOCIETY
R S 337.description: The growth and decline of religious groups and traditions; "cults" and new religions; comparative sociology of religion; the United States religious landscape; religion and individual health and well-being; spirituality and other aspects of social life. Religious Studies 337 and Sociology 343 may not both be counted.

R S 342F.title: BUDDHIST THOUGHT AND PRACTICE
R S 342F.description: Explore important themes in Buddhist thought, various ways in which Buddhists practice their religion, and the relationships between religious concepts and practices.

R S 346E.title: RELIGION AND FILM
R S 346E.description: Survey of representations of religious beliefs, practices, persons, and institutions in popular film. Examination of film as a vehicle to reflect how a shared religious imagination shaped the way Americans understand themselves.

R S 346K.title: BLACK CHURCH IN AFR AMER POLIT
R S 346K.description: Explore the political role of the Black church in the African American experience.

R S 346U.title: HISTORY OF ISLAM IN THE US-WB

R S 353J.title: REVELATION/APOCALYPTIC LIT
R S 353J.description: Examine the Book of Revelation in the context of early Christian and Jewish literature. Study other apocalyptic texts, methods of textual analysis, and the ongoing effects of Revelation in modern American culture. Classical Civilization 348I and Religious Studies 353J may not both be counted.

R S 357I.title: BYZANTINE ART
R S 357I.description: Examination of the medieval art and architecture of the eastern Roman empire, including related traditions (Coptic, Armenian, Georgian, Crusader, Norman).

R S 357V.title: HOLOCAUST AFTEREFFECTS
R S 357V.description: Explores the historical, political, psychological, theological, and cultural fallout of the Holocaust, as well as literary and cinematic responses in Europe and the United States.

R S 357W.title: RUS ORTHODOX RELIGION/CULTR
R S 357W.description: Examine the role and force of the Orthodox Church in Russian history, from the Christianization of the pagan Slavs in the 10th century, through the Russian Revolution into the Soviet era, and up to the present, while considering the prescriptive tenets of the Orthodox faith (religion) alongside descriptive experiences of life within and under Russian Orthodoxy (religiosity).

R S 358C.title: ISLAM AND POLITICS
R S 358C.description: An introduction to Islamic political thought.

R S 358K.title: ISLAMIC LAW
R S 358K.description: Designed to give students a foundation in the substantive teachings of the shariah, which comprises not only what we normally think of as law, but also ethics and etiquette.

R S 359D.title: ISLM EARLY MOD WRLD RLG/CUL
R S 359D.description: Examine a history of the religious and cultural development across the Islamic world between the thirteenth and eighteenth centuries, stemming from the rise of the Mongols and the end of the caliphate. Explore the early modern Muslim empires of the Ottomans, Safavids, and the Mughals.

R S 366N.title: AFRICAN RELIGION IN NEW WORLD
R S 366N.description: Explore the contributions of scholars and artists who engage African diaspora religions in their work through multiple conceptual approaches.

R S 368D.title: CHURCH/STATE IN LAT AMER
R S 368D.description: History of Church-state relations and religious politics in modern Latin America, with emphasis on the nineteenth to early twentieth-century periods.

R S 373D.title: HISTORY OF CHRISTMAS
R S 373D.description: A historical and cross-cultural examination of the evolution of the modern Christmas holiday, beginning with the birth stories of Jesus in the New Testament and concluding with the supposed "War on Christmas" in the 21st Century America. Religious Studies 373 (Topic: History of Christmas) and 373D may not both be counted.

R S 373M.title: BIOMEDICINE/ETHICS/CULTURE
R S 373M.description: Examines ethical questions such as the allocation of medical resources, stem cell research and cloning, organ transplantation, abortion, human experimentation, prolonging life and the right to die, suicide, euthanasia, and diagnosis and treatment of illnesses, explored from a global perspective.

R S 375S.title: 1-WHAT IS RELIGION?
R S 375S.description: Discussion and research-based study of topics in religious studies. Includes theoretical approaches to the study of religion. Examines classic theories about religion from the last 150 years of academic study: psychological, sociological, economic, phenomenological, and anthropological. Religious Studies 373 and 375S may not both be counted unless the topics vary. Religious Studies 373 (Topic: What is Religion?) and 375S (Topic 1) may not both be counted.

R S 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
R S 679HA.description: Supervised individual reading for one semester, followed by research and writing to produce a substantial paper on a specific topic in religious studies, to be completed during the second semester.

RBT 350.title: GATEWAY TO ROBOTICS
RBT 350.description: Explore math, hardware, and programming needed for advanced robotics. Discuss hardware; programming; modeling and control; sensing, perception, and control; and AI and machine learning. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

REE 301.title: INTRO RUS/E EUR/EURASN STDS
REE 301.description: An introduction to the former Soviet Union and Eastern Europe through each of the major disciplines represented in the program: language, literature, anthropology, geography, history, government, sociology, and economics.

REE 325.title: VAMPIRE: DOMINATION/DESIRE
REE 325.description: Examine the role and force of the Orthodox Church in Russian history, from the Christianization of the pagan Slavs in the 10th century, through the Russian Revolution into the Soviet era, and up to the present, while considering the prescriptive tenets of the Orthodox faith (religion) alongside descriptive experiences of life within and under Russian Orthodoxy (religiosity). Fulfills the basic Russian, East European, and Eurasian studies requirement in language, literature, and culture. Only one of the following may be counted: History 325Q, 366N (Topic: Rus Orthodox Religion/Cultr), Religious Studies 357 (Topic: Rus Orthodox Religion/Cultr), 357W, Russian, East European and Eurasian Studies 325 (Topic: Rus Orthodox Religion/Cultr), 325 (Topic 41).

REE 335.title: 21-CAUSES OF WAR
REE 335.description: Examine key contemporary security issues and policy dilemmas through the perspective of post-communist Eastern European countries in the context of European Union (EU) enlargement waves, and in the framework of their membership in supranational organizations. Consider the transformation of regional states' national security strategies, threat perception, and priorities transitioning from former Warsaw pact to full-fledged EU and NATO membership in shaping their respective national security priorities.

REE 345.title: 13-POWER/RESIST PUTIN'S RUSSIA
REE 345.description: Introduction to Russian political and protest cultures--beyond the headlines and outside the Kremlin walls. Fulfills the basic Russian, East European, and Eurasian studies requirement in sociology, geography, and anthropology. Only one of the following may be counted: Anthropology 322W, 324L (Topic: Power and Resistance in Rus), Russian, East European, and Eurasian Studies 345 (Topic: Power and Resistance in Rus), 345 (Topic 13).

RHE 306.title: RHETORIC AND WRITING
RHE 306.description: An introductory writing course that includes instruction in practical reasoning and the principles of rhetoric. Restricted (see headnote).

RHE 309J.title: RHET OF PUBLIC SPACE/MEMORY
RHE 309J.description: Study and practice methods of rhetorical analysis within the contexts of disputed issues of academic, political, or cultural significance.

RHE 309S.title: CRIT READ AND PERSUASIVE WRIT
RHE 309S.description: A writing course designed to teach advanced rhetorical analysis and advocacy on public issues.

RHE 321.title: PRINCIPLES OF RHETORIC
RHE 321.description: Examine major terms, issues, and approaches in the discipline of rhetoric and writing. Practice analysis and application.

RHE 328.title: 1-MAGAZINE WRITING/PUBLISHING
RHE 328.description: Examine genres, processes, contexts, and practices of technical and professional communication. Introduction to magazine writing, editing, and publishing, with an emphasis on the nonfiction article.

RHE 330C.title: GAMES FOR CHANGE
RHE 330C.description: Examine the role of information technologies in communication and culture. Explore the principles for designing tables, graphs, and figures that are accessible and persuasive to readers and that are accountable to the data. Examine and critique visualizations from academic and popular media. Create visualizations from real-world data sets. Rhetoric and Writing 330C (Topic: Rhet and Data Visualization) and 330C (Topic 13) may not both be counted.

RHE 330D.title: 7-RHET INVENTD/REVISED/RETOLD
RHE 330D.description: Geographically and historically situate and examine figures, movements, traditions, and productions in rhetoric and public argument. Examine how popular rhetorical theories and pedagogies were adapted to fit specific social, cultural, and political contexts and thus shaped the communication practices of girls and women in 19th and early 20th century America. Consider why females were excluded from the rhetorical tradition and the challenges scholars face in writing historical accounts. Rhetoric and Writing 330D (Topic: Women's Rhet Traditions) and 330D (Topic 12) may not both be counted.

RHE 330E.title: 6-NONARGUMNTATV RHET IN ZEN
RHE 330E.description: Critically explore contemporary rhetorical theories and their analytical applications. Exploration of unconventional uses of language including contradiction, negation, story, surprise, gesture, and silence are used in Zen training as resources for transformational change. Rhetoric and Writing 330E (Topic: Nonargumentative Rhetoric in Zen) and 330E (Topic 6) may not both be counted.

RHE 366.title: INTERNSHIP IN RHETORIC & WRIT
RHE 366.description: Research and staff experience working in an appropriate nonprofit, public-, or private-sector entity. May be repeated once for credit when the internships vary.

RHE 367R.title: CONF CRS IN RHETORIC/WRITING
RHE 367R.description: Supervised work on specific projects in rhetoric and writing.

RHE 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
RHE 679HA.description: Research and development of a thesis/project topic and proposal followed by the writing and defense of an honors thesis or development and defense of an honors project.

RIM 301.title: INTRO TO RACE/INDIGEN/MIGRATN
RIM 301.description: Conceptually explore key terms in the study of race, indigeneity and migration. Examine how race, indigeneity and migration have been central to the development of modern nations, economies and cultures through different epochs in United States history.

RIM 320C.title: POWER/PLACE MAKING TX HIST-WB
RIM 320C.description: Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

RTF 130N.title: INTERNSHIP IN MEDIA IND-WB
RTF 130N.description: Practical work experience related to media industries. Internships to be secured by students. Taught as a Web-based course.

RTF 301C.title: COMICS AND CARTOONS
RTF 301C.description: Explore techniques, conventions, history, and cultural space of comics and cartoons. Radio-Television-Film 301C and 344M (Topic: Comics and Cartoons) may not both be counted.

RTF 301D.title: MEDIA STORYTELLING/ANALYSIS
RTF 301D.description: Explore fundamentals of screenwriting and narrative storytelling form. Analyze media, such as film, television, and video games.

RTF 302D.title: HISTORY OF MEDIA INDUSTRIES
RTF 302D.description: Examine the historical development of the film, radio, television, and cable industries and roots of the digital. Investigate cultural, technological, and economic contexts to study production, distribution, and reception. Radio-Television-Film 302D and 308 may not both be counted.

RTF 303C.title: INTRO TO MEDIA/ENT INDUSTRIES
RTF 303C.description: Introduction to Media and Entertainment Industries provides a foundational social science understanding of media and entertainment industries. Drawing on insights from media studies, management, and sociology, uses social science theories, methods, and practices to examine social, political, and economic forces that shape and are shaped by media production, distribution, and monetization.

RTF 303D.title: MEDIA AND CULTURE
RTF 303D.description: Explore cultural values, beliefs, and ideologies that shape media. Examine how individuals and communities interpret media. Evaluate politics, economics, and social relationships that shape and reflect media, culture, and society. Radio-Television-Film 303D and 307 may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

RTF 304D.title: INTRO TO FILM PRODUCTION
RTF 304D.description: Explore the fundamentals of film, television, and digital production concepts and techniques. Radio-Television-Film 304D and 318 may not both be counted.

RTF 306.title: INTRO TO WORLD CINE HIST-UTNY
RTF 306.description: Survey of the aesthetic, cultural, economic, political, and technological trends impacting motion pictures around the world from their origins in the late nineteenth century through the present. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

RTF 321C.title: HISTORY OF AMERICAN TELEVISION
RTF 321C.description: Survey of the evolution of United States television programming, aesthetics, production, and policy. Radio-Television-Film 316 and 321C may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

RTF 321D.title: FILM HISTORY TO 1960-WB
RTF 321D.description: Introductory survey of the history of world cinema to 1960. Explores the development of film as a narrative medium, key filmmakers, films, and of the origins of the main elements of film grammar. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Taught as a Web-based course.

RTF 324C.title: INTRODUCTION TO GLOBAL MEDIA
RTF 324C.description: Introduces the role of global media as a set of texts, technologies, and industries that shape our identities, our social worlds, and our political practices.

RTF 329C.title: DIGITL MEDIA PRODUCTION-WB
RTF 329C.description: Interactive course designed to teach foundational Digital Media Production tools. Apply digital media tools and techniques to a variety of tasks in the pipeline of production from concept, storyboard, layout to compositor, VFX, CG and interactive design. Radio-Television-Film 329C and 344M (Topic: Digital Media Production may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

RTF 330N.title: INTERNSHIP IN MEDIA IND-WB
RTF 330N.description: Practical work experience related to media industries. Internships to be secured by students. Taught as a Web-based course.

RTF 331P.title: DIGITAL MEDIA HISTORIES
RTF 331P.description: Applications and potential effects of new telecommunications and information technologies in the home, workplace, and in education, social services, and politics.

RTF 333.title: INTRODUCTION TO SCREENWRITING
RTF 333.description: An introduction to screenwriting for features, short films, documentaries, and television. Lectures explore the basic theory of story, character, and structure. Students write original screenplays during workshop hours. Creative Writing 325S and Radio-Television-Film 333 may not both be counted.

RTF 335.title: 2-RACE/CLASS/GENDER IN AMER TV
RTF 335.description: Analysis of critical methods, selected television programs, and selected critics. Surveys history and contemporary debates regarding the construction of race, class, gender and sexual orientation in U.S. television from the late 1940s through the present day and related issues of television aesthetics, production practices, audiences, and advocacy. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

RTF 340.title: 4-MULTI-CAMERA TV DIRECTING
RTF 340.description: Introduction to production of television programs, including the multicamera format. Radio-Television-Film 340 (Topic: Multi-Cam Television Directing) and 340 (Topic 4) may not both be counted.

RTF 340D.title: PRODUCTION DESIGN BASICS
RTF 340D.description: Explore the role of design and how the creation and selection of sets, locations, and environments contribute to the visual language of film. Analyze fundamental elements of story, production, critical analysis, and the collaborative process of film making from the design perspective.

RTF 343.title: DIRECTING BRANDED CONTENT
RTF 343.description: Advanced study in film, television, and media production. Explores the cinematic form on an elemental level through the making of several short films. Utilizes cinema-making skills through attention to process and experimentation in order to move to a higher level of precision. Radio-Television-Film 343 (Topic: Cinema Laboratory) and 343 (Topic 8) may not both be counted.

RTF 343C.title: ACTING FOR FILMMAKERS
RTF 343C.description: Explores core elements of acting technique through scene study, improvisation, and other exercises. Radio-Television-Film 343 (Topic: Acting for Filmmakers) and 343C may not both be counted.

RTF 343D.title: CINEMATOGRAPHY
RTF 343D.description: Visual storytelling and the art of cinematography through practice in a workshop environment. Radio-Television-Film 343D and 368 (Topic 2) may not both be counted.

RTF 344M.title: GAME PROGRAMMING PARADIGMS
RTF 344M.description: Special topics in digital media theory, design, or development. May include visual effects and motion graphics or digital media and digital art. Explores storytelling possibilities of interactive mediums. Develop the skills and knowledge necessary to create characters, storylines, and dialogue for digital mediums.

RTF 344T.title: 1-GAME DEV CAPSTONE: 2D GAMES
RTF 344T.description: Capstone courses in game development. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

RTF 345.title: CONTEMPORARY GLOBAL CINEMAS
RTF 345.description: Critical assessment of major genres, periods, movements, and personalities in United States and international film history. Radio-Television-Film 345 (Topic: Women Behind the Camera) and 345 (Topic 9) may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

RTF 346.title: INTRODUCTION TO EDITING
RTF 346.description: Theory and practice of video and film editing techniques. Course uses AVID software.

RTF 347C.title: 1-L A: HOW HOLLYWOOD WORKS
RTF 347C.description: Survey of business practices in film, television, music, and digital media industries: development, production, distribution, and exhibition. Explores the commercial challenges that face producers and network and studio executives, and addresses the continual struggle between creativity and the bottom line. Taught in Los Angeles, California. Taught in Los Angeles, California.

RTF 347E.title: ADVANCED EDITING
RTF 347E.description: In-depth look at the principles, techniques, and aesthetics of editing; building a broad background for students' future professional development. Designed to provide a basic-to-intermediate level of familiarity with Avid editing software, by discussing and working on provided narrative, documentary, and technical exercises; various approaches to editing style in each case.

RTF 347S.title: GLOBAL FILM AESTHETICS
RTF 347S.description: Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

RTF 348.title: ANIMATION PIPELINE-L A
RTF 348.description: Examination of the economics and the production, research, management, and distribution practices of the film and electronic media industries. Examine how the music industry really works, how it relates to other divisions of the entertainment industry, and the effects that changing technologies have on it. Taught in Los Angeles, California.

RTF 350L.title: SEMESTER IN L A INT-L A
RTF 350L.description: Practical work experience related to the study of film, television, radio, or other media. Students must make their own arrangements to secure relevant internships. Communication 350L, 650L and Radio-Television-Film 350L, 650L may not both be counted. Taught in Los Angeles, California.

RTF 350N.title: SEMESTER IN NEW YORK INTERN
RTF 350N.description: Internships to be arranged by student and approved by instructor. Partially taught as a Web-based course. Taught in New York, New York.

RTF 359S.title: CLASSIC HWD STARS FANS CULTURE
RTF 359S.description: Special topics related to the critical analysis of media in cultural contexts. Survey of the representation and participation of Latinas and Latinos in U.S. English-language film and television from the silent film era to the present. Also focuses on issues Latina/os face as media producers, media consumers, and media activists. Radio-Television-Film 359 and 359S may not both be counted unless the topics vary.

RTF 366K.title: SOCIAL MEDIA PRODUCTION
RTF 366K.description: Workshop in contemporary documentary practice, including directing, producing, cinematography, editing, sound recording, and distribution.

RTF 366S.title: PRODUCTION SOUND TECHNIQUES
RTF 366S.description: Radio-Television-Film 337 (Topic 2) and 366S (Topic 3) may not both be counted.

RTF 367D.title: DIRECTING WORKSHOP
RTF 367D.description: Explores the role of the director, focusing on the director-actor relationship, narrative structure, and visual language.

RTF 367K.title: PRODUCING FILM AND TELEVISION
RTF 367K.description: Examine the roles and responsibilities of a producer; including development, financing, pre-production, production, post-production, marketing, and distribution.

RTF 368.title: DRAWING THE STORYBOARD
RTF 368.description: Study production crafts in film, television, and other digital media.

RTF 368P.title: 1-PRODUCING SHORT FILMS
RTF 368P.description: Explore various aspects of film and television producing. Radio-Television-Film 368P (Topic: Producing Short Films) and 368P (Topic 1) may not both be counted.

RTF 368S.title: 2-MEDIA STUDIES THESIS
RTF 368S.description: Advanced film production or media studies research. An independent research project based on primary data, resulting in a written summary of theoretical foundations, methodological approach, results, and a discussion.

RTF 369.title: ADV SCREENWRITING: SHORT FILMS
RTF 369.description: Students develop and write original screenplays. Includes advanced study of screenplay structure, critical analysis of student and professional work, and intensive writing each week toward a completed script. Students may not enroll in more than two sections of any Radio-Television-Film 369 course in the same semester. Students may not enroll in more than one section of Radio-Television-Film 369 (Topic 1) in the same semester.

RTF 369P.title: SCRIPT TO SCREEN: MOVIES
RTF 369P.description: Examine the behind-the-scenes production of feature films, commercials, and other media including scripts, shot lists, storyboards, shooting schedules, and visual effects.

RTF 370.title: 2-ANIMATION STUDIES
RTF 370.description: Analysis of critical methods, selected films, and selected critics. Radio-Television-Film 331K (Topic: Animation Studies) and 370 (Topic 2) may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

RTF 630N.title: INTERNSHIP IN MEDIA IND-WB
RTF 630N.description: Practical work experience related to media industries. Internships to be secured by students. Taught as a Web-based course.

RTF 650L.title: SEMESTER IN L A INT-L A
RTF 650L.description: Practical work experience related to the study of film, television, radio, or other media. Students must make their own arrangements to secure relevant internships. Communication 350L, 650L and Radio-Television-Film 350L, 650L may not both be counted. Taught in Los Angeles, California.

RUS 322C.title: INTER RUSSIAN THRU CONVRSTN
RUS 322C.description: Development of the basic skills of listening, speaking, reading, and writing, with special emphasis on oral communication. Russian 322C and 324 may not both be counted. Russian 322C and 325 may not both be counted.

RUS 326E.title: ADV RUSSIAN THROUGH HISTORY
RUS 326E.description: Practice Russian language skills in all areas of cultural and linguistic proficiency (speaking, listening, reading, and writing) while introducing major events and figures in Russian history from Kyivan Rus to the early twentieth century. Russian 326 (Topic: Adv Russian Thru History) and 326E may not both be counted.

RUS 406.title: FIRST-YEAR RUSSIAN I
RUS 406.description: Emphasis on four-skills proficiency: listening, speaking, reading, and writing. Taught as a Web-based course.

RUS 407.title: FIRST-YEAR RUSSIAN II-WB
RUS 407.description: Emphasis on four-skills proficiency: listening, speaking, reading, and writing. Taught online during scheduled times. Includes live-streaming video and requires real-time participation. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

RUS 412K.title: SECOND-YEAR RUSSIAN I-WB
RUS 412K.description: Listening, speaking, reading, and writing at the second-year level. Taught online during scheduled times. Includes live-streaming video and requires real-time participation. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

RUS 601C.title: INTENSIVE RUSSIAN I
RUS 601C.description: Not open to native or heritage speakers of Russian. Emphasis on developing oral proficiency in Russian using intensive methods of instruction. May not be counted by students with credit for Russian 406, 506, 407, 507. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

S S 302C.title: HON SOC SCI:METHODS/THEORY
S S 302C.description: An introduction to the study of the individual, society, or culture using the methods and theories of one of the Social Sciences. Social Science 301 and 302C may not both be counted.

S S 302D.title: HON SOCIAL SCI: PSYCHOLOGY
S S 302D.description: An introduction to the study of the individual, society, or culture using the methods of the social science discipline of Psychology. Social Science 301 and 302D may not both be counted.

S W 310.title: INTRO TO SOCL WORK & SOCL WELF
S W 310.description: Introduction to the profession of social work including its fields of practice; values and ethics; its history and the social welfare policies that undergird it; its interactions with contexts which shape practice; and its promotion of social and economic justice to advance human rights, alleviate critical social problems, and promote wellbeing. Discuss engaging diversity and differences in social work practice.

S W 312.title: GEN S W PRAC: KNOWL/VAL/SKILLS
S W 312.description: Introduction to the fundamental values, ethics, skills, and knowledge relevant to generalist social work practice.

S W 313.title: SOCIAL WORK RESEARCH METHODS
S W 313.description: Introduction to social work values, the research process, problem formulation and conceptualization, measurement, research design and inference, single-subject design and practice evaluation, sampling, alternative data gathering techniques and analyses, and uses of research in social work.

S W 318.title: SOCIAL WORK STATISTICS
S W 318.description: Introduction to the conceptual and quantitative tools used to describe and interpret data in the conduct of social work practice and research. Select, calculate, and interpret appropriate statistics applicable to common data analysis situations related to direct practice, administration and planning, and policy. Explore R statistical software to calculate statistics and present results. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

S W 323K.title: SOCIAL WELF PROG/POL/ISS
S W 323K.description: Analyze contemporary social welfare policy issues and programs and discuss the relationship between social policy and social work practice. Explore the historical, political, economic, and social conditions that influence policy development in the United States. Use policy analysis tools in order to examine how policy influences practice and planning decisions and how social workers can influence social welfare policy.

S W 327.title: HUMAN BEHAV/SOCIAL ENVIR
S W 327.description: Explore the biopsychosocial and cultural functioning of human beings across the life span using an ecological-systems lens and major developmental theories as a framework to guide the processes of assessment, intervention, and evaluation. Discuss the impact of the family, social groups, organizations, communities, and society on individuals during these stages of the life span. Examine empirically-based research reflecting a risk and resilience perspective in making client assessments and recommending appropriate interventions. Social Work 327 and Urban Studies 328S may not both be counted. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

S W 332.title: SOCL WORK PRAC WITH INDIV/FAMS
S W 332.description: Examine the helping process within the context of an ecosystems developmental framework. Explore engaging clients in an appropriate working relationship; communication skills; identifying issues, problems, needs, resources, and assets; and planning for service delivery. Pay special attention to understanding, affirming, and respecting people from diverse backgrounds, including (but not limited to) groups distinguished by race, ethnicity, culture, class, gender, sexual orientation, religion, physical or mental ability, age, and national origin. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

S W 333.title: SOCL WORK PRACTICE WITH GROUPS
S W 333.description: Explore group dynamics and effective leadership skills, through social work values and the ethical decision-making process. Discuss both task and treatment groups utilizing an ecosystems developmental framework. Pay special attention to understanding, affirming, and respecting groups with diverse backgrounds, including (but not limited to) race, ethnicity, culture, class, gender, sexual orientation, religion, physical or mental ability, age, and national origin. Explore social work group facilitation using empirically-based theories and interventions to achieve client goals.

S W 334.title: SOCL WORK PRAC IN ORG AND COMM
S W 334.description: Examine the organizational context within which social services are delivered, including how funding, mandate, and organizational arrangements influence service delivery and factors to consider in efforts to modify existing organizational arrangements. Explore the interlocking and complex nature of culture and personal identity, discussing vulnerable populations and examining the factors that contribute to and constitute being at risk. Define, design, and implement strategies for effective practice with persons from diverse backgrounds. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

S W 360K.title: APPLIED SOCIAL ETHICS
S W 360K.description: Designed to enable each student to undertake intensive study of selected aspects of social work practice. Examine social work practice in sporting environments and explore how to assist athletes at all levels of sports. Includes a focus on physical and mental health, parenting, youth, foster care, bereavement, and social services. Social Work 360K (Topic: Intro to Sport) and 360K (Topic: 23) may not both be counted.

S W 445.title: SOCIAL WORK APPLIED PRACTICE
S W 445.description: Explore the integration of theory and practice to strengthen the relationship between the classroom content and field. Examine the processes common to all social work practice, gender and cultural issues, and their impact on service delivery. Explore issues related to social and economic justice, vulnerable populations, cultural diversity, alleviating social problems, and the enhancement of human well-being. Social Work 444 and 445 may not both be counted.

S W 645F.title: SOCL WORK PRCTCM INSTRUCTION I
S W 645F.description: Participate in a practicum to apply knowledge and develop skills of social work practice.

S W 645G.title: SCL WORK PRCTCM INSTRUCTION II
S W 645G.description: Apply knowledge and develop skills in social work.

SAN 312K.title: SECOND-YEAR SANSKRIT I
SAN 312K.description: Introduction to classical Sanskrit prose literature; readings from the Epics and Kathasaritsagara.

SAN 330.title: 1-SANSKRIT DRAMA
SAN 330.description: Provides students with a firmer foundation in Sanskrit grammar, syntax, and vocabulary; and an appreciation of the aesthetics of Sanskrit drama. Sanskrit 330 (Topic: Sanskrit Drama) and 330 (Topic 1) may not both be counted.

SAN 506.title: FIRST-YEAR SANSKRIT I
SAN 506.description: Introduction to basic grammatical principles, with reading of Ramayana episodes as illustrations.

SDS 302F.title: FOUNDATIONS OF DATA ANALYSIS
SDS 302F.description: Introduction to data analysis and statistical methods. Subjects include random sampling; principles of observational study and experimental design; data summaries and graphics; and statistical models and inference, including the simple linear regression model and one-way analysis of variance. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

SDS 313.title: INTRO TO DATA SCIENCE
SDS 313.description: Introduction to the principles and practice of data science. Explore R and reproducible data analysis; summarizing data using descriptive statistics; data visualization and storytelling; data wrangling and relational data; basic prediction and classification using regression models; and programming in R.

SDS 320E.title: ELEMENTS OF STATISTICS
SDS 320E.description: Introduction to statistics. Subjects include probability; principles of observational study and experimental design; statistical models and inference, including the multiple linear regression model and one-way analysis of variance. R programming is introduced.

SDS 321.title: INTRO TO PROBABIL & STATISTICS
SDS 321.description: Covers fundamentals of probability, combinatorics, discrete and continuous random variables, jointly distributed random variables, and limit theorems. Using probability to introduce fundamentals of statistics, including Bayesian and classical inference. Statistics and Data Sciences 321 and 431 may not both be counted.

SDS 322E.title: ELEMENTS OF DATA SCIENCE
SDS 322E.description: Explore data science tools and examine data wrangling; exploratory data analysis and data visualization; markdown and data workflow; simulation-based inference; and classification methods. R programming is emphasized and Python programming is introduced.

SDS 324E.title: ELEMENTS OF REGRESSION ANLYSIS
SDS 324E.description: Explore the use of regression analysis in applied research and learn about multiple linear regression; ANOVA; logistic regression; random and mixed-effects models; and models for dependent data. Engage in the identification of appropriate statistical methods and interpretation of software output. R programming is introduced. Statistics and Data Sciences 324E and 332 may not both be counted.

SDS 326E.title: ELEMENTS OF STAT MACH LEARNING
SDS 326E.description: Introduction to the concepts and tools of data science, statistics, and machine learning used to draw inferences about large-scale and real-world data. Explore data visualization, linear and nonlinear models, regularization, classification, resampling, tree-based methods, support vector machines, and unsupervised learning. Statistics and Data Sciences 323 and 326E may not both be counted.

SDS 336.title: PRACTICAL MACHINE LEARNING
SDS 336.description: Introduction to machine learning for data science with an emphasis on Python programming. Explore comparing algorithm performance; decision-tree algorithms; classification algorithms; model averaging; unsupervised learning; and neural networks.

SDS 354.title: ADVANCED STATISTICAL METHODS
SDS 354.description: Explore advanced methods in statistics and data science. Examine modeling data with multilevel (hierarchical) structure and causal inference, including design and analysis strategies. Discuss smoothing methods; spatial and time series models; additive models; and models for network data.

SDS 357.title: CASE STUDIES IN DATA SCIENCE
SDS 357.description: Explore advanced case studies in data science, with an emphasis on the full data analysis pipeline. Examine data collection, identification of data limitations; data privacy; data preparation and exploration; building, using, and evaluating models; creating data products; and communication and persuasion with data.

SDS 365.title: 1-SPORTS ANALYTICS
SDS 365.description: Examine concepts of statistics and data science. Learn the fundamentals of creating data products using technologies like Shiny, R packages, and interactive graphics tools. Explore interactive maps, machine learning application programming interfaces, dashboards, and data storytelling. Focus on both the statistical details and the design process for building effective data products. Statistics and Data Sciences 365 (Topic 2) and 375 (Topic: Data Product Development) may not both be counted.

SDS 366.title: DATA VISUALIZATION
SDS 366.description: Explore how to visualize data sets. Reason about, and communicate with, data visualizations. Statistics and Data Sciences 366 and 375 (Topic: Data Viz in R) may not both be counted.

SDS 368.title: STATISTICAL THEORY
SDS 368.description: Introduction to the mathematical theory of statistics. Explore maximum likelihood estimation, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests and statistical decision theory, tail and concentration bounds, concentration of measure, and nonparametric statistics.

SDS 378.title: INTRO TO MATHEMATCL STATISTICS
SDS 378.description: Sampling distributions of statistics, estimation of parameters (confidence intervals, method of moments, maximum likelihood, comparison of estimators using mean square error and efficiency, sufficient statistics), hypothesis tests (p-values, power, likelihood ratio tests), and other topics. Mathematics 378K and Statistics and Data Sciences 378 may not both be counted. Inquiry-based learning format.

SDS 379H.title: HONORS UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
SDS 379H.description: Design an individual research project under the supervision of one or more faculty members.

SDS 379R.title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
SDS 379R.description: Students work on an individual research project under the supervision of one or more faculty members.

SDS 431.title: PROBABILITY/STAT INFERENCE
SDS 431.description: Introduction to probability and statistical inference. Examine events and random experiments; basic rules of probability; joint, conditional, and marginal probability and independence; discrete and continuous random variables; random sampling and estimation; large-sample theory results and central limit theorem-based inferential summaries; and maximum likelihood estimation. Statistics and Data Sciences 321 and 431 may not both be counted.

SED 303.title: AUTISM SPECTRUM DISORDER-WB
SED 303.description: Focus on approaches and strategies that help improve the lives of people with ASD. Examines the role research plays in what people know about various claims. Explores how research findings are communicated, and how to best translate research findings into everyday language. Taught as a Web-based course.

SED 322C.title: INDIVIDUAL DIFFERENCES
SED 322C.description: Explore individual differences among people through the life span. Examine areas of exceptionality within the context of typical development: current research trends; theoretical and legal considerations; and practice-related issues, including family involvement, cultural and linguistic diversity, and educational perspectives. Applied Learning and Development 322 and Special Education 322C may not both be counted.

SED 327.title: SOCIOCUL INFLUENCES ON LRN
SED 327.description: Discuss human learning in multisocial, multilingual, and multicultural contexts; realities of society and their impact on learning; and social concerns such as prejudice, stereotyping, cross-cultural attitudes, bilingual issues, and parent and community involvement.

SED 360R.title: TEACH RESIDENCY AND SEMINAR-WB
SED 360R.description: Participate in a supervised practicum in special education classroom teaching, conducted in cooperating schools, as part of the teacher preparation program including teaching, analysis, and evaluation. Taught as a Web-based course.

SED 376.title: FOUNDATIONS & ISS IN SPEC EDUC
SED 376.description: Key issues affecting decision-making and practices by special education teachers, assessment personnel, and administrators related to the treatment and education of students with disabilities. Required for students seeking special education certification.

SED 378C.title: TCH STU WTH INTS SUPP NEEDS-WB
SED 378C.description: Explore assessment and instructional strategies for educating students with autism and other developmental and physical disabilities. Focus on implementation and evaluation of instructional procedures for teaching a range of adaptive behaviors, such as self-care, and communication, social, and community living skills. Special Education 378S and 378C may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

SED 378G.title: TCH RDNG AND WRTG II
SED 378G.description: Explore the knowledge and skills associated with assessing, instructing, and monitoring the progress of students who experience mild to moderate difficulties with reading, as well as students with dyslexia. Focus on reading, spelling, and writing for kindergarten through grade five. Special Education 378R and 378G may not both be counted.

SLH 113P.title: HEARING SCIENCE: LABORATORY
SLH 113P.description: Examine laboratory techniques in hearing science research. Communication Sciences and Disorders 113P and Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences 113P may not both be counted.

SLH 118L.title: DEVELOPMENTAL LANG SCIENCE LAB
SLH 118L.description: Examine child language in a clinical laboratory setting. Communication Sciences and Disorders 118L and Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences 118L may not both be counted.

SLH 158L.title: ANAT/PHYSLGY SPCH/HEAR LAB
SLH 158L.description: Examine anatomy and physiology of structures involved in speech, language, hearing, and swallowing in a laboratory setting. Explore anatomical and physiological mechanisms of respiration, phonation, articulation, and hearing; and the central nervous system and blood supply to the speech and hearing mechanism. Communication Sciences and Disorders 158L and Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences 158L may not both be counted.

SLH 306K.title: INTRO SPEECH/LANG/HEARING SCIS
SLH 306K.description: Introduction to the study of processes and disorders of speech, language, and hearing. Communication Sciences and Disorders 306K and Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences 306K may not both be counted.

SLH 311K.title: PHONETIC DESCRIPTION OF SPEECH
SLH 311K.description: Examine speech production, physiological analysis and description of speech sounds, voice quality, and voice dynamics; notation; phonetic theory; applications of phonetics. Communication Sciences and Disorders 311K and Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences 311K may not both be counted.

SLH 312.title: DEVELOPMENTAL LANGUAGE SCIENCE
SLH 312.description: Introduction to language and literacy development from birth through adolescence. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

SLH 313L.title: HEARING SCIENCE
SLH 313L.description: Examine acoustical, physiological, and psychological bases of normal human hearing. Explore theories of audition. Communication Sciences and Disorders 313L and Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences 313L may not both be counted.

SLH 315S.title: SPEECH SCIENCE
SLH 315S.description: Neurophysiological mechanisms underlying the encoding and decoding of speech.

SLH 340P.title: CLNCL OBSRV SPCH LANG PATHO
SLH 340P.description: Explore evidence-based practice in speech and language pathology through guided observation. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

SLH 341.title: PRINCIPLES OF AUDIOLOGY
SLH 341.description: Explore the causes of hearing disorders and examine diagnostic procedures and treatment. Communication Sciences and Disorders 341 and Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences 341 may not both be counted.

SLH 350.title: LANGUAGE AND THE BRAIN
SLH 350.description: Explore the neuroanatomical and functional operations of the major brain structures that underlie speech/language. Examine hemispheric dominance for language, neurological and language breakdowns in aphasia, and brain imaging methods and studies of language representation.

SLH 352.title: 2-AUGMENTATV/ALTERNATV COMM
SLH 352.description: Examine augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) approaches for individuals with severe communication impairments. Explore foundational information for providing or referring services in AAC.

SLH 358.title: ANAT/PHYS SPEECH/HEARING MECH
SLH 358.description: Examine the anatomy and physiology of structures involved in speech, language, hearing, and swallowing in-depth. Explore anatomical and physiological mechanisms of respiration, phonation, articulation, and hearing; and the central nervous system and blood supply to the speech and hearing mechanism. Communication Sciences and Disorders 358 and Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences 358 may not both be counted.

SLH 367K.title: INTRO SP/LNG DIS ASSMT/TRT CHL
SLH 367K.description: Introduction to assessment procedures and treatment strategies for children with speech and language disorders. Communication Sciences and Disorders 367K and Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences 367K may not both be counted.

SLH 371.title: INTRO SP/LNG DIS ASSMT/TRT ADU
SLH 371.description: Introduction to assessment procedures and treatment strategies for adults with speech and language disorders. Communication Sciences and Disorders 371 and Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences 371 may not both be counted.

SLH 373.title: PRINS OF AURAL REHABILITATN-WB
SLH 373.description: Examine the rationale, methods, materials, procedures, and criteria for aural rehabilitation for hearing-impaired persons. Communication Sciences and Disorders 373 and Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences 373 may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

SLH 378C.title: EXPER LRNG IN CLINICAL RSRCH
SLH 378C.description: Explore experiential learning opportunities within the clinical environment observe clinical research, connect concepts, and reflect on processes that promote translation of theory to practice for stuttering and/or cluttering. Communication Sciences and Disorders 378C and Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences 378C may not both be counted.

SOC 302.title: INTRO TO STUDY OF SOCIETY-WB
SOC 302.description: The nature of human societies, social processes, social interaction, and the sociological approach. Taught online with established deadlines. Online activities and exams may be scheduled. Go to http://www.laits.utexas.edu/tower/online/courses/ for additional information and to test your computer and internet connectivity.

SOC 307C.title: AMER FAMILIES PAST AND PRESENT
SOC 307C.description: Examines trends over time in specific aspects of family life, including marriage, cohabitation, mate selection, divorce, parenthood, family structure, and work-family balance. Study of the methods that sociologists and demographers use to study the family. Sociology 307C and 308 (Topic: American Families Past and Present) may not both be counted.

SOC 307T.title: PUNISHMENT AND SOCIETY
SOC 307T.description: Focus on society's responses to crime. Introduction to the sociology of punishment and the death penalty, the American model of criminal punishment, and contextualization of the United States system and critical analysis of penal systems in general. Special emphasis on the nature of penal control in contemporary America. Sociology 307T and 308 (Topic: Punishment and Society) may not both be counted.

SOC 308S.title: INTRO TO HEALTH & SOCIETY
SOC 308S.description: A broad, multidisciplinary overview that introduces students to the study of health and society.

SOC 309C.title: CREATING SUSTAINABLE SOCTY
SOC 309C.description: Overview of sustainability as something human beings must strive to create in an era of global warming and ever greater social inequalities, both between and within countries.

SOC 317L.title: INTRO TO SOCIAL STATISTICS
SOC 317L.description: Measures of central tendency and dispersion, the binomial and chi-square distributions, tests of hypotheses and parameter estimation, and simple correlation and regression. Required of all sociology majors. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

SOC 321G.title: GLOBAL HEALTH ISSUES/SYSTEMS
SOC 321G.description: Overview of current global health challenges and issues from biological, cultural, and environmental perspectives. Examination, comparison, and contrasting of various health systems in the six World Health Organization geographic regions and countries within those regions, with regard to the specific health challenges they face.

SOC 321U.title: URBAN SOCIOLOGY
SOC 321U.description: Introduction to the study of the city and the suburbs. Specific focus on inequality in urban space, with emphasis on three major United States cities (New York, Chicago, and Los Angeles) and on several third world cities.

SOC 322L.title: LAW AND ORGANIZATIONS: HON
SOC 322L.description: Examine the intersection of law and organizational/workplace practices. Explore how law regulates the workplace (e.g., laws about sexual harassment), how organizations do or do not comply with the law, and the ways that organizational practices may affect legal standards.

SOC 322U.title: UNITED STATES IMMIGRATION
SOC 322U.description: Utilizes a sociological perspective to gain an understanding of what social forces drive migration to the United States, how migrants organize their migration, and how immigration affects United States' society.

SOC 323.title: THE FAMILY
SOC 323.description: The American family in historical and comparative perspective. Family history and origins; comparative family systems; social antecedents of family structure and process; family formation and dissolution; family and society; recent family changes and prospects for the future. Sociology 323 and Women's and Gender Studies 345 (Topic 2) may not both be counted.

SOC 323E.title: ECONOMIC SOCIOLOGY
SOC 323E.description: Examine sociological perspectives on a range of economic matters including money, activities of firms and corporations, consumption of goods and services, market behavior, organizational malfeasance and economic crisis, and the production of labor market inequality, as well as economic behavior in less traditional settings such as markets for intimacy and markets for human goods. Learn about some of the major economic transformations that have reshaped social life in recent decades, including financialization, rising income inequality, corporate decline, and shareholder value management. Consider how sociologists have contributed to understanding the causes or consequences of these trends. Sociology 321K (Topic: Economic Sociology) and 323E may not both be counted.

SOC 323F.title: FOOD AND SOCIETY
SOC 323F.description: Explores the social context of food; including food and identity, social class and culture, as well as who plans, purchases, and prepares food for our families. Food production and policies in the United States and government assistance for the hungry are also covered. Sociology 321K (Topic: Food and Society) and 323F may not both be counted.

SOC 323L.title: LATINA/O/X CITIZENSHIP
SOC 323L.description: Explore the concepts of membership, belonging, immigration and citizenship in the Latina/o/x experience. Examine how historically, politically, socially and culturally, Latinas/os/xs have been construed as belonging or not belonging members of the United States society.

SOC 324M.title: SOCIOLOGY OF MONEY
SOC 324M.description: Explore the social meanings of money, the distribution of economic resources, why some workers are paid more than others, how prices are determined, the modern financial system, unconventional markets, the connection between price and value, and money in personal relationships. Sociology 321K (Topic: Sociology of Money) and 324M may not both be counted.

SOC 324P.title: INTL POL ECON LOW-INCM COUNTR
SOC 324P.description: Explore the ways that China, India, Brazil and other lower-income countries are transforming the patterns in global finance, trade, aid, women's empowerment, and the control or corruption.

SOC 325K.title: CRIMINOLOGY
SOC 325K.description: An investigation into the nature of criminal events, including homicide, rape, robbery, property crimes, and white-collar crimes. Also examines the United States criminal justice system.

SOC 327M.title: SOCIAL RESEARCH METHODS
SOC 327M.description: Collect data to answer research questions by focusing on the meaning of variables, understanding causation, study design, basic sampling, and modes and methods in data collection. Sociology 317M and 327M may not both be counted.

SOC 330C.title: DEATH & DYING: SOCL PERSPECTVS
SOC 330C.description: Sociological perspectives on definitions of death; group differences in mortality rates and causes of death; social meanings of death and dying; treatment of the dying and the dead; and grief and bereavement.

SOC 330P.title: SOCIOLOGY & SOCIAL PSYCHOLOGY
SOC 330P.description: Examines how individuals perceive, interact with, and affect other individuals from the perspectives of both psychology and sociology.

SOC 340D.title: VIOLENCE
SOC 340D.description: An overview of the different theories of interpersonal and group violence. Includes criminological theories of violent crime, as well as feuding, ethnic and nationalist violence, political violence, and aggression in intimate relations.

SOC 340G.title: SOCIOLOGY OF SEXUALITIES
SOC 340G.description: Review of sociological perspectives on sexuality. Examines how social institutions in United States society shape sexual values, beliefs, and practices. Subjects include changing cultural images of sexuality, sexual identities, and social movements.

SOC 341C.title: MEDICAL SOCIOLOGY
SOC 341C.description: Explores the social context of health, illness, and the health care system in American society. Discussion of the processes of medicalization and demedicalization, social factors influencing health and health care, the organization of the health care delivery system and patient outcomes, and the social meaning and experiences of illness.

SOC 343.title: RELIGION AND SOCIETY
SOC 343.description: The growth and decline of religious groups and traditions; "cults" and new religions; comparative sociology of religion; the United States religious landscape; religion and individual health and well-being; spirituality and other aspects of social life. Religious Studies 337 and Sociology 343 may not both be counted.

SOC 345D.title: INEQUALITY IN US EDUC SYS: HON
