SOC 345D.description: Examine how inequality has developed and is maintained within the American public education system.

SOC 350M.title: SOCIOLOGY INTERNSHIP SEMINAR
SOC 350M.description: Intern in an agency, organization, or business under supervision. Apply the knowledge and skills acquired in sociology courses.

SOC 354K.title: SOCIOLOGY OF HEALTH & ILLNESS
SOC 354K.description: Application of sociological concepts and principles to the study of health professions, medical institutions, community medical organization, and the distribution of illness.

SOC 369K.title: POPULATION AND SOCIETY
SOC 369K.description: The study of populations, including their growth, age structure, and patterns of fertility, mortality, and migration; the social causes and consequences of these phenomena.

SOC 379M.title: SOCIOLOGICAL THEORY
SOC 379M.description: Critical examination of major sociological theories and their relevance to current research and social conditions.

SOC 379N.title: CONFERENCE COURSE
SOC 379N.description: Supervised individual study of selected problems in sociology.

SOC 379P.title: UNDERGRAD RESEARCH OPPORTUNITY
SOC 379P.description: Perform research-related activities with faculty members in the sociology department. Collect interview or survey data, analyze statistics, synthesize research literature, and code qualitative data.

SOC 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
SOC 679HA.description: Research into and development of a thesis topic and proposal followed by the writing and defense of a thesis.

SOC 679HB.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
SOC 679HB.description: Research into and development of a thesis topic and proposal followed by the writing and defense of a thesis.

SPB 301.title: INTRO SPORTS PROD/BROADCST I
SPB 301.description: Introduction to the foundational aspects of the sports broadcasting industry and the essential production skills required to be successful in the field. Communication 317 (Topic: Introduction to Sports Production and Broadcasting) and Sports Production and Broadcasting 301 may not both be counted.

SPB 302.title: INTRO SPORTS PROD/BROADCST II
SPB 302.description: Explore foundational aspects of sports broadcasting and production with an emphasis on editing. Communication 314 (Topic: Intro Sports Prod/Broadcst II) and Sports Production and Broadcasting 302 may not both be counted.

SPB 322.title: STORYTELLING SPORTS PROD/BROAD
SPB 322.description: Explore the art of storytelling in live and other forms of sports production.

SPB 370.title: REMOTE FIELD PRODUCTION
SPB 370.description: Discuss advanced skills and practice in production and/or broadcasting in a range of environments. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

SPC 320C.title: 8-MUSIC/POWER/POLITICS LAT AM
SPC 320C.description: Significant issues in Iberian or Latin American societies and cultures. Analyze how popular music and more sound-related media in Latin America have played central roles in the expression of global citizenship, national and transnational identities, social justice, race, gender, and sexuality.

SPN 311.title: INTERMEDIATE SPANISH
SPN 311.description: Focus on the development of speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills while building vocabulary. Review rules and terminology of Spanish grammar. Examine complex grammar structures and Spanish-speaking cultures.

SPN 311J.title: INTERMED SPANISH HERITAGE LRNR
SPN 311J.description: Designed primarily for students who have a cultural and linguistic connection to Spanish (i.e. native and near-native speakers, Spanish as home language).

SPN 314.title: SPANISH CONVERSATION AND CUL
SPN 314.description: Explore the development of oral skills and speaking strategies in different discourse genres including informal, formal, academic, and travel.

SPN 314J.title: WRITING/CUL CONTXT HRTG LRNRS
SPN 314J.description: Designed for students with a cultural and linguistic connection to Spanish (i.e. native and near-native speakers, Spanish as a home language). Examine Hispanic cultures and sociopolitical experiences and explore communicative abilities through a variety of interactive, student-centered activities.

SPN 327C.title: ADV GRAM & WRITING IN CONTEXT
SPN 327C.description: Study and practice of Spanish grammar that includes reading exercises and guided composition designed to develop writing skills across styles. Students will be exposed to various topics in Spanish language, literature, and culture. Spanish 327C and 327N may not both be counted.

SPN 327N.title: ACADEMIC WRTG HERITAGE SPKRS
SPN 327N.description: Designed specifically for heritage speakers of Spanish and includes a wide range of cultural readings and writing tasks. Aims to strengthen the clarity and organization of writing, promote critical thinking, and master grammar points of particular concern to heritage speakers. Spanish 327C and 327N may not both be counted.

SPN 327V.title: ADV WRITING/SERVICE LRNING
SPN 327V.description: Explore the ethical perspectives on issues in education, immigration, and community service. Spanish 327V and 327W may not both be counted.

SPN 328C.title: INTRO TO LITERATURES/CULS
SPN 328C.description: Overview of Iberian and/or Latin American literatures and cultures, including the arts and popular expressions, from a multidisciplinary perspective. Among the regions studied are Spain; North, Central, and South America; the Caribbean; and related areas in Africa.

SPN 330L.title: INTRO LANG AND LING IN SOCIETY
SPN 330L.description: Examines the dynamics of language structure and use throughout the Spanish-speaking world, and covers topics such as sound systems, grammatical structures, historical developments, language learning and loss, and dialect differences and their social significance. Spanish 330L and 345L may not both be counted.

SPN 346C.title: SPANISH PRONUNCIATION
SPN 346C.description: Review Spanish pronunciation. Examine the fundamental principles of phonetic analysis in order to show how Spanish sounds are produced, how they fall into patterns, and how they change in different environments. Spanish 346 and 346C may not both be counted.

SPN 350C.title: GEND ISS CONTEMP LAT AM CIN
SPN 350C.description: Studies Latin American cinema as a device of gender system formation and reinforcement, and as criticism of patriarchal hegemony; discusses questions related to sexuality depicted in Latin American films. Subjects covered include: maternity, prostitution, machismo, children's sexuality, homosexuality, heterosexuality, and gender violence in films from the 1930s to present. Conducted in Spanish.

SPN 350G.title: CUBAN REVOLUTION
SPN 350G.description: Explore diverse accounts and interpretations of the Cuban Revolution in fictional and documentary film and narrative produced in the island and abroad by pro-and counter-revolutionary, island and diaspora, Cuban and non-Cuban, local and international directors and writers.

SPN 351C.title: GLOBALZN/FILM/MEDIA LAT AMER
SPN 351C.description: Explore globalization in Latin America through the lens of film and mass media. Spanish 351C and 377C (Topic: Global/Film/Media in Lat Am) may not both be counted.

SPN 354C.title: LIT/EXILE IN SPAN CARIBBEAN
SPN 354C.description: Explore literary texts (e.g., poetry, chronicles, narratives, and short stories) that describe exile as a trope. Examine themes, such as literature, culture and colonialism, nationality and belonging (i.e., nation, transnation, and post-nation), nature, the sensorial and subjectivity, identity politics (i.e., race, gender, and sexuality), and the ethics of writing.

SPN 355C.title: FANTASTIC FICTION FROM LAT AM
SPN 355C.description: Analyze short stories by Latin American writers that in some way represent an alternative to realism.

SPN 367C.title: SPANISH HLTH CARE PROFESSNS
SPN 367C.description: Explore both spoken and written language in health care situations. Focus on complex grammatical concepts and cultural issues that affect professional activities in Spanish-speaking communities.

SPN 367D.title: BUSINESS IN HISPAN LIFE/CUL
SPN 367D.description: Explore business Spanish. Practice verbal communication in the context of meetings, exchanges, video conferences, presentations, and professional discussion, in Spanish and in areas related to finance, marketing, accounting, operations.

SPN 368E.title: BILINGUALISM
SPN 368E.description: Examine bilingualism in the Spanish-speaking world from the perspective of individuals in societies. Spanish 368C (Topic: Bilingualism) and 368E may not both be counted.

SPN 368F.title: SPAN IN MENTAL HEALTH CONTEXTS
SPN 368F.description: Develop the Spanish language skills needed to perform multiple interventions in the mental health field when providing care to Hispanic/Latino limited English proficiency patients. Spanish 368F and 377C (Topic: Spn in Mental Health Contexts) may not both be counted.

SPN 377H.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
SPN 377H.description: Supervised individual research on a literary, linguistic, or cultural problem, culminating in a written thesis.

SPN 379C.title: CAPSTONE SEMINAR IN LIT & CUL
SPN 379C.description: Brings together central issues, concepts, and themes that define Iberian or Latin American literatures and cultures, while focusing on a specific case-study or case-studies.

SPN 601D.title: FIRST-YEAR SPANISH I
SPN 601D.description: Explore the development of speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills; build vocabulary, review basic rules and terminology of grammar; and examine Spanish-speaking cultures.

SPN 604.title: ACC INTRO SPN HERITAGE LEARNER
SPN 604.description: Designed primarily for students who have a cultural and linguistic connection to Spanish (i.e. native and near-native speakers, Spanish as home language).

SPN 610D.title: FIRST-YEAR SPANISH II
SPN 610D.description: Continue to explore the development of speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills; build vocabulary, review basic rules and terminology of grammar; and examine Spanish-speaking cultures. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

STA 235.title: DATA SCI FOR BUSINESS APPLCTNS
STA 235.description: Examine data science for business applications at the intermediate level. Explore building and validating predictive models; advanced regression modeling, including an in-depth treatment of regression; models for binary outcomes; and causal inference. Statistics 235 and 235H may not both be counted. Taught as a Web-based course.

STA 235H.title: DATA SCI FOR BUSN APPS: HON
STA 235H.description: Examine data science for business applications at the intermediate level. Explore building and validating predictive models; advanced regression modeling, including an in-depth treatment of regression; models for binary outcomes; and causal inference. Statistics 235 and 235H may not both be counted.

STA 301.title: INTRODUCTION TO DATA SCIENCE
STA 301.description: An introduction to the principles and practice of data science for business applications. Explore tidying, summarizing, and visualizing data; statistical computing in R; linear regression; introduction to predictive modeling and out-of-sample model validation; uncertainty quantification using resampling methods; basic probability models, including the normal and binomial distributions; and statistical hypothesis testing.

STA 372T.title: 16-OPTIMIZATION METHOD FINANCE
STA 372T.description: Explore quantitative methods and techniques in optimization and simulation, and their use in financial decision making. Discuss theory and application in portfolio selection, options and other derivative pricing, index tracking, risk measures, volatility estimating. Examine linear, quadratic, nonlinear, and integer programming; dynamic programming; robust optimization; Monte Carlo methods and variance reduction techniques. Emphasis will be placed on problem solving with advanced computational programming languages.

SUS 301.title: INTRO TO SUSTAINABILTY STUDIES
SUS 301.description: Introduction to basic concepts in sustainability, including the varied disciplinary approaches used to theorize and practice sustainability. Explore these concepts through foundational sustainability scholarship, place-based experiential learning opportunities, and team-based projects.

SUS 374.title: CAPSTONE EXP IN SUS STUDIES
SUS 374.description: Work independently to complete a research project that assembles and integrates knowledge and skills acquired across your university career. Current concerns and methodology in the field of Sustainability Studies; an introduction to theory and research in Sustainability topics. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

SUS 379L.title: DIRECTED INTERNSHIPS IN SUS
SUS 379L.description: Experience working in an appropriate government agency, non-governmental organization, or private business. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students.

SUS 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
SUS 679HA.description: For honors candidates in sustainability studies. Read selected works individually for one semester, followed in the second semester by the writing of an honors thesis. Participate in regular conferences with the faculty supervisor.

SUS 679HB.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
SUS 679HB.description: For honors candidates in sustainability studies. Read selected works individually for one semester, followed in the second semester by the writing of an honors thesis. Participate in regular conferences with the faculty supervisor.

T C 125K.title: SUBJECTS IN ARTS & SCIENCES
T C 125K.description: Analysis of subjects in the arts, sciences, and social sciences through reading, discussion, and lectures.

T C 126.title: PLAN II/KIPP PARTNERSHIP
T C 126.description: Mentorship of younger students attending an Austin-area Knowledge Is Power Program (KIPP) school. Through mentoring and class meetings, students will be deeply immersed in issues related to education reform, (in)equality of educational opportunity, and in promising strategies, such as those used by KIPP, for addressing such issues. Class meets August 24 and 31, September 14, 21, and 28, October 5 and 26, November 2, 16, and 30.

T C 128K.title: VOLUNTEER SERVICE ORIENTATION
T C 128K.description: Designed for those who want to mentor or tutor a student at a school that serves first generation college-going students. Receive training to volunteer at one of the KIPP (Knowledge is Power Program) Texas-Austin schools, within one of the existing Plan II/KIPP Partnership service programs, KAL Mentoring or Plan Tutoring.

T C 302.title: ART, SPORT, MEANING OF LIFE
T C 302.description: Small-group seminar involving reading, discussion, writing, and oral reporting around a central interdisciplinary topic. Designed to introduce undergraduates to scholarly analysis from an interdisciplinary perspective. Includes an introduction to University resources, such as research facilities, museums, and attendance at University lectures or performances as assigned. Multiple sections may be offered in the fall and spring with various topics and instructors. Topic title: Creative Nonfiction: The Familiar Essay

T C 303C.title: PLAN II WORLD LIT PART I
T C 303C.description: Reading of masterpieces of world literature and intensive training in writing and in critical analysis of literature.

T C 358.title: BEST PICTURES
T C 358.description: Seminar sections of about fifteen students. The subjects vary, but in each the attempt is made through careful reading, discussion, and written work to analyze and compare varied approaches to topics of lasting importance. Students must take this course twice with different topics to fulfill degree requirements. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

T C 660HA.title: THESIS COURSE: HONORS
T C 660HA.description: Directed reading followed by the writing of a substantial essay.

T C 660HB.title: THESIS COURSE: HONORS
T C 660HB.description: Directed reading followed by the writing of a substantial essay.

T D 154P.title: PROJECTS IN DESIGN AND TECHNOL
T D 154P.description: Individual projects in theatre design and technology. Explore the development and management process including designing, assisting, and producing.

T D 202T.title: FLOOR-BARRE
T D 202T.description: Fundamental study of principles and vocabulary of dance. A study of the elements of classical ballet through exploration of movement principles as defined by traditional ballet technique. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Fine Arts degree with a major in dance. Only one of the following may be counted: Theater and Dance 302T (Topic: Ballet I), 302T (Topic: Topics in Dance: Ballet I-Nonmajors), 302T (Topic 1).

T D 212.title: FIRST YEAR SOMATICS
T D 212.description: Principles and techniques of physical conditioning.

T D 212P.title: FUNDMNTL PROJ DANCE PERF & REP
T D 212P.description: Preparation and performance laboratory related to production.

T D 222P.title: PROJECTS IN DANCE PERF AND REP
T D 222P.description: Preparation and performance laboratory related to production.

T D 232P.title: ADV PROJ IN DNC PERF & REP I

T D 254P.title: PROJECTS IN DESIGN AND TECHNOL
T D 254P.description: Individual projects in theatre design and technology.

T D 301.title: INTRODUCTION TO THEATRE-WB
T D 301.description: Open to all University students except majors in the Department of Theatre and Dance. A study of theatrical texts and practices of the past and present. Taught as a Web-based course.

T D 302S.title: ARTS INTEGRATIONS FOR MULTIDIS
T D 302S.description: Through readings, videos, in-class activities, discussion, field trips, micro-teaching, and written reflection, designed to develop a practical understanding of the techniques and skills associated with integrating the arts into various contexts. Provides experience in each art content area (dance, music, theatre arts, and visual arts) to develop an introductory understanding of each of these disciplines. Fine Arts 308 and Theatre and Dance 302S may not both be counted. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

T D 303.title: FUNDAMENTALS OF ACTING
T D 303.description: Not open to theatre and dance majors. Basic principles of acting and practical work in scenes from plays. Theatre and Dance 303 and 313C may not both be counted.

T D 306.title: INTRO TO IMPROVISATIONAL DRAMA
T D 306.description: General introduction to improvisational activities with application to the theatre.

T D 311C.title: PERFORMANCE AS PUBLIC PRACTICE
T D 311C.description: Examines current and historical performance policy in order to understand how the entertainment industry operates nationally. Theatre and Dance 311 and 311C may not both be counted.

T D 312C.title: CONTEMPORARY DANCE TECHNIQUE
T D 312C.description: Intensive study of principles, technique, and vocabulary of contemporary dance. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students. May be repeated for credit.

T D 312D.title: INTERMED CONTEMP DANCE TECHNIQ
T D 312D.description: Intensive study of intermediate-level theory, technique, and vocabulary of contemporary dance. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. May be repeated for credit.

T D 312F.title: BALLET TECHNIQUE
T D 312F.description: Intensive study of principles, technique, and vocabulary of ballet.

T D 312G.title: INTERMEDIATE BALLET TECHNIQUE
T D 312G.description: Study of intermediate-level theory, technique, and vocabulary of ballet as a supporting style.

T D 312M.title: MOVEMENT IMPROVISATION
T D 312M.description: Exploration and study of elements of movement design. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. May be repeated for credit.

T D 313C.title: ACTING I
T D 313C.description: Approaches to incorporating Stanislavski-based principles in acting, including dramatic action, given circumstances, subtext, sense memory, the magic if, and tempo/rhythm.

T D 313E.title: ACTING III
T D 313E.description: Continuation of Acting II, with greater emphasis on personalization of character needs, given circumstances, actor-to-actor communication, and emotional life. Theatre and Dance 313E and 324E may not both be counted.

T D 313F.title: VOICE AND MOVEMENT I
T D 313F.description: Understanding the relationship between voice and body in acting, with a focus on freeing tension, releasing vocal power, and enabling physical expression.

T D 313K.title: VOICE LABORATORY I
T D 313K.description: Advanced voice and speech work in breath, support, placement, focus, range, resonance, articulation and endurance. Emphasis placed on personalization of language and text from selected plays.

T D 313M.title: MOVEMENT LABORATORY I
T D 313M.description: Using the body as an instrument for playing action and bringing the actor to a deeper understanding of physical stage presence.

T D 314M.title: TECHNICAL THEATRE
T D 314M.description: Introduction to the technical aspects of theatrical performance--in costumes, lighting, scenery, and media. Develop technical theatre training in the areas of lighting, scenic, sound, costume, costume technology, and media.

T D 315.title: PLAYWRITING I
T D 315.description: The study and practice of writing plays. Creative Writing 315D and Theatre and Dance 315 may not both be counted.

T D 316D.title: DIRECTING I
T D 316D.description: Study and practice of the fundamentals of stage directing: composition, dramatic action, movement, and textual analysis. Theatre and Dance 316D and 323C may not both be counted.

T D 317C.title: THEATRE HISTORY THRU 18TH CEN
T D 317C.description: Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

T D 320J.title: INTRO TO CREATING NEW MEDIA
T D 320J.description: Introduces student actors to strategies to grow as generative artists and master the basics of a few common media production tools to give their creative voice a broader reach in the world.

T D 322C.title: CONTEMPORARY DANCE TECHNIQUE
T D 322C.description: Intensive study of principles, technique, and vocabulary of contemporary dance. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Fine Arts degree with a major in dance.

T D 322D.title: INTMED CONTEMP DANCE TECHNIQUE
T D 322D.description: Intensive study of intermediate-level theory, technique, and vocabulary of contemporary dance. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Fine Arts degree with a major in dance.

T D 322E.title: ADV CONTEMPORARY DANCE TECHNIQ
T D 322E.description: Intensive study of advanced theory, technique, and style of contemporary dance.

T D 322F.title: BALLET TECHNIQUE
T D 322F.description: Intensive study of principles, technique, and vocabulary of ballet. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Fine Arts degree with a major in dance. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. May be repeated for credit.

T D 322G.title: INTERMEDIATE BALLET TECHNIQUE
T D 322G.description: Study of intermediate-level theory, technique, and vocabulary of ballet as a supporting style. May not be counted toward the Bachelor of Fine Arts degree with a major in dance.

T D 322J.title: ADVANCED BALLET TECHNIQUE
T D 322J.description: Study of advanced theory, technique, and vocabulary of ballet as a supporting style.

T D 324P.title: ADVANCED PRODUCTION LABORATORY
T D 324P.description: Theatre and Dance 124P, 224P, 324P and Arts and Entertainment Technologies 124P, 224P, 324P may not both be counted.

T D 325.title: PLAYWRITING II
T D 325.description: Emphasis on the form and writing of the full-length play or equivalent. Creative Writing 340D and Theatre and Dance 325 may not both be counted. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

T D 326C.title: THTR STUDIES: CREATIVE DRAMA
T D 326C.description: Theory and practice of creative drama for children, both as an art form and as a process for emphasizing creative expression and aesthetic growth.

T D 326D.title: THTR STUDIES: YOUNG AUDIENCES
T D 326D.description: Theory and practice of all phases of play production for young audiences.

T D 332M.title: CHOREOGRAPHY
T D 332M.description: Advanced study of the principles and practices of choreography.

T D 332R.title: DANCE PEDAGOGY
T D 332R.description: Principles, techniques, and materials used in the teaching of dance.

T D 351T.title: PERFORMING JUSTICE PROJECT
T D 351T.description: Explore the Performing Justice Project (PJP), a flexible framework for devising original performance with young people that focuses on questions of gender and racial justice. Consider the PJP framework in relationship to justice and injustice in the community. Develop and implement residencies with youth participants.

T D 352.title: EXPERIENTIAL ANATOMY
T D 352.description: Principles and techniques of physical conditioning with practical applications to injury prevention for dance and theatre practitioners.

T D 353D.title: ACTING AND THE CAMERA I
T D 353D.description: Fundamentals of acting for the camera; adjusting from the stage to the demands of the camera. Theatre and Dance 351T (Topic: Acting for the Camera) and 353D may not both be counted. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

T D 353K.title: VOICE-OVER AND NARRATION
T D 353K.description: Microphone technique and the use of the voice to create character in various media applications such as narration, video games, and commercial copy.

T D 353Q.title: PORTFOLIO PREPARATION
T D 353Q.description: Selection and rehearsal of materials in preparation for professional acting interviews and auditions. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class.

T D 353T.title: ACTING SHAKESPEARE
T D 353T.description: Topics in acting including voice/speech, movement, and directing. Explore artistic citizenship, artivism, and socially engaged artists across disciplines including music, theatre, dance, and the visual arts. Study process, collaboration, and art-making as an act of world-building through readings, archival documentation and footage, guest lectures, and community site visits.

T D 354P.title: PROJECTS IN DESIGN AND TECHNOL
T D 354P.description: Individual projects in theatre design and technology.

T D 354T.title: ADV SCNC ART/ENVRNMNTL REND
T D 354T.description: Fundamentals of research, drawing, rendering, and script/character analysis and critique for costume design. Theatre and Dance T D 354T (Topic: Design Skills: Costume) and 354T (Topic 10) may not both be counted. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary. Study the process, methodology, and skills used in costume design through play analysis, character analysis (including the psychology of clothing and the performer), costume history research methods, color theory and use in design, and how to visually present and render your design.

T D 357T.title: CURATING ACROSS THE ARTS
T D 357T.description: Explore theatrical performances from Poland, Ukraine, and Belarus that engage with national identity construction from the turbulent twentieth century into the present moment. Examine historical and contemporary case studies over a period in which each country lost and regained sovereignty. Analyze and contextualize transnational minority performances, such as Jewish theatre, as they intersect with national frames.

T D 376H.title: READING TUTORIAL T D PROBS
T D 376H.description: Individual projects to be completed under faculty supervision. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Additional hour(s) to be arranged. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. May be repeated for credit.

T D 626E.title: THTR STUDIES: DIR YOUNG PRFRMR
T D 626E.description: Introduction to the theory and practice of directing and producing theatre with young performers, with emphasis on appropriate literature.

TUR 320K.title: INTERMEDIATE TURKISH I
TUR 320K.description: Not open to native speakers of Turkish. Intermediate to high-level Turkish in four basic language skills: speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Turkish culture.

TXA 151.title: PRE-INTERNSHIP SEMINAR
TXA 151.description: Preparation course for students seeking internships. Subjects include application, internship interview process, effective professional communication, ethical decision making, leadership development, site expectations, and the impact of business environments on personal and career effectiveness in the field of textiles and apparel.

TXA 301C.title: INTRO TO THE FASHION INDUSTRY
TXA 301C.description: Explore the apparel and textiles industry with an emphasis on ethical and sustainable issues related to the industry. Examine a broad view of the interrelated fields of design, merchandising, product development, and textiles alongside emerging themes in the field. Textiles and Apparel 301 and 301C may not both be counted.

TXA 305.title: TEXTILES
TXA 305.description: Chemical and physical properties of fibers and yarns, fabric construction, and finishes.

TXA 314C.title: COMP-AIDED DESIGN FOR APPAREL
TXA 314C.description: Explore computer technology used to create textile prints, weaves, illustrations, flat patterns, promotional pieces, and pattern markers. Textiles and Apparel 314C and 355C may not both be counted.

TXA 314K.title: PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
TXA 314K.description: Introduction to all stages of product development involved in fashion and functional design with an emphasis on target market identification, cost analysis, material selection, and the organization of product development within the industry structure. Apply product development skills and strategies involved in fashion and functional design. Textiles and Apparel 303 (Topic: Product Development) and 314K may not both be counted.

TXA 317R.title: MERCHANDISING MATH I
TXA 317R.description: Explore foundational concepts in retail math using Excel. Build analytical skills and a foundation in merchandising systems.

TXA 319R.title: MERCH PRESENTATION RETAIL ENV
TXA 319R.description: Explore an integrated approach to communicating, presenting and evaluating two-and three-dimensional design strategies for merchandise presentation (both digital and brick and mortar environments). Examine components of both design and execution rooted in a foundation of visual merchandising theory, skills, and terminology.

TXA 325L.title: HIST OF DRESS/CULTRL CHANGE I
TXA 325L.description: Social, economic, aesthetic, and political aspects of costume evolution from ancient times through the Renaissance. Textiles and Apparel 325K and 325L may not both be counted.

TXA 326.title: APPAREL II
TXA 326.description: Advanced apparel construction techniques using industry standards and portfolio development.

TXA 327C.title: SOC PSY OF DRESS/APPEARANCE
TXA 327C.description: Explore cultural, social, and psychological significance of dress and appearance. Examine theories related to the ways individuals communicate and interpret the self, identity, and social roles through dress and appearance in contemporary society. Textiles and Apparel 327 and 327C may not both be counted.

TXA 331E.title: TEXTILES/APPAREL GLBL MRKTPLCE
TXA 331E.description: Explore global textile and apparel industries and the factors affecting global sourcing from product concept to distribution with an emphasis on global issues. Examine the textile and apparel complex and its history, international trade and the effects of trade policy, the sourcing process, selection of sourcing locations and partners, sourcing regions of the world, and current trends. Textiles and Apparel 331 and 331E may not both be counted.

TXA 340T.title: SUSTAINABLE TEXTILE MANUFACT
TXA 340T.description: Explore sustainable textile manufacturing practices including renewable fiber manufacturing, textile circular manufacturing, and apparel recycling.

TXA 341E.title: ACCESSORY DSGN/PROD DEV/MERCH
TXA 341E.description: Connect with industry partners to explore design and product development processes through the brand's commercial lens. Textiles and Apparel 341E and TXA 365 (Topic: Accessory Design, Product Development and Merchandising) may not both be counted.

TXA 350D.title: ADVANCED APPAREL PORTFOLIO
TXA 350D.description: Examine sociological, cultural, and historic visual research for apparel design. Explore a range of projects that incorporate computer-aided design, product illustration, technical packets, proposed production plans, and target market identification. Textiles and Apparel 350 and 350D may not both be counted.

TXA 351D.title: ADVANCED APPAREL DESIGN
TXA 351D.description: Expand range of expertise in developing design proposals to include knitted or printed textiles, other apparel categories, menswear, size inclusivity, children's wear, and accessories. Focus on expanding depth and breadth of skills as relevant to career opportunities.

TXA 355.title: RSCH IN SUSTNBL FASHION MATLS
TXA 355.description: Intensive study of selected problems of an interdisciplinary nature.

TXA 356.title: INTERNSHIP
TXA 356.description: Reflect on the internship and faculty mentorship.

TXA 360L.title: ADVANCED TEXTILES
TXA 360L.description: Composition, structure, and properties of textile products; contributions of textile research.

TXA 365.title: SUSTNBL FASHION MATRLS-FRI
TXA 365.description: Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged. Designed to accommodate 35 or fewer students. Restricted enrollment; contact the department for permission to register for this class. Course number may be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

TXA 379R.title: STRATEGIC BRAND MANAGEMENT
TXA 379R.description: Examine strategic brand management and trademark protection. Explore the process of how an institution strategically manages a product category and grants rights to a specific group of licenses.

UGS 016.title: FIRST-YR INTEREST GROUP-COLA
UGS 016.description: Explore basic issues in various interdisciplinary fields of study.

UGS 016T.title: TRANSFER INTEREST GROUP SEM-WB
UGS 016T.description: Explore basic issues in various interdisciplinary fields of study. Taught as a Web-based course.

UGS 302.title: ACES/THE POWER OF CONNECTION
UGS 302.description: Seminar class focusing on a contemporary issue. Designed to introduce undergraduates to scholarly analysis from an interdisciplinary perspective. Includes an introduction to University resources, such as research facilities, museums, and attendance at University lectures or performances as assigned. Multiple sections may be offered in the fall and spring with various topics and instructors. Difficult Dialogues Signature Courses feature dialogue-based learning, in which students engage with the course topic from multiple points of view. Faculty are trained to teach students the skills they need to participate in constructive dialogue about controversial and potentially divisive issues. Topic description: We are all survivors of loss; many times, however, we shy away from discussing losses because of fear or discomfort, and we miss opportunities for genuine connection. This course aims to make this conversation less daunting by equipping students with the practical knowledge, attitudes, and skills to tackle difficult loss and grief topics while prioritizing their well-being.

UGS 303.title: ADVENTURE LAB
UGS 303.description: Large-group lecture and discussion class focusing on a contemporary issue. Designed to introduce undergraduates to scholarly analysis from an interdisciplinary perspective. Includes an introduction to University resources, such as research facilities, museums, and attendance at University lectures or performances as assigned. Multiple sections may be offered in the fall and spring with various topics and instructors. Difficult Dialogues Signature Courses feature dialogue-based learning, in which students engage with the course topic from multiple points of view. Faculty are trained to teach students the skills they need to participate in constructive dialogue about controversial and potentially divisive issues. Topic Description: Examines how LGBTQ communities and allies have challenged conservative norms in the South through activism and resilience. It highlights historical and modern efforts by queer and trans individuals to redefine the region and share their lived experiences.

UGS 312.title: RESEARCH METHODS-MCNAIR
UGS 312.description: Interdisciplinary introduction to research methods, designed to lead students through the process of designing an undergraduate research project.

URB 301.title: INTRODUCTION TO URBAN STUDIES
URB 301.description: A multidisciplinary study of cities and complex urban environments; historical and contemporary issues from both national and international perspectives. Geography 307C and Urban Studies 301 may not both be counted.

URB 321R.title: INTRO TO RISK MANAGEMENT
URB 321R.description: Principles of risk management for individuals and organizations, financial aspects of insurance companies and markets, industry structure, managerial aspects of underwriting and pricing, and public policy issues.

URB 321U.title: URBAN SOCIOLOGY
URB 321U.description: Introduction to the study of the city and the suburbs. Specific focus on inequality in urban space, with emphasis on three major United States cities (New York, Chicago, and Los Angeles) and on several third world cities.

URB 322R.title: RACE/SOCIAL JUSTICE/CITY
URB 322R.description: Examine the way that race and inequality are built into the structures and ideas surrounding urban life through the spatial and racial structures of capitalist development, racialized migration, and redevelopment and gentrification campaigns. Analyze how United States cities are racial geographies.

URB 325.title: HIST/THEORS OF LANDSCP ARCH I
URB 325.description: Course meets September 8-December 7. Taught in New York City, New York. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

URB 325E.title: THE HEALTHY, LIVABLE CITY
URB 325E.description: Explore the design of the built environment and its potential for addressing and preventing many childhood and adult health concerns in the United States. Examine how to create healthy, walkable, vital communities for all. Geography 325E and Urban Studies 325E may not both be counted.

URB 330F.title: URBAN EXPERIENCES IN E ASIA
URB 330F.description: Explores the process of urbanization in East and Southeast Asia focusing on the politics of capital accumulation and distribution.

URB 360.title: INTERNSHIP/SERVICE LEARNING
URB 360.description: Internship experience in an urban studies-related public or nonprofit agency. The opportunity to apply the knowledge, theory, and understanding gained from courses in areas of specialization to urban issues in a professional setting. Includes an academic service-learning component.

URB 370.title: SENIOR PROJECT
URB 370.description: Students identify an urban issue, develop a position paper, and work closely with a faculty adviser on a project. Students may use text or other media (such as video or portfolio) to present their arguments.

URB 379.title: CONFERENCE COURSE
URB 379.description: Supervised individual study of selected problems in urban studies.

URB 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
URB 679HA.description: Directed reading and research or creation of an honors project, followed by the writing of a thesis.

URD 312K.title: SECOND-YEAR URDU I
URD 312K.description: Not open to native speakers of Urdu. Introduction to the Urdu script, followed by Urdu reading, composition, and conversation. Urdu 612 and 312K may not both be counted.

URD 506.title: FIRST-YEAR URDU I
URD 506.description: Not open to native speakers of Urdu. Urdu 604 and 506 may not both be counted.

URD 604.title: ACCELERATED FIRST-YEAR URDU
URD 604.description: Designed for students who have some knowledge of spoken Urdu but have not studied Urdu formally. Hindi 604 and Urdu 604 may not both be counted; Hindi 506 and Urdu 604 may not both be counted; Hindi 507 and Urdu 604 may not both be counted; Urdu 604 and 506 may not both be counted; Urdu 604 and 507 may not both be counted.

UTL 101.title: INTRO TO TEACHING PROFESSION
UTL 101.description: Weekly seminar with a University faculty member plus a field experience in an area elementary school under the supervision of a cooperating teacher. Course readings and instruction complement the field experience. Seminar topics include basic classroom management, lesson design, assessment, technology, diversity, and special student populations.

UTL 202.title: INTRO TO TEACHING MIDDLE SCHL
UTL 202.description: Weekly seminar with a University faculty member plus a field experience in an area middle school under the supervision of a cooperating teacher. Course readings and instruction complement field experience. Seminar topics include classroom management, lesson design, assessment, technology, diversity, special student populations, conferencing techniques, and school organization.

UTL 360.title: PROBS/PRINS SECONDARY EDUC
UTL 360.description: Draws upon resources found in the directed-teaching environment; considers problems and issues in four broad areas: students in the school, the teacher's professional responsibilities, curriculum understandings, and administrative and organizational problems.

UTL 640.title: TEACHING IN SECONDARY SCH-LOTE
UTL 640.description: Course readings and instruction complement the field experience. Seminar topics include theoretical foundations for subject-specific methods, state and national standards and their implementation, teaching for proficiency, curriculum design, assessment, best practices, the instructional environment, and the role of technology in teaching.

UTL 670.title: DIR TEACHING IN SECONDARY SCHL
UTL 670.description: Observation and teaching in secondary school classrooms as well as an exploration of other roles of the secondary school teacher, under the joint supervision of University and public school personnel. Partially taught as a Web-based course.

UTS 101.title: SEC TCHR EDUC PREP: STEP 1
UTS 101.description: Introduction to mathematics, computer science, and science teaching as a career. Discussions include standards-based lesson design and various teaching and behavior management strategies. Fieldwork consists of five visits to a local elementary school, including planning and teaching three inquiry-based lessons to students in grades three to six. Chemistry 107 (Topic: STEP1-UTeach) and UTeach-Natural Sciences 101 may not both be counted.

UTS 110.title: SEC TEACHER EDUC PREP: STEP 2
UTS 110.description: Topics may include routes to teacher certification in mathematics, computer science, and science teaching; various teaching methods that are designed to meet instructional goals; and learner outcomes. Students develop and teach three inquiry-based lessons in their field in a middle school, and participate in peer coaching. Biology 101C (Topic: STEP 2) and UTeach-Natural Sciences 110 may not both be counted.

UTS 121F.title: EXPLORATIONS IN STEM EDU-WB
UTS 121F.description: Earn independent field hours through classroom observations, watching and reflecting on teaching videos, and teaching or tutoring students in the schools.

UTS 170.title: STUDENT TEACHING SEMINAR-MATH
UTS 170.description: Discussions include student teaching experiences, contemporary critical issues in education, and preparation for the state certification exam. Chemistry 107 (Topic: Special Topics Seminar) and UTeach-Natural Sciences 170 may not both be counted.

UTS 171.title: UTEACH INDUCTION SUPPORT
UTS 171.description: Engage in individualized, job-embedded activities to support student learning and analyze the impact of their work.

UTS 306J.title: HANDS-ON SCIENCE I
UTS 306J.description: The first of an integrated sequence of laboratory-based courses. Subjects include energy and motion, electrical circuits, atomic theory, waves, and sound. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

UTS 306K.title: HANDS-ON SCIENCE II
UTS 306K.description: The second of an integrated sequence of laboratory-based courses. Subjects include physical and chemical properties of matter, Earth's building blocks, plate tectonics, landforms, weathering, and geologic time. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

UTS 306L.title: HANDS-ON SCIENCE III
UTS 306L.description: The third of an integrated sequence of laboratory-based courses. Subjects include properties of life, compartments of living organisms, inheritance, adaptations, variations, and disease. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

UTS 306M.title: HANDS-ON SCIENCE IV
UTS 306M.description: The fourth of an integrated sequence of laboratory-based courses. Subjects include astronomy and the earth's climate. Laboratory hour(s) to be arranged.

UTS 360.title: PROJECT-BASED INSTRUCTION
UTS 360.description: Foundations of project-based, case-based, and problem-based learning environments; principles of project-based curriculum development in mathematics and science education; classroom management and organization of project-based learning classrooms. Curriculum and Instruction 365E and UTeach-Natural Sciences 360 may not both be counted.

UTS 675.title: STU TEACH SECONDARY/MID GRADES
UTS 675.description: Closely supervised field coursework in a cooperating school. Experience includes carrying out the duties of a secondary or middle grades teacher.

WGS 301.title: 3-INTRO TO LATINX BODY ART
WGS 301.description: Exploration of over two decades of work produced by and about black queer subjects throughout the circum-Atlantic world. Provides an introduction to various artists and intellectuals of the black queer diaspora, as well as an examination of the viability of black queer aesthetic practice as a form of theorizing.

WGS 303.title: INTRODUCTION TO LGBTQ STUDIES
WGS 303.description: Explore concepts of gender and sexuality, race, class, religion, and nation; as well as skills in theory, history, and research methods relevant to LGBTQ studies. Survey the making of modern understandings of sexual and LGBTQ identities in the last one hundred years and the implications of this history for broader understandings of gender and sexuality.

WGS 305.title: INTRO TO WOMEN'S & GENDER STDS
WGS 305.description: Introduction to the role of gender identity and representation in key social institutions and processes, including borders, displacements, and diasporas. Analysis of the social narratives of gender, race, and sexuality. Women's and Gender Studies 301 (Topic: Introduction to Women's and Gender Studies) and 305 may not both be counted.

WGS 322D.title: POPULATION AND SOCIETY
WGS 322D.description: The study of populations, including their growth, age structure, and patterns of fertility, mortality, and migration; the social causes and consequences of these phenomena.

WGS 322I.title: SOCIOLOGY OF SEXUALITIES
WGS 322I.description: Review of sociological perspectives on sexuality. Examines how social institutions in United States society shape sexual values, beliefs, and practices. Subjects include changing cultural images of sexuality, sexual identities, and social movements.

WGS 335.title: 3-AFRICAN QUEER STUDIES
WGS 335.description: The perspectives, experiences, and cultural contributions of lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and queer people, examined from different disciplinary and/or interdisciplinary perspectives according to the topic. Examines the development of African queer studies as the interdisciplinary study of sexuality on the African continent, and its impact on the academic and activist realm. Focus on intersectional feminist and queer theory and how this field offers tools that can be used to rethink issues of oppression and domination in relationship to race, nation, sex, gender, and sexuality on the African continent. Of particular interest will be LGBTQ activism and the ways in which queer Africans engage and shape their sexuality, and create forms of belonging in their countries and their communities.

WGS 335J.title: LATINX SEXUALITIES
WGS 335J.description: Examine Latinx sexualities from a historical perspective to comprehend the social, cultural, political, and economic factors, which have shaped these experiences.

WGS 335L.title: BLACK FIGURE AM ART/POP CUL
WGS 335L.description: Examine how popular culture and artistic practices facilitate or challenge social constructions of "Blackness" in the United States.

WGS 340.title: DESIRABLE FUTURES
WGS 340.description: Multidisciplinary course examining experiences and issues of gender in different cultures. Exploration of various experiences and theories of sex, intimacy, and desire alongside intellectual and artistic engagements with power hierarchies and spirituality across transnational black communities. Subjects include the concept of "erotic subjectivity" from various theoretical and methodological angles, principally within African diasporic contexts.

WGS 340L.title: BLACK QUEER LITERATURE/FILM
WGS 340L.description: Analyzes written works, films, and videos by and about lesbians, bisexual, transgender, and gay black people, with an emphasis on understanding the historical and theoretical construction of sexual and gender identities and sexual/cultural practices in black communities.

WGS 345.title: THEATRE FOR DIALOGUE
WGS 345.description: Explores the role of families and concepts of gender as expressed in key economic, social, political, and cultural patterns in early modern Europe.

WGS 345J.title: WOMEN'S AUTOBIOGRAPHCL WRTG
WGS 345J.description: Examines the autobiographical impulse in women's writing by exploring the concept of the individualistic self versus the sense of self as a part of community and duty, and the way in which that communal self can both partake of humankind and participate in self-actualization.

WGS 345T.title: RACE/GEND/POLIT ECON US LIT
WGS 345T.description: Explore the intersection of race, class, and gender relations, gender expression, and gender norms as constitutive of real and imagined political economies in literature and films by black, indigenous, and Latine authors and producers in the United States.

WGS 352R.title: RHETORIC OF ETHNIC STUDIES
WGS 352R.description: Study the histories of ethnic (and gender) studies in both higher education and K-12 institutions, and the rhetoric of attacks on, and defenses of, ethnic and gender studies through an examination of primary and secondary source materials.

WGS 356.title: INTRO TO FEMINIST RSCH METHODS
WGS 356.description: Introduction to feminist research methods across a range of traditional disciplines. Designed to prepare students to analyze research within gender studies and to develop their own research skills.

WGS 379L.title: INTERNSHIP IN WGS
WGS 379L.description: Experience working in the community or for a nonprofit agency.

WGS 679HA.title: HONORS TUTORIAL COURSE
WGS 679HA.description: Individual reading of selected works for one semester, followed in the second semester by the writing of an honors thesis.

YID 604.title: ACCELERATED FIRST-YEAR YIDDISH
YID 604.description: Yiddish 406 and 604 may not both be counted; Yiddish 407 and 604 may not both be counted.

YOR 601C.title: BEGINNING YORUBA
YOR 601C.description: An intensive beginning course with an emphasis on basic skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing. Yoruba 601C and 506 may not both be counted. Yoruba 601C and 507 may not both be counted.
